# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**Q:** State the Principle of Virtual Work as applied to robotic manipulators. Using it, derive the relationship between joint torques and end-effector forces.

**Model Answer:**

The **Principle of Virtual Work (PVW)** states that for a mechanism in static equilibrium, the total virtual work done by all external forces during any virtual displacement consistent with constraints is zero.

For a manipulator with joint variables $\theta$ and end-effector pose $X$:

$$F^T \delta X - \tau^T \delta \theta = 0$$

Since $\delta X = J(\theta) \delta \theta$:

$$F^T J \delta \theta = \tau^T \delta \theta$$

This must hold for all admissible $\delta\theta$, therefore:

$$\tau = J^T(\theta) F$$

This is the **fundamental static force equation** of a robotic manipulator.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**Q:** What is a **force ellipsoid**? How is it related to the velocity ellipsoid, and what does it physically signify?

**Model Answer:**

The **force ellipsoid** is a geometric representation in task space that maps the unit-magnitude joint torque vector to the corresponding end-effector wrench. It is defined by the equation:

$$F^T (JJ^T)^{-1} F = 1$$

Its principal axes are aligned with the eigenvectors of $JJ^T$, and semi-axes lengths are $1/\sigma_i$, where $\sigma_i$ are the singular values of the Jacobian.

**Relationship to velocity ellipsoid:** The force ellipsoid is the **inverse** of the velocity ellipsoid. The semi-axis of the force ellipsoid along a direction equals the reciprocal of the corresponding velocity ellipsoid semi-axis.

**Physical significance:** A long force-ellipsoid axis indicates a direction in which the robot can exert large end-effector forces from small joint torques (high mechanical advantage). A short axis indicates a direction of poor force transmission (low mechanical advantage), typically near a kinematic singularity.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Module 2]`**

**Q:** For a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $l_1 = 0.8 \, m$ and $l_2 = 0.6 \, m$, in the configuration $\theta_1 = 60°$ and $\theta_2 = 30°$:

**(a)** Derive the Jacobian matrix relating joint velocities to end-effector linear velocities. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** If the end-effector applies a force $F = [20, -15]^T \, N$ on a workpiece, determine the joint torques required to maintain static equilibrium. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Write forward kinematics**

The end-effector position is:

$$x = l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$
$$y = l_1 \sin\theta_1 + l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate to obtain velocity equations**

$$\dot{x} = -l_1 \sin\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 - l_2 \sin(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2)$$
$$\dot{y} = l_1 \cos\theta_1 \cdot \dot\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2)(\dot\theta_1 + \dot\theta_2)$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange into Jacobian form**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \dot{x} \\ \dot{y} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -l_1 \sin\theta_1 - l_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) & -l_2 \sin(\theta_1+\theta_2) \\ l_1 \cos\theta_1 + l_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) & l_2 \cos(\theta_1+\theta_2) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \dot\theta_1 \\ \dot\theta_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values**

Given $l_1 = 0.8$, $l_2 = 0.6$, $\theta_1 = 60°$, $\theta_2 = 30°$:

- $\sin 60° = 0.866$, $\cos 60° = 0.5$
- $\sin 30° = 0.5$, $\cos 30° = 0.866$
- $\sin(90°) = 1.0$, $\cos(90°) = 0$

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -(0.8)(0.866) - (0.6)(1.0) & -(0.6)(1.0) \\ (0.8)(0.5) + (0.6)(0) & (0.6)(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.293 & -0.600 \\ 0.400 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{J = \begin{bmatrix} -1.293 & -0.600 \\ 0.400 & 0 \end{bmatrix}}$$

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Apply the static force equation**

$$\tau = J^T F$$

**Step 2 — Transpose the Jacobian**

$$J^T = \begin{bmatrix} -1.293 & 0.400 \\ -0.600 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply with the force vector**

$$\begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.293 & 0.400 \\ -0.600 & 0 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ -15 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute element-wise**

$$\tau_1 = (-1.293)(20) + (0.400)(-15) = -25.86 - 6.00 = -31.86 \, N \cdot m$$
$$\tau_2 = (-0.600)(20) + (0)(-15) = -12.00 + 0 = -12.00 \, N \cdot m$$

**Step 5 — State the result with proper units and sign convention**

$$\boxed{\tau_1 \approx -31.86 \, N \cdot m, \quad \tau_2 = -12.00 \, N \cdot m}$$

The negative sign indicates that the joint torques must act in the direction **opposite** to the positive $\theta$ direction to balance the applied force — i.e., the actuators must provide a counter-torque of $31.86 \, N \cdot m$ at joint 1 and $12.00 \, N \cdot m$ at joint 2.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Module 2]`**

**Q:**

**(a)** Explain the concept of the **force ellipsoid** in a robotic manipulator. Derive its equation starting from the unit joint torque condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-DOF manipulator with Jacobian $J = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$ at a particular configuration, compute the **force manipulability measure** $W_F = \sqrt{\det(JJ^T)}$. Comment on the configuration quality. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Define the concept**

A force ellipsoid visualizes the set of all end-effector wrenches $F$ that can be generated by unit-magnitude joint torque vectors. It is the dual of the velocity ellipsoid.

**Step 2 — Start with the unit-torque condition**

$$\|\tau\|^2 = \tau^T \tau = 1$$

**Step 3 — Apply the static force relation**

Using $\tau = J^T F$, we have $\tau^T = F^T J$. Substituting:

$$(F^T J)(J^T F) = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F^T (J J^T) F = 1$$

**Step 4 — Singular Value Decomposition**

Let $J = U \Sigma V^T$ with $\Sigma = \text{diag}(\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \ldots)$. Then:

$$J J^T = U \Sigma^2 U^T$$

**Step 5 — Transform to principal axes**

Let $\bar{F} = U^T F$, so $F = U \bar{F}$. Substituting:

$$\bar{F}^T \Sigma^2 \bar{F} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{\bar{F}_i^2}{(1/\sigma_i)^2} = 1$$

This is the standard ellipsoid equation with semi-axes $1/\sigma_i$ along the principal directions defined by $U$.

**Step 6 — Physical interpretation**

- **Long axis** = direction of high force amplification
- **Short axis** = direction of low force amplification
- **Degenerate case** ($1/\sigma_{min} \to 0$) = near singularity, infinite joint torques needed for finite force in degenerate direction

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute $JJ^T$**

$$J J^T = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

Computing element-wise:

$$\begin{aligned}
(JJ^T)_{11} &= (1)(1) + (2)(2) = 1 + 4 = 5 \\
(JJ^T)_{12} &= (1)(3) + (2)(4) = 3 + 8 = 11 \\
(JJ^T)_{21} &= (3)(1) + (4)(2) = 3 + 8 = 11 \\
(JJ^T)_{22} &= (3)(3) + (4)(4) = 9 + 16 = 25
\end{aligned}$$

$$J J^T = \begin{bmatrix} 5 & 11 \\ 11 & 25 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the determinant**

$$\det(JJ^T) = (5)(25) - (11)(11) = 125 - 121 = 4$$

**Step 3 — Compute the manipulability measure**

$$W_F = \sqrt{\det(JJ^T)} = \sqrt{4} = 2$$

**Step 4 — Compute singular values for further insight**

Characteristic equation: $\lambda^2 - 30\lambda + 4 = 0$

$$\lambda = \frac{30 \pm \sqrt{900 - 16}}{2} = \frac{30 \pm \sqrt{884}}{2} = \frac{30 \pm 29.73}{2}$$

$$\sigma_1^2 \approx 29.87, \quad \sigma_2^2 \approx 0.135$$

$$\sigma_1 \approx 5.465, \quad \sigma_2 \approx 0.367$$

**Step 5 — Compute the condition number**

$$\kappa(J) = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\sigma_{min}} = \frac{5.465}{0.367} \approx 14.9$$

**Step 6 — Comment on configuration quality**

$$\boxed{W_F = 2, \quad \kappa(J) \approx 14.9}$$

A condition number of $14.9$ indicates that this configuration is **moderately ill-conditioned** — not at a singularity, but not optimally configured either. The robot exhibits **anisotropic force transmission**, with a strong force capability along the principal axis corresponding to $\sigma_1 = 5.465$ and a much weaker capability along the orthogonal direction.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Critical!):**
> 1. **Sign convention omission:** Many students forget to explicitly state the sign convention for the joint torques. Always include a concluding line such as *"The negative sign indicates the joint actuator must produce torque in the clockwise/opposite sense."* This is worth **1 mark**.
> 2. **Unit inconsistency:** Mixing $cm$ with $m$ in length parameters loses 1–2 marks. Convert all lengths to **meters** before computation.
> 3. **Skipping the Jacobian derivation:** For 7-mark sub-part (a), you must show all four steps — kinematics, differentiation, matrix assembly, and numerical substitution. Skipping the substitution step loses **2 marks**.
> 4. **Forgetting the transpose:** Writing $\tau = J F$ (instead of $J^T F$) is a common conceptual error that deducts **at least 1 mark** and may be flagged in moderation.
> 5. **Not interpreting the result:** A bare numerical answer without interpretation (e.g., negative sign meaning) loses 1 mark in (b).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 2 — Static Forces in Manipulators: 10 High-Yield Points**

1. **Core Equation:** $\tau = J^T F$ — Joint torques equal the **transpose** of the Jacobian times the Cartesian wrench. Memorize this verbatim.

2. **Derivation Source:** Always derive the static equation from the **Principle of Virtual Work (PVW)** — not from force balance directly. KTU values this method.

3. **Wrench Structure:** A 3D wrench is 6D: $F = [f_x, f_y, f_z, m_x, m_y, m_z]^T$. A planar wrench is 3D: $F = [f_x, f_y, m_z]^T$.

4. **Jacobian Origin:** The Jacobian is derived from the partial derivatives of forward kinematics: $J_{ij} = \partial x_i / \partial \theta_j$. Always re-derive for the specific manipulator — do not rely on memorized forms.

5. **Gravity Loading:** For a payload $m_p$, the Cartesian wrench due to gravity (in base frame, Z-up) is $F = [0, 0, -m_p g, 0, 0, 0]^T$. The joint torque is $\tau_g = J^T F$.

6. **Force Ellipsoid Equation:** $F^T (JJ^T)^{-1} F = 1$, with semi-axes $1/\sigma_i$.

7. **Velocity–Force Duality:** Force ellipsoid is the **inverse** of the velocity ellipsoid. Best force direction = worst velocity direction (and vice versa).

8. **Singularity Effect:** Near a singularity ($\sigma_{min} \to 0$), the force ellipsoid collapses. **Infinite joint torques** may be required to balance a finite end-effector force.

9. **Units Discipline:** Forces in **N**, torques in **N·m**, lengths in **m**, masses in **kg**, and $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$.

10. **Numerical Protocol:** Show four explicit steps — (i) Jacobian derivation, (ii) numerical substitution, (iii) transpose, (iv) matrix multiplication — for full marks on KTU ESE numericals.

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Aid:** *"Transpose maps Cartesian to Joint; Inverse maps Joint to Cartesian — never confuse them in static force analysis."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Jacobian Framework of Robotics**, the relationship between **joint-space velocities** and **Cartesian (task-space) velocities** at the end-effector is mathematically expressed as:

$$\dot{X} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$$

where $\dot{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the end-effector velocity vector in Cartesian space, $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the **Geometric Jacobian** (also called the **velocity Jacobian**), and $\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint velocity vector. For a six-DOF spatial manipulator, $m = 6$ and $n = 6$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Force Domain Jacobian Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Statement):**
> The **Jacobian in the Force Domain** establishes the static relationship between the generalized forces (torques) applied at the robot joints and the equivalent wrench (force/torque) applied at the end-effector. Using the **Principle of Virtual Work**, this relationship is rigorously derived as:
> $$\tau = J(q)^{T}\,F$$
> where $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the joint torque vector and $F \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the Cartesian wrench (static force/moment) exerted at the end-effector.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cartesian Transformation of Velocities and Static Forces — Core Idea:**
> The Jacobian matrix acts as a **bidirectional linearizing operator** between two distinct vector spaces — the joint space (configuration space) and the Cartesian space (operational/task space). It transforms both *kinematic* quantities (velocities) and *static* quantities (forces) in a mathematically consistent manner.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of the **Jacobian in the Force Domain** like a **mechanical gear-and-lever system** in an old watchmaker's bench:

- Imagine a robotic arm pressing a pen against a sheet of paper. The *small forces* you apply at the joints (turning gears with your fingers) get *amplified or redirected* at the pen tip depending on the arm's configuration.
- When the arm is **stretched out fully**, even tiny joint torques produce **large Cartesian forces** at the tip — the arm is acting like a long lever with high mechanical advantage.
- When the arm is **folded compactly**, the same joint torques produce **smaller Cartesian forces** at the tip, but you can move the tip very *fast* with small joint motions.
- The **Jacobian Transpose** $J^{T}$ is the **mathematical "translator"** that computes exactly what joint torques you need to produce a desired end-effector force.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Steering Wheel of a Car:**
> A small angular motion of the steering wheel (joint space) translates into a large lateral motion of the front wheels (Cartesian space). The Jacobian captures this transmission ratio. Similarly, a small force at the steering column produces a magnified force at the wheel-ground contact patch — this is the force-domain counterpart, governed by $J^{T}$.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)** of a spatial manipulator: **6** (3 translations + 3 rotations).
- **Planar manipulator DOF**: **3** (2 translations + 1 rotation).
- Standard SI units:
    - Joint torque $\tau$: **N·m (Newton-meter)**
    - Cartesian force $F$: **N (Newton)**
    - Cartesian moment $n$: **N·m (Newton-meter)**
    - Joint velocity $\dot{q}$: **rad/s** (revolute) or **m/s** (prismatic)
    - Cartesian velocity $\dot{X}$: **m/s** and **rad/s** (mixed units)
- The Jacobian entries have **mixed units** depending on the corresponding joint type (linear for prismatic, angular for revolute).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structured Logic Breakdown — The Velocity-to-Force Duality

The Jacobian matrix plays a **dual role** in robotic analysis. The following bullet logic captures the complete "Why" and "How" of this duality:

- **Step 1 — Velocity Mapping (Kinematic Role):**
    - The Jacobian $J(q)$ is fundamentally a **first-order partial derivative matrix** of the forward kinematics function $X = f(q)$ with respect to the joint variables.
    - $J_{ij} = \partial f_i / \partial q_j$ relates an infinitesimal joint motion $\delta q_j$ to the resulting infinitesimal Cartesian motion $\delta X_i$.
    - **Why**: This is the linearization of the nonlinear mapping $f(q)$ around the current configuration.
    - **How**: $\dot{X} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\dot{q} = J(q)\dot{q}$.

- **Step 2 — Virtual Work Postulate (Energy Conservation):**
    - The **Principle of Virtual Work** states that for a static (or quasi-static) system in equilibrium, the *virtual work* done by all forces through any kinematically admissible virtual displacement is zero.
    - In joint space: $\delta W_{\text{joint}} = \tau^{T}\,\delta q$.
    - In Cartesian space: $\delta W_{\text{Cartesian}} = F^{T}\,\delta X$.
    - Equating: $\tau^{T}\,\delta q = F^{T}\,\delta X$.

- **Step 3 — Substituting the Velocity Relationship:**
    - Since $\delta X = J\,\delta q$ (infinitesimal version of $\dot{X} = J\dot{q}$), we get:
    - $\tau^{T}\,\delta q = F^{T}\,J\,\delta q$.
    - For this to hold for *all* admissible $\delta q \neq 0$, we must have:
    - $\tau^{T} = F^{T}\,J$ → equivalently, $\tau = J^{T}\,F$.

- **Step 4 — The Transpose Operator (Geometric Insight):**
    - $J^{T}$ maps forces from the **Cartesian space to the joint space**.
    - $J$ maps velocities from the **joint space to the Cartesian space**.
    - The transpose is a consequence of the **dual relationship** between linear velocities and static forces in Lagrangian mechanics (generalized coordinates and generalized forces).

- **Step 5 — Singularities (Critical Operational Insight):**
    - When $\det(J) = 0$ (or $\text{rank}(J) < \min(m,n)$), the manipulator is at a **singular configuration**.
    - In such configurations:
        - **Velocity Domain**: The end-effector loses one or more degrees of freedom in Cartesian space — it cannot move in certain directions regardless of joint motions.
        - **Force Domain**: The end-effector can resist arbitrary forces in the *lost directions* using *zero* joint torques — finite Cartesian forces require *infinite* joint torques to balance.
    - Near singularities, the **force transmission capability** becomes extreme (huge mechanical advantage) but motion control becomes ill-conditioned.

- **Step 6 — Physical Units Consistency Check:**
    - $[J] = [\text{m/rad}, \text{m/m}, \text{rad/rad}, \text{rad/m}]$ — mixed units per entry.
    - $[J^{T}] = [\text{N·m/N}, \text{N·m/(N·m)}]$ etc. — must yield $\tau$ in **N·m**.
    - The mixed-unit nature is preserved through the transpose and is one of the most common sources of student error in exam problems.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Equation** | **Name / Description** | **Domain** | **Dimensions** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X = f(q)$ | Forward Kinematics | Configuration → Cartesian | $X \in \mathbb{R}^{m},\ q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 2 | $\dot{X} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$ | Velocity Kinematics | Joint vel. → Cartesian vel. | $\dot{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| 3 | $\delta X = J(q)\,\delta q$ | Differential Kinematics | Joint disp. → Cartesian disp. | Infinitesimal |
| 4 | $J_{ij} = \partial f_i / \partial q_j$ | Jacobian Definition | Partial derivative of FK | $m \times n$ matrix |
| 5 | $\tau = J^{T}\,F$ | Static Force Relationship | Cartesian wrench → Joint torque | $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n},\ F \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| 6 | $F = (J^{T})^{+}\,\tau$ | Force from Joint Torques | Joint torque → Cartesian wrench | $(J^{T})^{+}$ is pseudo-inverse |
| 7 | $\delta W = \tau^{T}\,\delta q = F^{T}\,\delta X$ | Virtual Work Equation | Energy conservation principle | Scalar (Joules) |
| 8 | $v = J_v\,\dot{q}$ | Linear Velocity Part | $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ | $J_v \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ |
| 9 | $\omega = J_\omega\,\dot{q}$ | Angular Velocity Part | $\mathbb{R}^{3}$ | $J_\omega \in \mathbb{R}^{3 \times n}$ |
| 10 | $J = \begin{bmatrix} J_v \\ J_\omega \end{bmatrix}$ | Stacked Jacobian | $\mathbb{R}^{6 \times n}$ | For 6-DOF spatial arm |
| 11 | $\sigma = J\,J^{T}$ (manipulability) | Force Ellipsoid | Scalar measure | $\sigma$ — manipulability index |
| 12 | $\det(J) = 0$ | Singularity Condition | Rank deficiency | Scalar condition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Convention Note for KTU Exams:**
> When the Jacobian is derived for a **spatial manipulator** with a spherical wrist, the upper $3 \times n$ block $J_v$ gives **linear velocity** and the lower $3 \times n$ block $J_\omega$ gives **angular velocity**. The **wrench** vector is conventionally written as $F = [f^{T}\ n^{T}]^{T}$ where $f$ is the force and $n$ is the moment at the end-effector.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Industrial Assembly Robots**: The force-domain Jacobian is used to compute the joint torques required when a robot presses a part into a fixture with a specified contact force (e.g., inserting a bearing).
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci System)**: The principle of force reflection and Cartesian stiffness control relies directly on $J^{T}$ to map surgeon-applied forces at the master handle to joint torques at the slave manipulator.
- **Humanoid Robots (ASIMO, Atlas)**: Ground reaction forces measured at the feet are transformed into joint torques using the Jacobian transpose for whole-body balance control.
- **Haptic Devices**: The rendering of virtual force feedback to the operator's hand uses the $J^{T}$ mapping in real-time at kHz rates.
- **Force-Controlled Machining**: Robotic polishing and deburring applications use $J^{T}$ to maintain a constant normal force on the workpiece despite surface irregularities.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Velocity Kinematics: From Forward Kinematics to the Jacobian

We derive the relationship between joint velocities and Cartesian (end-effector) velocities rigorously.

**Given:** The forward kinematics mapping for a serial manipulator is

$$X = f(q) = \begin{bmatrix} f_1(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n) \\ f_2(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n) \\ \vdots \\ f_m(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_n) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate $X$ with respect to time $t$:**
Each Cartesian coordinate $X_i$ is a function of all joint variables $q_j$, both of which vary with time.

$$\frac{dX_i}{dt} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{\partial f_i}{\partial q_j}\,\frac{dq_j}{dt}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the partial derivative as the Jacobian entry:**
By definition, the $(i,j)$-th entry of the Jacobian is $J_{ij} = \partial f_i / \partial q_j$. Thus,

$$\dot{X}_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} J_{ij}\,\dot{q}_j$$

**Step 3 — Write in compact matrix form:**
Stacking all $m$ components of $\dot{X}$ into a column vector,

$$\dot{X} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{X}_1 \\ \dot{X}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{X}_m \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} J_{11} & J_{12} & \cdots & J_{1n} \\ J_{21} & J_{22} & \cdots & J_{2n} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ J_{m1} & J_{m2} & \cdots & J_{mn} \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \dot{q}_1 \\ \dot{q}_2 \\ \vdots \\ \dot{q}_n \end{bmatrix} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$$

This completes the derivation of the **velocity-domain Jacobian**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force-Domain Jacobian via the Principle of Virtual Work

This is the **central derivation** of this module — the bridge from kinematics to statics.

**Given:**
- A serial manipulator with $n$ joints in static equilibrium.
- An end-effector wrench $F \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ applied externally.
- Joint actuators must supply generalized forces $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ to maintain equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Virtual work in joint space:**
The virtual work done by all joint actuators through an arbitrary virtual joint displacement $\delta q$ is

$$\delta W_{\text{joint}} = \tau^{T}\,\delta q = \tau_1\,\delta q_1 + \tau_2\,\delta q_2 + \cdots + \tau_n\,\delta q_n$$

**Step 2 — Virtual work in Cartesian space:**
The virtual work done by the end-effector wrench $F$ through the corresponding virtual Cartesian displacement $\delta X$ is

$$\delta W_{\text{Cartesian}} = F^{T}\,\delta X = f_1\,\delta X_1 + f_2\,\delta X_2 + \cdots + f_m\,\delta X_m$$

**Step 3 — Equate virtual work (Principle of Virtual Work):**
By the principle of virtual work, for a system in static equilibrium, the total virtual work of all *active* (applied) forces through any virtual displacement consistent with the constraints is zero. Equivalently, the work done by joint forces equals the work done by the Cartesian wrench:

$$\tau^{T}\,\delta q = F^{T}\,\delta X$$

**Step 4 — Use the differential kinematics relation $\delta X = J\,\delta q$:**
Substitute the linearized differential kinematics:

$$\tau^{T}\,\delta q = F^{T}\,(J\,\delta q) = (F^{T}\,J)\,\delta q = (J^{T}\,F)^{T}\,\delta q$$

**Step 5 — Equate coefficients of the arbitrary $\delta q$:**
Since the virtual displacement $\delta q$ is *arbitrary* and *non-zero* in general, the coefficient vectors on both sides must be equal:

$$\tau^{T} = (J^{T}\,F)^{T} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \tau^{T} = F^{T}\,J$$

**Step 6 — Take the transpose of both sides to obtain the canonical form:**

$$\boxed{\tau = J^{T}\,F}$$

This is the **Force-Domain Jacobian relationship** — the foundation of static force analysis in robot manipulators.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Jacobian Structure for a 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

**Given:** A 2-revolute planar arm with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and joint angles $q_1, q_2$.

**Step 1 — Forward kinematics (end-effector position):**
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ L_1\sin(q_1) + L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Partial derivative of $x$ w.r.t. $q_1$:**
$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} = -L_1\sin(q_1) - L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 3 — Partial derivative of $x$ w.r.t. $q_2$:**
$$\frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} = -L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 4 — Partial derivative of $y$ w.r.t. $q_1$:**
$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} = L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 5 — Partial derivative of $y$ w.r.t. $q_2$:**
$$\frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} = L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 6 — Assemble the $2 \times 2$ Jacobian:**

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin(q_1) - L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) \\ L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) & L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 — Force-domain relationship (for planar arm, $F = [f_x, f_y]^{T}$ and $\tau = [\tau_1, \tau_2]^{T}$):**

$$\tau = J^{T}\,F = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin(q_1) - L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) & L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ -L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) & L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} f_x \\ f_y \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 8 — Singularity condition:** For this planar arm, $\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin(q_2)$. Singularities occur when $q_2 = 0$ or $q_2 = \pi$ (arm fully extended or fully folded back).

## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Velocity vs. Force Domain

| **Property** | **Velocity Domain** | **Force Domain** |
|---|---|---|
| Fundamental relation | $\dot{X} = J\,\dot{q}$ | $\tau = J^{T}\,F$ |
| Direction of mapping | Joint space → Cartesian space | Cartesian space → Joint space |
| Transpose used | No (direct $J$) | Yes ($J^{T}$) |
| Energy principle used | Instantaneous power balance | Virtual work |
| At singularity $J$ | End-effector cannot move in null-space direction | End-effector can resist forces with zero joint torque |
| Inverse (when $J$ is square) | $\dot{q} = J^{-1}\,\dot{X}$ | $F = (J^{T})^{-1}\,\tau = J^{-T}\,\tau$ |
| Redundant case ($n > m$) | $\dot{q} = J^{+}\,\dot{X} + (I - J^{+}J)\,\dot{q}_0$ | $\tau = J^{T}\,F$ (no unique inverse) |
| Physical analogy | Gear ratio — angular vel. to linear vel. | Lever — force to torque amplification |
