# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and explain the principle of virtual work as applied to derive the force-domain Jacobian relationship for a robotic manipulator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Principle of Virtual Work states that for a mechanical system in static equilibrium, the total virtual work done by all active forces through any kinematically admissible virtual displacement is zero. For a serial manipulator with joint torques $\tau$ producing end-effector wrench $F$, we equate work in joint space and Cartesian space:

$$\tau^{T}\,\delta q = F^{T}\,\delta X$$

Using the differential kinematics $\delta X = J\,\delta q$ and equating coefficients, we obtain $\tau = J^{T}\,F$, which is the force-domain Jacobian relationship. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between the velocity Jacobian and the force (static) Jacobian. What is the transpose relationship connecting them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The velocity Jacobian $J(q)$ is a $m \times n$ matrix that maps joint velocities $\dot{q}$ to end-effector Cartesian velocities $\dot{X}$ via $\dot{X} = J\dot{q}$. The force Jacobian describes the static transmission of Cartesian wrenches to joint torques through the relationship $\tau = J^{T}F$, where $J^{T}$ is the transpose of the velocity Jacobian. The transpose arises naturally from the principle of virtual work as a consequence of energy conservation between the two spaces. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** For a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1 = 1\,\text{m}$ and $L_2 = 0.5\,\text{m}$, derive the **forward kinematics equations** and the **velocity Jacobian matrix**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the principle of virtual work, derive the **force-domain Jacobian relationship** for a general $n$-DOF serial manipulator, and apply it to compute the joint torques required when the end-effector exerts a force of $\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 5 \end{bmatrix}^{T}\,\text{N}$ (in the $x$ and $y$ directions) at the configuration $q_1 = 30^{\circ}$, $q_2 = 45^{\circ}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

##### Part (a) — Forward Kinematics and Velocity Jacobian

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics:** The end-effector position $(x, y)$ for a 2-DOF planar arm:

$$x = L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1 + q_2) = 1\cdot\cos(q_1) + 0.5\cdot\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

$$y = L_1\sin(q_1) + L_2\sin(q_1 + q_2) = 1\cdot\sin(q_1) + 0.5\cdot\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 2 — Partial derivatives (Jacobian entries):**

$$J_{11} = \frac{\partial x}{\partial q_1} = -L_1\sin(q_1) - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

$$J_{12} = \frac{\partial x}{\partial q_2} = -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

$$J_{21} = \frac{\partial y}{\partial q_1} = L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)$$

$$J_{22} = \frac{\partial y}{\partial q_2} = L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 3 — Jacobian matrix:**

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1\sin(q_1) - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) & -L_2\sin(q_1+q_2) \\ L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) & L_2\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Determinant (for singularity check):**

$$\det(J) = J_{11}J_{22} - J_{12}J_{21}$$

$$= [-L_1\sin(q_1) - L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)][L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)] - [-L_2\sin(q_1+q_2)][L_1\cos(q_1) + L_2\cos(q_1+q_2)]$$

$$= -L_1 L_2 \sin(q_1)\cos(q_1+q_2) - L_2^{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\cos(q_1+q_2) + L_1 L_2 \sin(q_1+q_2)\cos(q_1) + L_2^{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\cos(q_1+q_2)$$

$$= L_1 L_2 [\sin(q_1+q_2)\cos(q_1) - \sin(q_1)\cos(q_1+q_2)]$$

Using the trigonometric identity $\sin(A)\cos(B) - \cos(A)\sin(B) = \sin(A-B)$:

$$\det(J) = L_1 L_2 \sin(q_2)$$

Singularities: $q_2 = 0$ (arm folded) and $q_2 = \pm\pi$ (arm fully extended opposite).

##### Part (b) — Force-Domain Derivation and Numerical Application

**Step 1 — Derive $\tau = J^{T}F$ from virtual work:** (See Derivation 2 in Section 3.2 for the full step-by-step proof.)

**Step 2 — Compute $J$ at $q_1 = 30^{\circ}$, $q_2 = 45^{\circ}$:**

$q_1 + q_2 = 75^{\circ}$.

$\sin(30^{\circ}) = 0.5$, $\cos(30^{\circ}) = 0.866$, $\sin(75^{\circ}) = 0.9659$, $\cos(75^{\circ}) = 0.2588$.

$$J_{11} = -1\cdot(0.5) - 0.5\cdot(0.9659) = -0.5 - 0.4830 = -0.9830$$

$$J_{12} = -0.5\cdot(0.9659) = -0.4830$$

$$J_{21} = 1\cdot(0.866) + 0.5\cdot(0.2588) = 0.866 + 0.1294 = 0.9954$$

$$J_{22} = 0.5\cdot(0.2588) = 0.1294$$

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & -0.4830 \\ 0.9954 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $J^{T}$:**

$$J^{T} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.9830 & 0.9954 \\ -0.4830 & 0.1294 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\tau = J^{T}F$ with $F = \begin{bmatrix} 10 \\ 5 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N}$:**

$$\tau_1 = (-0.9830)(10) + (0.9954)(5) = -9.830 + 4.977 = -4.853\,\text{N·m}$$

$$\tau_2 = (-0.4830)(10) + (0.1294)(5) = -4.830 + 0.647 = -4.183\,\text{N·m}$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:** Joint 1 must supply approximately $4.85\,\text{N·m}$ (clockwise) and Joint 2 must supply $4.18\,\text{N·m}$ (clockwise) to maintain the specified end-effector force.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **transformation pipeline** from joint variables to Cartesian velocity and to joint torque, identifying the role of the Jacobian at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 3-DOF Cartesian (gantry) robot has joint variables $d_1, d_2, d_3$ (all prismatic, in meters). Derive the Jacobian. If the end-effector must apply a force vector $F = \begin{bmatrix} 20 & -10 & 15 \end{bmatrix}^{T}\,\text{N}$ while moving, find the joint velocities $\dot{q}$ required to produce a Cartesian velocity $\dot{X} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 & 0.2 & -0.1 \end{bmatrix}^{T}\,\text{m/s}$. Also compute the joint torques/forces. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

##### Part (a) — Transformation Pipeline Block Diagram Explanation

**Step 1 — Pipeline Stages (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2):**
- **Stage 1 — Forward Kinematics**: Maps joint variables $q$ to Cartesian position $X = f(q)$.
- **Stage 2 — Jacobian Computation**: $J(q) = \partial f / \partial q$.
- **Stage 3a — Velocity Branch**: $J$ multiplied by $\dot{q}$ gives $\dot{X}$ in Cartesian space.
- **Stage 3b — Force Branch**: $J^{T}$ multiplied by $F$ gives $\tau$ in joint space.
- **Stage 4 — Singularity Monitor**: Continuously checks $\det(J) = 0$ to flag ill-conditioned configurations.
- **Stage 5 — Output**: Cartesian velocity (for trajectory control) and joint torques (for actuator sizing).

**Step 2 — Role of Jacobian at each stage:**
- Acts as a *linearizing operator* between nonlinear configuration and Cartesian spaces.
- In velocity domain: it is the direct mapping $J$.
- In force domain: it appears in its transpose $J^{T}$ due to the virtual work principle.
- Provides the manipulability measure $\sqrt{\det(J J^{T})}$ for path planning and obstacle avoidance.

**Step 3 — Energy consistency:** The pipeline ensures power conservation:
$$P_{\text{joint}} = \tau^{T}\dot{q} = (J^{T}F)^{T}\dot{q} = F^{T}J\dot{q} = F^{T}\dot{X} = P_{\text{Cartesian}}$$

##### Part (b) — 3-DOF Cartesian Robot Analysis

**Step 1 — Forward Kinematics for a Cartesian robot:**
$$X = \begin{bmatrix} x \\ y \\ z \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} d_1 \\ d_2 \\ d_3 \end{bmatrix}$$

(Each prismatic joint contributes directly to one Cartesian coordinate.)

**Step 2 — Jacobian by direct partial differentiation:**

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} \partial x / \partial d_1 & \partial x / \partial d_2 & \partial x / \partial d_3 \\ \partial y / \partial d_1 & \partial y / \partial d_2 & \partial y / \partial d_3 \\ \partial z / \partial d_1 & \partial z / \partial d_2 & \partial z / \partial d_3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = I_{3}$$

The Jacobian of a Cartesian robot is the **identity matrix**.

**Step 3 — Joint velocities from $\dot{X} = J\dot{q}$:**

$$\dot{q} = J^{-1}\dot{X} = I_{3}\begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5 \\ 0.2 \\ -0.1 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 4 — Joint forces (for prismatic joints, $\tau$ is linear force in N) from $\tau = J^{T}F$:**

$$\tau = I_{3}^{T}\begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ -10 \\ 15 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ -10 \\ 15 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{N}$$

**Step 5 — Power consistency check:**
$$P = \tau^{T}\dot{q} = (20)(0.5) + (-10)(0.2) + (15)(-0.1) = 10 - 2 - 1.5 = 6.5\,\text{W}$$
$$P = F^{T}\dot{X} = (20)(0.5) + (-10)(0.2) + (15)(-0.1) = 6.5\,\text{W} \quad\checkmark$$

The energy balance confirms the correctness of the Jacobian-based computation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls — DO NOT LOSE MARKS:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency Trap (2–3 marks lost frequently)**: When a Jacobian has both revolute and prismatic columns, students often write $J^{T}F$ without verifying the units. The Jacobian for a prismatic joint has **m/m** entries (dimensionless), while for a revolute joint the entries are **m/rad**. Forgetting this causes unit mismatch in the final torque/force answer. Always carry units through symbolically.
> 2. **Singularity Oversight (up to 4 marks lost)**: When a problem gives you a configuration near singularity (e.g., $q_2 = 0$ for a 2R arm), students often proceed to invert $J$ and get a numerically huge (or zero) answer. Always compute $\det(J)$ *first* and comment on its value.
> 3. **Virtual Work Statement Incomplete**: Many students write "$\tau^{T}\delta q = F^{T}\delta X$" without justifying *why* the two works are equal. The correct justification is: "By the principle of conservation of energy, the work done by joint forces must equal the work done by the end-effector forces for a system in static equilibrium."
> 4. **Wrong Stacking of Jacobian**: For a 6-DOF spatial arm, the Jacobian is conventionally stacked as $J = \begin{bmatrix} J_v \\ J_\omega \end{bmatrix}$. Some students stack it horizontally, which is wrong. The wrench vector must be $F = [f^{T}\ n^{T}]^{T}$ in the *same stacking order*.
> 5. **Confusing Velocity and Force Jacobians**: A student may write $\tau = J F$ (forgetting the transpose). This is one of the most common errors and is penalized heavily. The transpose is *non-negotiable* and arises from virtual work.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exams:**

- ✅ The **velocity Jacobian** is defined as $J_{ij}(q) = \partial f_i(q)/\partial q_j$, where $f$ is the forward kinematics map.
- ✅ The **velocity relationship** is $\dot{X} = J(q)\dot{q}$ — joint to Cartesian velocity mapping.
- ✅ The **force-domain relationship** is $\tau = J^{T}(q) F$ — Cartesian wrench to joint torque mapping.
- ✅ The transpose in $\tau = J^{T}F$ is **derived from the principle of virtual work**, not assumed.
- ✅ The **virtual work equation** is $\tau^{T}\delta q = F^{T}\delta X$ — equate works, then substitute $\delta X = J\delta q$.
- ✅ At a **singular configuration** ($\det J = 0$): the manipulator loses one or more Cartesian DOF; **finite Cartesian forces can be balanced by zero joint torques** (in the null-space direction).
- ✅ **Power invariance** is the energy-consistency check: $\tau^{T}\dot{q} = F^{T}\dot{X}$ — this equation is always true when $J$ is used correctly.
- ✅ For a **redundant manipulator** ($n > m$), the force map $\tau = J^{T}F$ has *no unique inverse*; the velocity map uses the pseudo-inverse $J^{+}$.
- ✅ The Jacobian of a **Cartesian (gantry) robot** is the identity matrix — the simplest non-trivial case.
- ✅ The Jacobian of a **2R planar arm** has determinant $L_1 L_2 \sin(q_2)$ — singular when $q_2 = 0, \pi$.
- ✅ **Force ellipsoid**: Set of all end-effector wrenches producible by unit-norm joint torques — described by $F^{T}(J J^{T})^{-1}F \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Velocity ellipsoid**: Set of all end-effector velocities producible by unit-norm joint velocities — described by $\dot{X}^{T}(J J^{T})^{-1}\dot{X} \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Manipulability index**: $w = \sqrt{\det(J J^{T})}$ — a scalar measure of how "isotropic" (well-conditioned) the manipulator is in a given configuration.
- ✅ Always carry **units** through the Jacobian computation; the mixed-unit nature (m/rad vs m/m) is the most common source of dimensional errors in KTU exam answers.
- ✅ The order of stacking in $F = [f^{T}\ n^{T}]^{T}$ **must match** the order of rows in $J = [J_v^{T}\ J_\omega^{T}]^{T}$ for a 6-DOF spatial arm.
- ✅ The relationship $\tau = J^{T}F$ holds in **quasi-static** conditions (low accelerations); for full dynamics, additional terms (Coriolis, gravitational, inertial) must be added: $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau - J^{T}F$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceleration of a Rigid Body
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Acceleration of a Rigid Body

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU 2024 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceleration of a Rigid Body** is the time rate of change of the velocity field of all particles comprising the body, expressed as the sum of (i) the translational acceleration of a reference point, (ii) the tangential (Euler) acceleration due to angular acceleration, and (iii) the centripetal (normal) acceleration due to rotation. For a robot manipulator, this governs the inertial forces at every link and is the precursor to Newton–Euler and Lagrangian dynamic formulations.

Mathematically, for a rigid body rotating about an instantaneous axis with angular velocity **Ω** and angular acceleration **α**, the acceleration of any arbitrary particle **P** located at position vector **r** from a reference point **O** is given by:

$$
\mathbf{a}_P \;=\; \mathbf{a}_O \;+\; \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \big(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}\big)
$$

where the three right-hand terms represent **translational**, **Euler (tangential)**, and **centripetal** acceleration components respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Spinning Merry-Go-Round**
> Imagine you are sitting on a merry-go-round holding a stick outward. Even if you keep the stick perfectly still in your hand, the **tip of the stick is accelerating** because it is tracing a circle. This centripetal pull toward the center is what we call the *centripetal component* — it exists purely because of rotation, **Ω × (Ω × r)**.
>
> Now, if you **twist the stick faster** (i.e., the wheel speeds up), the tip also gains a *tangential* acceleration in the direction of motion — this is the **Euler / tangential component**, **α × r**.
>
> If the entire merry-go-round is also being **pushed forward** by a worker, every point on it shares that forward push equally — this is the **translational component**, **a_O**.

So *every* particle of a moving rigid body experiences a combination of these three effects. This is precisely why a robot's end-effector acceleration profile is never trivial — even during a "simple" joint move, the wrist is simultaneously being **translated, spun faster, and pulled inward** by the linkage.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> The standard **SI unit** of linear acceleration is **m/s²**, and angular acceleration is **rad/s²**. In KTU board problems, always keep these consistent — mixing degrees and radians is the most common single-mark loss.

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Vector / Scalar |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear acceleration | **a** | **m/s²** | Vector |
| Angular acceleration | **α** | **rad/s²** | Vector |
| Angular velocity | **Ω** | **rad/s** | Vector |
| Position vector | **r** | **m** | Vector |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Acceleration Equation

The acceleration of a point P on a rigid body executing **general plane motion** (or 3D motion) is the vector sum of three physically distinct contributions. Each one arises from a different kinematic phenomenon:

- **Term 1 — Translational Acceleration `a_O`**
  - *Why:* Captures the acceleration of the chosen reference point O (origin of the body-fixed or moving frame).
  - *How:* All particles of the rigid body share this component **identically** (translation preserves relative geometry).
  - *Engineering Utility:* In a robot arm, `a_O` is the linear acceleration of the shoulder, base, or any chosen link origin.

- **Term 2 — Euler (Tangential) Acceleration `α × r_{P/O}`**
  - *Why:* Arises when the magnitude of angular velocity is changing (the body is "speeding up" or "slowing down" its spin).
  - *How:* The vector product `α × r` is perpendicular to both `α` and `r`, and lies **tangent** to the circular path traced by P.
  - *Engineering Utility:* Critical in trajectory planning when a robot joint ramps its velocity (acceleration phase) or decelerates before stopping.

- **Term 3 — Centripetal (Normal) Acceleration `Ω × (Ω × r_{P/O})`**
  - *Why:* Arises whenever the body is rotating, **regardless of whether it is speeding up or slowing down**. It is the "inward pull" required to keep P on a curved path.
  - *How:* Directed from P **toward the instantaneous center of rotation**, with magnitude `Ω²·r`.
  - *Engineering Utility:* Generates the largest inertial forces in high-speed manipulators (e.g., SCARA, delta robots) and dominates payload stress analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Board-Exam Insight:** Even when `α = 0` (constant angular velocity), the centripetal term **does not vanish**. Many students incorrectly drop it in uniform-rotation problems — this is a guaranteed half-mark loss per KTU valuation norms.

## 2.2 General Motion — Two-Reference-Frame (Moving Frame) Acceleration

When a point P moves **relative** to a rotating reference frame `xyz` which itself moves with respect to a fixed frame `XYZ`, the **absolute acceleration** contains **four** components:

$$
\mathbf{a}_P \;=\; (\mathbf{a}_P)_{xyz} \;+\; 2\,\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\mathbf{v}_P)_{xyz} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \big(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}\big) \;+\; \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}
$$

- `(a_P)_xyz` — Acceleration of P **as seen by an observer on the rotating frame**.
- `2Ω × v_rel` — **Coriolis acceleration** — the famous "extra" term that has no Newtonian counterpart.
- `Ω × (Ω × r)` — Centripetal (transport) acceleration.
- `α × r` — Euler (transport) acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Coriolis Effect Intuition:** Imagine walking radially outward on the spinning merry-go-round. From the ground, your path is a **spiral**, not a straight line — your velocity vector is constantly being "twisted" by the rotation beneath your feet. That twisting is the Coriolis acceleration, and it grows linearly with both your walking speed and the platform's spin rate.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{a}_P = \mathbf{a}_O + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O})$ | General acceleration of point P on a rigid body | Most common 7-mark problem |
| 2 | $\mathbf{a}_n = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r})$ | Centripetal (normal) component — directed toward O | Uniform rotation problems |
| 3 | $\mathbf{a}_t = \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}$ | Tangential (Euler) component — tangent to path | Variable rotation problems |
| 4 | $a_n = \Omega^2\, r$ | Magnitude of centripetal acceleration | Scalar numerical problems |
| 5 | $a_t = \alpha\, r$ | Magnitude of tangential acceleration | Scalar numerical problems |
| 6 | $\mathbf{a}_P = (\mathbf{a}_P)_{rel} + 2\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{v}_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}) + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}$ | Two-frame absolute acceleration (Coriolis form) | Slider-on-rotating-arm type problems |
| 7 | $\mathbf{a}_{Coriolis} = 2\,\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{v}_{rel}$ | Coriolis acceleration vector | Direct 3-mark conceptual question |
| 8 | $a_{P/Q} = \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/Q} - \Omega^2 \mathbf{r}_{P/Q}$ | Acceleration of P relative to Q (planar form) | Internal choice problems |

> [!TIP]
> **Sign & Direction Discipline:** When using scalar forms, treat `a_n` and `a_t` as **directed along** the local n–t axes. In KTU scripts, drawing a clear free-body vector diagram and labeling n-t at the point P can earn the diagram mark even if the final algebra is slightly off.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In robotics and mechanical engineering, the acceleration equation is the **gateway** to:

- **Inverse Dynamics** — Computing the joint torques required to produce a desired end-effector acceleration (Newton–Euler formulation).
- **Trajectory Planning** — Designing smooth, jerk-limited paths that respect actuator force limits.
- **Vibration & Structural Analysis** — Predicting inertial loads on links, gears, and bearings.
- **MEMS & Gyroscope Design** — The Coriolis term is exactly the principle behind vibrating-structure gyroscopes.
- **CNC & Machine Tool Dynamics** — Predicting cutting forces under high-speed spindle rotation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Acceleration of P from Velocity Equation

**Starting Point:** The velocity of a point P on a rigid body with respect to a fixed frame, given a reference point O, is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_P \;=\; \mathbf{v}_O \;+\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate both sides with respect to time (the definition of acceleration).**

By definition, $\mathbf{a}_P = \dfrac{d\mathbf{v}_P}{dt}$ and $\mathbf{a}_O = \dfrac{d\mathbf{v}_O}{dt}$.

$$
\frac{d\mathbf{v}_P}{dt} \;=\; \frac{d\mathbf{v}_O}{dt} \;+\; \frac{d}{dt}\big(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}\big)
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the product rule of differentiation to the cross product term.**

Let $f(t) = \boldsymbol{\Omega}(t)$ and $g(t) = \mathbf{r}_{P/O}(t)$. Then:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}) \;=\; \frac{d\boldsymbol{\Omega}}{dt} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \frac{d\mathbf{r}_{P/O}}{dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the derivatives.**

- $\dfrac{d\boldsymbol{\Omega}}{dt} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}$ — the **angular acceleration** vector.
- $\dfrac{d\mathbf{r}_{P/O}}{dt} = \mathbf{v}_{P/O} = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}$ — the velocity of P relative to O due to rotation alone.

Substitute these into Step 2:

$$
\frac{d}{dt}(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}) \;=\; \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O})
$$

**Step 4 — Assemble the final acceleration equation.**

$$
\mathbf{a}_P \;=\; \mathbf{a}_O \;+\; \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O})
$$

This is the **fundamental acceleration equation** for a rigid body in general 3D motion. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coriolis Acceleration from Two-Frame Kinematics

**Starting Point:** A point P has absolute position $\mathbf{r}_P$ expressed in a fixed frame `XYZ`. A rotating frame `xyz` (with origin at O) has angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ and angular acceleration $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ relative to the fixed frame.

**Step 1 — Decompose the position vector using a rotating unit-vector basis.**

Let $\hat{\mathbf{i}}, \hat{\mathbf{j}}, \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ be the rotating basis vectors attached to `xyz`. Then:

$$
\mathbf{r}_P \;=\; x\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} \;+\; y\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} \;+\; z\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}
$$

where $(x, y, z)$ are the coordinates of P in the rotating frame.

**Step 2 — Differentiate once to get velocity. Use the product rule, noting that the basis vectors are time-varying.**

$$
\mathbf{v}_P \;=\; \dot{x}\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \dot{y}\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \dot{z}\,\hat{\mathbf{k}} \;+\; x\,\dot{\hat{\mathbf{i}}} + y\,\dot{\hat{\mathbf{j}}} + z\,\dot{\hat{\mathbf{k}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Use the kinematic identity for the time derivative of rotating basis vectors.**

For any unit vector $\hat{\mathbf{e}}$ in a frame rotating with $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$:

$$
\dot{\hat{\mathbf{e}}} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \hat{\mathbf{e}}
$$

So $\dot{\hat{\mathbf{i}}} = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \hat{\mathbf{i}}$, etc. Substituting:

$$
x\,\dot{\hat{\mathbf{i}}} + y\,\dot{\hat{\mathbf{j}}} + z\,\dot{\hat{\mathbf{k}}} \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (x\hat{\mathbf{i}} + y\hat{\mathbf{j}} + z\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P
$$

**Step 4 — Combine into the velocity equation.**

$$
\mathbf{v}_P \;=\; (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P
$$

where $(\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} = \dot{x}\hat{\mathbf{i}} + \dot{y}\hat{\mathbf{j}} + \dot{z}\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ is the velocity of P **as observed from the rotating frame**.

**Step 5 — Differentiate the velocity equation a second time to obtain acceleration.**

$$
\mathbf{a}_P \;=\; \frac{d}{dt}\big[(\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P\big] \;=\; \frac{d(\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel}}{dt} \;+\; \frac{d\boldsymbol{\Omega}}{dt} \times \mathbf{r}_P \;+\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \frac{d\mathbf{r}_P}{dt}
$$

**Step 6 — Expand each term.**

- $\dfrac{d(\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel}}{dt} = (\mathbf{a}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel}$ — the relative acceleration plus the rotation-induced twist of the basis vectors.
- $\dfrac{d\boldsymbol{\Omega}}{dt} \times \mathbf{r}_P = \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_P$ — Euler term.
- $\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \dfrac{d\mathbf{r}_P}{dt} = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \big[(\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P\big] = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P)$

**Step 7 — Collect all four terms.**

$$
\mathbf{a}_P \;=\; (\mathbf{a}_P)_{rel} \;+\; 2\,\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} \;+\; \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P) \;+\; \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_P
$$

This is the **Coriolis acceleration theorem**. The factor 2 in front of $\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel}$ arises from the two distinct sources of cross-product coupling (Step 6). ∎

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Planar Motion: Tangential and Normal Components

**Starting Point:** For a particle P moving along a curved path in a plane, choose the reference point O on the body such that $\mathbf{r}_{P/O}$ has constant magnitude $r$.

**Step 1 — Express the centripetal term using the BAC-CAB identity.**

$$
\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}) \;=\; \boldsymbol{\Omega}\,(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \cdot \mathbf{r}) \;-\; \mathbf{r}\,(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \cdot \boldsymbol{\Omega})
$$

For planar motion, $\boldsymbol{\Omega} \perp \mathbf{r}$, so $\boldsymbol{\Omega} \cdot \mathbf{r} = 0$. Also, $\boldsymbol{\Omega} \cdot \boldsymbol{\Omega} = \Omega^2$.

$$
\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}) \;=\; -\,\Omega^2 \mathbf{r}
$$

**Step 2 — Therefore the acceleration in planar motion simplifies to:**

$$
\mathbf{a}_P \;=\; \mathbf{a}_O \;+\; \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} \;-\; \Omega^2\, \mathbf{r}_{P/O}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the two scalar magnitudes along the local n–t axes.**

- Along the **tangent** (perpendicular to $\mathbf{r}$, in the direction of motion): $a_t = \alpha\, r$
- Along the **normal** (radially inward, toward O): $a_n = \Omega^2\, r$

This is the form most KTU board problems expect when only a numerical answer is required. ∎
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow — Acceleration Components of a Rigid Body

The following block diagram maps the **logical decomposition** of the rigid-body acceleration vector into its three principal physical contributions, including the Coriolis branch for two-frame analysis.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rigid Body Acceleration of Point P] --> B[Translational Component aO]
    A --> C[Rotational Component about Reference Point O]
    C --> D[Angular velocity Ω exists]
    C --> E[Angular acceleration α exists]
    D --> F[Centripetal Term Ω × Ω × r]
    E --> G[Tangential Euler Term α × r]
    A --> H[Two Frame Moving Reference Case]
    H --> I[Relative Acceleration a rel]
    H --> J[Coriolis Term 2Ω × v rel]
    H --> K[Transport Centripetal Ω × Ω × r]
    H --> L[Transport Euler α × r]
    B --> M[Final Vector Sum aP]
    F --> M
    G --> M
    I --> M
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Output aP in m per s squared]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Architecture — Engineering Application Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Robot Joint Motion Command] --> B[Joint Position q of t]
    B --> C[Joint Velocity dq by dt]
    C --> D[Joint Acceleration d2q by dt2]
    D --> E[Forward Kinematics]
    E --> F[Linear Acceleration of End Effector]
    E --> G[Angular Acceleration of End Effector]
    F --> H[Newton Euler Inverse Dynamics]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Required Joint Torques tau]
    I --> J[Motor Sizing and Control Signal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The first diagram is the **kinematic decomposition** (what this module covers directly). The second is the **downstream engineering application** (why this module matters for Module 4 / 5 robot dynamics). Together they answer the KTU examiner's frequent question: *"Where is this used in robotics?"*

## 4.3 Physical Geometry — Planar Rigid Body Acceleration

> [!TIP]
> Since physical 2D free-body sketches of a rotating link cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following textual schematic is provided. **Reproduce this diagram in your answer script** to earn the diagram mark.

```
                  ↑ y
                  │
        ●─────────●  ← Point P (tip of link)
        │        ╱
        │      ╱  r = OP
        │    ╱
   O    │  ╱  ← a_t (tangent, along motion)
   ●────┼╱──────────→ x
        │
        ↓
       a_n (centripetal, toward O)
```

- **a_t** — Tangential (Euler) acceleration: perpendicular to **r**, magnitude $\alpha r$.
- **a_n** — Centripetal (normal) acceleration: along **−r**, magnitude $\Omega^2 r$.
- The **total** acceleration of P is the vector sum $\mathbf{a}_P = \mathbf{a}_t + \mathbf{a}_n + \mathbf{a}_O$ (with `a_O` shown at O).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**Q: Define Coriolis acceleration. State the expression for Coriolis acceleration and mention the conditions under which it becomes zero.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Coriolis acceleration is the acceleration experienced by a particle that moves with a velocity $\mathbf{v}_{rel}$ relative to a frame rotating with angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$. It is the extra inertial term that arises purely because of the coupling between the relative motion and the rotation of the reference frame.

> **Expression (1 mark):**
> $$\mathbf{a}_{Cor} = 2\,\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{v}_{rel}$$

> **Conditions for it to be zero (1 mark):** Coriolis acceleration vanishes when **either** of the following holds:
> 1. The reference frame is not rotating, i.e., $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = \mathbf{0}$.
> 2. The particle has no relative motion with respect to the rotating frame, i.e., $\mathbf{v}_{rel} = \mathbf{0}$.
> 3. $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ and $\mathbf{v}_{rel}$ are parallel (or anti-parallel) — the cross product then vanishes geometrically.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: Distinguish between Euler (tangential) acceleration and centripetal (normal) acceleration of a point on a rigid body.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Euler / Tangential Acceleration (1.5 marks):**
> - Expression: $\mathbf{a}_t = \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}$
> - Magnitude: $a_t = \alpha\, r$
> - Direction: Tangent to the path of P, perpendicular to $\mathbf{r}_{P/O}$.
> - Exists only when the body has **non-zero angular acceleration** ($\alpha \neq 0$).
> - Represents the rate of change of **speed** of P along its circular path.

> **Centripetal / Normal Acceleration (1.5 marks):**
> - Expression: $\mathbf{a}_n = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}) = -\Omega^2\,\mathbf{r}_{P/O}$
> - Magnitude: $a_n = \Omega^2\, r$
> - Direction: From P toward the instantaneous center of rotation O (radially inward).
> - Exists whenever the body is rotating, **even at constant angular velocity** ($\alpha = 0$ but $\Omega \neq 0$).
> - Represents the rate of change of **direction** of the velocity vector.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]
**A robotic link OP of length 0.5 m rotates about a fixed point O. At a given instant, the angular velocity is 4 rad/s and the angular acceleration is 6 rad/s², both directed along the +z axis (out of the page). Point P is located at $(0.5, 0, 0)$ m. The reference point O has translational acceleration $\mathbf{a}_O = (2\hat{\mathbf{i}} - 3\hat{\mathbf{j}})$ m/s².**

**(a)** Derive the general expression for the acceleration of a point P on a rigid body rotating about a point O. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Determine the total acceleration of point P in vector form and compute its magnitude. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — State the velocity equation for point P on a rotating rigid body (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{v}_P = \mathbf{v}_O + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to time (1 mark):**
$$
\frac{d\mathbf{v}_P}{dt} = \frac{d\mathbf{v}_O}{dt} + \frac{d}{dt}\big(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}\big)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the product rule to the cross product (2 marks):**
$$
\frac{d}{dt}\big(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}\big) = \frac{d\boldsymbol{\Omega}}{dt} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \frac{d\mathbf{r}_{P/O}}{dt}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the kinematic identities (2 marks):**
- $\dfrac{d\mathbf{v}_O}{dt} = \mathbf{a}_O$ — translational acceleration of O.
- $\dfrac{d\boldsymbol{\Omega}}{dt} = \boldsymbol{\alpha}$ — angular acceleration vector.
- $\dfrac{d\mathbf{r}_{P/O}}{dt} = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}$ — velocity of P relative to O.

**Step 5 — Assemble the final acceleration equation (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{a}_P = \mathbf{a}_O + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \big(\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}\big)
$$

This is the **fundamental acceleration equation** of a rigid body in general 3D motion. ∎

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given Data:**
- $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = 4\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ rad/s
- $\boldsymbol{\alpha} = 6\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ rad/s²
- $\mathbf{r}_{P/O} = 0.5\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}$ m
- $\mathbf{a}_O = 2\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} - 3\,\hat{\mathbf{j}}$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Compute the Euler (tangential) term (2 marks):**
$$
\mathbf{a}_t = \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} = (6\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times (0.5\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}) = 3.0\,(\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{i}}) = 3.0\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the centripetal term (2 marks):**
$$
\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O} = (4\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times (0.5\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}) = 2.0\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} \text{ m/s}
$$
$$
\mathbf{a}_n = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_{P/O}) = (4\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times (2.0\,\hat{\mathbf{j}}) = 8.0\,(\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{j}}) = -8.0\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Add all three components (2 marks):**
$$
\mathbf{a}_P = (2\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} - 3\,\hat{\mathbf{j}}) + (3.0\,\hat{\mathbf{j}}) + (-8.0\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}) = (2 - 8.0)\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + (-3 + 3.0)\,\hat{\mathbf{j}}
$$
$$
\mathbf{a}_P = -6.0\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + 0\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} = -6.0\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Magnitude (1 mark):**
$$
\vert \mathbf{a}_P \vert = \sqrt{(-6.0)^2 + 0^2} = 6.0 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\mathbf{a}_P = -6.0\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}$ m/s², $\vert \mathbf{a}_P \vert = 6.0$ m/s². ∎

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]
**A slider P moves along a straight rod which itself rotates about a fixed vertical axis with constant angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = 5\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ rad/s. At the instant considered, the slider is at distance $r = 0.4$ m from the axis and is moving outward (away from the axis) with relative speed $v_{rel} = 2$ m/s. The radial position is increasing at $\dot{r} = 2$ m/s and $\ddot{r} = 1$ m/s².**

**(a)** State and derive the general expression for the absolute acceleration of a point P moving in a rotating reference frame, clearly identifying all four terms. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the absolute acceleration of slider P at this instant, expressing it in vector components. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Statement (1 mark):**
The absolute acceleration of a point P that moves with relative velocity $\mathbf{v}_{rel}$ in a frame rotating with angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ and angular acceleration $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ about a fixed point O is:

$$
\mathbf{a}_P = (\mathbf{a}_P)_{rel} + 2\,\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{v}_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P) + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_P
$$

**Step 2 — Derivation begins with the position vector in the rotating frame (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{r}_P = x\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + y\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} + z\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}
$$

**Step 3 — First differentiation (velocity) using $\dot{\hat{\mathbf{e}}} = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \hat{\mathbf{e}}$ (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{v}_P = (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P
$$

**Step 4 — Second differentiation, applying the product rule (2 marks):**
$$
\mathbf{a}_P = \frac{d(\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel}}{dt} + \frac{d\boldsymbol{\Omega}}{dt} \times \mathbf{r}_P + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \frac{d\mathbf{r}_P}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute identities and expand (1 mark):**
- $\dfrac{d(\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel}}{dt} = (\mathbf{a}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel}$
- $\dfrac{d\mathbf{r}_P}{dt} = (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P$

$$
\mathbf{a}_P = (\mathbf{a}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_P + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\mathbf{v}_P)_{rel} + \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P)
$$

**Step 6 — Collect the four terms (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{a}_P = \underbrace{(\mathbf{a}_P)_{rel}}_{\text{relative}} + \underbrace{2\,\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{v}_{rel}}_{\text{Coriolis}} + \underbrace{\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P)}_{\text{centripetal}} + \underbrace{\boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_P}_{\text{Euler}}
$$

∎

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- $\boldsymbol{\Omega} = 5\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}$ rad/s (constant, so $\boldsymbol{\alpha} = \mathbf{0}$)
- $r = 0.4$ m, $\dot{r} = 2$ m/s, $\ddot{r} = 1$ m/s²
- $\mathbf{v}_{rel} = 2\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}$ (radially outward) m/s

**Step 1 — Relative acceleration (2 marks):**
Since the slider moves only along the rod, the relative acceleration is purely radial:
$$
(\mathbf{a}_P)_{rel} = \ddot{r}\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} = 1.0\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Coriolis acceleration (2 marks):**
$$
\mathbf{a}_{Cor} = 2\,\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{v}_{rel} = 2(5\,\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \times (2\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}) = 20\,(\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{i}}) = 20\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Centripetal acceleration (1 mark):**
With $\mathbf{r}_P = 0.4\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}$:
$$
\mathbf{a}_n = \boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}_P) = -\Omega^2\,\mathbf{r}_P = -(25)(0.4)\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} = -10\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Euler term (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{a}_e = \boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}_P = \mathbf{0}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum all components (1 mark):**
$$
\mathbf{a}_P = (1.0\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}) + (20\,\hat{\mathbf{j}}) + (-10\,\hat{\mathbf{i}}) + \mathbf{0} = -9.0\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + 20\,\hat{\mathbf{j}} \text{ m/s}^2
$$

> **Final Answer:** $\mathbf{a}_P = -9.0\,\hat{\mathbf{i}} + 20.0\,\hat{\mathbf{j}}$ m/s², magnitude $\vert \mathbf{a}_P \vert = \sqrt{81 + 400} = \sqrt{481} \approx 21.93$ m/s². ∎

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in this topic (KTU 2024 valuation key):**
> 1. **Dropping the centripetal term under uniform rotation** — even when $\alpha = 0$, the $\Omega^2 r$ term **must** be included. (−1 mark per occurrence)
> 2. **Sign errors in $\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{i}} = \hat{\mathbf{j}}$** — always re-derive the cyclic permutation $\hat{\mathbf{i}} \to \hat{\mathbf{j}} \to \hat{\mathbf{k}} \to \hat{\mathbf{i}}$ before computing cross products. (−0.5 to −1 mark)
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Coriolis acceleration** — the cross product appears twice during differentiation, so the coefficient is **2**, not 1. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Confusing $\mathbf{r}_{P/O}$ direction** — the vector points **from O to P**, never from P to O. Wrong direction ⇒ completely wrong sign in tangential term. (−1 mark)
> 5. **Mixing scalar $a_t = \alpha r$ with vector form** — if a 7-mark sub-question asks for the **vector** form, scalar magnitude alone earns only 3 marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- The acceleration of any point P on a rigid body is the **vector sum** of three terms: $\mathbf{a}_O$, $\boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}$, and $\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r})$.
- **Euler (tangential) term** $\boldsymbol{\alpha} \times \mathbf{r}$ — exists only when $\alpha \neq 0$, directed tangent to the path.
- **Centripetal (normal) term** $\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times (\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{r}) = -\Omega^2 \mathbf{r}$ — always present during rotation, directed radially inward.
- For a particle moving **in a rotating frame**, an **additional Coriolis term** $2\boldsymbol{\Omega} \times \mathbf{v}_{rel}$ appears.
- The BAC–CAB identity $\mathbf{A} \times (\mathbf{B} \times \mathbf{C}) = \mathbf{B}(\mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{C}) - \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{A}\cdot\mathbf{B})$ simplifies cross products of cross products in scalar problems.
- For **planar motion**, magnitudes reduce to $a_t = \alpha r$ and $a_n = \Omega^2 r$.
- Angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\Omega}$ and angular acceleration $\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ are **always along the rotation axis** (z-axis by convention).
- Unit vectors follow cyclic permutation: $\hat{\mathbf{i}} \times \hat{\mathbf{j}} = \hat{\mathbf{k}}$, $\hat{\mathbf{j}} \times \hat{\mathbf{k}} = \hat{\mathbf{i}}$, $\hat{\mathbf{k}} \times \hat{\mathbf{i}} = \hat{\mathbf{j}}$.
- **Always draw a vector diagram** in the answer script — KTU examiners award up to 1 mark for a clear, labeled n–t diagram.
- This equation is the **direct kinematic input** to Newton–Euler and Lagrangian robot dynamic formulations (covered in later modules).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equations of Motion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Equations of Motion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of rigid-body robotic manipulators, the **Equations of Motion (EoM)** are a coupled set of second-order, non-linear ordinary differential equations that relate the **generalized joint forces/torques** (actuator inputs) to the resulting **joint accelerations, velocities, and positions** of a multi-degree-of-freedom (n-DOF) serial chain, under the action of inertia, Coriolis/centrifugal effects, gravity, and external loads.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3, OECPT822):**
> "The Equations of Motion describe the mathematical model governing the dynamic behaviour of a robotic manipulator, expressed in joint space as a vector–matrix differential equation that captures inertial, Coriolis, centrifugal, and gravitational effects acting on the links."

The canonical manipulator dynamic model in joint space is written in compact form as:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})
$$

Where:
- $\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}, \ddot{\mathbf{q}} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ are the joint position, velocity, and acceleration vectors.
- $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is the symmetric, positive-definite $n \times n$ **mass (inertia) matrix**.
- $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ is the **Coriolis and centrifugal force/torque vector**.
- $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ is the **gravitational force/torque vector**.
- $\boldsymbol{\tau} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the vector of **generalized joint torques/forces** applied by the actuators.

When the end-effector interacts with the environment, an additional term appears:
$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) + \mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\,\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}
$$

where $\mathbf{J}(\mathbf{q})$ is the Jacobian and $\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$ is the external wrench at the tool.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Acrobat on a Trapeze"

Imagine an acrobat swinging on a trapeze. Three things dictate how their muscles must fire at every instant:

1. **Inertia** — how heavy the body and trapeze are (depends on the body's current shape, i.e., joint angles).
2. **Speed effects** — swinging fast sideways pulls the legs outward (centrifugal push) and twisting while moving forward creates a sideways whip (Coriolis). Both of these are *velocity-squared* effects that change with the acrobat's pose.
3. **Gravity** — a constant downward tug that the muscles must fight against to keep any limb raised.

The manipulator equation $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ is exactly this story written in math. The "muscle command" ($\boldsymbol{\tau}$) at any instant must account for the **inertial push** ($\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$), the **speed-dependent whip** ($\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$), and the **weight tug** ($\mathbf{G}$). If you ignore any of the three, the arm will either drift, vibrate, or sag.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Generalized"?** The symbol $\tau_i$ is a *force* $F$ for a prismatic (sliding) joint and a *torque* $T$ for a revolute (rotating) joint. Lagrange's formalism lets us treat them with one unified notation because they are both *conjugate* to the corresponding joint coordinate $q_i$.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Engineering Metrics

| Quantity | Symbol | Typical Unit (SI) | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Link mass | $m_i$ | **kg** | Inertial resistance to translation |
| Link inertia tensor | $\mathbf{I}_i$ | **kg·m²** | Inertial resistance to rotation about CoM |
| Gravity vector | $\mathbf{g}$ | **9.81 m/s²** along $-\hat{z}$ | Source of gravity loading |
| Joint torque | $\tau_i$ | **N·m** (revolute) or **N** (prismatic) | Actuator effort |
| Mass matrix | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ | kg or kg·m² | Configuration-dependent effective inertia |
| Coriolis matrix | $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | kg/s or kg·m²/s | Velocity coupling between joints |
| Christoffel symbols | $c_{ijk}$ | kg or kg·m² | Building blocks of the Coriolis term |

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget that the mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **configuration-dependent**. A fully extended arm has a *different* effective inertia than a folded one. This is precisely why a controller tuned for one pose fails at another.

---

## SECTION 1 SUMMARY

The Equations of Motion convert the *kinematic description* (how the links move) into a *dynamic law* (what forces/torques are needed to produce that motion). For an n-DOF manipulator, they are a set of $n$ coupled, non-linear, second-order ODEs structured as $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$, which is the fundamental input to model-based control, simulation, and trajectory planning.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Classical Derivation Routes

KTU Module 3 expects you to know **both** formalisms. They are *equivalent* but computationally and conceptually different.

### Route A — Lagrangian (Energy) Formulation
- **Principle:** D'Alembert's principle / principle of virtual work applied to *energies*.
- **Inputs needed:** Kinetic energy $K(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ and Potential energy $P(\mathbf{q})$.
- **Output:** The Euler–Lagrange equations.
- **Pros:** Compact, symbolic, ideal for symbolic software (Maple / Mathematica / SymPy).
- **Cons:** Computationally expensive for $n \ge 4$ (every partial derivative explodes in cost).

### Route B — Newton–Euler (Force-Balance) Formulation
- **Principle:** $\mathbf{F} = m\mathbf{a}$ and $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\alpha}$ written on each link.
- **Inputs needed:** Link masses, inertias, joint types, kinematic recursion.
- **Output:** Forward (kinematic) pass + Backward (kinetic) pass.
- **Pros:** $O(n)$ computational cost — real-time implementable.
- **Cons:** Yields *no* explicit closed-form $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$; the matrix form must be reconstructed if needed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** "Compare Lagrangian and Newton–Euler formulations. Which is preferred for real-time control and why?" (See Question Bank in Section 5.)

## 2.2 The Lagrangian Derivation in Steps

**Step 1 — Define the Lagrangian.**
The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ of a mechanical system is the difference between total kinetic and total potential energy.
$$
\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \;=\; K(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \;-\; P(\mathbf{q})
$$

**Step 2 — Write the kinetic energy of the manipulator.**
For an $n$-link serial chain, the total kinetic energy is the sum of translational KE of each link's centre of mass and the rotational KE about that centre of mass.
$$
K \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n}\,\left[\tfrac{1}{2}\,m_i\,\mathbf{v}_{c_i}^{T}\mathbf{v}_{c_i} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_i^{T}\mathbf{I}_{c_i}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\right]
$$
Through the Jacobian, this collapses to the celebrated **quadratic form**:
$$
K \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\,\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the potential energy.**
For rigid links under gravity $\mathbf{g} = [g_x, g_y, g_z]^T$ with no other conservative fields:
$$
P(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; -\sum_{i=1}^{n}\,m_i\,\mathbf{g}^{T}\,\mathbf{r}_{c_i}(\mathbf{q})
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Euler–Lagrange equation for each joint $i$.**
$$
\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} \;=\; \tau_i
$$

**Step 5 — Expand and identify the three terms.**
After expanding, the result is *forced* into the canonical form:
$$
\tau_i \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{n} m_{ij}(q)\,\ddot{q}_j \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk}(q)\,\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k \;+\; g_i(q)
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the Christoffel symbols of the first kind.**
$$
c_{ijk}(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial m_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial m_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial m_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)
$$
These build the Coriolis/centrifugal matrix $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$.

**Step 7 — Reassemble the matrix form.**
$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} \;=\; \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} \;+\; \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \;+\; \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})
$$

## 2.3 The Newton–Euler Derivation in Steps

**Step 1 — Forward recursion (kinematics, base → tip).**
Propagate link velocities and accelerations using:
$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i+1} \;=\; \mathbf{R}_{i}^{i+1}\!\left(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i + \dot{q}_{i+1}\,\hat{z}_{i+1}\right)
$$
$$
\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i+1} \;=\; \mathbf{R}_{i}^{i+1}\!\left(\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i + \ddot{q}_{i+1}\,\hat{z}_{i+1} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \dot{q}_{i+1}\,\hat{z}_{i+1}\right)
$$
$$
\mathbf{a}_{c_i} \;=\; \mathbf{a}_{i-1} + \dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i-1,c_i} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{r}_{i-1,c_i})
$$

**Step 2 — Backward recursion (dynamics, tip → base).**
Apply Newton–Euler on each link:
$$
\mathbf{F}_i \;=\; m_i\,\mathbf{a}_{c_i}
$$
$$
\mathbf{N}_i \;=\; \mathbf{I}_{c_i}\,\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \left(\mathbf{I}_{c_i}\,\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\right)
$$
Force/torque balance at joint $i$:
$$
\mathbf{f}_i - \mathbf{f}_{i+1} + m_i\mathbf{g} \;=\; \mathbf{F}_i
$$
$$
\mathbf{n}_i - \mathbf{n}_{i+1} - \mathbf{r}_{i,c_i} \times \mathbf{f}_i + \mathbf{r}_{i-1,c_i} \times \mathbf{f}_{i+1} \;=\; \mathbf{N}_i
$$

**Step 3 — Extract the joint torque/force.**
$$
\tau_i \;=\; \mathbf{n}_i^{T}\hat{z}_i \quad \text{(revolute)} \qquad \tau_i \;=\; \mathbf{f}_i^{T}\hat{z}_i \quad \text{(prismatic)}
$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning / When to Use | KTU Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ | Canonical manipulator EoM | ★★★★★ |
| 2 | $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Total kinetic energy as a quadratic form | ★★★★★ |
| 3 | $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ | Lagrangian definition | ★★★★★ |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | Euler–Lagrange equation | ★★★★★ |
| 5 | $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\partial m_{ij}/\partial q_k + \partial m_{ik}/\partial q_j - \partial m_{jk}/\partial q_i\right)$ | Christoffel symbol (1st kind) | ★★★★ |
| 6 | $C_{ij} = \sum_{k} c_{ijk}\,\dot{q}_k$ | Element of Coriolis matrix | ★★★★ |
| 7 | $G_i = \partial P/\partial q_i$ | Gravity term = potential-energy gradient | ★★★★ |
| 8 | $\dot{\mathbf{M}} = \mathbf{C} + \mathbf{C}^{T}$ | Skew-symmetry property of $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ | ★★★ |
| 9 | $\mathbf{F}_i = m_i\mathbf{a}_{c_i}, \quad \mathbf{N}_i = \mathbf{I}_{c_i}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_{c_i}\boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$ | Newton–Euler link equations | ★★★★ |
| 10 | $\tau_i = \mathbf{J}_v^{T}\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}\big\vert_{i}$ | Generalized force from external wrench via Jacobian transpose | ★★★ |
| 11 | $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{q}) = \sum_i m_i\mathbf{J}_{v_i}^{T}\mathbf{J}_{v_i} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}^{T}\mathbf{R}_i\mathbf{I}_{c_i}\mathbf{R}_i^{T}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_i}$ | Operational-space mass matrix | ★★ |
| 12 | $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}(\mathbf{x}) = \left(\mathbf{J}\mathbf{M}^{-1}\mathbf{J}^{T}\right)^{-1}$ | Cartesian-space inertia matrix | ★★ |
| 13 | $\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}} - \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{\mathbf{q}}}\right)^{T} - \left(\dfrac{\partial K}{\partial \mathbf{q}}\right)^{T}$ | Equivalent to Lagrange expansion | ★★★ |
| 14 | $\tau_i = \dfrac{d}{dt}\dfrac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \dfrac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} + \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$ | Energy form (since $P$ has no $\dot{q}$ dependence) | ★★★★ |
| 15 | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is symmetric, positive-definite, $n \times n$ | Physical and mathematical property | ★★★★★ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Heuristic:** Of the 14 marks for a Module-3 EoM problem, **8 marks are awarded for the derivation** and **6 marks for the final assembled matrix equation** with the three terms clearly identified. Always show the Lagrangian step — it is the "money step."

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Why the EoM matters |
|---|---|
| **Industrial robotics (KUKA, ABB, FANUC)** | The Computed-Torque Controller inverts the EoM in real time: $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ with $\mathbf{u} = \ddot{\mathbf{q}}_d + \mathbf{K}_d\dot{\mathbf{e}} + \mathbf{K}_p\mathbf{e}$. |
| **Humanoid robots (Atlas, ASIMO)** | Whole-body control requires dynamic models at 1 kHz for balance. |
| **Space manipulators (Canadarm, Dextre)** | Micro-gravity zeros out $\mathbf{G}$; Coriolis dominates. |
| **Surgical robots (Da Vinci)** | Friction and stiction models are *added* to the EoM as $\mathbf{F}_v\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{F}_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$. |
| **Vehicle dynamics (cars, drones)** | The same Lagrangian principle is used; quaternion-based extension of the EoM. |
| **Biomechanics** | Estimating joint torques during walking from measured $\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}, \ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ via inverse dynamics. |

---

## SECTION 2 SUMMARY

Two parallel routes — Lagrangian (energy-based) and Newton–Euler (force-balance) — both yield the canonical joint-space equation $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$. The Lagrangian is elegant and symbolic; the Newton–Euler is $O(n)$ and real-time. Both share the same physical ingredients: configuration-dependent inertia, velocity-squared coupling, and gravity.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical Derivation of the Manipulator Equation via Lagrangian Mechanics

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **flagship derivation** of Module 3. Memorize the structure; the algebra is identical for any $n$-DOF chain.

### Working Example: 2-DOF Planar Manipulator

Consider a 2-DOF planar arm with two uniform links of masses $m_1, m_2$ and lengths $L_1, L_2$ in the vertical plane, with joint variables $q_1$ (shoulder angle) and $q_2$ (elbow angle). The joint angles are measured from the horizontal (i.e., $q_i = 0$ means the link points along $+x$).

**Step 1 — Locate the centre-of-mass positions.**

Position of the centre of mass of link 1 (mid-point of link 1):
$$
x_{c_1} \;=\; \tfrac{L_1}{2}\cos q_1
$$
$$
y_{c_1} \;=\; \tfrac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1
$$

Position of the centre of mass of link 2 (mid-point of link 2, located at the end of link 1):
$$
x_{c_2} \;=\; L_1\cos q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$
$$
y_{c_2} \;=\; L_1\sin q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1 + q_2)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the velocities by time differentiation.**

$$
\dot{x}_{c_1} \;=\; -\tfrac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1 \cdot \dot{q}_1
$$
$$
\dot{y}_{c_1} \;=\; \tfrac{L_1}{2}\cos q_1 \cdot \dot{q}_1
$$
$$
\dot{x}_{c_2} \;=\; -L_1\sin q_1 \cdot \dot{q}_1 - \tfrac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\cdot(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)
$$
$$
\dot{y}_{c_2} \;=\; L_1\cos q_1 \cdot \dot{q}_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)\cdot(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the squared speeds $v_{c_i}^{2} = \dot{x}_{c_i}^{2} + \dot{y}_{c_i}^{2}$.**

For link 1:
$$
v_{c_1}^{2} \;=\; \dot{x}_{c_1}^{2} + \dot{y}_{c_1}^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{L_1^{2}}{4}\!\left(\sin^{2}q_1 + \cos^{2}q_1\right)\dot{q}_1^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{L_1^{2}}{4}\,\dot{q}_1^{2}
$$

For link 2 (squaring the sums and using $\sin^{2}+\cos^{2}=1$ on the inner products):
$$
\begin{aligned}
v_{c_2}^{2} &\;=\; \dot{x}_{c_2}^{2} + \dot{y}_{c_2}^{2} \\
&\;=\; L_1^{2}\dot{q}_1^{2} + \tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4}(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^{2} + L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)
\end{aligned}
$$

The cross term $L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$ comes from expanding $(\dot{x}_{c_1} \cdot \dot{x}_{c_2} + \dot{y}_{c_1}\cdot\dot{y}_{c_2})$ twice and is the famous **velocity coupling** between the two joints.

**Step 4 — Compute the rotational kinetic energy of each link.**

For a uniform slender rod rotating about its centre of mass (out-of-plane axis $\hat{z}$):
$$
I_{c_1} \;=\; \tfrac{m_1 L_1^{2}}{12}, \qquad I_{c_2} \;=\; \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{12}
$$
$$
\omega_1 \;=\; \dot{q}_1, \qquad \omega_2 \;=\; \dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2
$$

Rotational KE:
$$
K_{\text{rot}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}I_{c_1}\omega_1^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}I_{c_2}\omega_2^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{m_1 L_1^{2}}{24}\dot{q}_1^{2} + \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{24}(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Assemble total kinetic energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
K &\;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}m_1 v_{c_1}^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}m_2 v_{c_2}^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}I_{c_1}\omega_1^{2} + \tfrac{1}{2}I_{c_2}\omega_2^{2} \\
&\;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\tfrac{m_1 L_1^{2}}{4} + \tfrac{m_1 L_1^{2}}{12}\right]\dot{q}_1^{2} \\
&\quad + \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{4} + \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{12}\right](\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^{2} \\
&\quad + \tfrac{1}{2}m_2\!\left[L_1^{2}\dot{q}_1^{2} + L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)\right] \\
&\;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\tfrac{m_1 L_1^{2}}{3} + m_2 L_1^{2} + \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{3} + m_2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2\right]\dot{q}_1^{2} \\
&\quad + \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[\tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{3}\right](\dot{q}_2^{2} + 2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2) + \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left[m_2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2\right]\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Read off the mass matrix entries by collecting coefficients of $\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$.**

Matching $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ with $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = [\dot{q}_1,\dot{q}_2]^{T}$:

$$
M_{11} \;=\; \tfrac{m_1 L_1^{2}}{3} + m_2 L_1^{2} + \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{3} + m_2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2
$$
$$
M_{12} \;=\; M_{21} \;=\; \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{6} + \tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\cos q_2
$$
$$
M_{22} \;=\; \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \tfrac{m_1 L_1^{2}}{3} + m_2 L_1^{2} + \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{3} + m_2 L_1 L_2 \cos q_2 & \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{6} + \tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\cos q_2 \\[6pt] \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{6} + \tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\cos q_2 & \tfrac{m_2 L_2^{2}}{3} \end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the potential energy (gravity is the only conservative field).**

Take $\mathbf{g} = [0, -g, 0]^{T}$ (downward along $-y$):
$$
P(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; m_1 g\,y_{c_1} + m_2 g\,y_{c_2} \;=\; \tfrac{m_1 g L_1}{2}\sin q_1 + m_2 g\!\left[L_1\sin q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1 + q_2)\right]
$$

**Step 8 — Compute the gravity vector $G_i = \partial P/\partial q_i$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
G_1 &\;=\; \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_1} \;=\; \tfrac{m_1 g L_1}{2}\cos q_1 + m_2 g L_1 \cos q_1 + \tfrac{m_2 g L_2}{2}\cos(q_1 + q_2) \\
&\;=\; \left(\tfrac{m_1}{2} + m_2\right)g L_1 \cos q_1 + \tfrac{m_2 g L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{aligned}
$$
$$
G_2 \;=\; \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_2} \;=\; \tfrac{m_2 g L_2}{2}\cos(q_1 + q_2)
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \left(\tfrac{m_1}{2} + m_2\right)g L_1 \cos q_1 + \tfrac{m_2 g L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \\[6pt] \tfrac{m_2 g L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}\;}
$$

**Step 9 — Compute the Coriolis matrix via Christoffel symbols.**

Compute the partial derivatives of the mass-matrix entries:
$$
\dfrac{\partial M_{11}}{\partial q_2} \;=\; -m_2 L_1 L_2 \sin q_2
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial M_{12}}{\partial q_2} \;=\; -\tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\sin q_2
$$
$$
\dfrac{\partial M_{22}}{\partial q_2} \;=\; 0
$$

Apply $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}(\partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k + \partial M_{ik}/\partial q_j - \partial M_{jk}/\partial q_i)$ for the only $q$-dependent variable $q_k = q_2$:

$$
c_{112} \;=\; c_{121} \;=\; c_{211} \;=\; -\tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\sin q_2
$$
$$
c_{122} \;=\; c_{212} \;=\; c_{221} \;=\; -\tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\sin q_2
$$
$$
c_{222} \;=\; 0
$$

Build the Coriolis matrix elements $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k = c_{ij1}\dot{q}_1 + c_{ij2}\dot{q}_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{11} &\;=\; c_{111}\dot{q}_1 + c_{112}\dot{q}_2 \;=\; -\tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_2 \\
C_{12} &\;=\; c_{121}\dot{q}_1 + c_{122}\dot{q}_2 \;=\; -\tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\sin q_2 \cdot (\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \\
C_{21} &\;=\; c_{211}\dot{q}_1 + c_{212}\dot{q}_2 \;=\; -\tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1 \\
C_{22} &\;=\; c_{221}\dot{q}_1 + c_{222}\dot{q}_2 \;=\; -\tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\sin q_2 \cdot \dot{q}_1
\end{aligned}
$$

Factoring out $\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ via $C_{ij}\dot{q}_j$ form yields the *commonly tabulated* symmetric form:

$$
\boxed{\;\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -h\dot{q}_2 & -h(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) \\[4pt] h\dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}\quad\text{with}\quad h \;=\; \tfrac{m_2 L_1 L_2}{2}\sin q_2\;}
$$

**Step 10 — Final assembly of the manipulator equation.**

$$
\boxed{\;\boldsymbol{\tau} \;=\; \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{\mathbf{q}} \;+\; \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\,\dot{\mathbf{q}} \;+\; \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})\;}
$$

With $\boldsymbol{\tau} = [\tau_1, \tau_2]^{T}$ and the explicit $2 \times 2$ matrices derived above. Expanding for clarity:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &\;=\; M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 - h\dot{q}_2^{2} - 2h\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + G_1 \\
\tau_2 &\;=\; M_{21}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + h\dot{q}_1^{2} + G_2
\end{aligned}
$$

where $M_{ij}$, $h$, and $G_i$ are the previously derived closed-form expressions.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Property $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is Skew-Symmetric

This property is critical for **passivity-based control** (e.g., Slotine–Li adaptive controller), and KTU frequently asks to *prove* it.

**Starting identity:** the Christoffel symbol definition.
$$
c_{ijk} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \dfrac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \dfrac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)
$$

**Step 1 — Time-derivative of the mass matrix element.**
$$
\dot{M}_{ij} \;=\; \sum_{k}\dfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k
$$

**Step 2 — Build the combination $C_{ij} + C_{ji}$.**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{ij} + C_{ji} &\;=\; \sum_k \left(c_{ijk} + c_{jik}\right)\dot{q}_k \\
&\;=\; \sum_k \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \dfrac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \dfrac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i} + \dfrac{\partial M_{ji}}{\partial q_k} + \dfrac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i} - \dfrac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j}\right)\dot{q}_k \\
&\;=\; \sum_k \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(2\dfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\right)\dot{q}_k \quad \text{(by symmetry } M_{ij}=M_{ji}) \\
&\;=\; \sum_k \dfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k \;=\; \dot{M}_{ij}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Conclude.** Therefore $\dot{\mathbf{M}} = \mathbf{C} + \mathbf{C}^{T}$, equivalently $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ is skew-symmetric. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering meaning:** This identity guarantees that the *kinetic-energy storage function* $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ satisfies $\dot{K} = \dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\boldsymbol{\tau}$ *only if* the Coriolis term is chosen to satisfy this skew-symmetry — it is the *conservation-of-energy* constraint that picks out a unique $\mathbf{C}$ from the infinitely many possible decompositions of the $c_{ijk}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k$ term.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Energy, Force, and Coefficient Mapping

| Term in $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Physical Origin | Energy Source | Coefficient Function | Symmetry Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Inertia (resistance to acceleration) | $\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | $M_{ij} = \sum_k \left(m_k \mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{i}\cdot\mathbf{J}_{v_k}^{j} + \mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{i}\cdot\mathbf{R}_k\mathbf{I}_{c_k}\mathbf{R}_k^{T}\mathbf{J}_{\omega_k}^{j}\right)$ | Symmetric, positive-definite |
| $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Coriolis + Centrifugal | $K$ (velocity-squared) | $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$ | $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$ skew-symmetric |
| $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ | Gravity loading | $P(\mathbf{q}) = -\sum m_i\mathbf{g}^{T}\mathbf{r}_{c_i}$ | $G_i = \partial P/\partial q_i$ | Conservative (gradient field) |
| $\mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$ | Contact / environment | — | Virtual-work conjugate | Non-conservative in general |
| $\mathbf{F}_v\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ | Viscous friction | Dissipative | Diagonal positive | Symmetric positive-definite |
| $\mathbf{F}_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | Coulomb friction | Dissipative | Diagonal positive | Non-smooth |

---

## SECTION 3 SUMMARY

We performed the *complete* Lagrangian derivation of the manipulator equation for the 2-DOF planar arm, recovered the mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$, the Coriolis matrix $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$, and the gravity vector $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ element-by-element, and proved the skew-symmetry property of $\dot{\mathbf{M}} - 2\mathbf{C}$. The three-term canonical form $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ is the universal deliverable.
