# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy-Flow Architecture of the Manipulator Equation

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the physical and mathematical interactions within the dynamic model. It is rendered as a flow diagram rather than a physical link drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ACTUATOR TORQUE INPUT<br/>tau] --> B[JOINT SPACE<br/>DYNAMIC MODEL]
    B --> C[INERTIAL TERM<br/>M of q times q double dot]
    B --> D[CORIOLIS CENTRIFUGAL<br/>C of q q dot times q dot]
    B --> E[GRAVITY TERM<br/>G of q]
    B --> F[FRICTION TERMS<br/>F v q dot plus F c sgn q dot]
    B --> G[EXTERNAL LOAD<br/>J transpose F ext]
    H[MEASURED JOINT STATE<br/>q q dot q double dot] --> I[FEEDBACK CONTROLLER<br/>Computed Torque or Slotine Li]
    I --> A
    C --> J[ROBOT LINKS<br/>RIGID BODY MOTION]
    D --> J
    E --> J
    F --> J
    G --> J
    J --> K[TASK SPACE RESPONSE<br/>x x dot x double dot]
    K --> L[END EFFECTOR AND ENVIRONMENT]
    M[ENERGY STORAGE KINETIC<br/>0.5 q dot transpose M q dot] -.feeds.-> C
    N[ENERGY STORAGE POTENTIAL<br/>P of q] -.feeds.-> E
    O[LAGRANGIAN LAGR<br/>K minus P] -.orchestrates.-> B
```

**How to read the chart:**
- The **green inputs** are the actuator command $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ and the *measured state feedback* $(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}},\ddot{\mathbf{q}})$.
- The **central block** is the manipulator equation, decomposed into the five physical terms.
- The **dashed arrows** show the *energy bookkeeping*: kinetic energy $K$ contributes to the inertial and Coriolis terms; potential energy $P$ contributes to the gravity term; the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ orchestrates the whole model.
- The **robot links** translate the joint-space torques into the task-space response of the end-effector.

## 4.2 Lagrangian Derivation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[STEP 1<br/>Define Lagrangian<br/>L equals K minus P] --> S2[STEP 2<br/>Express K and P<br/>as functions of q and q dot]
    S2 --> S3[STEP 3<br/>Apply Euler Lagrange<br/>d by dt dL by d q dot<br/>minus dL by dq<br/>equals tau]
    S3 --> S4[STEP 4<br/>Expand derivatives<br/>Use chain rule and Jacobian]
    S4 --> S5[STEP 5<br/>Collect terms in q double dot q dot squared q]
    S5 --> S6[STEP 6<br/>Identify M of q C of q q dot and G of q]
    S6 --> S7[STEP 7<br/>Assemble matrix form<br/>tau equals M q double dot plus C q dot plus G]
```

**Reading the chart:** The pipeline moves from *scalar energy* to *vector equation of motion* in seven well-defined steps. Each step is a guaranteed-mark gain in a KTU written answer.

## 4.3 Newton–Euler Two-Pass Recursion

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FORWARD[FORWARD PASS BASE TO TIP KINEMATICS]
        F1[Link 0 base state<br/>omega zero equals zero<br/>q dot zero equals zero] --> F2[Link 1<br/>omega 1 q dot 1 z 1<br/>a c 1 by rigid body formula]
        F2 --> F3[Link 2<br/>omega 2 omega 1 q dot 2 z 2<br/>a c 2 by rigid body formula]
        F3 --> F4[Link n<br/>omega n a c n]
    end
    subgraph BACKWARD[BACKWARD PASS TIP TO BASE DYNAMICS]
        B1[Tip link n<br/>f n plus 1 equals zero<br/>n n plus 1 equals zero<br/>external wrench] --> B2[Link n Newton Euler<br/>F n equals m n a c n<br/>N n equals I n alpha n plus omega cross I omega]
        B2 --> B3[Link n minus 1<br/>solve f n and n n from balance]
        B3 --> B4[Link 1<br/>solve f 1 and n 1]
        B4 --> B5[Joint torques<br/>tau i equals n i transpose z i revolute<br/>tau i equals f i transpose z i prismatic]
    end
    FORWARD --> BACKWARD
```

**Reading the chart:** Two synchronized passes — *forward* propagates motion, *backward* propagates force. The connection between them is at the tip: the *forward* pass computes what motion the tip undergoes, and the *backward* pass starts with the (possibly zero) external wrench at the tip and propagates the joint torques needed to produce that motion.

## 4.4 Block Diagram of the Computed-Torque Controller (Application of EoM)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    QD[DESIRED TRAJECTORY<br/>q d q dot d q double dot d] --> DIFF[ERROR COMPUTATION<br/>e equals q d minus q]
    DIFF --> GAIN[FEEDBACK GAINS<br/>K p e plus K d e dot]
    GAIN --> INV[MODEL INVERSION<br/>u equals q double dot d plus K p e plus K d e dot]
    INV --> EOM[MANIPULATOR EOM<br/>tau equals M u plus C q dot plus G]
    EOM --> ROBOT[PHYSICAL ROBOT<br/>joints and links]
    ROBOT --> SENS[ENCODERS AND SENSORS<br/>q and q dot]
    SENS --> DIFF
    style INV fill:#e1f5e1
    style EOM fill:#ffe1e1
    style GAIN fill:#e1e1ff
```

**Reading the chart:** The computed-torque controller **inverts** the manipulator equation in real time. The feedback gains $\mathbf{K}_p, \mathbf{K}_d$ are chosen by the user, but the dynamic inversion $\mathbf{M}^{-1}$ and the feedforward $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ depend *entirely* on the EoM derived in Module 3. This is the central engineering reason why deriving the EoM is non-negotiable.

---

## SECTION 4 SUMMARY

Four diagrams collectively describe the **EoM system**: (1) the energy-flow architecture of the dynamic model, (2) the seven-step Lagrangian pipeline, (3) the two-pass Newton–Euler recursion, and (4) the computed-torque controller that *uses* the EoM in real time. Together they constitute the *visual syllabus* of Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OECPT822, Q1a]**
**Define the Lagrangian of a mechanical system. State the Euler–Lagrange equation with generalized forces and identify each term.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ is defined as the difference between the total kinetic energy $K(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ and the total potential energy $P(\mathbf{q})$ of a mechanical system:
> $$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) = K(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}}) - P(\mathbf{q})$$
> The Euler–Lagrange equation with generalized force $\tau_i$ acting on the $i$-th generalized coordinate $q_i$ is:
> $$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$
> The first term, $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}_i$, is the **generalized momentum** conjugate to $q_i$. Its time derivative is the **inertial force**. The second term, $-\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial q_i$, is the **generalized conservative force**. The right-hand side $\tau_i$ is the **non-conservative generalized force** applied by the actuators.
> 
> **Marking key:** 1 mark for Lagrangian definition, 1 mark for Euler–Lagrange equation, 1 mark for term identification.

---

### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OECPT822, Q2b]**
**What is the physical significance of each term in the manipulator dynamic equation $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$? Mention one property of the mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> - **$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ — Inertial term:** represents the joint torques required to accelerate the manipulator links against their configuration-dependent inertia.
> - **$\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ — Coriolis and centrifugal term:** represents the joint torques required to overcome the velocity-dependent coupling forces that arise because rotating joints induce motion in other joints.
> - **$\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ — Gravity term:** represents the joint torques required to support the manipulator's weight against gravity; it is configuration-dependent and vanishes in micro-gravity.
> 
> **Property of the mass matrix:** The mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ is **symmetric**, **positive-definite**, and therefore non-singular (invertible) for all $\mathbf{q}$. It is also configuration-dependent and its diagonal entries $M_{ii}$ are strictly positive, while off-diagonal entries $M_{ij}$ can be positive, negative, or zero depending on the geometry.
> 
> **Marking key:** 1 mark per term + 1 mark (split) for the property. Do not write just "symmetric" — write "symmetric, positive-definite, configuration-dependent."

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Format:** Each Part-B question is 14 marks, split into two sub-parts of 7 marks each. The student attempts **one** of the two alternative full questions (A or B). Both A and B below are calibrated to a 14-mark answer.

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Lagrangian Derivation of 2-DOF Planar Arm*

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, OECPT822, Q7a/b, adapted]**

Consider a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link masses $m_1$ and $m_2$, link lengths $L_1$ and $L_2$, with both links uniform slender rods. The joints are revolute and operate in a vertical plane under gravity $g$. Take $q_1$ as the angle of link 1 from the horizontal and $q_2$ as the angle of link 2 *relative* to link 1.

#### (a) **[7 marks]** Derive the mass matrix $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ and the gravity vector $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ for this manipulator using the Lagrangian formulation.

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Locate CoM positions** (as in Section 3.1, Step 1):
$$x_{c_1}=\tfrac{L_1}{2}\cos q_1,\;\; y_{c_1}=\tfrac{L_1}{2}\sin q_1$$
$$x_{c_2}=L_1\cos q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2),\;\; y_{c_2}=L_1\sin q_1 + \tfrac{L_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

2. **Time-derivatives and squared speeds** (Section 3.1, Steps 2–3):
$$v_{c_1}^{2} = \tfrac{L_1^{2}}{4}\dot{q}_1^{2},\quad v_{c_2}^{2}=L_1^{2}\dot{q}_1^{2}+\tfrac{L_2^{2}}{4}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^{2}+L_1 L_2\cos q_2\,\dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)$$

3. **Total kinetic energy** (Section 3.1, Step 5):
$$K=\tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$$

4. **Mass matrix** (1 mark for form, 1 mark per off-diagonal, 1 mark for $M_{22}$, 1 mark for $M_{11}$):
$$\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}\tfrac{m_1L_1^{2}}{3}+m_2L_1^{2}+\tfrac{m_2L_2^{2}}{3}+m_2L_1L_2\cos q_2 & \tfrac{m_2L_2^{2}}{6}+\tfrac{m_2L_1L_2}{2}\cos q_2 \\[6pt] \tfrac{m_2L_2^{2}}{6}+\tfrac{m_2L_1L_2}{2}\cos q_2 & \tfrac{m_2L_2^{2}}{3}\end{bmatrix}$$

5. **Potential energy** (1 mark):
$$P=\tfrac{m_1gL_1}{2}\sin q_1+m_2gL_1\sin q_1+\tfrac{m_2gL_2}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

6. **Gravity vector** (1 mark for $G_1$, 1 mark for $G_2$):
$$\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \begin{bmatrix}\left(\tfrac{m_1}{2}+m_2\right)gL_1\cos q_1 + \tfrac{m_2gL_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)\\[6pt] \tfrac{m_2gL_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)\end{bmatrix}$$

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Derive the Coriolis matrix $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ and write the complete manipulator equation $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ in expanded form.

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Partial derivatives of $M_{ij}$ wrt $q_2$** (1 mark):
$$\tfrac{\partial M_{11}}{\partial q_2}=-m_2L_1L_2\sin q_2,\quad \tfrac{\partial M_{12}}{\partial q_2}=-\tfrac{m_2L_1L_2}{2}\sin q_2,\quad \tfrac{\partial M_{22}}{\partial q_2}=0$$

2. **Christoffel symbols $c_{ijk}$** (2 marks — must show all six non-zero entries):
$$c_{112}=c_{121}=c_{211}=c_{122}=c_{212}=c_{221}=-\tfrac{m_2L_1L_2}{2}\sin q_2$$

3. **Coriolis matrix** (2 marks):
$$h = \tfrac{m_2L_1L_2}{2}\sin q_2$$
$$\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})=\begin{bmatrix}-h\dot{q}_2 & -h(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\\[4pt] h\dot{q}_1 & 0\end{bmatrix}$$

4. **Verify skew-symmetry property** $\dot{\mathbf{M}}-\mathbf{C}-\mathbf{C}^{T}=0$ (1 mark — examiner-pleaser): differentiating the entries of $\mathbf{M}$ and confirming that $\dot{M}_{11}=-m_2L_1L_2\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_2 = -2h\dot{q}_2$ and matching $C_{11}+C_{11}^{T}=-2h\dot{q}_2$ etc.

5. **Expanded manipulator equation** (1 mark for structure, 1 mark for algebra):
$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 &= \left(\tfrac{m_1L_1^{2}}{3}+m_2L_1^{2}+\tfrac{m_2L_2^{2}}{3}+m_2L_1L_2\cos q_2\right)\ddot{q}_1 \\
&\quad + \left(\tfrac{m_2L_2^{2}}{6}+\tfrac{m_2L_1L_2}{2}\cos q_2\right)\ddot{q}_2 \\
&\quad - \tfrac{m_2L_1L_2}{2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_2^{2} - m_2L_1L_2\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 \\
&\quad + \left(\tfrac{m_1}{2}+m_2\right)gL_1\cos q_1 + \tfrac{m_2gL_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \\[6pt]
\tau_2 &= \left(\tfrac{m_2L_2^{2}}{6}+\tfrac{m_2L_1L_2}{2}\cos q_2\right)\ddot{q}_1 + \tfrac{m_2L_2^{2}}{3}\ddot{q}_2 \\
&\quad + \tfrac{m_2L_1L_2}{2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1^{2} + \tfrac{m_2gL_2}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Newton–Euler & Lagrangian Comparison*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, OECPT822, Q8a/b, adapted]**

#### (a) **[7 marks]** State and explain the Newton–Euler formulation for a serial $n$-DOF manipulator. Write the forward and backward recursion equations.

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Principle statement (2 marks):** The Newton–Euler formulation is a *force-balance* approach. Newton's second law $\mathbf{F}=m\mathbf{a}$ is applied for translation and Euler's equation $\mathbf{N}=\mathbf{I}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}+\boldsymbol{\omega}\times(\mathbf{I}\boldsymbol{\omega})$ is applied for rotation, on each link of the manipulator. The joint torques are recovered by projecting the link forces/torques onto the joint axes.

2. **Forward recursion — kinematics, base to tip (3 marks):** Starting from $\boldsymbol{\omega}_0=\mathbf{0}$ and $\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_0=\mathbf{0}$, propagate the angular velocity, angular acceleration, and linear acceleration of each link's centre of mass:
$$\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i} = \mathbf{R}_{i-1}^{i}(\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}+\dot{q}_i\hat{z}_i)$$
$$\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i}=\mathbf{R}_{i-1}^{i}(\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{i-1}+\ddot{q}_i\hat{z}_i+\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}\times\dot{q}_i\hat{z}_i)$$
$$\mathbf{a}_{c_i}=\mathbf{R}_{i-1}^{i}\mathbf{a}_{c_{i-1}}+\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i\times\mathbf{r}_{i-1,c_i}+\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\times(\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\times\mathbf{r}_{i-1,c_i})$$

3. **Backward recursion — dynamics, tip to base (2 marks):** Starting from $\mathbf{f}_{n+1}=\mathbf{0},\;\mathbf{n}_{n+1}=\mathbf{0}$ (or the external tip wrench), apply on each link:
$$\mathbf{F}_i=m_i\mathbf{a}_{c_i},\qquad \mathbf{N}_i=\mathbf{I}_{c_i}\dot{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_i+\boldsymbol{\omega}_i\times(\mathbf{I}_{c_i}\boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$$
Force and moment balance:
$$\mathbf{f}_i=\mathbf{R}_{i}^{i+1}\mathbf{f}_{i+1}+\mathbf{F}_i-m_i\mathbf{g},\qquad \mathbf{n}_i=\mathbf{R}_{i}^{i+1}\mathbf{n}_{i+1}+\mathbf{N}_i+\mathbf{r}_{c_i,i}\times\mathbf{F}_i+\mathbf{R}_{i}^{i+1}\mathbf{r}_{i+1,c_{i+1}}\times\mathbf{f}_{i+1}$$
The joint torque/force is recovered by:
$$\tau_i=\mathbf{n}_i^{T}\hat{z}_i\;\;(\text{revolute}),\qquad \tau_i=\mathbf{f}_i^{T}\hat{z}_i\;\;(\text{prismatic})$$

#### (b) **[7 marks]** Compare the Newton–Euler and Lagrangian formulations. Which is more suitable for real-time control and why? State the canonical form of the manipulator equation.

**Step-by-step model solution:**

1. **Comparison table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Lagrangian (Energy) | Newton–Euler (Force) |
|---|---|---|
| Foundation | D'Alembert / Hamilton's principle | Newton's 2nd law + Euler's equation |
| Variables | Energies $K$, $P$ (scalar) | Forces, accelerations (vectors) |
| Required inputs | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}),\;P(\mathbf{q})$ | $\mathbf{I}_{c_i},m_i,\mathbf{J}_i$ for each link |
| Output form | Closed-form $\mathbf{M},\mathbf{C},\mathbf{G}$ | Direct $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ values per joint |
| Computation order | $O(n^{4})$ — explodes for $n\geq 6$ | $O(n)$ — linear in DOF |
| Real-time suitability | Poor for high-DOF | Excellent — used in industrial controllers |
| Insight gained | Physical structure (symmetric $\mathbf{M}$, skew-symmetric $\dot{\mathbf{M}}-2\mathbf{C}$) | Direct force flow, easy to add friction/contact |
| Implementation | Symbolic (Maple/Mathematica) | Numerical (real-time C-code) |

2. **Real-time answer (2 marks):** The **Newton–Euler formulation** is preferred for real-time control because its $O(n)$ computational complexity allows it to run at kHz rates on industrial processors, while the Lagrangian formulation's $O(n^{4})$ cost makes it impractical for $n \geq 6$ in real time. However, the Lagrangian form is *derived once, offline*, to obtain the symbolic $\mathbf{M},\mathbf{C},\mathbf{G}$ matrices used in model-based controllers like the **computed-torque controller**.

3. **Canonical manipulator equation (1 mark):**
$$\boldsymbol{\tau}=\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}}+\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}}+\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})+\mathbf{J}^{T}(\mathbf{q})\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls on Equations-of-Motion Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the rotational kinetic energy** $\tfrac{1}{2}I_{c_i}\omega_i^{2}$. A student who includes only translational KE loses **2–3 marks** outright.
> 2. **Writing $\sin q_2$ instead of $\cos q_2$** in the cross-coupling term of $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ — the geometry is from the position, not the velocity. *Slow down* and re-derive.
> 3. **Stating "$\mathbf{M}$ is symmetric" without "positive-definite"** — half a mark lost, repeated twice across the paper = full mark.
> 4. **Mixing $q_2$ as absolute vs. relative angle.** This is *the* classic trap. The KTU problem statement always specifies "relative to link 1" or "absolute from base" — read it twice.
> 5. **Forgetting $\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$ in the canonical form** when the question says "with external load." Lose 1 mark.
> 6. **Omitting units on numerical values of $G_i$.** Always write $g = 9.81$ m/s² or $9.8$ m/s² — the absence of units costs 0.5 marks under strict KTU marking.
> 7. **Writing $c_{ijk} = \partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k$** — this is the wrong Christoffel form. The correct symmetric form must be used; half-mark penalty.
> 8. **Skipping the verification of $\dot{\mathbf{M}}=\mathbf{C}+\mathbf{C}^{T}$.** Examiners specifically award a free 1-mark for this check; leaving it out forfeits it.
> 9. **In Newton–Euler, swapping forward and backward passes.** Memorize: *Forward is motion (kinematics), Backward is force (dynamics).* A swapped recursion is logically inconsistent.
> 10. **Treating $\tau_i$ as always a torque.** For a *prismatic* joint, $\tau_i$ is a force, and the formula uses $\mathbf{f}_i^{T}\hat{z}_i$ not $\mathbf{n}_i^{T}\hat{z}_i$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Equations of Motion (Module 3)**

- [x] **Canonical form:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$. Memorize it verbatim.
- [x] **Lagrangian definition:** $\mathcal{L} = K - P$. Kinetic + Potential.
- [x] **Euler–Lagrange equation:** $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial\dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial\mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$.
- [x] **Energy quadratic form:** $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{\mathbf{q}}^{T}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\dot{\mathbf{q}}$. Read off $M_{ij}$ from the coefficient of $\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$.
- [x] **Three physical terms and their origins:**
  - $\mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ ← inertia
  - $\mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}}$ ← Coriolis + centrifugal (velocity-squared)
  - $\mathbf{G}$ ← gravity (potential-energy gradient)
- [x] **Christoffel symbols:** $c_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \dfrac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \dfrac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$.
- [x] **Skew-symmetry property:** $\dot{\mathbf{M}} = \mathbf{C} + \mathbf{C}^{T}$ — used in Slotine–Li adaptive control.
- [x] **Mass matrix properties:** symmetric, positive-definite, $n \times n$, configuration-dependent.
- [x] **Gravity vector:** $G_i = \partial P/\partial q_i$. Vanishes in micro-gravity.
- [x] **Newton–Euler pass direction:** Forward = kinematics (base→tip), Backward = dynamics (tip→base).
- [x] **Computational complexity:** Lagrangian $O(n^{4})$, Newton–Euler $O(n)$.
- [x] **Real-time preference:** Newton–Euler is the workhorse for industrial controllers; Lagrangian is the theoretical foundation.
- [x] **External load term:** Add $\mathbf{J}^{T}\mathbf{F}_{\text{ext}}$ whenever the end-effector touches the environment.
- [x] **Friction model (bonus):** $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{fric}} = \mathbf{F}_v\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{F}_c\,\text{sgn}(\dot{\mathbf{q}})$.
- [x] **Joint-type duality:** Revolute ⇒ $\tau_i = \mathbf{n}_i^{T}\hat{z}_i$ (torque). Prismatic ⇒ $\tau_i = \mathbf{f}_i^{T}\hat{z}_i$ (force).
- [x] **Energy conservation check:** The Coriolis matrix must be chosen to satisfy $\dot{\mathbf{M}}-2\mathbf{C}$ skew-symmetric, otherwise the kinetic energy leaks.

---

### 📌 Final Memory Aid — *The 3-2-1 Mnemonic for Module 3*

> **3 energy sources:** Kinetic $K$, Potential $P$, Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = K - P$.
> **2 formulation routes:** Lagrangian (symbolic elegance) and Newton–Euler (real-time efficiency).
> **1 canonical equation:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$ — the *one* formula to rule them all.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Forward and Inverse Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Forward and Inverse Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Forward Dynamics — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Forward Dynamics** is the problem of computing the resulting motion of a manipulator (i.e., the joint accelerations $\ddot{q}$ and the generalized forces at the end-effector) when the joint torques/forces $\tau$ are known *a priori*.

Mathematically, given the manipulator dynamics equation:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

Forward Dynamics solves this implicit second-order differential equation for the unknown joint accelerations $\ddot{q}$:

$$\ddot{q} = M(q)^{-1}\left[\tau - C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} - G(q)\right]$$

This is essentially a **"cause-to-effect"** problem.

### 1.2 Inverse Dynamics — Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Dynamics** is the problem of computing the joint torques/forces $\tau$ required to produce a prescribed motion trajectory (i.e., specified $q$, $\dot{q}$, $\ddot{q}$), while overcoming the inertial, Coriolis, centrifugal, and gravitational effects acting on the manipulator links.

Substituting the known motion into the dynamics equation:

$$\tau = M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q)$$

This is essentially a **"effect-to-cause"** problem.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Driving a Car on a Curved Hill**
> 
> Imagine you are driving an automatic car up a steep, curved mountain road.
> 
> - **Forward Dynamics** is like asking: *"If I press the accelerator pedal with a force of **X Newtons** for **Y seconds**, what speed and position will the car reach?"* — You know the **input force**, and the **motion is the output**.
> - **Inverse Dynamics** is like asking: *"To reach the next hairpin bend at exactly 40 km/h in 5 seconds, how hard and for how long must I press the accelerator (and brakes)?"* — You know the **desired motion**, and the **required forces/torques are the output**.
> 
> In a robot manipulator: motors apply **torques** (cause) → links **move** (effect). Inverse dynamics is far more common in **control engineering**, because we want a robot to *follow* a desired trajectory, and we must compute the torques needed to do so.

### 1.4 Key Distinction Table

| Aspect | Forward Dynamics | Inverse Dynamics |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Known Quantity** | Joint torques $\tau$ | Joint motion $q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q}$ |
| **Unknown Quantity** | Joint accelerations $\ddot{q}$ | Joint torques $\tau$ |
| **Mathematical Nature** | Integration of ODEs | Closed-form algebraic evaluation |
| **Computational Cost** | High ($O(n^3)$) | Lower ($O(n)$) via RNEA |
| **Common Application** | Simulation, animation, crash testing | Model-based control, trajectory planning |
| **Key Algorithm** | Articulated-Body Algorithm (ABA) | Recursive Newton-Euler Algorithm (RNEA) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Dynamics is Needed (Beyond Kinematics)

Kinematics describes *how* links move relative to each other. Dynamics explains *why* they move — the relationship between **forces/torques** and **motion**. For any practical robot, dynamics is the gateway to:

- **High-performance servo control** (computed-torque, feed-forward cancellation)
- **Realistic simulation** (digital twins, virtual commissioning)
- **Structural design** (motor sizing, gearbox selection)
- **Energy optimization** (minimum-torque trajectories)

### 2.2 The Two Principal Formulations

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes **two equivalent routes** to obtain the dynamics equation. They must yield the *same* $\tau$, but they differ in computational philosophy.

#### A. Lagrangian (Energy-Based) Formulation

Steps to remember:
1. Compute the **kinetic energy** $K(q,\dot{q})$ of every link.
2. Compute the **potential energy** $P(q)$ of every link (gravity contribution).
3. Form the **Lagrangian** $L = K - P$.
4. Apply the **Euler-Lagrange equation** for each joint $i$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

5. After expansion, the result is the **standard manipulator dynamics equation**:

$$\tau_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\,\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} \Gamma_{ijk}(q)\,\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + G_i(q)$$

- The first term is the **inertial torque** (joint space mass matrix $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$, which is symmetric and positive-definite).
- The second term captures **Coriolis and centrifugal** effects via the Christoffel symbols $\Gamma_{ijk}$.
- The third term $G_i(q)$ is the **gravitational torque**.

#### B. Newton–Euler (Force-Balance) Formulation

Steps to remember:
1. **Outward recursion (i = n … 1):** propagate link velocities $\omega_i, v_i$ and accelerations $\dot{\omega}_i, \dot{v}_i$ from the base outward to the end-effector.
2. **Inward recursion (i = n … 1):** propagate forces and moments $f_i, n_i$ from the end-effector back to the base.
3. The joint torque is extracted as the projection of the moment onto the joint axis.

This algorithm is $O(n)$ computationally and is the workhorse of real-time industrial controllers.

### 2.3 Standard (Compact) Manipulator Dynamics Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single vector-matrix equation is the **most tested equation** in KTU Module 3 and must be memorized.

$$\boxed{\,M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau\,}$$

Where:
- $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}$ — **Joint-space mass (inertia) matrix**, symmetric, positive-definite.
- $C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — **Coriolis and centrifugal torque vector**.
- $G(q) \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — **Gravitational torque vector**.
- $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — **Generalized joint force/torque vector** applied by the actuators.
- $q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — Joint position, velocity, and acceleration.

Including friction and external forces:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau + \tau_{\text{ext}}$$

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Formula / Meaning | Units (SI) | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Lagrangian | $L = K - P$ | J (Joule) | Energy-based derivation |
| Euler-Lagrange | $\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | N·m | Derive joint torque symbolically |
| Mass matrix $M(q)$ | $M = \sum_{i} \left[m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T \, {}^{i}\!I_i \, J_{\omega_i}\right]$ | kg·m² | Inertial coupling between joints |
| Coriolis matrix | $C_{ij} = \sum_k \Gamma_{ijk}\,\dot{q}_k$ | N·m·s | Velocity-dependent torques |
| Christoffel symbol | $\Gamma_{ijk} = \tfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\tfrac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \tfrac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \tfrac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ | kg·m²/s | Computes Coriolis & centrifugal |
| Gravity vector | $G_i = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$ | N·m | Static load on motor at rest |
| Kinetic energy (link $i$) | $K_i = \tfrac{1}{2}\,m_i v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\omega_i^T \,{}^{i}\!I_i\, \omega_i$ | J | Total kinetic energy $K = \sum K_i$ |
| Potential energy (link $i$) | $P_i = m_i\, g^T\, p_{c_i}$ | J | Total potential energy $P = \sum P_i$ |
| Forward dynamics | $\ddot{q} = M^{-1}\!\left[\tau - C\dot{q} - G\right]$ | rad/s² | Simulation, animation |
| Inverse dynamics | $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ | N·m | Computed-torque control |
| RNEA complexity | $O(n)$ operations | — | Real-time control |
| ABA complexity | $O(n^2)$ for forward dynamics | — | High-fidelity simulation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The mass matrix $M(q)$ is *configuration-dependent* — a robot is hardest to accelerate when it is stretched out (long lever arm) and easiest when folded. This is exactly why humanoid robots walk with bent knees!
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Euler–Lagrange Equation for a Single Revolute Joint

**Step 1 — Position of the center of mass of link $i$:**

The center of mass of link $i$, expressed in the base frame, is some function of all preceding joint variables:

$$p_{c_i} = p_{c_i}(q_1, q_2, \ldots, q_i)$$

**Step 2 — Velocity of the center of mass of link $i$ (chain rule):**

$$v_{c_i} = \frac{d\,p_{c_i}}{dt} = \sum_{j=1}^{i} \frac{\partial p_{c_i}}{\partial q_j}\,\dot{q}_j = \sum_{j=1}^{i} J_{v_i}^{(j)}\, \dot{q}_j$$

Here $J_{v_i}^{(j)} = \dfrac{\partial p_{c_i}}{\partial q_j}$ is the **linear Jacobian contribution** of joint $j$ to the velocity of link $i$'s COM.

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy of link $i$ (translational + rotational):**

$$K_i = \frac{1}{2}\,m_i\,v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2}\,\omega_i^T\,{}^{i}\!I_i\,\omega_i$$

Express angular velocity similarly: $\omega_i = \sum_{j=1}^{i} J_{\omega_i}^{(j)}\,\dot{q}_j$.

Substituting and summing over all $n$ links:

$$K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} \left[m_i\, J_{v_i}^{(j)\,T} J_{v_i}^{(k)} + J_{\omega_i}^{(j)\,T}\,{}^{i}\!I_i\, J_{\omega_i}^{(k)}\right]\,\dot{q}_j\,\dot{q}_k$$

**Step 4 — Define the joint-space mass matrix entry:**

$$M_{jk}(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[m_i\, J_{v_i}^{(j)\,T} J_{v_i}^{(k)} + J_{\omega_i}^{(j)\,T}\,{}^{i}\!I_i\, J_{\omega_i}^{(k)}\right]$$

Therefore the total kinetic energy becomes the elegant quadratic form:

$$K = \frac{1}{2}\,\dot{q}^T\,M(q)\,\dot{q}$$

**Step 5 — Potential energy of link $i$ (gravity acts along $-\hat{z}$ in base frame):**

$$P_i = m_i\,g^T\,p_{c_i}(q) \quad\Rightarrow\quad P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,g^T\,p_{c_i}(q)$$

**Step 6 — Form the Lagrangian and apply Euler–Lagrange for joint $i$:**

$$L = K - P = \frac{1}{2}\,\dot{q}^T\,M(q)\,\dot{q} - P(q)$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\,\dot{q}_j$$

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\,\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\,\dot{q}_j\,\dot{q}_k$$

$$\frac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\,\dot{q}_j\,\dot{q}_k - \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$$

**Step 7 — Combine (note the index tricks that yield the Christoffel symbols):**

$$\tau_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\,\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\underbrace{\left[\frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)\right]}_{\Gamma_{ijk}(q)}\,\dot{q}_j\,\dot{q}_k \;-\; \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$$

This is the **Euler–Lagrange equation in index form**. Identifying the components:

- Inertial term: $\sum_j M_{ij}\,\ddot{q}_j$
- Coriolis/centrifugal term: $\sum_j (C_{ij})\,\dot{q}_j$ where $C_{ij} = \sum_k \Gamma_{ijk}\,\dot{q}_k$
- Gravitational term: $G_i = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$

**Step 8 — Final compact vector-matrix form:**

$$\boxed{\;M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau\;}$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — 2-Link Planar Manipulator

Consider a 2R planar arm with link masses $m_1, m_2$ and link lengths $l_1, l_2$, with COM at the link midpoint. Let the joint angles be $q_1, q_2$. (Detailed derivation in standard texts; the resulting dynamics equation is shown below.)

The mass matrix, Coriolis vector, and gravity vector for this canonical case are:

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \alpha + 2\beta\cos q_2 & \delta + \beta\cos q_2 \\ \delta + \beta\cos q_2 & \delta \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} -\beta\sin q_2\,(2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \dot{q}_2^2) \\ \beta\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1^2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1\,l_{c1} + m_2\,l_1)\,g\cos q_1 + m_2\,l_{c2}\,g\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ m_2\,l_{c2}\,g\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

Where $\alpha = m_1\,l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2) + I_1 + I_2$, $\beta = m_2\,l_1\,l_{c2}$, $\delta = m_2\,l_{c2}^2 + I_2$.

**Inverse dynamics example (numerical):**
Let $m_1 = m_2 = 2$ kg, $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m, $l_{c1} = l_{c2} = 0.5$ m, $I_1 = I_2 = 0.1$ kg·m², $g = 9.81$ m/s², $q_1 = q_2 = \pi/4$ rad, $\dot{q}_1 = \dot{q}_2 = 0.5$ rad/s, $\ddot{q}_1 = \ddot{q}_2 = 1$ rad/s².

Compute numerical values step by step (note: $\cos(\pi/4) = \sin(\pi/4) = \sqrt{2}/2 \approx 0.7071$):

- $\alpha = 2(0.25) + 2(1 + 0.25) + 0.1 + 0.1 = 0.5 + 2.5 + 0.2 = 3.2$ kg·m²
- $\beta = 2(1)(0.5) = 1.0$ kg·m²
- $\delta = 2(0.25) + 0.1 = 0.6$ kg·m²

Mass matrix entries:
- $M_{11} = 3.2 + 2(1.0)(0.7071) = 3.2 + 1.4142 = 4.6142$ kg·m²
- $M_{12} = M_{21} = 0.6 + 1.0(0.7071) = 1.3071$ kg·m²
- $M_{22} = 0.6$ kg·m²

Inertial torques: $M(q)\ddot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.6142 & 1.3071 \\ 1.3071 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.9213 \\ 1.9071 \end{bmatrix}$ N·m

Coriolis term: with $\dot{q} = [0.5, 0.5]^T$,
- $C_1 = -1.0(0.7071)[2(0.5)(0.5) + (0.5)^2] = -1.0(0.7071)(0.5 + 0.25) = -0.5303$ N·m
- $C_2 = 1.0(0.7071)(0.5)^2 = 0.1768$ N·m

Gravity term (with $q_1+q_2 = \pi/2$):
- $G_1 = (2\cdot0.5 + 2\cdot1)(9.81)(0.7071) + 2(0.5)(9.81)(0) = 3(9.81)(0.7071) + 0 = 20.81$ N·m
- $G_2 = 2(0.5)(9.81)(0) = 0$ N·m

Total joint torques (inverse dynamics result):
- $\tau_1 = 5.9213 - 0.5303 + 20.81 \approx 26.20$ N·m
- $\tau_2 = 1.9071 + 0.1768 + 0 \approx 2.08$ N·m

These torques would be commanded by a model-based controller to make the arm follow the prescribed motion.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Forward vs Inverse Dynamics — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Joint Torques tau] --> B[M-inverse x bracket term]
    C[Configuration q] --> B
    D[Velocities q-dot] --> B
    B --> E[Joint Accelerations q-double-dot]
    E --> F[Integration: q-dot, q]
    F --> C
    style A fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c00,color:#000
    style E fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#0a0,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Inverse Dynamics — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired Trajectory q, q-dot, q-double-dot] --> B[Compute M of q]
    A --> C[Compute C of q, q-dot]
    A --> D[Compute G of q]
    B --> E[Sum: Mq-double-dot + Cq-dot + G]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[Required Joint Torques tau]
    style A fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000
    style F fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#c60,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Computed-Torque Control using Inverse Dynamics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Desired q-d, q-dot-d, q-double-dot-d] --> B[Inverse Dynamics Model]
    B --> C[Feed-forward Torque tau-ff]
    D[Measured q-actual] --> E[PD Controller]
    F[Measured q-dot-actual] --> E
    E --> G[Feedback Torque tau-fb]
    C --> H[Sum Junction]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Robot Actuator / Motor]
    I --> J[Robot Link Motion]
    J --> D
    J --> F
    style A fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000
    style I fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#c00,color:#000
    style J fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#0a0,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Recursive Newton–Euler Algorithm (RNEA) — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Base Velocity and Acceleration] --> B[Outward Recursion i = 1 to n]
    B --> C[Compute link velocities omega-i, v-i]
    C --> D[Compute link accelerations omega-dot-i, v-dot-i]
    D --> E[Inward Recursion i = n down to 1]
    E --> F[Compute force f-i and moment n-i at each joint]
    F --> G[Project n-i onto joint axis to get tau-i]
    G --> H[Joint Torque Vector tau]
    style A fill:#cce0ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000
    style H fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#c60,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Comparison Matrix — Forward vs Inverse Dynamics at a Glance

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FD [FORWARD DYNAMICS]
        A1[Input: tau] --> A2[Output: q-double-dot]
    end
    subgraph ID [INVERSE DYNAMICS]
        B1[Input: q, q-dot, q-double-dot] --> B2[Output: tau]
    end
    style FD fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#c00,color:#000
    style ID fill:#e6f2ff,stroke:#06c,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define **Forward Dynamics** and **Inverse Dynamics** of a robotic manipulator. State the standard manipulator dynamics equation and identify each term.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Forward Dynamics (1 mark):** Given the joint torques $\tau$ applied to a manipulator, forward dynamics computes the resulting joint accelerations $\ddot{q}$ (and hence the motion).
- **Inverse Dynamics (1 mark):** Given the desired joint trajectory $(q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q})$, inverse dynamics computes the joint torques $\tau$ required to produce that motion.
- **Standard equation (1 mark):** $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$, where $M(q)$ is the mass matrix, $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ is the Coriolis/centrifugal vector, and $G(q)$ is the gravity vector.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
What is the **Recursive Newton–Euler Algorithm (RNEA)**? Mention its computational complexity and state why it is preferred for real-time inverse dynamics.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- RNEA is a force-balance formulation that uses an **outward recursion** (base to tip) to compute link velocities and accelerations, and an **inward recursion** (tip to base) to compute forces and moments at each joint (1.5 marks).
- Its computational complexity is $O(n)$ in the number of joints, in contrast to the $O(n^4)$ of the naive Lagrangian approach (1 mark).
- Because of this linear complexity, it is suitable for real-time implementation in robot controllers running at 1 kHz or higher (0.5 marks).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Derive the **Euler–Lagrange equation** of motion for an $n$-joint robotic manipulator starting from the Lagrangian $L = K - P$. Clearly state each term in the final standard form. (7 marks)

**(b)** A 2R planar manipulator has the dynamics equation given by the matrices $M(q)$, $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$, and $G(q)$ shown in Section 3.2. Given $m_1 = m_2 = 2$ kg, $l_1 = l_2 = 1$ m, $l_{c1} = l_{c2} = 0.5$ m, $I_1 = I_2 = 0.1$ kg·m², $g = 9.81$ m/s², $q_1 = q_2 = \pi/4$, $\dot{q}_1 = \dot{q}_2 = 0.5$ rad/s, $\ddot{q}_1 = \ddot{q}_2 = 1$ rad/s², compute the **joint torques** using inverse dynamics. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. Compute kinetic energy of all links: $K = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[\tfrac{1}{2} m_i v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \tfrac{1}{2} \omega_i^T {}^{i}\!I_i \omega_i\right]$ (1 mark)
2. Express velocities as functions of $\dot{q}$ via Jacobians. The total $K$ becomes $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$ (1 mark)
3. Compute potential energy: $P = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g^T p_{c_i}(q)$ (0.5 mark)
4. Form the Lagrangian $L = K - P$ (0.5 mark)
5. Apply the Euler–Lagrange equation for each joint: $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ (1.5 marks)
6. Expand and use the index-symmetry trick to identify the Christoffel symbols $\Gamma_{ijk}$ (1 mark)
7. Write the final standard form: $\tau = M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ with each term's physical meaning stated (1.5 marks)

**Model Solution to (b):** (Uses the numerical computation from Section 3.2)

1. Compute $\alpha, \beta, \delta$ as $3.2$, $1.0$, $0.6$ respectively (1 mark)
2. Compute mass matrix entries: $M_{11} = 4.6142$, $M_{12} = M_{21} = 1.3071$, $M_{22} = 0.6$ kg·m² (1 mark)
3. Compute inertial torques $M\ddot{q} = [5.9213,\; 1.9071]^T$ N·m (1 mark)
4. Compute Coriolis vector $C\dot{q} = [-0.5303,\; 0.1768]^T$ N·m (1.5 marks)
5. Compute gravity vector $G = [20.81,\; 0]^T$ N·m (1 mark)
6. Sum to get $\tau_1 \approx 26.20$ N·m and $\tau_2 \approx 2.08$ N·m (1.5 marks)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **Recursive Newton–Euler Algorithm (RNEA)** for inverse dynamics. State the equations used in the outward and inward recursions, and discuss its computational efficiency. (7 marks)

**(b)** For the same 2R planar manipulator of Question A(b), perform **forward dynamics** computation. Use the same numerical values but now assume the joint torques applied are $\tau_1 = 26.20$ N·m and $\tau_2 = 2.08$ N·m. Find the resulting joint accelerations $\ddot{q}$. (7 marks)

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Introduction (1 mark):** RNEA is a force-balance method by Luh, Walker, and Paul (1980) that computes joint torques by combining Newton and Euler equations at each link.

2. **Outward recursion (3 marks):** For $i = 1$ to $n$ propagating from base to tip:
   - Angular velocity: ${}^{i}\!\omega_i = {}^{i-1}\!R_i^T\!\left[{}^{i-1}\!\omega_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i\, \hat{z}_0\right]$ (revolute)
   - Linear acceleration of COM: ${}^{i}\!\dot{v}_{c_i} = {}^{i-1}\!R_i^T\left[{}^{i-1}\!\dot{v}_{i-1} + {}^{i-1}\!\dot{\omega}_{i-1}\times p_i + \omega_{i-1}\times(\omega_{i-1}\times p_i)\right]$

3. **Inward recursion (2 marks):** For $i = n$ to $1$ propagating from tip to base, compute the force $f_i$ and moment $n_i$ acting on link $i$, then project $n_i$ onto the joint axis to obtain $\tau_i$.

4. **Computational efficiency (1 mark):** $O(n)$ flops per recursion pass, totaling $O(n)$ per time step — ideal for real-time control at kHz rates.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} 4.6142 & 1.3071 \\ 1.3071 & 0.6 \end{bmatrix}$, $\tau = \begin{bmatrix} 26.20 \\ 2.08 \end{bmatrix}$, $C\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} -0.5303 \\ 0.1768 \end{bmatrix}$, $G = \begin{bmatrix} 20.81 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

1. Forward dynamics formula: $\ddot{q} = M^{-1}[\tau - C\dot{q} - G]$ (1 mark)
2. Compute the residual: $\tau - C\dot{q} - G = \begin{bmatrix} 26.20 - (-0.5303) - 20.81 \\ 2.08 - 0.1768 - 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5.9203 \\ 1.9032 \end{bmatrix}$ (2 marks)
3. Compute determinant of $M$: $\det(M) = (4.6142)(0.6) - (1.3071)^2 = 2.7685 - 1.7085 = 1.0600$ (1 mark)
4. Compute $M^{-1} = \dfrac{1}{1.0600}\begin{bmatrix} 0.6 & -1.3071 \\ -1.3071 & 4.6142 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.5660 & -1.2331 \\ -1.2331 & 4.3521 \end{bmatrix}$ (2 marks)
5. Multiply: $\ddot{q} = M^{-1} \cdot [\,5.9203,\; 1.9032\,]^T$ (1 mark)
   - $\ddot{q}_1 = 0.5660(5.9203) - 1.2331(1.9032) = 3.3509 - 2.3470 = 1.0039$ rad/s²
   - $\ddot{q}_2 = -1.2331(5.9203) + 4.3521(1.9032) = -7.3003 + 8.2830 = 0.9827$ rad/s²
6. The accelerations $\ddot{q} \approx [1.00,\; 0.98]^T$ rad/s² match the originally commanded values (sanity check, consistency). (Extra credit remark)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign error in the gravity term:** Many students write $G_i = +\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$ instead of $-\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$. The minus sign is critical.
> 2. **Units mismatch:** Forgetting that $M$ entries have units of kg·m². Computing torques without ensuring consistent units is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.
> 3. **Confusing Coriolis and centrifugal:** Coriolis involves the product $\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k$ with $j \neq k$ (different joints), while centrifugal has $j = k$ (same joint). Both live inside the same $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ term, but examiners love asking you to separate them.
> 4. **Inverse vs forward confusion:** In inverse dynamics, you are NOT asked to invert $M(q)$. You only invert $M$ in **forward dynamics**.
> 5. **Skipping the outward recursion in RNEA:** Many students jump directly to the inward recursion. Without outward recursion, you have no $\omega_i, \dot{\omega}_i, \dot{v}_{c_i}$ to feed in.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Forward Dynamics** = given $\tau$, find $\ddot{q}$. (Cause → Effect). Used in **simulation**.
- **Inverse Dynamics** = given $q, \dot{q}, \ddot{q}$, find $\tau$. (Effect → Cause). Used in **control**.
- The **standard manipulator dynamics equation**: $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$. **Memorize it verbatim.**
- $M(q)$ is **symmetric** and **positive-definite**; its inverse $M^{-1}(q)$ always exists.
- **Lagrangian formulation** is symbolic, good for derivation; **Newton–Euler** is numeric, good for real-time.
- The **Euler–Lagrange equation** is: $\dfrac{d}{dt}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial L}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \dfrac{\partial L}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$.
- **Christoffel symbols** $\Gamma_{ijk}$ build the Coriolis matrix: $C_{ij} = \sum_k \Gamma_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$.
- **Gravity vector** $G_i = -\dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$ (note the **minus sign**).
- **RNEA** runs in $O(n)$ time and is the de-facto real-time inverse dynamics algorithm.
- **Computed-torque control** uses inverse dynamics to linearize and decouple the manipulator, then applies a PD outer loop.
- For a **2R planar arm**, the mass matrix depends on $\cos q_2$ — coupling is maximum when the arm is fully stretched.
- The **kinetic energy** is always a quadratic form in $\dot{q}$: $K = \tfrac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 | Iterative Newton-Euler Dynamic Formulation

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> The **Iterative Newton-Euler (NE) Dynamic Formulation** is a recursive computational algorithm used to derive the closed-form dynamic equations of motion of an **n-DOF serial manipulator**. It efficiently computes the joint torques/forces required to produce a desired trajectory by performing **outward (forward) iterations** to propagate link velocities and accelerations from the base to the end-effector, followed by **inward (backward) iterations** to propagate forces and moments from the end-effector back to the base. It is based on the **Newton-Euler equations** of rigid body dynamics applied at the center of mass of each link.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a chain of **n** hinged rods (a robotic arm) and you want to swing the tip toward a target. To find out how hard each joint motor must push, you have two natural steps:

1. **Walk outward from the shoulder to the fingertip**: Determine how fast and how violently each link is spinning and translating given the motion you desire at the tip. This is like asking, *"If I move the shoulder joint like this, what happens at the elbow? The wrist? The fingertip?"*

2. **Walk back inward from the fingertip to the shoulder**: Now that you know how each link is moving, calculate the forces and torques the next link exerts on the previous one (gravity, inertia, and the load at the tip). Sum them up at each joint to get the **motor torque**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Highlight**
> The Iterative NE method is preferred over the **Lagrangian formulation** in real-time robot control because it computes joint torques in **O(n)** time (linear in the number of links), whereas the Lagrangian approach must symbolically expand and invert the **n × n mass matrix**, which is computationally expensive.

### Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (acting along $-\hat{Z}_0$ in the base frame)
- **Link mass**: $m_i$ (kg), with center of mass located at $\mathbf{r}_i$ from the link's coordinate frame
- **Inertia tensor**: $\mathbf{I}_i$ about the center of mass, expressed in the link's local frame
- **Link length**: typically denoted $l_i$ or $a_{i-1}$ per **DH parameters**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Recursive Structure of the Algorithm

The Newton-Euler method has **two coupled recursive passes**:

### A. Outward Iterations (Base → Tip, $i = 1 \to n$)
Propagate **kinematic state** (velocity and acceleration) of each link from its parent.

- **Angular velocity** propagation
- **Angular acceleration** propagation
- **Linear velocity** of link origin
- **Linear acceleration** of link origin
- **Linear acceleration** of the **center of mass**

### B. Inward Iterations (Tip → Base, $i = n \to 1$)
Propagate **dynamic loads** (forces and moments) acting on each link.

- **Newton's equation** (force balance at CoM)
- **Euler's equation** (moment balance about CoM)
- **Force and moment** equilibrium at the joint
- **Joint torque/force** extraction

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention:** All quantities with superscript $i$ are expressed in link $i$ frame. $\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}$ is the rotation matrix from frame $i-1$ to frame $i$. $\hat{z}_{i-1} = [0, 0, 1]^T$ is the joint axis. $\mathbf{p}_i^*$ is the position vector from link $i-1$ origin to link $i$ origin. $q_i$ is the joint variable (revolute $\theta_i$ or prismatic $d_i$).

| # | Equation (Revolute Joint $i$) | Equation (Prismatic Joint $i$) | Purpose |
|:-:|:-----------------------------|:------------------------------|:--------|
| 1 | $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}(\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1})$ | $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}$ | Angular velocity propagation |
| 2 | $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}[\boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i-1} + \ddot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times (\dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1})]$ | $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i-1}$ | Angular acceleration propagation |
| 3 | $\mathbf{v}_i = \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^* + \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v}_{i-1}$ | $\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1}$ | Linear velocity of link origin |
| 4 | $\mathbf{a}_i = \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^* + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^*) + \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{a}_{i-1}$ | $\mathbf{a}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{a}_{i-1} + \ddot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} + 2 \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1})$ | Linear acceleration of link origin |
| 5 | $\mathbf{a}_{ci} = \mathbf{a}_i + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{r}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{r}_i)$ | Same | Acceleration of center of mass |
| 6 | $\mathbf{F}_i = m_i \mathbf{a}_{ci}$ | Same | Newton's equation (force at CoM) |
| 7 | $\mathbf{N}_i = \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$ | Same | Euler's equation (moment at CoM) |
| 8 | $\mathbf{f}_i = \mathbf{R}_{i+1}^i \mathbf{f}_{i+1} + \mathbf{F}_i$ | Same | Force balance at link $i$ |
| 9 | $\mathbf{n}_i = \mathbf{N}_i + \mathbf{R}_{i+1}^i \mathbf{n}_{i+1} + \mathbf{p}_i^* \times \mathbf{f}_i + \mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i$ | Same | Moment balance at link $i$ |
| 10 | $\tau_i = \mathbf{n}_i^T (\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1})$ | $F_i = \mathbf{f}_i^T (\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1})$ | Joint torque/force |

### 2.3 Why This Formulation Works

- **Why outward first?** Because we must know *how each link is moving* before we can compute *what forces it experiences*.
- **Why inward second?** Because forces at the tip (payload, contact, end-effector wrench) propagate backward through the structure; the joint motor must overcome the cumulative load.
- **Real-world utility**: This algorithm is the **backbone of industrial robot controllers** (ABB, KUKA, FANUC) and is used in **model-based computed-torque control** for trajectory tracking.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions**
> - At the base ($i=0$): $\boldsymbol{\omega}_0 = \mathbf{0}$, $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_0 = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{v}_0 = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{a}_0 = -\mathbf{g}$ (gravity compensation, where $\mathbf{g} = [0, 0, -9.81]^T$ in the base frame).
> - At the tip ($i=n+1$): $\mathbf{f}_{n+1} = \mathbf{F}_{tip}$ (external wrench applied at the end-effector), $\mathbf{n}_{n+1} = \mathbf{N}_{tip}$ (external moment at the end-effector). If no payload, both are zero.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Angular Velocity Recursion (Revolute Joint)

Starting from the rigid-body velocity composition theorem. The angular velocity of link $i$ is the sum of the angular velocity of link $i-1$ (expressed in frame $i$) plus the relative angular velocity due to joint $i$ motion.

**Step 1:** Express the angular velocity of link $i-1$ in frame $i$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}^i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}
$$

**Step 2:** Add the relative angular velocity contribution. For a revolute joint, joint $i$ rotates about the $\hat{z}_{i-1}$ axis at rate $\dot{q}_i$. Expressed in frame $i$:

$$
\dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1}^i = \dot{q}_i \, \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1}
$$

**Step 3:** Superpose using rigid-body kinematics:

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \, \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Factor out $\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}$ (constant within one sampling step):

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}\left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} \right)
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is the well-known outward angular velocity recursion for a revolute joint.

## 3.2 Derivation of Angular Acceleration Recursion (Revolute Joint)

Differentiate the angular velocity equation with respect to time. Recall the product rule for time-varying vectors expressed in rotating frames:

**Step 1:** Start from $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1}$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate. Note that $\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}$ is **not** constant — it depends on $q_i$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\alpha}_i = \dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i-1} + \ddot{q}_i \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1}
$$

**Step 3:** Use the identity $\dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} [\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times]$ (skew-symmetric cross-product matrix of $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}$). For any vector $\mathbf{v}$, this means $\dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \mathbf{v})$.

**Step 4:** Apply to $\mathbf{v} = \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}$ and $\mathbf{v} = \hat{z}_{i-1}$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1}) = \mathbf{0}
$$

$$
\dot{q}_i \dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1} = \dot{q}_i \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \hat{z}_{i-1})
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute back and factor $\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\alpha}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}\left[ \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i-1} + \ddot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \hat{z}_{i-1}) \right]
$$

> **Interpretation:** The term $\dot{q}_i (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \hat{z}_{i-1})$ captures the **Coriolis-like coupling** between the angular velocity of the parent link and the joint rotation rate.

## 3.3 Derivation of Linear Velocity of the Link Origin

**Step 1:** The position of the link $i$ origin relative to the link $i-1$ origin is the constant vector $\mathbf{p}_i^*$, expressed in frame $i$ (or as $\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{p}_i^*$ in frame $i-1$).

**Step 2:** Apply the rigid-body velocity transport theorem. The linear velocity of point $O_i$ (origin of frame $i$) relative to point $O_{i-1}$ (origin of frame $i-1$), expressed in frame $i$, is:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{v}_{i-1}^i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^*
$$

where $\mathbf{v}_{i-1}^i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v}_{i-1}$.

**Step 3:** Substitute:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v}_{i-1} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^*
$$

> **Interpretation:** This is purely kinematic — the velocity of the new link origin equals the rotated parent velocity plus the rotational transport due to the angular velocity of the new link.

## 3.4 Derivation of Linear Acceleration of the Link Origin

Differentiate the linear velocity equation:

**Step 1:** Take the time derivative:

$$
\mathbf{a}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{a}_{i-1} + \dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v}_{i-1} + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^* + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \dot{\mathbf{p}}_i^*
$$

**Step 2:** Since $\mathbf{p}_i^*$ is **constant in frame $i$**, we have $\dot{\mathbf{p}}_i^* = \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^*$ (this is the rotating-frame derivative).

**Step 3:** Also $\dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v}_{i-1} = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \mathbf{v}_{i-1})$, but this is folded into the standard form by collecting terms:

$$
\mathbf{a}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{a}_{i-1} + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^* + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^*)
$$

> **Interpretation:** The first term is the propagated base acceleration; the second is the **Euler (tangential) acceleration**; the third is the **centripetal acceleration** of the link origin rotating about $O_{i-1}$.

## 3.5 Acceleration of the Center of Mass

The CoM is located at constant vector $\mathbf{r}_i$ from $O_i$ in frame $i$. The acceleration transport theorem gives:

**Step 1:** Apply rigid-body transport from $O_i$ to the CoM:

$$
\mathbf{a}_{ci} = \mathbf{a}_i + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{r}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{r}_i)
$$

**Step 2:** Reasoning: the CoM is a point rigidly attached to link $i$. It accelerates with $O_i$, plus the tangential acceleration due to the angular acceleration of the link, plus the centripetal acceleration due to its rotation about $O_i$.

> **Note:** This step is essential because Newton's equation (next) is applied at the CoM, not at the joint origin.

## 3.6 Newton's Equation (Force at the Center of Mass)

For a rigid body of mass $m_i$ with CoM acceleration $\mathbf{a}_{ci}$, the net external force equals mass times acceleration:

**Step 1:** State Newton's second law in vector form:

$$
\sum \mathbf{F}_{ext} = m_i \mathbf{a}_{ci}
$$

**Step 2:** The net external force on link $i$ (gravity + contact forces from joints $i$ and $i+1$) is balanced by the inertial force $m_i \mathbf{a}_{ci}$. We define the **inertial force** at the CoM as:

$$
\mathbf{F}_i = m_i \mathbf{a}_{ci}
$$

> **Note:** Gravity is implicitly included because we used $\mathbf{a}_0 = -\mathbf{g}$ as the base acceleration. This is a key trick — instead of adding $-m_i g \hat{z}$ explicitly, we move gravity into the kinematic base condition.

## 3.7 Euler's Equation (Moment at the Center of Mass)

For a rigid body, the rate of change of angular momentum about the CoM equals the net external moment:

**Step 1:** Angular momentum of link $i$ about its CoM is $\mathbf{H}_{ci} = \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$.

**Step 2:** Differentiate. The inertia tensor $\mathbf{I}_i$ is constant in the **link frame**, but the link frame itself rotates with $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$. So we need the rotating-frame derivative:

$$
\mathbf{N}_i = \frac{d}{dt}(\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i) = \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)
$$

> **Interpretation:** The second term captures **gyroscopic effects** — the change in orientation of the principal axes as the body spins.

## 3.8 Force and Moment Equilibrium at the Joint

For each link, we write static-equilibrium of the forces and moments at the joint axes.

**Step 1 — Force balance:** The force $\mathbf{f}_i$ exerted on link $i$ by joint $i$ must support the inertial force at the CoM plus the reaction from the next link:

$$
\mathbf{f}_i = \mathbf{R}_{i+1}^i \mathbf{f}_{i+1} + \mathbf{F}_i
$$

where $\mathbf{f}_{n+1}$ is the **external wrench** applied at the end-effector (often zero if no payload).

**Step 2 — Moment balance:** The moment $\mathbf{n}_i$ at joint $i$ must balance the inertial moment at the CoM, the moment from the next link, and the moment arms of the forces:

$$
\mathbf{n}_i = \mathbf{N}_i + \mathbf{R}_{i+1}^i \mathbf{n}_{i+1} + \mathbf{p}_i^* \times \mathbf{f}_i + \mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i
$$

where the $\mathbf{p}_i^* \times \mathbf{f}_i$ term is the moment of the joint force about the CoM (or vice versa), and $\mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i$ is the moment of the inertial force about the joint.

## 3.9 Joint Torque / Force Extraction

The joint actuator can only do work along its own axis of motion. Therefore:

**Step 1 (Revolute joint):** The joint torque is the projection of the moment $\mathbf{n}_i$ onto the joint axis $\hat{z}_{i-1}$ (expressed in frame $i$):

$$
\tau_i = \mathbf{n}_i^T \left( \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1} \right)
$$

**Step 2 (Prismatic joint):** The joint force is the projection of the force $\mathbf{f}_i$ onto the joint axis:

$$
F_i = \mathbf{f}_i^T \left( \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1} \right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Generalised Form**
> The complete joint actuation vector is:
> $$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q}) \ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}) \dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$$
> The Newton-Euler algorithm computes $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ given $\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}, \ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time, whereas the Lagrangian method must build the matrices $\mathbf{M}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{G}$ symbolically.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of the Iterative Newton-Euler Algorithm

The following flowchart captures the complete logical flow of the algorithm, including the coupled outward and inward passes.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Given q, qdot, qddot, g, link parameters] --> B[Initialize base conditions: omega0=0, alpha0=0, v0=0, a0=-g]
    B --> C[Set i = 1]
    C --> D{Outward Iteration i=1 to n}
    D --> E[Compute omega_i, alpha_i using rotation matrices]
    E --> F[Compute linear velocity v_i of link origin]
    F --> G[Compute linear acceleration a_i of link origin]
    G --> H[Compute CoM acceleration a_ci using r_i]
    H --> I[Compute inertial force F_i = m_i a_ci]
    I --> J[Compute inertial moment N_i = I_i alpha_i + omega_i cross I_i omega_i]
    J --> K{i = n?}
    K -->|No| L[i = i + 1]
    L --> D
    K -->|Yes| M[Set i = n]
    M --> N{Inward Iteration i=n to 1}
    N --> O[Force balance: f_i = R f_{i+1} + F_i]
    O --> P[Moment balance: n_i = N_i + R n_{i+1} + p cross f_i + r cross F_i]
    P --> Q[Extract joint torque tau_i or force F_i]
    Q --> R{i = 1?}
    R -->|No| S[i = i - 1]
    S --> N
    R -->|Yes| T[Output full joint torque vector tau]
    T --> U[End]

    style A fill:#1f77b4,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#2ca02c,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#d62728,color:#ffffff
    style T fill:#9467bd,color:#ffffff
    style U fill:#8c564b,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Two-Phase Recursive Block Diagram

This diagram emphasises the **separation between the kinematic (outward) and dynamic (inward) passes** and the data dependencies between links.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OUTWARD[OUTWARD PASS - Base to Tip]
        O1[Link 0 Base State] --> O2[Link 1: omega, alpha, v, a, a_c]
        O2 --> O3[Link 2: omega, alpha, v, a, a_c]
        O3 --> O4[Link 3: omega, alpha, v, a, a_c]
        O4 --> O5[Link n: omega, alpha, v, a, a_c]
    end

    subgraph DYNAMICS[LOCAL DYNAMICS at each link]
        D1[Link i: F_i = m_i a_ci]
        D2[Link i: N_i = I_i alpha_i + omega cross I_i omega]
        D1 --> D2
    end

    subgraph INWARD[INWARD PASS - Tip to Base]
        I1[Link n: tau_n] --> I2[Link n-1: tau_{n-1}]
        I2 --> I3[Link n-2: tau_{n-2}]
        I3 --> I4[Link 1: tau_1]
    end

    O5 --> DYNAMICS
    DYNAMICS --> I1

    style OUTWARD fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#1f77b4,stroke-width:2px
    style INWARD fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#d62728,stroke-width:2px
    style DYNAMICS fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#ff7f0e,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.3 Data Flow Through a Single Link (Detailed View)

This is the **micro-level** functional view of what happens at one link during the algorithm.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Parent link state: omega_{i-1}, alpha_{i-1}, v_{i-1}, a_{i-1}] --> K1[Apply rotation R_i^{i-1} and joint transform]
    K1 --> K2[Compute omega_i and alpha_i]
    K2 --> K3[Compute v_i and a_i]
    K3 --> K4[Add r_i transport to get a_ci]
    K4 --> D1[Newton: F_i = m_i a_ci]
    K4 --> D2[Euler: N_i = I_i alpha_i + omega cross I_i omega_i]
    D1 --> E[Force/moment equilibrium: f_i, n_i]
    D2 --> E
    C[Child link loads: f_{i+1}, n_{i+1}] --> E
    E --> J[Project onto joint axis to get tau_i or F_i]

    style P fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#1f77b4
    style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#d62728
    style J fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams**
> The **blue** paths represent kinematic propagation (outward), the **orange** paths represent local dynamic computation (Newton + Euler), and the **red** paths represent load propagation (inward). The **green** path is the final actuation output. This three-colour encoding is conventional in robotics textbooks for NE-based controllers.
