# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List the two recursive passes of the Iterative Newton-Euler dynamic formulation and state the boundary conditions used at the base of the manipulator.**

**Model Answer:**

The Iterative Newton-Euler formulation consists of two recursive passes:

1. **Outward (forward) iteration** from the base ($i=1$) to the end-effector ($i=n$): computes link angular velocity $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i$, angular acceleration $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_i$, linear velocity $\mathbf{v}_i$, linear acceleration $\mathbf{a}_i$ of the link origin, and linear acceleration $\mathbf{a}_{ci}$ of the center of mass.

2. **Inward (backward) iteration** from the end-effector ($i=n$) to the base ($i=1$): computes inertial force $\mathbf{F}_i$ and moment $\mathbf{N}_i$ at the CoM, then propagates the joint reaction force $\mathbf{f}_i$ and moment $\mathbf{n}_i$ backward, and finally extracts the joint torque $\tau_i$ or force $F_i$.

**Boundary conditions at the base** ($i=0$):

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_0 = \mathbf{0}, \quad \boldsymbol{\alpha}_0 = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{v}_0 = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{a}_0 = -\mathbf{g}
$$

> **Mark distribution (3 marks):** Two passes (1 mark) + base conditions (1.5 marks) + tip conditions (0.5 marks).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Why is the Iterative Newton-Euler formulation preferred over the Lagrangian formulation for real-time robot control? Justify with computational complexity.**

**Model Answer:**

The Lagrangian formulation produces a closed-form matrix equation $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$, where the $n \times n$ mass matrix $\mathbf{M}$, the Coriolis matrix $\mathbf{C}$, and the gravity vector $\mathbf{G}$ must be computed symbolically and stored. Computing $\mathbf{M}$ alone requires $\mathcal{O}(n^4)$ operations in the general case.

The Newton-Euler formulation directly computes $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ given the instantaneous $\mathbf{q}, \dot{\mathbf{q}}, \ddot{\mathbf{q}}$ in $\mathcal{O}(n)$ time per sampling instant. There is no need to explicitly form the mass matrix. This makes it ideal for **real-time computed-torque control** at high sampling rates (1 kHz and above) in industrial manipulators.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the recursive **angular velocity and angular acceleration** equations for a **revolute joint $i$** in the Iterative Newton-Euler formulation. Clearly state all vector quantities. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the expressions for the **force and moment balance** at link $i$ in the inward iteration, and show how the **joint torque** $\tau_i$ is finally extracted for a revolute joint. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1 — Angular Velocity:**

Consider link $i$ connected to its parent $i-1$ by a revolute joint. The angular velocity of link $i$ is the sum of the angular velocity of link $i-1$ (transformed to frame $i$) and the relative angular velocity due to joint $i$ rotating about $\hat{z}_{i-1}$ at rate $\dot{q}_i$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \, \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\omega}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} \right)}
$$

**Step 2 — Angular Acceleration:**

Differentiate the angular velocity equation with respect to time. Note that $\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}$ depends on $q_i$ and therefore varies with time:

$$
\boldsymbol{\alpha}_i = \frac{d\boldsymbol{\omega}_i}{dt} = \dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1}\left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} \right) + \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}\left( \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i-1} + \ddot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} \right)
$$

Using $\dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v} = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \mathbf{v})$ and noting that $\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1}\left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} \right) = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} \right)
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\boldsymbol{\alpha}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \left[ \boldsymbol{\alpha}_{i-1} + \ddot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} + \dot{q}_i \left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \hat{z}_{i-1} \right) \right]}
$$

The term $\dot{q}_i (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \hat{z}_{i-1})$ is the **Coriolis angular acceleration** due to coupling between the parent's spin and the joint rotation.

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

**Step 1 — Inertial Force at the CoM (Newton's Equation):**

$$
\mathbf{F}_i = m_i \mathbf{a}_{ci}
$$

**Step 2 — Inertial Moment at the CoM (Euler's Equation):**

$$
\mathbf{N}_i = \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Force Equilibrium at Joint $i$:**

The joint force $\mathbf{f}_i$ on link $i$ from joint $i$ must support the inertial force at the CoM and the reaction from the next link:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{f}_i = \mathbf{R}_{i+1}^i \mathbf{f}_{i+1} + \mathbf{F}_i}
$$

**Step 4 — Moment Equilibrium at Joint $i$:**

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{n}_i = \mathbf{N}_i + \mathbf{R}_{i+1}^i \mathbf{n}_{i+1} + \mathbf{p}_i^* \times \mathbf{f}_i + \mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i}
$$

where $\mathbf{p}_i^* \times \mathbf{f}_i$ is the moment of the joint reaction force about the CoM, and $\mathbf{r}_i \times \mathbf{F}_i$ is the moment of the inertial force about the joint.

**Step 5 — Joint Torque Extraction:**

The actuator can only exert work along the joint axis $\hat{z}_{i-1}$. Project $\mathbf{n}_i$ onto this axis (transformed to frame $i$):

$$
\boxed{\tau_i = \mathbf{n}_i^T \left( \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1} \right)}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Derive the recursive **linear velocity, linear acceleration** of the link origin, and the **acceleration of the center of mass** for link $i$ in the outward iteration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF planar manipulator with both joints revolute moves in a vertical plane. For the first link ($m_1, I_1, l_1$, center of mass at $l_1/2$ from joint), write the complete outward iteration equations to compute $\boldsymbol{\omega}_1, \boldsymbol{\alpha}_1, \mathbf{a}_{c1}$ given $\mathbf{a}_0 = -g \hat{y}$ and rotation matrix $\mathbf{R}_1^0$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Part (a)

**Step 1 — Linear Velocity of the Link Origin:**

The link origin $O_i$ is offset from $O_{i-1}$ by a constant vector $\mathbf{p}_i^*$ in frame $i$. Using the velocity transport theorem:

$$
\mathbf{v}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v}_{i-1} + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^*
$$

The first term is the velocity of the parent's origin expressed in frame $i$; the second is the rotational transport from $O_{i-1}$ to $O_i$ due to the angular velocity of link $i$.

**Step 2 — Linear Acceleration of the Link Origin:**

Differentiate with respect to time. Use $\dot{\mathbf{p}}_i^* = \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^*$ (rotating frame derivative, since $\mathbf{p}_i^*$ is constant in frame $i$):

$$
\mathbf{a}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{a}_{i-1} + \dot{\mathbf{R}}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{v}_{i-1} + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^* + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^*)
$$

For a stationary base ($\mathbf{v}_{i-1} = \mathbf{0}$ in frame $i-1$, and the base acceleration is absorbed into $\mathbf{a}_0$), this reduces to:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{a}_i = \mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \mathbf{a}_{i-1} + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^* + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{p}_i^*)}
$$

The three terms are: **(1)** propagated base acceleration, **(2)** tangential (Euler) acceleration, and **(3)** centripetal acceleration of the link origin about the parent's origin.

**Step 3 — Acceleration of the Center of Mass:**

The CoM is at constant $\mathbf{r}_i$ from $O_i$ in frame $i$. Transport from $O_i$ to CoM:

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{a}_{ci} = \mathbf{a}_i + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i \times \mathbf{r}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times \mathbf{r}_i)}
$$

#### Model Solution for Part (b)

For the 2-DOF planar manipulator, link 1 has:
- Length $l_1$, mass $m_1$, moment of inertia $I_1$ about CoM
- Center of mass at $\mathbf{r}_1 = (l_1/2, 0, 0)^T$ in frame 1
- Offset $\mathbf{p}_1^* = (l_1, 0, 0)^T$ in frame 1

**Base conditions:**
$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_0 = \mathbf{0}, \quad \boldsymbol{\alpha}_0 = \mathbf{0}, \quad \mathbf{a}_0 = -g \hat{y} = (0, -g, 0)^T
$$

**Step 1 — Angular velocity of link 1:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 = \mathbf{R}_1^0 \left( \boldsymbol{\omega}_0 + \dot{q}_1 \hat{z}_0 \right) = \mathbf{R}_1^0 \left( 0, 0, \dot{q}_1 \right)^T
$$

Since the rotation is about $\hat{z}$, $\mathbf{R}_1^0 \hat{z}_0 = \hat{z}_0$, so:

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 = \left( 0, 0, \dot{q}_1 \right)^T
$$

**Step 2 — Angular acceleration of link 1:**

$$
\boldsymbol{\alpha}_1 = \mathbf{R}_1^0 \left[ \boldsymbol{\alpha}_0 + \ddot{q}_1 \hat{z}_0 + \dot{q}_1 (\boldsymbol{\omega}_0 \times \hat{z}_0) \right]
$$

With $\boldsymbol{\omega}_0 = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\alpha}_1 = \mathbf{R}_1^0 \left( 0, 0, \ddot{q}_1 \right)^T = \left( 0, 0, \ddot{q}_1 \right)^T
$$

**Step 3 — Linear acceleration of link 1 origin:**

$$
\mathbf{a}_1 = \mathbf{R}_1^0 \mathbf{a}_0 + \boldsymbol{\alpha}_1 \times \mathbf{p}_1^* + \boldsymbol{\omega}_1 \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 \times \mathbf{p}_1^*)
$$

Substituting $\mathbf{a}_0 = (0, -g, 0)^T$, $\mathbf{p}_1^* = (l_1, 0, 0)^T$, $\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 = (0, 0, \dot{q}_1)^T$, $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_1 = (0, 0, \ddot{q}_1)^T$:

- $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_1 \times \mathbf{p}_1^* = (0, 0, \ddot{q}_1) \times (l_1, 0, 0) = (0, l_1 \ddot{q}_1, 0)^T$
- $\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 \times \mathbf{p}_1^* = (0, 0, \dot{q}_1) \times (l_1, 0, 0) = (0, l_1 \dot{q}_1, 0)^T$
- $\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 \times \mathbf{p}_1^*) = (0, 0, \dot{q}_1) \times (0, l_1 \dot{q}_1, 0) = (-l_1 \dot{q}_1^2, 0, 0)^T$

Therefore:
$$
\mathbf{a}_1 = \mathbf{R}_1^0 (0, -g, 0)^T + (0, l_1 \ddot{q}_1, 0)^T + (-l_1 \dot{q}_1^2, 0, 0)^T
$$

**Step 4 — Acceleration of CoM of link 1:**

With $\mathbf{r}_1 = (l_1/2, 0, 0)^T$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\alpha}_1 \times \mathbf{r}_1 = (0, (l_1/2) \ddot{q}_1, 0)^T
$$

$$
\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 \times (\boldsymbol{\omega}_1 \times \mathbf{r}_1) = (-(l_1/2) \dot{q}_1^2, 0, 0)^T
$$

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{a}_{c1} = \mathbf{R}_1^0 (0, -g, 0)^T + (0, l_1 \ddot{q}_1, 0)^T + (-l_1 \dot{q}_1^2, 0, 0)^T + (0, (l_1/2)\ddot{q}_1, 0)^T + (-(l_1/2)\dot{q}_1^2, 0, 0)^T}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting gravity in $\mathbf{a}_0$:** Many students omit $\mathbf{a}_0 = -\mathbf{g}$ and then try to add $m_i g \hat{z}$ terms later. This is a **fatal 1–2 mark deduction** because it duplicates gravity handling.
> 2. **Sign of joint axis $\hat{z}$:** Always use $\hat{z}_{i-1} = [0, 0, 1]^T$ in frame $i-1$ and transform it via $\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1}$ when needed. Mixing up frames is a common source of zero marks in part (b).
> 3. **Order of cross product in Euler's equation:** The term is $\boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$, not $(\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i) \times \boldsymbol{\omega}_i$. The order is non-commutative.
> 4. **For prismatic joints, the angular recursion has NO $\dot{q}_i \hat{z}$ term.** Students often blindly copy the revolute formula and lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Joint torque is a SCALAR**, obtained by projecting a 3D moment vector onto a unit axis. Do not present $\tau_i$ as a vector.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Two passes:** Outward (kinematics: $i=1 \to n$) and Inward (dynamics: $i=n \to 1$).
- [x] **Base conditions:** $\boldsymbol{\omega}_0 = \mathbf{0}$, $\boldsymbol{\alpha}_0 = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{v}_0 = \mathbf{0}$, $\mathbf{a}_0 = -\mathbf{g}$ (gravity trick).
- [x] **Tip conditions:** $\mathbf{f}_{n+1}, \mathbf{n}_{n+1}$ = external end-effector wrench (zero if no payload).
- [x] **Revolute vs prismatic:** Revolute adds $\dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1}$ to angular velocity and $\dot{q}_i (\boldsymbol{\omega}_{i-1} \times \hat{z}_{i-1})$ to angular acceleration; prismatic adds $\dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1}$ to linear velocity and $\ddot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1} + 2 \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\dot{q}_i \hat{z}_{i-1})$ to linear acceleration.
- [x] **Newton at CoM:** $\mathbf{F}_i = m_i \mathbf{a}_{ci}$ (always in frame $i$).
- [x] **Euler at CoM:** $\mathbf{N}_i = \mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\alpha}_i + \boldsymbol{\omega}_i \times (\mathbf{I}_i \boldsymbol{\omega}_i)$ — gyroscopic term is critical.
- [x] **Force/moment balance:** Always pull the child reaction forward: $\mathbf{f}_i = \mathbf{R}_{i+1}^i \mathbf{f}_{i+1} + \mathbf{F}_i$.
- [x] **Joint torque extraction:** $\tau_i = \mathbf{n}_i^T (\mathbf{R}_i^{i-1} \hat{z}_{i-1})$ — project onto joint axis.
- [x] **Complexity:** $\mathcal{O}(n)$ per time step — suitable for real-time control.
- [x] **Gravity handling:** Implicitly via $\mathbf{a}_0 = -\mathbf{g}$, NOT by adding $-m_i g \hat{z}$ in Newton's equation.
- [x] **Frame convention:** All vectors are expressed in the link's own frame; cross products are taken in that same frame.
- [x] **Why iterative?** It avoids symbolic expansion of $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ and works directly with numerical joint trajectories.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closed Form Dynamic Equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Closed Form Dynamic Equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3: CLOSED FORM DYNAMIC EQUATIONS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Closed-Form Dynamic Equations** of a robotic manipulator refer to the explicit, analytical, matrix-structured representation of the equations of motion that govern the relationship between the generalized joint forces/torques applied at the actuators and the resulting joint accelerations, velocities, and positions. They are derived symbolically (i.e., in closed algebraic form) prior to numerical evaluation, in contrast to the recursive Newton–Euler formulation which proceeds link by link.

The canonical closed-form representation, also known as the **Manipulator Equation** in joint space, is expressed as:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$$

where every term is a known analytical function of the joint variables $q$, $\dot{q}$, and (where applicable) $\ddot{q}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> Closed-form dynamic equations unify the contributions of **link inertia, velocity coupling (Coriolis + centrifugal), gravity loading, and joint friction** into a single matrix-vector differential equation. Mastery of this form is essential for model-based control strategies such as **Computed Torque Control, Adaptive Control, and Sliding Mode Control**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Skeletal Equation of a Robot"

Imagine a **human arm lifting a heavy bucket of water** while the bucket swings. Several invisible forces conspire to resist your motion:

- The bucket's own weight pulls down (**gravity**).
- The swinging water inside the bucket tugs sideways, changing with how fast you swing (**Coriolis and centripetal forces**).
- Your own bones and muscles possess mass; quick movements demand extra effort to accelerate that mass (**inertia**).
- Your elbow joint feels a slight sticky resistance (**friction**).

The Closed-Form Dynamic Equation is simply the **mathematical ledger** that tallies *all* these hidden forces simultaneously and tells the controller exactly how much muscle (torque) to apply at each joint. Instead of estimating on the fly, the controller "knows" the dynamics symbolically and can **pre-compensate** — this is the foundation of modern robotic control.

> [!NOTE]
> **The word "Closed-Form"** signifies that the equations are written as explicit algebraic expressions of $q$, $\dot{q}$, $\ddot{q}$ — no recursion, no iteration. They are the *opposite* of the recursive Newton–Euler form, which is computationally efficient but not symbolic.

### 1.3 Standard KTU Terminology & Physical Constants

- **Degrees of Freedom (DOF)**, $n$: Number of independent joint variables.
- **Generalized Coordinates**, $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$: Joint position vector.
- **Generalized Forces**, $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^n$: Joint torque/force vector (units: **N·m** for revolute, **N** for prismatic).
- **Mass Matrix Inertia Bound**: $\mu_1 \le \Vert M(q) \Vert \le \mu_2$, with $\mu_1, \mu_2 > 0$ for all $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Decomposition of the Manipulator Equation

The closed-form dynamic equation can be conceptually decomposed into **four interacting physical phenomena**, each represented by a distinct mathematical term:

| # | Term | Physical Meaning | Mathematical Nature |
|---|------|------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $M(q)\ddot{q}$ | Inertial forces (mass + moment of inertia) | Symmetric, Positive Definite, $n \times n$ matrix |
| 2 | $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ | Coriolis & Centrifugal forces | Velocity-dependent, $n \times 1$ vector |
| 3 | $G(q)$ | Gravitational loading | Configuration-dependent, $n \times 1$ vector |
| 4 | $F(\dot{q})$ | Friction (viscous + Coulomb) | Velocity-dependent, $n \times 1$ vector |
| 5 | $\tau$ | Actuator generalized forces | Control input, $n \times 1$ vector |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Closed-Form" over Recursive?**
> - ✅ Provides explicit analytical structure — useful for **control law design**, **stability analysis (Lyapunov)**, and **adaptive parameter estimation**.
> - ✅ Reveals important **structural properties** (symmetry, positive definiteness, skew-symmetry) crucial for proving stability.
> - ❌ Computationally expensive for $n \ge 4$ — hence the recursive Newton–Euler is preferred for *real-time* torque computation.

## 2.2 Detailed Role of Each Term

### 2.2.1 The Mass (Inertia) Matrix $M(q)$
- **Definition:** $M(q)$ maps the joint acceleration vector to the inertial torque vector.
- **Properties (KTU High-Yield):**
  - $M(q)$ is **symmetric**: $M(q) = M^T(q)$.
  - $M(q)$ is **positive definite**: $x^T M(q) x > 0, \ \forall x \neq 0, \ \forall q \in \mathbb{R}^n$.
  - $M(q)$ is **bounded**: $0 < \mu_1 I \le M(q) \le \mu_2 I < \infty$.
- **Physical Interpretation:** It encodes the *configuration-dependent moment of inertia* — when the arm is fully extended, $M$ has large values; when folded, $M$ has smaller values.

### 2.2.2 The Coriolis/Centrifugal Matrix $C(q, \dot{q})$
- **Definition:** Encapsulates the velocity-dependent inertial coupling between joints.
- **Element-wise Form (Christoffel Symbols):**

$$c_{ij}(q,\dot{q}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk}(q)\dot{q}_k$$

where the Christoffel symbols are:

$$c_{ijk}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial m_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial m_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial m_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$$

- **The Fundamental Skew-Symmetry Property (CRITICAL for KTU):**

$$\dot{M}(q) = C(q,\dot{q}) + C^T(q,\dot{q})$$

Equivalently, the matrix $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})$ is **skew-symmetric**:

$$x^T\left[\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})\right]x = 0, \ \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

This property is the cornerstone of **passivity-based control** and **Lyapunov stability proofs**.

### 2.2.3 The Gravity Vector $G(q)$
- **Definition:** $G(q) = \frac{\partial P(q)}{\partial q}$, where $P(q)$ is the total gravitational potential energy.
- **Physical Interpretation:** Each element $G_i(q)$ represents the torque/force needed at joint $i$ to *statically* hold the manipulator against gravity at configuration $q$.

### 2.2.4 The Friction Vector $F(\dot{q})$
- **Standard Model:** $F_i(\dot{q}_i) = f_{v,i}\dot{q}_i + f_{c,i}\,\mathrm{sgn}(\dot{q}_i)$
  - $f_{v,i}$: **viscous friction coefficient**.
  - $f_{c,i}$: **Coulomb friction coefficient**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| Symbol | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|--------|---------|---------|-------|
| Manipulator Equation | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$ | Full closed-form dynamics | — |
| Kinetic Energy | $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$ | Total kinetic energy | J |
| Potential Energy | $P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i g^T {}^{0}r_{c_i}(q)$ | Gravitational potential | J |
| Gravity Vector | $G(q) = \dfrac{\partial P(q)}{\partial q}$ | Gravitational joint torque | N·m or N |
| Christoffel Symbol | $c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial m_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \dfrac{\partial m_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \dfrac{\partial m_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ | Coriolis coupling coefficient | kg or kg·m² |
| Coriolis Element | $c_{ij} = \displaystyle\sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$ | Element of $C$ matrix | — |
| Skew-Symmetry | $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric | Passivity property | — |
| Euler-Lagrange | $\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | Foundation of closed-form | — |
| Kinetic Energy Matrix | $M_{ij} = \sum_{k=\max(i,j)}^{n} \mathrm{tr}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial {}^{0}T_k}{\partial q_i}I_k\dfrac{\partial {}^{0}T_k^T}{\partial q_j}\right)$ | Component formula | kg·m² |
| State-Space Form | $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\tau$ | Nonlinear affine form | — |
| Operational Space | $\Lambda(q)\ddot{x} + \mu(q,\dot{q})\dot{x} + \rho(q) = F$ | Cartesian-space dynamics | — |

> [!NOTE]
> The **absolute-value bar** in $\vert x \vert$ or conditional norm $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ must be typeset as $\lvert x \rvert$ or $\Vert \cdot \Vert$ to prevent table-rendering errors in markdown.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Closed-form dynamics are foundational in:

1. **Computed Torque Control (CTC):** $\tau = M(q)[a] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$ — exactly linearizes the plant.
2. **Adaptive Control:** Used to estimate unknown payload parameters on-line.
3. **Operational Space Control:** Translates the joint-space model to Cartesian space for end-effector control.
4. **Trajectory Planning:** Predicts actuator loading before executing aggressive maneuvers.
5. **Bipedal & Humanoid Robotics:** Essential for zero-moment-point (ZMP) computations.
6. **Industrial CNC & Welding Robots:** Pre-compensates gravity to maintain path accuracy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Form Equation from Lagrangian Mechanics

### 3.1.1 Starting Point: The Lagrangian

The Lagrangian $\mathcal{L}$ of an $n$-DOF manipulator is defined as:

$$\mathcal{L}(q,\dot{q}) = T(q,\dot{q}) - P(q)$$

where $T$ is the kinetic energy and $P$ is the potential energy.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation of $M(q)$

The total kinetic energy of the manipulator is the sum over all links:

$$T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{1}{2}\, m_i\, v_{c_i}^T v_{c_i} + \frac{1}{2}\, {}^{i}\omega_i^T\, I_i\, {}^{i}\omega_i$$

Using the Jacobian $J_i$ of the center of mass of link $i$:

$$v_{c_i} = J_{v_i}(q)\dot{q}, \quad {}^{i}\omega_i = J_{\omega_i}(q)\dot{q}$$

Substituting and collecting terms:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dot{q}^T\left[m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T\, R_i\, I_i\, R_i^T J_{\omega_i}\right]\dot{q} \\
&= \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T \underbrace{\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T R_i I_i R_i^T J_{\omega_i}\right)\right]}_{M(q)} \dot{q}
\end{aligned}$$

> **Logic Step 1:** $T$ is quadratic in $\dot{q}$ — therefore the bracketed term is precisely the **mass matrix** $M(q)$.

$$M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T R_i I_i R_i^T J_{\omega_i}\right)$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of $G(q)$

The total gravitational potential energy is:

$$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\, g^T\, {}^{0}r_{c_i}(q)$$

where $g = [0, 0, -9.81]^T\,\text{m/s}^2$.

Differentiating with respect to $q$:

$$G(q) = \frac{\partial P(q)}{\partial q} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\, g^T\, \frac{\partial {}^{0}r_{c_i}(q)}{\partial q}$$

### 3.1.4 Euler-Lagrange Equations of Motion

Applying the Euler-Lagrange operator to each generalized coordinate $q_i$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

Substituting $\mathcal{L} = T - P$ and $\tau = [Q_a]$ (actuator forces):

$$\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial T}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

Computing each derivative (using $M_{ij}$ as elements of $M(q)$):

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} &= \sum_{j} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_j \\
\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial T}{\partial \dot{q}_i} &= \sum_{j} M_{ij}(q)\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j,k} \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k \\
\frac{\partial T}{\partial q_i} &= \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j,k} \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back into the Euler-Lagrange equation:

$$\sum_{j} M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j,k}\left[\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right]\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + G_i(q) = \tau_i$$

Introducing the **Christoffel symbol** $c_{ijk}$ (which symmetrizes the bracket):

$$c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right]$$

We can rewrite the velocity product term using the identity (valid for any symmetric $M$):

$$\sum_{j,k}\left[\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right]\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k = \sum_{j,k} c_{ijk}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k$$

Therefore the component form is:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\left(\sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k\right)\dot{q}_j + G_i(q) = \tau_i$$

In matrix-vector notation, with $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

This is the **closed-form manipulator equation** in joint space.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Skew-Symmetry Property

Starting from $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$, differentiate with respect to time:

$$\dot{M}_{ij}(q) = \sum_{k}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k$$

By the cyclic property of the Christoffel symbols, the following identity holds:

$$c_{ijk} + c_{jik} = \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{M}_{ij} &= \sum_k \left(c_{ijk} + c_{jik}\right)\dot{q}_k \\
&= C_{ij} + C_{ji} = C_{ij} + C_{ij}^T
\end{aligned}$$

Hence, in matrix form:

$$\dot{M}(q) = C(q,\dot{q}) + C^T(q,\dot{q})$$

Rearranging, $\dot{M} - 2C = C^T - C$ is **skew-symmetric**, i.e.,

$$x^T[\dot{M} - 2C]x = 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

> **Logic Step 2:** This property guarantees that the manipulator is a **passive system** with respect to the input $\tau$ and output $\dot{q}$. It is mathematically equivalent to conservation of energy in the absence of dissipative forces and external torques.

## 3.3 Derivation of the State-Space Representation

The closed-form equation can be rearranged for control design. Define the state vector:

$$x = \begin{bmatrix} q \\ \dot{q} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^{2n}$$

Solving for $\ddot{q}$:

$$\ddot{q} = M^{-1}(q)[\tau - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} - G(q) - F(\dot{q})]$$

This yields the nonlinear affine state-space form:

$$\dot{x} = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q} \\ M^{-1}(q)[\tau - C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} - G(q)] \end{bmatrix} = f(x) + g(x)\tau$$

where:

$$f(x) = \begin{bmatrix} \dot{q} \\ -M^{-1}(q)[C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)] \end{bmatrix}, \quad g(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ M^{-1}(q) \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Logic Step 3:** The "affine-in-input" structure ($f(x) + g(x)\tau$) is the prerequisite for applying **feedback linearization**, **computed torque control**, and **sliding-mode** techniques — all of which are KTU Module 4 / 5 territory built upon this Module 3 foundation.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Operational Space Form

Define the end-effector position/orientation $x \in \mathbb{R}^m$ (typically $m=6$). The relationship is:

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$$

Substituting into the joint-space equation and pre-multiplying by $J M^{-1}$:

$$J M^{-1} M \ddot{q} + J M^{-1} C \dot{q} + J M^{-1} G = J M^{-1}\tau$$

$$J\ddot{q} + J M^{-1} C \dot{q} + J M^{-1} G = J M^{-1}\tau$$

Since $\ddot{x} = J\ddot{q} + \dot{J}\dot{q}$:

$$\ddot{x} = \dot{J}\dot{q} + J M^{-1}\tau - J M^{-1}C\dot{q} - J M^{-1}G$$

Defining the **operational-space inertia matrix**:

$$\Lambda(q) = (J M^{-1} J^T)^{-1}$$

And pre-multiplying by $\Lambda J M^{-1}$ throughout:

$$\Lambda(q)\ddot{x} + \underbrace{\Lambda J M^{-1} C \dot{q} - \Lambda \dot{J} \dot{q}}_{\mu(q,\dot{q})\dot{x}} + \underbrace{\Lambda J M^{-1} G}_{\rho(q)} = \underbrace{\Lambda J M^{-1}\tau}_{F}$$

The compact operational-space form is:

$$\Lambda(q)\ddot{x} + \mu(q,\dot{q})\dot{x} + \rho(q) = F$$

> **Logic Step 4:** The operational-space formulation decouples the **task** (force at the tool tip) from the **actuation** (joint torques), making it natural for force-controlled assembly, polishing, and contact tasks.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of Closed-Form Dynamic Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Joint Position q"] --> B["Mass Matrix M q"]
    A --> C["Coriolis Matrix C q dq"]
    A2["Joint Velocity dq"] --> C
    A2 --> E["Friction Vector F dq"]
    A --> D["Gravity Vector G q"]
    B --> F["Inertial Torque M q * ddq"]
    C --> G["Velocity Coupling C * dq"]
    D --> H["Gravity Loading G q"]
    E --> I["Friction Losses F dq"]
    F --> J["SUMMATION NODE - Total Joint Dynamics"]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    K["ACTUATOR TORQUE tau"] --> J
    J --> L["Robot Joint Acceleration ddq"]
    L --> M["Updated State x = q dq"]
    M -.feedback.-> A
    M -.feedback.-> A2
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: From Lagrangian to Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Step 1: System Definition"] --> P2["Step 2: Compute Kinetic Energy T"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Compute Potential Energy P"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: Form Lagrangian L = T - P"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: Apply Euler-Lagrange Operator"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: Extract Mass Matrix M q"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: Compute Christoffel Symbols cijk"]
    P7 --> P8["Step 8: Assemble C q dq"]
    P8 --> P9["Step 9: Compute G q from grad P"]
    P9 --> P10["Step 10: Combine M ddq + C dq + G = tau"]
    P10 --> P11["Step 11: Convert to State-Space xdot = f x + g x tau"]
    P11 --> P12["Step 12: Derive Operational-Space Form"]
    P12 --> P13["Step 13: Apply Computed Torque or Adaptive Control"]
```

## 4.3 Physical-Meaning Mapping Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T1["PHYSICAL DOMAIN"]
        T1A["Link Mass & Inertia"] 
        T1B["Joint Motion Coupling"] 
        T1C["Gravitational Field"] 
        T1D["Joint Friction"]
    end
    subgraph T2["MATHEMATICAL DOMAIN"]
        T2A["M q ddotq"]
        T2B["C q dq dq"]
        T2C["G q"]
        T2D["F dq"]
    end
    subgraph T3["PROPERTY DOMAIN"]
        T3A["Symmetric Positive Definite"]
        T3B["Skew-Symmetric Companion"]
        T3C["Conservative"]
        T3D["Dissipative"]
    end
    T1A --> T2A --> T3A
    T1B --> T2B --> T3B
    T1C --> T2C --> T3C
    T1D --> T2D --> T3D
```

## 4.4 Closed-Form vs Recursive Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Manipulator Dynamics Problem"] --> B["Choose Method"]
    B --> C["Closed-Form Lagrangian"]
    B --> D["Recursive Newton-Euler"]
    C --> C1["Symbolic Derivation"]
    C1 --> C2["Explicit M q , C, G"]
    C2 --> C3["O n^4 Complexity"]
    C2 --> C4["Used for Control Design and Stability Analysis"]
    D --> D1["Iterative Link-by-Link"]
    D1 --> D2["Implicit Form"]
    D2 --> D3["O n Complexity"]
    D2 --> D4["Used for Real-Time Torque Computation"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State and explain the importance of the Christoffel symbols in deriving the Coriolis matrix $C(q,\dot{q})$ of a robotic manipulator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Christoffel symbols of the first kind are defined as:

$$c_{ijk}(q) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial m_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial m_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial m_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$$

where $m_{ij}$ are the elements of the mass matrix $M(q)$. They serve two essential purposes:

1. **Coupling representation:** They capture how the inertia of joint $i$ couples with joint $j$ as joint $k$ moves — i.e., the velocity-dependent inertia effects (Coriolis and centripetal).
2. **Construction of $C(q,\dot{q})$:** The $(i,j)$ element of the Coriolis matrix is constructed as $C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$.

The importance of Christoffel symbols lies in their role in proving the **skew-symmetry property** $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric, which is critical for **passivity-based control** and **Lyapunov-based stability proofs**.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List the four key properties of the mass matrix $M(q)$ and briefly state why each is important in robot control.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four key properties of the mass matrix $M(q)$ are:

1. **Symmetry:** $M(q) = M^T(q)$ — this is a consequence of Newton's third law and the reciprocity of inertia. It simplifies stability proofs and energy analysis.

2. **Positive Definiteness:** $x^T M(q) x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$ and all $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ — guarantees that the manipulator cannot accelerate without applied torque, and that $M^{-1}(q)$ exists everywhere.

3. **Boundedness:** $0 < \mu_1 I \le M(q) \le \mu_2 I < \infty$ — ensures the inertia is finite and well-conditioned; this is required for the existence of continuous, bounded control laws.

4. **Configuration Dependence:** $M(q)$ is a function of joint position $q$ — this means the inertia the controller must overcome changes as the arm moves, motivating the need for model-based control (computed torque, adaptive control).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**(a) Derive the closed-form dynamic equation of an n-DOF robotic manipulator in joint space using the Euler-Lagrange formulation. Clearly define each term in the resulting manipulator equation.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) For a 2-DOF planar manipulator with both links having mass $m_1 = m_2 = m$, length $l_1 = l_2 = l$, and inertia $I_1 = I_2 = I$, derive the mass matrix $M(q)$ and the gravity vector $G(q)$. Assume gravity acts along the negative y-direction.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question A(a) — Model Answer (7 marks)**

**Step 1:** Define the Lagrangian:

$$\mathcal{L}(q,\dot{q}) = T(q,\dot{q}) - P(q)$$

**Step 2:** Express kinetic energy as a quadratic form in $\dot{q}$:

$$T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$$

where $M(q)$ is the symmetric positive-definite mass matrix:

$$M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + J_{\omega_i}^T R_i I_i R_i^T J_{\omega_i}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Express potential energy:

$$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\, g^T\, {}^{0}r_{c_i}(q)$$

**Step 4:** Apply Euler-Lagrange equation for each joint:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i} - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

**Step 5:** Substituting $\mathcal{L} = T - P$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\sum_j M_{ij}\dot{q}_j\right) - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j,k}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

**Step 6:** Expanding the time derivative:

$$\sum_j M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j,k}\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k - \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j,k}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + G_i(q) = \tau_i$$

where $G_i(q) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$.

**Step 7:** Combining using the Christoffel symbol identity:

$$\sum_j M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j,k} c_{ijk}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + G_i(q) = \tau_i$$

**Step 8:** Defining $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$ and converting to matrix form:

$$\boxed{M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau}$$

**Term Definitions:**
- $M(q)\ddot{q}$: inertial torque — required to accelerate the manipulator.
- $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$: Coriolis and centripetal torque — velocity-dependent coupling.
- $G(q)$: gravitational torque — required to hold the arm statically.
- $\tau$: generalized joint force vector from actuators.

---

#### **Solution to Question A(b) — Model Answer (7 marks)**

**Setup:** For a 2-DOF planar RR manipulator with both links identical:

Joint variables: $q = [q_1, q_2]^T$, link lengths $l_1 = l_2 = l$, masses $m_1 = m_2 = m$.

**Step 1: Positions of Centers of Mass**

$$x_{c_1} = \frac{l}{2}\cos q_1, \quad y_{c_1} = \frac{l}{2}\sin q_1$$

$$x_{c_2} = l\cos q_1 + \frac{l}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2), \quad y_{c_2} = l\sin q_1 + \frac{l}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 2: Velocities (Differentiate w.r.t. time)**

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_{c_1} &= -\frac{l}{2}\dot{q}_1\sin q_1 \\
\dot{y}_{c_1} &= \frac{l}{2}\dot{q}_1\cos q_1
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}_{c_2} &= -l\dot{q}_1\sin q_1 - \frac{l}{2}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\sin(q_1+q_2) \\
\dot{y}_{c_2} &= l\dot{q}_1\cos q_1 + \frac{l}{2}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos(q_1+q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Squared Velocities**

$$\dot{x}_{c_1}^2 + \dot{y}_{c_1}^2 = \frac{l^2}{4}\dot{q}_1^2$$

For link 2, squaring and collecting terms:

$$\dot{x}_{c_2}^2 + \dot{y}_{c_2}^2 = l^2\dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{l^2}{4}(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2 + l^2\dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)\cos q_2$$

**Step 4: Kinetic Energy**

$$T = \frac{1}{2}m_1(\dot{x}_{c_1}^2+\dot{y}_{c_1}^2) + \frac{1}{2}I_1\dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2(\dot{x}_{c_2}^2+\dot{y}_{c_2}^2) + \frac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{q}_1+\dot{q}_2)^2$$

Expanding and collecting into the form $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M \dot{q}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{1}{2}\Big\{\left[\frac{1}{4}m_1 l^2 + I_1 + m_2 l^2 + m_2 l^2\cos q_2 + I_2\right]\dot{q}_1^2 \\
&\quad + \left[\frac{1}{4}m_2 l^2 + I_2\right]\dot{q}_2^2 \\
&\quad + \left[m_2 l^2\cos q_2 + 2I_2\right]\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2\Big\}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Mass Matrix $M(q)$**

$$\boxed{M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{1}{4}m l^2 + I + m l^2 + m l^2 \cos q_2 + I & m l^2 \cos q_2 + 2I \\ m l^2 \cos q_2 + 2I & \frac{1}{4}m l^2 + I \end{bmatrix}}$$

Simplifying (since $m_1 = m_2 = m$ and $I_1 = I_2 = I$):

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{5}{4}ml^2 + 2I + ml^2\cos q_2 & ml^2\cos q_2 + 2I \\ ml^2\cos q_2 + 2I & \frac{1}{4}ml^2 + I \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6: Potential Energy**

$$P = m_1 g y_{c_1} + m_2 g y_{c_2} = \frac{mgl}{2}\sin q_1 + mg\left[l\sin q_1 + \frac{l}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)\right]$$

$$P = \frac{3mgl}{2}\sin q_1 + \frac{mgl}{2}\sin(q_1+q_2)$$

**Step 7: Gravity Vector**

$$\boxed{G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_1} \\ \dfrac{\partial P}{\partial q_2} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \frac{3mgl}{2}\cos q_1 + \frac{mgl}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \\ \frac{mgl}{2}\cos(q_1+q_2) \end{bmatrix}}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a) State and prove the skew-symmetry property of the manipulator equation: $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})$ is skew-symmetric. Explain its significance in passivity-based control.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Starting from the joint-space closed-form dynamic equation, derive the operational-space dynamic equation:**
$$\Lambda(q)\ddot{x} + \mu(q,\dot{q})\dot{x} + \rho(q) = F$$
**Clearly define $\Lambda(q)$, $\mu(q,\dot{q})$, $\rho(q)$, and $F$.** *(7 marks)*

---

#### **Solution to Question B(a) — Model Answer (7 marks)**

**Statement:** For any set of generalized coordinates $q$ and velocities $\dot{q}$, the matrix $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})$ is skew-symmetric, i.e.,

$$x^T[\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})]x = 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}^n$$

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** Time-derivative of the mass matrix element $M_{ij}$:

$$\dot{M}_{ij}(q) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k$$

**Step 2:** Use the symmetry $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$. By the cyclic property of partial derivatives:

$$\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} = c_{ijk} + c_{jik}$$

where Christoffel symbols are:

$$c_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the time derivative:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dot{M}_{ij} &= \sum_k (c_{ijk} + c_{jik})\dot{q}_k \\
&= \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k + \sum_k c_{jik}\dot{q}_k \\
&= C_{ij} + C_{ji}
\end{aligned}$$

since by definition $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$.

**Step 4:** In matrix form, this means:

$$\dot{M}(q) = C(q,\dot{q}) + C^T(q,\dot{q})$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging:

$$\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q}) = C^T(q,\dot{q}) - C(q,\dot{q})$$

The right-hand side is the **transpose-minus-original** form of a skew-symmetric matrix, since $(C^T - C)^T = C - C^T = -(C^T - C)$. Therefore, $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric, completing the proof.

**Significance in Passivity-Based Control:**

- The skew-symmetry property implies that the **rate of change of kinetic energy** is exactly $\frac{dT}{dt} = \dot{q}^T \tau - \dot{q}^T F(\dot{q})$.
- This means the manipulator, viewed as a map from $\tau$ to $\dot{q}$, is **passive** — it cannot generate energy on its own.
- Passivity forms the foundation of **energy-based Lyapunov functions** for control design, guaranteeing **global asymptotic stability** without requiring exact model knowledge.

---

#### **Solution to Question B(b) — Model Answer (7 marks)**

**Starting Point:** The joint-space closed-form equation:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Step 1:** Express end-effector velocity via the Jacobian:

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$$

Differentiating:

$$\ddot{x} = J(q)\ddot{q} + \dot{J}(q)\dot{q}$$

Solving for $\ddot{q}$:

$$\ddot{q} = J^{-1}(\ddot{x} - \dot{J}\dot{q})$$

**Step 2:** Solve the joint equation for $\ddot{q}$:

$$\ddot{q} = M^{-1}(q)[\tau - C\dot{q} - G]$$

**Step 3:** Equate the two expressions for $\ddot{q}$ and pre-multiply by $J M^{-1}$:

$$J\ddot{q} = J M^{-1}[\tau - C\dot{q} - G]$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $\ddot{x} = J\ddot{q} + \dot{J}\dot{q}$:

$$\ddot{x} - \dot{J}\dot{q} = J M^{-1}\tau - J M^{-1}C\dot{q} - J M^{-1}G$$

**Step 5:** Pre-multiply by $\Lambda(q) = (J M^{-1} J^T)^{-1}$:

$$\Lambda\ddot{x} - \Lambda \dot{J}\dot{q} = \Lambda J M^{-1}\tau - \Lambda J M^{-1}C\dot{q} - \Lambda J M^{-1}G$$

**Step 6:** Define the operational-space quantities:

$$\boxed{\Lambda(q) = (J(q) M^{-1}(q) J^T(q))^{-1}}$$

$$\boxed{\mu(q,\dot{q}) = \Lambda J M^{-1} C - \Lambda \dot{J}}$$

$$\boxed{\rho(q) = \Lambda J M^{-1} G}$$

$$\boxed{F = \Lambda J M^{-1}\tau}$$

**Step 7:** Rearranging gives the final operational-space equation:

$$\boxed{\Lambda(q)\ddot{x} + \mu(q,\dot{q})\dot{x} + \rho(q) = F}$$

**Term Definitions:**
- $\Lambda(q)$: **Operational-space inertia matrix** — the perceived inertia of the robot as seen from the end-effector frame.
- $\mu(q,\dot{q})\dot{x}$: **Operational-space Coriolis/centrifugal term** — velocity-dependent coupling in task space.
- $\rho(q)$: **Operational-space gravity vector** — gravitational force at the end-effector.
- $F$: **Generalized operational force** — the equivalent end-effector force produced by joint torques.

**Significance:** The operational-space formulation allows the controller to command **Cartesian forces and motions** directly, decoupling the task specification from the underlying joint-level actuation. This is essential for **compliant control, force-controlled assembly, and humanoid balance control**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Coriolis term's dependence on $\dot{q}$:** Many students write $C(q)\dot{q}$ instead of $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$, which is **incorrect** — $C$ itself depends on velocity.
>
> 2. **Mixing up $c_{ijk}$ and $c_{kij}$:** The Christoffel symbols are **not** generally symmetric in all indices. The symmetry $c_{ijk} = c_{jik}$ holds, but $c_{ijk} \neq c_{kij}$ in general.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the kinetic-energy Jacobian formula:** Students often try to derive $M(q)$ by inspection; instead, always use $M_{ij} = \sum_k \mathrm{tr}\!\left(\frac{\partial T_k}{\partial q_i} I_k \frac{\partial T_k^T}{\partial q_j}\right)$ for guaranteed correctness.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in the gravity vector:** $G(q) = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q}$, and the sign of $g$ depends on convention. With $g = -9.81\,\hat{j}$, gravity produces a **negative** $P$ derivative for downward $y$, leading to a **positive** $G$ for positive $q$ (counter-clockwise rotation). Verify with the energy method.
>
> 5. **Confusing joint-space and operational-space forms:** $\Lambda(q)$ is **not** $M(q)$ — it is $(J M^{-1} J^T)^{-1}$ and is generally **not diagonal**, even when $M$ is.
>
> 6. **Missing the friction term:** In some textbooks, $F(\dot{q})$ is omitted for clarity, but a KTU board answer that omits it loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **closed-form dynamic equation** is $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$.
- ✅ $M(q)$ is **symmetric and positive definite**, with bounded eigenvalues.
- ✅ $C(q,\dot{q})$ is built from **Christoffel symbols**: $C_{ij} = \sum_k c_{ijk}\dot{q}_k$.
- ✅ The **skew-symmetry property** $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric is the cornerstone of passivity-based control.
- ✅ $G(q) = \frac{\partial P(q)}{\partial q}$ where $P$ is the gravitational potential energy.
- ✅ The **state-space form** is affine in $\tau$: $\dot{x} = f(x) + g(x)\tau$.
- ✅ The **operational-space form** is $\Lambda(q)\ddot{x} + \mu(q,\dot{q})\dot{x} + \rho(q) = F$ with $\Lambda = (J M^{-1} J^T)^{-1}$.
- ✅ Closed-form is preferred for **control design & stability analysis**; recursive Newton–Euler is preferred for **real-time torque computation**.
- ✅ Derivation is based on the **Euler-Lagrange equation**: $\frac{d}{dt}\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}} - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q} = \tau$.
- ✅ Always remember to **include the friction term** for full-marks answers in KTU 2024 scheme questions.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lagrangian Formulation of Manipulator Dynamics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lagrangian Formulation of Manipulator Dynamics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Lagrangian Formulation of Manipulator Dynamics** is a systematic, energy-based analytical methodology used to derive the closed-form equations of motion of a multi-body robotic manipulator. It is built upon classical Lagrangian mechanics, where the dynamic behavior of the manipulator is derived from a single scalar function — the **Lagrangian** $\mathcal{L}$ — defined as the difference between the total **kinetic energy** $K$ and the total **potential energy** $P$ of the system.

$$\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}) = K(q, \dot{q}) - P(q)$$

Here, $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$ is the vector of **generalized joint coordinates**, and $\dot{q}$ is the corresponding joint velocity vector. The governing dynamic equation is then obtained by applying the **Euler-Lagrange equation** for each generalized coordinate $q_i$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

For an $n$-DOF manipulator, applying this procedure to all $n$ joints produces the canonical **manipulator equation of motion**:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 mandates that students must be able to *derive* (not just state) the Lagrangian dynamic model for at least a 2-DOF planar manipulator. Plug-and-chug numerical problems are also routinely asked.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are managing the **energy budget of a household** over one month. Money in (income) is like **kinetic energy** — the energy of motion and action. Savings held in fixed deposits (which can be encashed but are tied to position/height) are like **potential energy** — energy stored due to configuration. The **Lagrangian** is simply the difference: "spendable energy" minus "locked-in energy."

The Euler-Lagrange equation then says: *"The rate of change of how easily you can spend energy on a particular activity, minus how that activity is influenced by your overall configuration, must equal the external effort (torque) you apply."*

In a robot manipulator:
- **Kinetic energy $K$** comes from link motions (linear + rotational).
- **Potential energy $P$** comes from gravity acting on link masses.
- **Generalized torques $\tau_i$** are the motor efforts at each joint.

This energy approach is far more elegant than writing Newton's second law for every link and resolving constraint forces — the Lagrangian method naturally absorbs constraints and produces compact, symmetric equations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Lagrangian formulation requires only scalar energy functions, *never* constraint reaction forces. This is why it is preferred over the Newton-Euler method for symbolic derivation of manipulator dynamics.

### 1.3 Generalized Coordinates & Joint Variables

For an $n$-DOF serial manipulator, the **minimum set of independent coordinates** that completely describes the configuration is the vector of **joint variables**:

$$q = \begin{bmatrix} q_1 \\ q_2 \\ \vdots \\ q_n \end{bmatrix}$$

These are typically **rotational** ($q_i = \theta_i$, in **radians**) for revolute joints or **prismatic** ($q_i = d_i$, in **meters**) for sliding joints.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit (SI) |
|---|---|---|
| Mass of link $i$ | $m_i$ | **kg** |
| Moment of inertia of link $i$ | $I_i$ | **kg·m²** |
| Link length | $l_i$ | **m** |
| Center-of-mass offset | $l_{ci}$ | **m** |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Joint torque | $\tau_i$ | **N·m** |
| Joint angle | $q_i, \theta_i$ | **rad** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Theoretical Framework

The Lagrangian formulation of manipulator dynamics proceeds through the following structured pipeline:

**Step 1 — Identify Generalized Coordinates**
- Choose the vector $q = [q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n]^T$ of independent joint variables.

**Step 2 — Compute Link Positions and Velocities**
- Use forward kinematics to express the position of each link's center of mass $p_i$ as a function of $q$.
- Differentiate with respect to time to obtain the linear velocity $\dot{p}_i$.
- Compute the angular velocity $\omega_i$ of each link in terms of $\dot{q}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the Total Kinetic Energy $K$**
- The kinetic energy of a rigid body is the sum of translational and rotational parts:
$$K_i = \frac{1}{2}\,m_i\,\dot{p}_i^T \dot{p}_i + \frac{1}{2}\,I_i\,\omega_i^T \omega_i$$
- Sum over all $n$ links:
$$K(q, \dot{q}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} K_i$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Total Potential Energy $P$**
- For gravity-only loading (most common in KTU problems):
$$P(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i\,g\,h_i(q)$$
where $h_i(q)$ is the vertical height of the center of mass of link $i$.

**Step 5 — Form the Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = K - P$**
$$\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}) = K(q, \dot{q}) - P(q)$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Euler-Lagrange Equation for Each Joint**
$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange into Canonical Form**
$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the Euler-Lagrange equation work?** It is derived from **Hamilton's Principle of Stationary Action**, which states that the actual path taken by a system between two states is the one that makes the action integral $S = \int \mathcal{L}\,dt$ stationary. This is a variational principle, and the EL equation is the necessary condition.

### 2.2 The Canonical Manipulator Equation

After applying the EL equations, the dynamics always reduce to:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) + F(\dot{q}) = \tau$$

| Term | Name | Physical Meaning | Dimensions |
|---|---|---|---|
| $M(q)$ | Mass / Inertia Matrix | Configuration-dependent inertia; relates joint accelerations to inertial torques | $n \times n$ (kg·m²) |
| $C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q}$ | Coriolis & Centrifugal Vector | Torques arising from coupling between moving links (velocity-dependent) | $n \times 1$ (N·m) |
| $G(q)$ | Gravity Vector | Gravitational torques due to link weights | $n \times 1$ (N·m) |
| $F(\dot{q})$ | Friction Vector | Viscous/Coulomb friction torques | $n \times 1$ (N·m) |
| $\tau$ | Generalized Torque Vector | Actuator torques at each joint | $n \times 1$ (N·m) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The matrix $M(q)$ is **symmetric** ($M = M^T$) and **positive definite** ($x^T M x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$). These properties are exploited in **computed-torque control** and stability proofs.

### 2.3 Properties of the Inertia Matrix $M(q)$

- $M(q)$ is **symmetric**: $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$
- $M(q)$ is **positive definite** for all $q$
- $M(q)$ is **bounded**: $\mu_1 I \leq M(q) \leq \mu_2 I$ for constants $\mu_1, \mu_2 > 0$
- The **kinetic energy** can be written as $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$

### 2.4 The Coriolis Matrix $C(q, \dot{q})$

The Coriolis matrix is **not unique** (only $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ is unique). A common representation uses the **Christoffel symbols**:

$$C_{ij}(q, \dot{q}) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \Gamma_{ijk}(q)\,\dot{q}_k$$

where

$$\Gamma_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$$

A useful property: $\dot{M}(q) - 2C(q,\dot{q})$ is **skew-symmetric** when $C$ is chosen appropriately (Christoffel form). This is critical for Lyapunov-based control design.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ | Lagrangian definition |
| 2 | $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i$ | Euler-Lagrange equation for joint $i$ |
| 3 | $K_i = \frac{1}{2}m_i v_{ci}^2 + \frac{1}{2}I_i \omega_i^2$ | KE of link $i$ (point mass at CoM + rotation) |
| 4 | $P_i = m_i g h_i(q)$ | PE of link $i$ due to gravity |
| 5 | $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ | Canonical manipulator equation |
| 6 | $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$ | KE in matrix form |
| 7 | $M_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\left[m_k\,J_{v_k}^{(i)}\cdot J_{v_k}^{(j)} + I_k\,J_{\omega_k}^{(i)}\cdot J_{\omega_k}^{(j)}\right]$ | General inertia matrix element via Jacobians |
| 8 | $G_i = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$ | Gravity torque at joint $i$ |
| 9 | $\Gamma_{ijk} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)$ | Christoffel symbol of the first kind |
| 10 | $\dot{M} = C + C^T$ | Relationship for Christoffel form of $C$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:** The Lagrangian model is the cornerstone of **model-based control** strategies — Computed Torque Control, Adaptive Control, Sliding-Mode Control, and Impedance Control all require the exact $M(q)$, $C(q,\dot{q})$, and $G(q)$ matrices. It is also used in **dynamic simulation** (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink SimMechanics, Gazebo, Adams) to predict manipulator behavior before fabrication.

### 2.6 Why the Lagrangian Approach is Preferred for Manipulators

| Feature | Lagrangian Method | Newton-Euler Method |
|---|---|---|
| Recursive formulation | ❌ Not natural | ✅ Yes |
| Symbolic closed-form derivation | ✅ Excellent | ❌ Tedious |
| Requires constraint forces | ❌ No | ✅ Yes |
| Computational efficiency for real-time | ⚠️ Slower (symbolic) | ✅ Faster (recursive) |
| Insight into energy/storage | ✅ Direct | ❌ Indirect |
| KTU exam relevance | ✅ Very High | ✅ High |

## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: 2-DOF Planar Manipulator (Full Lagrangian Derivation)

**Problem Setup (canonical KTU 2024 problem):**

Consider a 2R planar manipulator in the vertical plane with:
- Link 1: length $l_1$, mass $m_1$, moment of inertia $I_1$ about its center of mass, center of mass at distance $l_{c1}$ from joint 1.
- Link 2: length $l_2$, mass $m_2$, moment of inertia $I_2$ about its center of mass, center of mass at distance $l_{c2}$ from joint 2.
- Joint variables: $q_1$ (angle of link 1 w.r.t. horizontal), $q_2$ (relative angle of link 2 w.r.t. link 1).
- Gravity $g$ acts downward (negative $y$-direction).

**Goal:** Derive the full dynamic model and express it in the canonical form $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$.

---

#### **Step 1 — Forward Kinematics of Each Center of Mass**

Position of the center of mass of link 1:

$$x_1 = l_{c1}\cos(q_1), \qquad y_1 = l_{c1}\sin(q_1)$$

Position of the center of mass of link 2:

$$x_2 = l_1\cos(q_1) + l_{c2}\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

$$y_2 = l_1\sin(q_1) + l_{c2}\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

---

#### **Step 2 — Time Derivatives (Velocities)**

Differentiating the above positions w.r.t. time:

$$\dot{x}_1 = -l_{c1}\sin(q_1)\,\dot{q}_1$$

$$\dot{y}_1 = l_{c1}\cos(q_1)\,\dot{q}_1$$

$$\dot{x}_2 = -l_1\sin(q_1)\,\dot{q}_1 - l_{c2}\sin(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

$$\dot{y}_2 = l_1\cos(q_1)\,\dot{q}_1 + l_{c2}\cos(q_1 + q_2)(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

Squared speed of link 1's CoM:

$$v_1^2 = \dot{x}_1^2 + \dot{y}_1^2 = l_{c1}^2\sin^2(q_1)\dot{q}_1^2 + l_{c1}^2\cos^2(q_1)\dot{q}_1^2 = l_{c1}^2\,\dot{q}_1^2$$

Squared speed of link 2's CoM:

$$v_2^2 = \dot{x}_2^2 + \dot{y}_2^2$$

Expanding term-by-term using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$:

$$v_2^2 = l_1^2\dot{q}_1^2 + l_{c2}^2(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2 + 2\,l_1 l_{c2}\cos(q_2)\,\dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)$$

The angular velocities of the two links are:

$$\omega_1 = \dot{q}_1, \qquad \omega_2 = \dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2$$

---

#### **Step 3 — Kinetic Energy $K$**

KE of link 1:

$$K_1 = \frac{1}{2}m_1 v_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}I_1\omega_1^2 = \frac{1}{2}(m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1)\dot{q}_1^2$$

KE of link 2:

$$K_2 = \frac{1}{2}m_2 v_2^2 + \frac{1}{2}I_2\omega_2^2$$

$$K_2 = \frac{1}{2}m_2 l_1^2\dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{1}{2}m_2 l_{c2}^2(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2 + m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos(q_2)\dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2) + \frac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2$$

Total KE:

$$K = K_1 + K_2$$

After expanding and grouping terms in powers of $\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j$:

$$K = \frac{1}{2}M_{11}\dot{q}_1^2 + M_{12}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \frac{1}{2}M_{22}\dot{q}_2^2$$

where the **inertia matrix elements** (read off from KE coefficients) are:

$$M_{11} = m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_1 + I_2$$

$$M_{12} = m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2$$

$$M_{22} = m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** The matrix $M$ is symmetric ($M_{12} = M_{21}$), and since all masses, lengths, and inertias are positive, $M$ is positive definite — as expected.

---

#### **Step 4 — Potential Energy $P$**

Taking the reference (zero) potential at $y = 0$:

$$P_1 = m_1 g\,y_1 = m_1 g\,l_{c1}\sin(q_1)$$

$$P_2 = m_2 g\,y_2 = m_2 g\,[l_1\sin(q_1) + l_{c2}\sin(q_1 + q_2)]$$

Total PE:

$$P = (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g\sin(q_1) + m_2 l_{c2} g\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

---

#### **Step 5 — Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = K - P$**

$$\mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{2}M_{11}\dot{q}_1^2 + M_{12}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \frac{1}{2}M_{22}\dot{q}_2^2 - (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g\sin(q_1) - m_2 l_{c2} g\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

---

#### **Step 6 — Apply Euler-Lagrange Equation**

##### EL Equation for $q_1$:

Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_1}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_1} = M_{11}\dot{q}_1 + M_{12}\dot{q}_2$$

Compute $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right)$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_1}\right) = M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + \dot{M}_{11}\dot{q}_1 + \dot{M}_{12}\dot{q}_2$$

Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_1}$ (note: $M_{11}$, $M_{12}$ depend on $q_2$ only, not $q_1$; only $P$ depends on $q_1$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_1} = -(m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g\cos(q_1) - m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

Apply EL:

$$\tau_1 = M_{11}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{12}\ddot{q}_2 + \dot{M}_{11}\dot{q}_1 + \dot{M}_{12}\dot{q}_2 + (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g\cos(q_1) + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

Now compute $\dot{M}_{11}$ and $\dot{M}_{12}$ (only $q_2$-dependence):

$$\dot{M}_{11} = -2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_2$$

$$\dot{M}_{12} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_2$$

Substitute:

$$\dot{M}_{11}\dot{q}_1 + \dot{M}_{12}\dot{q}_2 = -2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\dot{q}_2^2$$

Therefore, the **first equation of motion** is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 = &\; [m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_1 + I_2]\,\ddot{q}_1 \\
&+ [m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2]\,\ddot{q}_2 \\
&- 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_2^2 \\
&+ (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g\cos(q_1) + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

##### EL Equation for $q_2$:

Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_2}$:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_2} = M_{12}\dot{q}_1 + M_{22}\dot{q}_2$$

Compute $\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_2}\right)$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_2}\right) = M_{12}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + \dot{M}_{12}\dot{q}_1 + \dot{M}_{22}\dot{q}_2$$

But $M_{22}$ is constant (no $q$-dependence), so $\dot{M}_{22} = 0$. Also $\dot{M}_{12} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\dot{q}_2$.

Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2}$ (only $M_{11}$, $M_{12}$, and $P$ depend on $q_2$):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2} = \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{11}}{\partial q_2}\dot{q}_1^2 + \frac{\partial M_{12}}{\partial q_2}\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

With:

$$\frac{\partial M_{11}}{\partial q_2} = -2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2), \qquad \frac{\partial M_{12}}{\partial q_2} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)$$

So:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2} = -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\dot{q}_1^2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2)$$

Apply EL:

$$\tau_2 = M_{12}\ddot{q}_1 + M_{22}\ddot{q}_2 + \dot{M}_{12}\dot{q}_1 - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_2}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_2 = &\; [m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2]\,\ddot{q}_1 + [m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2]\,\ddot{q}_2 \\
&+ m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

---

#### **Step 7 — Assemble Canonical Form**

Reading off the matrices:

$$\begin{aligned}
M(q) &= \begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_1 + I_2 & m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2 \\ m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2 & m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
C(q, \dot{q}) &= \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_2 \\ m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin(q_2)\,\dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
G(q) &= \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g\cos(q_1) + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2) \end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

**Final compact form:**

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} \tau_1 \\ \tau_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

---

### 3.2 Verification: Special Case (Point Masses, No Inertia)

As a sanity check, set $I_1 = I_2 = 0$, $l_{c1} = l_1$, $l_{c2} = l_2$. Then:

$$M_{11} = (m_1 + m_2)l_1^2 + m_2 l_2^2 + 2 m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2$$

$$M_{12} = m_2 l_2^2 + m_2 l_1 l_2 \cos q_2$$

$$M_{22} = m_2 l_2^2$$

This matches the **standard 2R planar manipulator result** in Spong, Hutchinson & Vidyasagar (the canonical reference). The derivation is internally consistent.

### 3.3 General $n$-DOF Manipulator Energy Expressions

For an $n$-DOF manipulator, the **kinetic energy** in matrix form is:

$$K = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\,\dot{q}_i\dot{q}_j = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M(q)\dot{q}$$

The **gravity vector** element is:

$$G_i = \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} m_k\,g\,\frac{\partial h_k}{\partial q_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** In a 14-mark derivation question, students must show the *complete* EL chain for *both* joints, including the time derivative of the partial. Skipping the $\frac{d}{dt}$ step is a guaranteed 2–3 mark deduction.

## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block: Lagrangian Formulation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step A: Define generalized coordinates q1 to qn] --> B[Step B: Forward kinematics for each link]
    B --> C[Step C: Compute CoM positions and velocities]
    C --> D[Step D: Compute angular velocities of each link]
    D --> E[Step E: Form total kinetic energy K of q, qdot]
    E --> F[Step F: Form total potential energy P of q]
    F --> G[Step G: Lagrangian L = K minus P]
    G --> H[Step H: For each qi compute partial L by partial qidot]
    H --> I[Step I: Time differentiate the result]
    I --> J[Step J: Compute partial L by partial qi]
    J --> K[Step K: Form EL equation and set equal to joint torque tau i]
    K --> L[Step L: Collect terms into M of q, C of q qdot, G of q]
    L --> M[Final: M q qddot plus C q qdot qdot plus G q equals tau]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Model-Based Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Desired Trajectory qd, qddot d] --> B[Reference Model]
    B --> C[Tracking Error e = qd minus q]
    C --> D[Computed Torque Controller]
    E[Lagrangian Dynamic Model M of q, C of q qdot, G of q] --> D
    D --> F[Joint Torque Command tau]
    F --> G[Robot Manipulator Plant]
    G --> H[Sensor Feedback q, qdot]
    H --> I[Jacobians and Inertia Computations]
    I --> E
    H --> C
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block: Energy Flow in a Manipulator

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Actuator Torque tau] --> B[Accelerates Links]
    B --> C[Stores Kinetic Energy K]
    A --> D[Works against Gravity]
    D --> E[Stores Potential Energy P]
    B --> F[Coupled link motion]
    F --> G[Coriolis and Centrifugal terms]
    G --> H[Velocity dependent torques]
    C --> I[Total Lagrangian L = K minus P]
    E --> I
    I --> J[Euler-Lagrange Equation]
    J --> K[M q qddot + C q qdot qdot + G q = tau]
```

### 4.4 Schematic: 2-DOF Planar Manipulator (Reference for Derivation)

```
                y
                │
                │      ● C2  (CoM of link 2)
                │     ╱
                │    ╱  l_c2
                │   ╱
                │  ●─────●  q2 (joint 2)
                │  l1    
                │  │
                │  │ l_c1
                │  ● C1 (CoM of link 1)
                │  │
                │ ─┼───────  q1 (joint 1, base)
                │ ╱
                │╱  x
```

- Joint 1 is at the origin (base).
- Link 1 has length $l_1$ along angle $q_1$ from horizontal.
- Joint 2 is at the end of link 1.
- Link 2 has length $l_2$ along angle $q_1 + q_2$ from horizontal.
- $C_1$ and $C_2$ are centers of mass.
