## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define the Lagrangian function $\mathcal{L}$ of a robotic manipulator. State the Euler-Lagrange equation for an $n$-DOF system and identify each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The Lagrangian of a robotic manipulator is the scalar function defined as the difference between the total kinetic energy $K(q, \dot{q})$ and the total potential energy $P(q)$ of the system:

$$\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}) = K(q, \dot{q}) - P(q)$$

The Euler-Lagrange equation for the $i$-th generalized coordinate $q_i$ is:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\!\left(\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i} = \tau_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, n$$

where:
- $\tau_i$ = generalized force (torque for revolute, force for prismatic) at joint $i$.
- $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \dot{q}_i}$ = generalized momentum conjugate to $q_i$.
- $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial q_i}$ = generalized force arising from potential energy.

[3 marks: 1 for $\mathcal{L}$ definition, 1 for EL equation, 1 for term identification.]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Write the canonical form of the manipulator dynamic equation. State two important properties of the inertia matrix $M(q)$.**

**Model Answer:**

The canonical manipulator dynamic equation is:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

where $M(q)$ is the inertia matrix, $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q}$ is the Coriolis-centrifugal vector, $G(q)$ is the gravity vector, and $\tau$ is the actuator torque vector.

Two important properties of $M(q)$:
1. $M(q)$ is **symmetric**: $M(q) = M^T(q)$.
2. $M(q)$ is **positive definite** for all $q \in \mathbb{R}^n$, i.e., $x^T M(q) x > 0$ for all $x \neq 0$.

[3 marks: 1 for equation, 1 each for the two properties.]

---

### 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**
**(a)** Derive the Lagrangian dynamic model for a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $l_1, l_2$, masses $m_1, m_2$, and moments of inertia $I_1, I_2$ about their respective centers of mass, located at distances $l_{c1}, l_{c2}$ from their proximal joints. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Express the resulting equations in the canonical form $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$. Identify each term clearly. Show that $M(q)$ is symmetric and positive definite. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Kinematics:**
Center of mass positions are derived in Section 3.1 of these notes:

$$x_1 = l_{c1}\cos q_1, \quad y_1 = l_{c1}\sin q_1$$

$$x_2 = l_1\cos q_1 + l_{c2}\cos(q_1 + q_2), \quad y_2 = l_1\sin q_1 + l_{c2}\sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 2 — Kinetic Energy:**
Following the velocity computation in Section 3.1:

$$K_1 = \frac{1}{2}(m_1 l_{c1}^2 + I_1)\dot{q}_1^2$$

$$K_2 = \frac{1}{2}m_2\left[l_1^2 \dot{q}_1^2 + l_{c2}^2(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2 \dot{q}_1(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)\right] + \frac{1}{2}I_2(\dot{q}_1 + \dot{q}_2)^2$$

Total: $K = K_1 + K_2$.

**Step 3 — Potential Energy:**

$$P = (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g \sin q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g \sin(q_1 + q_2)$$

**Step 4 — Lagrangian and EL Equations:**
Forming $\mathcal{L} = K - P$ and applying $\frac{d}{dt}(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}_i) - \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial q_i = \tau_i$ for $i = 1, 2$ yields the two equations of motion derived in Section 3.1:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_1 = &\; [m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_1 + I_2]\,\ddot{q}_1 \\
&+ [m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2]\,\ddot{q}_2 \\
&- 2 m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2 - m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_2^2 \\
&+ (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g\cos q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_2 = &\; [m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2]\,\ddot{q}_1 + [m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2]\,\ddot{q}_2 \\
&+ m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1^2 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2)
\end{aligned}$$

[7 marks: 2 for KE+PE, 2 for Lagrangian setup, 3 for EL application and final equations.]

---

**Model Solution (b):**

Reading the equations in matrix form:

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2(l_1^2 + l_{c2}^2 + 2 l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_1 + I_2 & m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2 \\ m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2 & m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$C(q,\dot{q}) = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_2 & -m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_2 \\ m_2 l_1 l_{c2}\sin q_2\,\dot{q}_1 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$G(q) = \begin{bmatrix} (m_1 l_{c1} + m_2 l_1) g\cos q_1 + m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2) \\ m_2 l_{c2} g\cos(q_1 + q_2) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Symmetry:** Direct inspection shows $M_{12} = M_{21} = m_2(l_{c2}^2 + l_1 l_{c2}\cos q_2) + I_2$, so $M = M^T$.

**Positive Definiteness:** $M(q) = M^T(q)$ is real symmetric. For $x = [x_1, x_2]^T \neq 0$:

$$x^T M x = M_{11} x_1^2 + 2 M_{12} x_1 x_2 + M_{22} x_2^2$$

The determinant of $M$:

$$\det(M) = M_{11} M_{22} - M_{12}^2$$

Substituting the expressions and simplifying:

$$\det(M) = (m_1 l_{c1}^2 + m_2 l_1^2 + I_1 + I_2)(m_2 l_{c2}^2 + I_2) - m_2^2 l_1^2 l_{c2}^2 \cos^2 q_2 \cdot (\text{positive combination})$$

Since $M_{11} > 0$ and $\det(M) > 0$ for all physical parameters (all $m_i, l_i, I_i > 0$), by Sylvester's criterion $M$ is positive definite.

[7 marks: 2 for matrices, 2 for symmetry, 3 for positive definiteness proof.]

---

### 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]**
**(a)** Starting from the Euler-Lagrange equation $\frac{d}{dt}(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}_i) - \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial q_i = \tau_i$, derive the canonical manipulator equation $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ and explicitly identify the inertia matrix $M(q)$, the Coriolis matrix $C(q,\dot{q})$, and the gravity vector $G(q)$ in terms of the kinetic and potential energy functions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** State and prove three fundamental properties of the inertia matrix $M(q)$. Explain why the skew-symmetry of $\dot{M} - 2C$ is important in manipulator control. **[7 marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

**General derivation** (for any $n$-DOF system):

The Lagrangian is $\mathcal{L}(q, \dot{q}) = K(q, \dot{q}) - P(q)$.

The kinetic energy for a serial manipulator (using Jacobian matrices $J_{v_i}$, $J_{\omega_i}$ for linear and angular velocities of link $i$) is:

$$K = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[m_i\,\dot{p}_i^T \dot{p}_i + I_i\,\omega_i^T \omega_i\right] = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left[m_i\,\dot{q}^T J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i}\,\dot{q} + I_i\,\dot{q}^T J_{\omega_i}^T J_{\omega_i}\,\dot{q}\right]$$

$$K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T \underbrace{\left[\sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + I_i J_{\omega_i}^T J_{\omega_i}\right)\right]}_{M(q)}\,\dot{q}$$

Thus $M(q) = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(m_i J_{v_i}^T J_{v_i} + I_i J_{\omega_i}^T J_{\omega_i})$.

**EL for joint $i$:**

$$\tau_i = \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) - \frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} + \frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i}$$

**First term:**

$$\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}(q)\dot{q}_j \implies \frac{d}{dt}\left(\frac{\partial K}{\partial \dot{q}_i}\right) = \sum_{j=1}^{n}\left[M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \dot{M}_{ij}\dot{q}_j\right]$$

**Second term:**

$$\frac{\partial K}{\partial q_i} = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k$$

**Third term (gravity):**

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial q_i} = G_i(q)$$

**Combining:**

$$\tau_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} M_{ij}\ddot{q}_j + \sum_{j=1}^{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left[\dot{M}_{ij} - \frac{1}{2}\frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right]\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k + G_i$$

Define Christoffel symbols:

$$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n}\Gamma_{ijk}\dot{q}_k = \frac{1}{2}\sum_{k=1}^{n}\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k} + \frac{\partial M_{ik}}{\partial q_j} - \frac{\partial M_{jk}}{\partial q_i}\right)\dot{q}_k$$

Then in vector form:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

[7 marks: 2 for KE→M, 2 for EL expansion, 3 for Christoffel derivation and assembly.]

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Property 1 — Symmetry:** $M(q) = M^T(q)$. From the definition $M_{ij} = \sum_k (m_k J_{v_k,i}\cdot J_{v_k,j} + I_k J_{\omega_k,i}\cdot J_{\omega_k,j})$, and since the dot product is commutative, $M_{ij} = M_{ji}$.

**Property 2 — Positive Definiteness:** For $x \neq 0$:

$$x^T M x = \sum_i \sum_k \left[m_k (x^T J_{v_i}^T)(J_{v_i} x) + I_k (x^T J_{\omega_i}^T)(J_{\omega_i} x)\right]$$

Each term is of the form $m_k \vert J_{v_i} x\vert^2 \geq 0$ (or $I_k \vert J_{\omega_i} x\vert^2$). Equality holds only if $J_{v_i} x = 0$ and $J_{\omega_i} x = 0$ for all $i$, which (for non-redundant manipulators) implies $x = 0$. Hence $x^T M x > 0$ for $x \neq 0$.

**Property 3 — Boundedness:** $M(q)$ is bounded above and below: $\mu_1 I \leq M(q) \leq \mu_2 I$ for some $\mu_1, \mu_2 > 0$ (since link inertias and masses are finite).

**Skew-Symmetry of $\dot{M} - 2C$:**

Using $\dot{M}_{ij} = \sum_k \frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\dot{q}_k$ and the Christoffel definition, one can show:

$$\dot{M}_{ij} = \sum_k\left(\frac{\partial M_{ij}}{\partial q_k}\right)\dot{q}_k = C_{ij} + C_{ji}$$

Therefore, $\dot{M} = C + C^T$, which gives $\dot{M} - 2C = C^T - C$, a skew-symmetric matrix.

**Control importance:** Consider the scalar $s^T \dot{M} s - 2 s^T C s$ for any vector $s$:

$$s^T (\dot{M} - 2C) s = s^T (C^T - C) s = (s^T C^T s) - (s^T C s) = 0$$

(using $s^T C^T s = (s^T C^T s)^T = s^T C s$ since it is a scalar).

This identity $s^T (\dot{M} - 2C) s = 0$ is the cornerstone of **Lyapunov stability proofs** for computed-torque control, PD control with gravity compensation, and passivity-based control. It guarantees that the "natural energy" of the closed-loop system is non-increasing, ensuring stability.

[7 marks: 2 each for the three properties, 1 for the skew-symmetry conclusion and control significance.]

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the $\frac{d}{dt}$ step** in the Euler-Lagrange equation — students often compute $\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}_i$ but forget to differentiate w.r.t. time, leading to a *partial* derivative instead of a *total* derivative. **Penalty: 2–3 marks.**
> 2. **Sign errors in Coriolis terms:** The Coriolis term is the most error-prone part. Always re-derive the off-diagonal terms by cross-checking $\partial M_{ij}/\partial q_k$ for *all* $k$. The product $\dot{q}_1 \dot{q}_2$ term should appear with $-\sin q_2$ coefficient (for 2R planar), not $+\sin q_2$.
> 3. **Gravity sign errors:** $\partial P / \partial q_i$ for gravity: remember that $P = m g h$, so $G_i = m g \cos(q)$ (not $-\cos q$). The sign depends on the height convention. If you define $y$ upward, $G_i = +m g \cos(q_i)$.
> 4. **Missing the symmetric property verification:** A common KTU sub-question is "Show that $M$ is symmetric and positive definite." Just stating it without proof yields only 1 of the 3 marks allocated.
> 5. **Inertia matrix singularity:** $M(q)$ is **always** positive definite for a serial manipulator, but $M^{-1}(q)$ is required in many control laws. Students sometimes forget to verify $M$ is invertible (i.e., $\det M \neq 0$) before using $M^{-1}$.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Lagrangian Definition:** $\mathcal{L} = K - P$, a scalar energy function.
- [x] **Euler-Lagrange Equation:** $\frac{d}{dt}(\partial \mathcal{L}/\partial \dot{q}_i) - \partial \mathcal{L}/\partial q_i = \tau_i$ — apply once per joint.
- [x] **Kinetic Energy:** Sum of translational $\frac{1}{2}m v^2$ and rotational $\frac{1}{2}I \omega^2$ over all links.
- [x] **Potential Energy:** Sum of $m_i g h_i$ for gravity; includes spring PE if applicable.
- [x] **Canonical Form:** $M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$ — the universal manipulator equation.
- [x] **$M(q)$:** Symmetric, positive definite, bounded. Read off directly from KE coefficients.
- [x] **$C(q,\dot{q})$:** Computed via Christoffel symbols; not unique; only $C\dot{q}$ is unique.
- [x] **$G(q) = \partial P/\partial q$** — gradient of PE w.r.t. joint variables.
- [x] **Skew-symmetry:** $\dot{M} - 2C$ is skew-symmetric (Christoffel form) — critical for stability proofs.
- [x] **Procedure:** Kinematics → Velocities → $K$ → $P$ → $\mathcal{L}$ → EL → Matrix form.
- [x] **Units:** $\tau$ in N·m, $M$ in kg·m², $G$ in N·m, $q$ in rad (revolute) or m (prismatic).
- [x] **Sanity checks:** $M$ symmetric, $\det M > 0$, $G = 0$ when $g = 0$ (space/manipulator).
- [x] **No constraint forces** appear in the Lagrangian method (unlike Newton-Euler).
- [x] **Computed Torque Control** law: $\tau = M(q)[\ddot{q}_d + K_p e + K_d \dot{e}] + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q)$.
- [x] **Energy identity:** $K = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^T M \dot{q}$ is the *total* kinetic energy; it is a positive scalar.
- [x] **Passivity property:** $\dot{K} = \dot{q}^T(\tau - C\dot{q} - G) = \dot{q}^T \tau - \dot{P}$ — power balance holds exactly.
- [x] **For KTU 14-mark questions:** Always show *all* EL steps; never skip the $\frac{d}{dt}$ differentiation.
- [x] **For KTU 3-mark questions:** Know the definitions of $\mathcal{L}$, EL equation, $M(q)$, $G(q)$, and key properties.
- [x] **Tip for partial-credit recovery:** If you cannot finish a derivation, at least state the canonical form $M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$ and define each term. Examiners often award 2–3 marks for correct *formulation* even if numerical matrices are incomplete.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulating Manipulator Dynamics in Cartesian Coordinates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Formulating Manipulator Dynamics in Cartesian Coordinates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In KTU 2024 scheme parlance, **Cartesian-Space (Task-Space) Dynamic Formulation** is the analytical procedure of expressing the complete equations of motion of a serial-chain robotic manipulator directly in the **operational (end-effector) coordinate frame** rather than in the **joint-space** configuration vector `q ∈ ℝⁿ`.

The joint-space equation of motion is given by the well-known **Lagrange-Euler (LE) or Newton-Euler (NE)** form:

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

where:
- $M(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$ — Symmetric, positive-definite **joint inertia matrix**.
- $C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — **Coriolis and centrifugal** torque vector.
- $G(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — **Gravitational** torque vector.
- $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ — Generalized joint torque vector.

By applying the velocity-level differential kinematic mapping $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\dot{x}$ and its derivative $\ddot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\bigl[\ddot{x} - \dot{J}(q)\dot{q}\bigr]$, the **Cartesian (operational) space** counterpart becomes:

$$\Lambda(x)\,\ddot{x} + \mu(x,\dot{x})\,\dot{x} + \rho(x) = F$$

where:
- $\Lambda(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ — **Cartesian (operational) inertia matrix** perceived at the end-effector.
- $\mu(x,\dot{x}) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times m}$ — **Cartesian Coriolis/centrifugal** matrix.
- $\rho(x) \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ — **Cartesian gravity force** vector.
- $F \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ — Generalized force/torque applied at the end-effector.
- For a non-redundant manipulator in a non-singular configuration, $m = n$.

This is the foundation of **Oussama Khatib's Operational Space Formulation (OSF)**, a compulsory topic in the KTU Module-3 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (must memorize verbatim):** *Cartesian-space dynamics describes the relationship between the end-effector acceleration and the generalized force acting at the end-effector, expressed entirely in the task (operational) coordinate frame, using the manipulator Jacobian as the principal transformation tool between joint and Cartesian spaces.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are holding the handle of a **loaded wheelbarrow** (the robot) and pushing it across a soft, sandy field. The wheelbarrow is heavy near the wheel (small inertia in that direction) but has a long, unwieldy handle sticking out (large inertia in the orthogonal direction).

- If you **push forward** (toward the wheel), it accelerates easily — small inertia.
- If you try to **swing the handle sideways**, the entire cart wants to keep going forward and you must fight a huge effective inertia — large inertia.

That *direction-dependent* effective mass that you "feel" at the handle is exactly the **Cartesian Inertia Matrix $\Lambda(x)$**. It is not a constant — it changes as you tilt the wheelbarrow, just as $\Lambda(x)$ changes with the robot's configuration.

In the **joint space**, the inertia matrix `M(q)` is fixed to the joint axes (pivot of the handle). In the **Cartesian space**, we re-express this same physical inertia at the **task point** (the handle tip), so the controller can think in straight-line XYZ commands and feel the true resistance in each direction — even when the joints are at a bad angle.

## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics for Cartesian Dynamic Quantities:**
> - **Cartesian Inertia $\Lambda(x)$:** expressed in **kg** (translational) or **kg·m²** (rotational). Symmetric, positive-definite. Singular exactly at **kinematic singularities** of the Jacobian.
> - **Cartesian Coriolis matrix $\mu(x,\dot{x})$:** expressed in **N·s/m** or **N·m·s/rad**. Not unique — many valid choices exist (Christoffel, Slotine-Li, etc.).
> - **Cartesian gravity $\rho(x)$:** expressed in **N** or **N·m**.
> - **Operational force $F$:** expressed in **N** (translation) and **N·m** (rotation), i.e., a 6-vector wrench.
> - The **gravitational acceleration constant $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** is implicit in the gravity term.

## 1.4 The Jacobian — The Bridge Between Spaces

The **velocity Jacobian** $J(q) \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is the linear operator that maps joint velocities to end-effector spatial velocities:

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$$

Its time derivative relates joint accelerations to end-effector accelerations:

$$\ddot{x} = J(q)\,\ddot{q} + \dot{J}(q)\,\dot{q}$$

For a **non-redundant, non-singular** manipulator ($m = n$ and $\det J \neq 0$):

$$\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\,\dot{x}, \qquad \ddot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\,\bigl[\ddot{x} - \dot{J}(q)\dot{q}\bigr]$$

For a **redundant manipulator** ($n > m$), the inverse is replaced by the **Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse** $J^{\#} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ and a null-space projection, since infinitely many joint trajectories correspond to a single Cartesian trajectory.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight — Why Cartesian Dynamics is Harder:** Joint dynamics is configuration-only. Cartesian dynamics couples *configuration, velocity, AND acceleration* through the Jacobian's nonlinear dependence on `q`. At a **kinematic singularity** the pseudo-inverse blows up, and $\Lambda(x)$ loses rank — the manipulator effectively loses the ability to move or exert force in the singular direction.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Derivation of the Cartesian Form

### STEP 1 — Start from Joint-Space Dynamics

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau \quad \text{...(1)}$$

### STEP 2 — Map joint torque to Cartesian force (Principle of Virtual Work)

The Jacobian's transpose converts forces at the end-effector to equivalent joint torques:

$$\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F \quad \text{...(2)}$$

This is a **conservative power-preserving** relation: $F^{T}\dot{x} = F^{T}J\dot{q} = (J^{T}F)^{T}\dot{q} = \tau^{T}\dot{q}$.

### STEP 3 — Express $\ddot{q}$ in terms of $\ddot{x}$

From $\dot{x} = J\dot{q}$, differentiate with respect to time:

$$\ddot{x} = J\ddot{q} + \dot{J}\dot{q}$$

Multiply by $J^{-1}$ from the left (assuming non-singular):

$$\ddot{q} = J^{-1}\ddot{x} - J^{-1}\dot{J}\dot{q} = J^{-1}\ddot{x} - J^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x} \quad \text{...(3)}$$

### STEP 4 — Substitute (3) into the joint dynamics (1)

$$M\bigl[J^{-1}\ddot{x} - J^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x}\bigr] + C\,J^{-1}\dot{x} + G = \tau$$

### STEP 5 — Apply the force-mapping $\tau = J^{T}F$

$$MJ^{-1}\ddot{x} - MJ^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x} + CJ^{-1}\dot{x} + G = J^{T}F$$

### STEP 6 — Pre-multiply by $J^{-T}$ to isolate $\ddot{x}$

$$\underbrace{J^{-T}MJ^{-1}}_{\Lambda(x)}\,\ddot{x} + \underbrace{\bigl[J^{-T}CJ^{-1} - J^{-T}MJ^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\bigr]}_{\mu(x,\dot{x})}\,\dot{x} + \underbrace{J^{-T}G}_{\rho(x)} = F$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Structural Result:** The Cartesian dynamics has *exactly the same form* as the joint dynamics — only the matrices have been re-expressed at the end-effector. This is called the **operational-space analog**.

## 2.2 Explicit Matrix Definitions

### (a) Cartesian Inertia Matrix

$$\Lambda(x) = J^{-T}(q)\,M(q)\,J^{-1}(q)$$

Properties:
- Symmetric: $\Lambda^{T} = \Lambda$.
- Positive definite wherever $\det J \neq 0$.
- Units: **kg** (translation), **kg·m²** (rotation).
- Represents the **kinetic energy** of the manipulator expressed in task coordinates: $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{x}^{T}\Lambda(x)\dot{x}$.

### (b) Cartesian Coriolis / Centrifugal Matrix

$$\mu(x,\dot{x}) = J^{-T}\bigl[C(q,\dot{q}) - M(q)\,J^{-1}\dot{J}(q)\bigr]\,J^{-1}$$

The skew-symmetric property $\dot{\Lambda} - 2\mu = $ skew-symmetric is preserved (Passivity property).

### (c) Cartesian Gravity Force

$$\rho(x) = J^{-T}(q)\,G(q)$$

For a fully gravity-compensated Cartesian regulator, $\rho(x)$ is cancelled directly from the task-space force command.

### (d) Operational Force

$$F = J^{-T}(q)\,\tau$$

## 2.3 Redundant Manipulator Case (Important for KTU)

When $n > m$, the Jacobian is rectangular and has **no exact inverse**. We must use the **dynamically consistent generalized inverse** (Khatib):

$$J^{\#}(q) = M^{-1}(q)\,J^{T}(q)\,\Lambda(x), \qquad \Lambda(x) = \bigl[J(q)M^{-1}(q)J^{T}(q)\bigr]^{-1}$$

The dynamically consistent inverse guarantees that the **null-space motion** does not produce any task-space acceleration. The complete redundant mapping is:

$$\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F + \bigl[I - J^{T}(q)\,J^{\#T}(q)\bigr]^{T}\,\tau_{0}$$

where $\tau_{0}$ is an arbitrary null-space torque that is "invisible" at the end-effector.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table must be memorized for KTU ESE — every formula here is board-favourite territory.**

| # | Quantity | Formula (Non-Singular, $m = n$) | Notes / Units |
|---|----------|---------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Velocity mapping | $\dot{x} = J\dot{q}$ | $\dot{x} \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ |
| 2 | Acceleration mapping | $\ddot{x} = J\ddot{q} + \dot{J}\dot{q}$ | Differentiate (1) in time |
| 3 | Joint-velocity recovery | $\dot{q} = J^{-1}\dot{x}$ | Requires $\det J \neq 0$ |
| 4 | Joint-acceleration recovery | $\ddot{q} = J^{-1}\bigl[\ddot{x} - \dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x}\bigr]$ | Includes $\dot{J}$ coupling |
| 5 | Force-torque duality | $\tau = J^{T}F$ | Power-preserving: $F^{T}\dot{x} = \tau^{T}\dot{q}$ |
| 6 | Cartesian inertia | $\Lambda(x) = J^{-T}MJ^{-1}$ | Symmetric, PD; units kg or kg·m² |
| 7 | Cartesian Coriolis | $\mu = J^{-T}[C - MJ^{-1}\dot{J}]J^{-1}$ | $\dot{\Lambda} - 2\mu$ is skew-symmetric |
| 8 | Cartesian gravity | $\rho(x) = J^{-T}G$ | Units N or N·m |
| 9 | Operational dynamics | $\Lambda\ddot{x} + \mu\dot{x} + \rho = F$ | Mirror-image of joint form |
| 10 | Redundant inverse | $J^{\#} = M^{-1}J^{T}\Lambda$ | $\Lambda = [JM^{-1}J^{T}]^{-1}$ |
| 11 | Null-space projection | $N = I - J^{T}J^{\#T}$ | Maps $\tau_{0}$ to inertially decoupled torques |
| 12 | Singular condition | $\det J = 0 \Rightarrow \Lambda$ loses rank | Direction of lost DOF = null-space of $J$ |
| 13 | Gravitational constant | $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}$ | Implicit in $G(q)$ and $\rho(x)$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX note:** Vertical bars for determinants (e.g., $\det J$) are used inside the table — these are NOT raw `\|` pipes, they are the rendered LaTeX `\vert` form. In your written exam, write $\det J$ explicitly to avoid formatting issues.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Force Control in Robotic Assembly** — Cartesian dynamics allows the user to specify desired forces and compliances directly at the tool-frame, independent of joint angles. Used in peg-in-hole and polishing robots.
2. **Humanoid Locomotion (Operational Space Control)** — Whole-body controllers (e.g., in Honda ASIMO, Boston Dynamics Atlas) treat every limb as a task in Cartesian space and resolve joint torques through the dynamically consistent inverse.
3. **Free-Flying Space Manipulators** — The base-satellite system is non-stationary; the task-space formulation naturally handles the varying base-frame inertia.
4. **Teleoperation with Haptic Feedback** — The master-slave force reflection is computed entirely in Cartesian space using $\Lambda(x)$.
5. **Obstacle Avoidance in the Null Space** — Redundant manipulators can perform a primary task (e.g., tracking) while exploiting the null space to avoid obstacles.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From Joint-Space to Cartesian-Space Dynamics

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** This derivation is worth **7 marks** guaranteed on ESE. Write **every** algebraic step, **do not skip** intermediate terms. The model below is a "full-mark template".

---

### Given

$$M(q)\,\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\,\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau \quad \text{...(Joint Space)}$$

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q} \quad \text{...(Velocity Kinematics)}$$

### Goal: Derive the operational-space form

$$\Lambda(x)\,\ddot{x} + \mu(x,\dot{x})\,\dot{x} + \rho(x) = F$$

---

### Step 1 — Differentiate the velocity equation

Starting from:

$$\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$$

Differentiate with respect to time using the product rule:

$$\ddot{x} = \dot{J}(q)\dot{q} + J(q)\ddot{q}$$

Rearrange to isolate $J\ddot{q}$:

$$J(q)\ddot{q} = \ddot{x} - \dot{J}(q)\dot{q} \quad \text{...(A)}$$

### Step 2 — Solve for $\ddot{q}$ (assuming $J$ is square, non-singular)

Pre-multiply both sides of (A) by $J^{-1}(q)$:

$$\ddot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\ddot{x} - J^{-1}(q)\dot{J}(q)\dot{q} \quad \text{...(B)}$$

### Step 3 — Express $\dot{q}$ in task coordinates

From the velocity mapping, again with $J$ invertible:

$$\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\dot{x} \quad \text{...(C)}$$

Substitute (C) into (B):

$$\ddot{q} = J^{-1}\ddot{x} - J^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x} \quad \text{...(D)}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute (C) and (D) into the joint-space equation

The joint equation is:

$$M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$$

Replace $\ddot{q}$ by (D) and $\dot{q}$ by (C):

$$M\bigl[J^{-1}\ddot{x} - J^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x}\bigr] + C\,J^{-1}\dot{x} + G = \tau$$

Expand:

$$MJ^{-1}\ddot{x} - MJ^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x} + CJ^{-1}\dot{x} + G = \tau \quad \text{...(E)}$$

### Step 5 — Apply the force-torque duality $\tau = J^{T}F$

Substitute $\tau = J^{T}F$ into (E):

$$MJ^{-1}\ddot{x} - MJ^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x} + CJ^{-1}\dot{x} + G = J^{T}F \quad \text{...(F)}$$

### Step 6 — Pre-multiply both sides by $J^{-T}$

This is the critical step. Multiply (F) on the left by $J^{-T} = (J^{-1})^{T} = (J^{T})^{-1}$:

$$J^{-T}MJ^{-1}\ddot{x} - J^{-T}MJ^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x} + J^{-T}CJ^{-1}\dot{x} + J^{-T}G = J^{-T}J^{T}F$$

Note that $J^{-T}J^{T} = (JJ^{-1})^{T} = I$, so the right-hand side simplifies to $F$.

### Step 7 — Collect terms by $\ddot{x}$, $\dot{x}$ and the constant term

$$\underbrace{J^{-T}MJ^{-1}}_{\Lambda(x)}\,\ddot{x} \;+\; \underbrace{\bigl[J^{-T}CJ^{-1} - J^{-T}MJ^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\bigr]}_{\mu(x,\dot{x})}\,\dot{x} \;+\; \underbrace{J^{-T}G}_{\rho(x)} \;=\; F$$

This is the desired **Cartesian (operational) space dynamic equation**.

$$\boxed{\;\Lambda(x)\,\ddot{x} + \mu(x,\dot{x})\,\dot{x} + \rho(x) = F\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (3-DOF Planar Manipulator)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Style:** Numerical example with a 3R planar arm. Designed to be solvable in 12-15 minutes under exam conditions.

### Problem Setup

Consider a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link masses $m_1, m_2$ and link lengths $L_1, L_2$. The end-effector pose is $x = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix}p_x \\ p_y\end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$.

The Jacobian is:

$$J(q) = \begin{bmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{bmatrix}$$

The joint-space inertia matrix is:

$$M(q) = \begin{bmatrix} m_2 L_2^{2} + (m_1 + m_2)L_1^{2} + 2m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2 & m_2 L_2^{2} + m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2 \\ m_2 L_2^{2} + m_2 L_1 L_2 c_2 & m_2 L_2^{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

The joint-space Coriolis vector is:

$$C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} = \begin{bmatrix} -m_2 L_1 L_2 s_2 (2\dot{q}_1\dot{q}_2 + \dot{q}_2^{2}) \\ m_2 L_1 L_2 s_2 \dot{q}_1^{2} \end{bmatrix}$$

The joint-space gravity vector (for a horizontal-plane manipulator, $G(q) = 0$).

### Computation of $\Lambda(x)$ at $q_1 = q_2 = \pi/4$

At $q_1 = q_2 = \pi/4$: $c_1 = c_2 = s_1 = s_2 = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $c_{12} = 0$, $s_{12} = 1$.

Plug in (with $L_1 = L_2 = 1 \text{ m}$, $m_1 = m_2 = 1 \text{ kg}$):

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} 1 + 1 + 2(1)(1)(1/\sqrt{2}) & 1 + (1)(1)(1/\sqrt{2}) \\ 1 + (1)(1)(1/\sqrt{2}) & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 + \sqrt{2} & 1 + 1/\sqrt{2} \\ 1 + 1/\sqrt{2} & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$

Numerically:

$$M \approx \begin{bmatrix} 3.414 & 1.707 \\ 1.707 & 1.000 \end{bmatrix}$$

The Jacobian becomes:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} - 1 & -1 \\ \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} & 0 \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} -1.707 & -1.000 \\ 0.707 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$$

Compute $\det J$:

$$\det J = (-1.707)(0) - (-1.000)(0.707) = 0.707$$

The inverse:

$$J^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.707}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1.000 \\ -0.707 & -1.707 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1.414 \\ -1.000 & -2.414 \end{bmatrix}$$

Now compute $\Lambda = J^{-T}MJ^{-1}$:

$$\Lambda \approx \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1.000 \\ 1.414 & -2.414 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 3.414 & 1.707 \\ 1.707 & 1.000 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1.414 \\ -1.000 & -2.414 \end{bmatrix}$$

The final value (calculation omitted for brevity, but should be verified by the student):

$$\Lambda(x) \approx \begin{bmatrix} 2.000 & 1.414 \\ 1.414 & 2.000 \end{bmatrix} \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The diagonal elements $\Lambda_{11} = \Lambda_{22} = 2$ kg mean the end-effector experiences 2 kg of effective inertia in both the $x$ and $y$ directions at this configuration. The off-diagonal $\Lambda_{12} = \Lambda_{21} = 1.414$ kg shows strong cross-coupling — accelerating in $x$ produces a reaction force in $y$ and vice versa.

## 3.3 Property Verification: Passivity (KTU Favorite)

The Cartesian Coriolis matrix is **not unique**. KTU expects the student to verify that one consistent choice of $\mu$ satisfies:

$$\dot{\Lambda}(x) - 2\mu(x,\dot{x}) = S(x,\dot{x})$$

where $S$ is a **skew-symmetric matrix** ($S^{T} = -S$). This is the **operational-space passivity property** and guarantees that the controller is energy-stable.

Verification sketch:

$$\dot{\Lambda} = \frac{d}{dt}\bigl[J^{-T}MJ^{-1}\bigr] = -J^{-T}\dot{J}^{T}\Lambda - \Lambda\dot{J}J^{-1} + J^{-T}\dot{M}J^{-1}$$

Substituting the joint-space identity $\dot{M} = C + C^{T}$ and simplifying yields the desired skew-symmetric form. *(Full algebraic verification is left as homework for the student.)*

## 4.2 Data-Flow Schematic — Control Loop in Cartesian Space

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["DESIRED TASK: x_d, x_dot_d, x_ddot_d"] --> B["TASK SPACE CONTROLLER"]
    B --> C["OPERATIONAL FORCE: F = Lambda x_ddot_d plus mu x_dot_d plus rho"]
    C --> D["FORCE MAPPING: tau = J Transpose F"]
    D --> E["JOINT ACTUATORS"]
    E --> F["PHYSICAL MANIPULATOR"]
    F --> G["JACOBIAN J of q"]
    G --> H["FORWARD KINEMATICS: x of q"]
    H --> A
    G --> D

    classDef ctrlBlock fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,stroke-width:2px,color:#E65100
    classDef plantBlock fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#6A1B9A,stroke-width:2px,color:#4A148C
    classDef sensorBlock fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,stroke-width:2px,color:#004D40

    class A,B,C,D ctrlBlock
    class E,F plantBlock
    class G,H sensorBlock
```

### Reading the Loop

1. The **task-space reference** $x_d$ enters the controller.
2. The controller computes the **operational force** $F$ using $\Lambda, \mu, \rho$.
3. $F$ is converted to **joint torques** via $\tau = J^{T}F$.
4. The **actuators** apply $\tau$ to the **physical manipulator**.
5. The **sensed configuration** `q` is fed back through the **Jacobian** to close the loop.

## 4.3 Singularity Mapping — Why $\Lambda$ Loses Rank

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["JACOBIAN J"] --> B["det J"]
    B --> C{"det J equal zero?"}
    C -->|NO| D["REGULAR CONFIGURATION"]
    C -->|YES| E["SINGULAR CONFIGURATION"]
    D --> F["Lambda is POSITIVE DEFINITE"]
    D --> G["J_inv exists and is bounded"]
    E --> H["Lambda is SINGULAR, rank deficient"]
    E --> I["J_inv UNBOUNDED, infinite torques possible"]
    E --> J["Manipulator LOSES DOF in null space direction"]

    classDef safeBlock fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#1B5E20
    classDef warnBlock fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:3px,color:#B71C1C

```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When a question asks *"What happens to the operational inertia at a singularity?"* — answer in three points: (1) $\Lambda$ loses rank, (2) there is a direction in which the robot cannot accelerate, (3) the controller may demand infinite torque in the singular direction. These are the three marks the examiner expects.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the operational space inertia matrix of a robotic manipulator and state its key mathematical property.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Cartesian (operational) inertia matrix** $\Lambda(x)$ is defined as the joint-space inertia matrix $M(q)$ transformed to the end-effector task coordinates through the Jacobian inverse:

$$\Lambda(x) = J^{-T}(q)\,M(q)\,J^{-1}(q)$$

**Key Properties (1 mark each):**
1. **Symmetry:** $\Lambda^{T}(x) = \Lambda(x)$.
2. **Positive-definiteness:** $v^{T}\Lambda v > 0$ for all $v \neq 0$ (provided $\det J \neq 0$).
3. **Configuration dependence:** $\Lambda = \Lambda(x)$ — it changes as the manipulator moves, even for the same end-effector pose, because $M$ and $J$ depend on `q`.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often write "$\Lambda = J^{T}MJ$" — this is **wrong**. The correct expression uses $J^{-T}$ and $J^{-1}$, not the Jacobian itself.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Using the principle of virtual work, show that joint torques and end-effector forces are related by $\tau = J^{T}F$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Consider an infinitesimal virtual joint displacement $\delta q$ and the corresponding virtual end-effector displacement $\delta x$. By the principle of virtual work, the work done by the joint torques and the end-effector force must be equal:

$$\delta W = \tau^{T}\delta q = F^{T}\delta x$$

But the differential kinematics gives:

$$\delta x = J(q)\,\delta q$$

Substituting:

$$\tau^{T}\delta q = F^{T}J\delta q = (J^{T}F)^{T}\delta q$$

Since $\delta q$ is arbitrary:

$$\boxed{\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F}$$

This is the **principle of virtual work** applied to a robotic manipulator, and it is the cornerstone duality relation used to derive the operational space formulation. **(1 mark: setup, 1 mark: substitution, 1 mark: final boxed result.)**

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions on Module 3 carry 14 marks each with **internal choice**. You must answer **one** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. We provide two complete, fully-solved alternatives below.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

**For a 2-DOF planar manipulator with link lengths $L_1, L_2$ and joint angles $q_1, q_2$:**
**(a) Derive the operational-space dynamics equation from the joint-space equation of motion. (7 marks)**
**(b) For the same manipulator, with the Jacobian $J(q) = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} -L_1 s_1 - L_2 s_{12} & -L_2 s_{12} \\ L_1 c_1 + L_2 c_{12} & L_2 c_{12} \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$, and joint inertia matrix $M = \bigl[\begin{smallmatrix} a + 2b c_2 & d + b c_2 \\ d + b c_2 & d \end{smallmatrix}\bigr]$, where $a = m_1 L_1^{2} + m_2(L_1^{2} + L_2^{2})$, $b = m_2 L_1 L_2$, $d = m_2 L_2^{2}$, compute the Cartesian inertia matrix $\Lambda$ at $q_1 = \pi/3$, $q_2 = \pi/6$. Assume $L_1 = L_2 = 1 \text{ m}$, $m_1 = m_2 = 1 \text{ kg}$. (7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** State the joint-space equation:

$$M(q)\ddot{q} + C(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + G(q) = \tau$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** State the force-torque duality:

$$\tau = J^{T}(q)F \quad \text{(from principle of virtual work)}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Differentiate $\dot{x} = J\dot{q}$ to obtain:

$$\ddot{x} = J\ddot{q} + \dot{J}\dot{q} \Rightarrow \ddot{q} = J^{-1}\ddot{x} - J^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Substitute the above into the joint dynamics:

$$M\bigl[J^{-1}\ddot{x} - J^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x}\bigr] + C J^{-1}\dot{x} + G = \tau$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Apply the force duality:

$$MJ^{-1}\ddot{x} - MJ^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\dot{x} + CJ^{-1}\dot{x} + G = J^{T}F$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Pre-multiply by $J^{-T}$:

$$J^{-T}MJ^{-1}\ddot{x} + \bigl[J^{-T}CJ^{-1} - J^{-T}MJ^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}\bigr]\dot{x} + J^{-T}G = F$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Define the matrices and write the final boxed form:

$$\boxed{\Lambda(x)\ddot{x} + \mu(x,\dot{x})\dot{x} + \rho(x) = F}$$

with $\Lambda = J^{-T}MJ^{-1}$, $\mu = J^{-T}CJ^{-1} - J^{-T}MJ^{-1}\dot{J}J^{-1}$, $\rho = J^{-T}G$.

#### Solution (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute trigonometric values at $q_1 = \pi/3$, $q_2 = \pi/6$:

- $c_1 = \cos(\pi/3) = 0.5$, $s_1 = \sin(\pi/3) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$
- $c_2 = \cos(\pi/6) = \sqrt{3}/2 \approx 0.866$, $s_2 = \sin(\pi/6) = 0.5$
- $c_{12} = \cos(\pi/2) = 0$, $s_{12} = \sin(\pi/2) = 1$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute $M(q)$:

- $a = 1(1)^2 + 1(1^2 + 1^2) = 1 + 2 = 3$
- $b = (1)(1)(1) = 1$
- $d = (1)(1)^2 = 1$

$$M = \begin{bmatrix} 3 + 2(1)(0.866) & 1 + 1(0.866) \\ 1 + 1(0.866) & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.732 & 1.866 \\ 1.866 & 1.000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the Jacobian:

$$J = \begin{bmatrix} -1(0.866) - 1(1) & -1(1) \\ 1(0.5) + 1(0) & 1(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.866 & -1.000 \\ 0.500 & 0.000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute $\det J$ and $J^{-1}$:

$$\det J = (-1.866)(0) - (-1.000)(0.500) = 0.500$$

$$J^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.500}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 1.000 \\ -0.500 & -1.866 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2.000 \\ -1.000 & -3.732 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute $J^{-T}$:

$$J^{-T} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1.000 \\ 2.000 & -3.732 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute the product $MJ^{-1}$:

$$MJ^{-1} = \begin{bmatrix} 4.732 & 1.866 \\ 1.866 & 1.000 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0 & 2.000 \\ -1.000 & -3.732 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -1.866 & 2.500 \\ -1.000 & 0.000 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Compute $\Lambda = J^{-T}MJ^{-1}$:

$$\Lambda = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & -1.000 \\ 2.000 & -3.732 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} -1.866 & 2.500 \\ -1.000 & 0.000 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 & 0.000 \\ 0.000 & 5.000 \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\boxed{\Lambda(x) = \begin{bmatrix} 1.000 & 0.000 \\ 0.000 & 5.000 \text{ kg} \end{bmatrix}}$$

**Physical interpretation (bonus):** The end-effector experiences **1 kg** of inertia along the $x$-direction and **5 kg** along the $y$-direction at this configuration — a strong **anisotropy** caused by the manipulator's geometry.

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**For a redundant manipulator with $n > m$ where $n$ is the number of joints and $m$ is the task-space dimension:**
**(a) Explain why the standard inverse Jacobian $J^{-1}$ does not exist for redundant manipulators. Describe the Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse and the dynamically consistent generalized inverse. (7 marks)**
**(b) Derive the operational-space dynamics of a redundant manipulator using the dynamically consistent generalized inverse $J^{\#}$, and show that null-space joint torques do not affect the task-space acceleration. (7 marks)**

#### Solution (a) — Redundant Jacobian Inversion (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** Explanation of redundancy:

A manipulator is **redundant** if its number of joints $n$ exceeds the dimension of its task space $m$. The Jacobian $J \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ is therefore a **wide rectangular matrix** (more columns than rows). For a square matrix, $J^{-1}$ exists only if $\det J \neq 0$. For a rectangular matrix, the conventional inverse is undefined; one can only define **left** or **right** inverses depending on which space is mapped to which.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse $J^{+}$:

The Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse is defined as:

$$J^{+} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times m}$$

It satisfies $JJ^{+} = I_{m \times m}$ (left identity) and gives the **minimum-norm** joint velocity $\dot{q} = J^{+}\dot{x}$. However, it does **not** account for the manipulator's inertia, so it does not preserve the natural kinetic-energy metric of the mechanism.

**Step 3 (3 marks):** Dynamically consistent generalized inverse $J^{\#}$:

To preserve the inertia metric, **Khatib** defined the dynamically consistent inverse as:

$$J^{\#}(q) = M^{-1}(q)\,J^{T}(q)\,\Lambda(x)$$

where the **operational inertia** is:

$$\Lambda(x) = \bigl[J(q)\,M^{-1}(q)\,J^{T}(q)\bigr]^{-1}$$

This inverse satisfies $J\,J^{\#} = I_{m \times m}$ and is **kinetically optimal**: among all joint motions that produce a given task-space velocity, $J^{\#}\dot{x}$ minimizes the kinetic energy $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{q}^{T}M\dot{q}$.

#### Solution (b) — Derivation of Redundant Operational Dynamics (7 marks)

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define the projection operators.

The general joint acceleration consistent with task-space acceleration $\ddot{x}$ and an arbitrary null-space acceleration $\ddot{q}_{null}$ is:

$$\ddot{q} = J^{\#}\ddot{x} + (I - J^{\#}J)\,q_{a}$$

where $q_{a}$ is an arbitrary vector and $(I - J^{\#}J)$ is the **null-space projector**.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Express the joint-space dynamics in the redundant case.

The joint dynamics remains:

$$M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = \tau$$

This is partitioned into **task-consistent** and **null-space** components using the projection:

$$\tau = J^{T}F + (I - J^{T}J^{\#T})\tau_{0}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply the dynamically consistent inverse.

Using the definition of $J^{\#}$ and the fact that $J M^{-1}(I - J^{\#}J) = 0$ (no coupling), the task-space dynamics decouple cleanly into:

$$\Lambda(x)\ddot{x} + \mu(x,\dot{x})\dot{x} + \rho(x) = F$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Show null-space decoupling.

If $F = 0$ (no task-space force), the operational dynamics gives $\Lambda\ddot{x} + \mu\dot{x} + \rho = 0$. Applying the null-space projector $N = I - J^{\#}J$ on the right of the joint equation:

$$M\ddot{q} + C\dot{q} + G = (I - J^{T}J^{\#T})\tau_{0}$$

Multiplying by $J M^{-1}$ from the left:

$$J\ddot{q} + J M^{-1}C\dot{q} + J M^{-1}G = J M^{-1}(I - J^{T}J^{\#T})\tau_{0} = 0$$

by construction of $J^{\#}$. Thus **the null-space torque $\tau_0$ produces zero task-space acceleration**, confirming the dynamic decoupling.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **THE FIVE MOST COMMON MARK-LOSING MISTAKES — KTU 2024 BOARD PATTERN**
>
> 1. **The "Inverse" sign error:** Writing $\Lambda = J^{T}MJ$ instead of $J^{-T}MJ^{-1}$. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Missing the $\dot{J}$ term:** Forgetting that $\ddot{x} = J\ddot{q} + \dot{J}\dot{q}$ (not just $J\ddot{q}$). **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. **Ignoring the null-space in redundant cases:** Treating $J^{\#}$ as a normal inverse. **Loss: 3 marks.**
> 4. **Confusing $\tau = J^{T}F$ with $\tau = JF$:** The transpose is *not* optional — it follows from virtual work. **Loss: 1-2 marks.**
> 5. **Forgetting the units:** Writing $\Lambda$ without specifying kg or kg·m². **Loss: 1 mark.** Always state units in numerical problems.
>
> **Pro Tip:** Examiners reward **clean algebraic steps**. Write each line of the derivation on a new line with **explicit identification of the substitutions used** (e.g., "Substituting $\tau = J^{T}F$ from Step 2..."). This earns the *method marks* even if the final numerical answer is slightly off.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Print this and revise one hour before the exam.**

- ✅ **Operational dynamics form** mirrors joint dynamics: $\Lambda\ddot{x} + \mu\dot{x} + \rho = F$.
- ✅ **Cartesian inertia** $\Lambda = J^{-T}MJ^{-1}$ — symmetric, positive definite, configuration-dependent.
- ✅ **Force-torque duality** $\tau = J^{T}F$ — derived from **principle of virtual work**.
- ✅ **Velocity duality** $\dot{x} = J\dot{q}$; **acceleration duality** $\ddot{x} = J\ddot{q} + \dot{J}\dot{q}$.
- ✅ **Redundancy** requires the **dynamically consistent inverse** $J^{\#} = M^{-1}J^{T}\Lambda$.
- ✅ **Singularities** are configurations where $\det J = 0$, causing $\Lambda$ to **lose rank** and $J^{-1}$ to be **unbounded**.
- ✅ **Passivity property:** $\dot{\Lambda} - 2\mu$ is **skew-symmetric** (energy conservation).
- ✅ **The Jacobian** is the **bridge** between joint and Cartesian spaces — both kinematic AND dynamic mappings flow through it.
- ✅ **Null-space torques** $\tau_0$ do **not** affect task-space motion in the dynamically consistent formulation — this is what enables obstacle avoidance in redundant manipulators.
- ✅ **Memorize the gravity constant** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ for numerical problems.
- ✅ **Khatib's OSF** is the **standard reference framework** for the KTU Module-3 syllabus.
- ✅ **Redundant manipulators** need the **generalized inverse** approach, not the standard matrix inverse.
- ✅ **Two metrics** to track: (1) kinetic energy $T = \frac{1}{2}\dot{x}^{T}\Lambda\dot{x}$, (2) potential energy via $\rho(x) = J^{-T}G$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for this module:**
> *"From joints to tasks, the Jacobian transforms. From torques to forces, the transpose of J transmits. From joint inertia to Cartesian inertia, the double-inverse sandwich propagates. Singularities are the cliff — avoid them, and the operational world is yours."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Joint Space Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Joint Space Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Joint Space Schemes** are a class of robot trajectory planning techniques in which the motion of the manipulator is specified, parameterized, and interpolated directly in terms of the **generalized joint coordinates** $\mathbf{q}(t) = [q_1(t), q_2(t), \dots, q_n(t)]^T$ rather than in terms of the Cartesian (task-space) position and orientation of the end-effector.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme parlance (Module 4: Trajectory Planning), a joint space scheme defines a continuous time-function for **each** actuated joint variable $q_i(t)$ of an $n$-DOF manipulator, such that:

$$
q_i(0) = q_{i,\text{initial}} \quad \text{and} \quad q_i(t_f) = q_{i,\text{final}}
$$

subject to continuity of position, velocity, and (often) acceleration at the trajectory endpoints and at the via-points.

The end-effector Cartesian path $\mathbf{x}(t) = f\bigl(\mathbf{q}(t)\bigr)$ is then obtained implicitly by mapping the joint trajectory through the **forward kinematics** function $f(\cdot)$.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Puppet on Strings:**
> Imagine you are a puppeteer controlling a wooden marionette. You don't consciously think about where the puppet's hand is in 3D space; instead, you pull each of the **strings (joints)** a little, then a little more, until the desired gesture is achieved. *Joint space planning is exactly this philosophy*: tell every motor *how far* and *how fast* to rotate, and the resulting tip of the hand will trace *some* path in space — usually a smooth, predictable one.

**Real-world industrial parallel:** When a CNC operator writes **G-code** as joint-axis command words (X, Y, Z, A, B), or when a 6-axis articulated robot is jogged in **joint mode** (J1, J2, …, J6) using the teach pendant, joint-space schemes are being used implicitly.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Boundary Conditions

The standard engineering metrics used to evaluate a joint space scheme are highlighted below:

- **Trajectory duration:** $t_f$ in **seconds (s)**
- **Joint displacement:** $\Delta q_i = q_{i,f} - q_{i,0}$ in **radians (rad)** for revolute joints or **meters (m)** for prismatic joints
- **Joint velocity bound:** $\dot{q}_{i,\max}$ in **rad/s** or **m/s**
- **Joint acceleration bound:** $\ddot{q}_{i,\max}$ in **rad/s²** or **m/s²**
- **Jerk bound:** $\dddot{q}_{i,\max}$ in **rad/s³** or **m/s³** (used in quintic / jerk-limited schemes)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Merit of Joint Space Schemes:**
> They **avoid kinematic singularities** because the inverse Jacobian $\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})$ is *never* evaluated along the path — only the forward kinematics map $f(\mathbf{q})$ is used. This makes them the **default, safest choice** in industrial controllers whenever the Cartesian path is not strictly specified.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Joint Space Schemes

Joint space schemes are broadly classified by the **order of the interpolating polynomial** and the **shape of the velocity profile**:

1. **Linear Joint Interpolation** — straight-line $\theta(t)$, discontinuous acceleration
2. **Cubic Polynomial Scheme** — 3rd-order, continuous velocity at endpoints
3. **Quintic Polynomial Scheme** — 5th-order, continuous acceleration at endpoints
4. **Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)** — trapezoidal velocity
5. **Higher-Order / Jerk-Limited Schemes** — 7th-order or S-curve profiles
6. **Spline-based Schemes (B-spline, Cubic Spline)** — used when there are multiple via-points

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

### Why plan in joint space?

- **Singularity-free:** The Jacobian may become singular ($\det \mathbf{J}=0$) at certain configurations; joint space planning sidesteps this entirely.
- **Computational efficiency:** Only the forward kinematics (a one-time evaluation) is needed; no inverse kinematics is solved per interpolation step.
- **Predictable motor commands:** Each motor receives a smooth, monotonically varying command signal — easy for the servo loop to track.
- **No Cartesian overshoot:** The manipulator's links are guaranteed to stay within their physical limits, since each $q_i(t)$ is bounded between its initial and final values.

### How does it work? (Step-by-step logic)

1. Specify the **start** $\mathbf{q}_0$ and **end** $\mathbf{q}_f$ joint configurations (obtained from inverse kinematics of the start and end Cartesian poses).
2. Choose a total trajectory time $t_f$.
3. For each joint $i$, pick an interpolation function $q_i(t)$ satisfying:
   - **Position continuity:** $q_i(0) = q_{i,0}$, $q_i(t_f) = q_{i,f}$
   - **Velocity continuity:** $\dot{q}_i(0) = \dot{q}_{i,0}$, $\dot{q}_i(t_f) = \dot{q}_{i,f}$ (often zero for a "stop-start" motion)
   - **Acceleration continuity:** $\ddot{q}_i(0) = \ddot{q}_{i,0}$, $\ddot{q}_i(t_f) = \ddot{q}_{i,f}$ (for quintic and higher)
4. Sample $q_i(t)$ at the controller's rate (e.g., **1 kHz**) and command the joint servo.
5. The Cartesian path emerges from $f(\mathbf{q}(t))$ — its shape is **not** controlled.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Scheme | Position $q(t)$ (core form) | Boundary Conditions Used | Velocity Profile | Acc. Continuity |
|---|--------|------------------------------|--------------------------|------------------|------------------|
| 1 | Linear | $q(t) = q_0 + \Delta q \cdot (t/t_f)$ | Position only | Triangular (piecewise) | **No** |
| 2 | Cubic | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | Position + Velocity (4 constraints) | Smooth S-curve | No |
| 3 | Quintic | $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | Position + Velocity + Acc. (6 constraints) | Smoother bell-shape | **Yes** |
| 4 | LSPB (Trapezoidal) | Three regions: accel / constant / decel | Position + max velocity | Trapezoidal | Yes (piecewise) |
| 5 | S-Curve (Jerk-limited) | 7th-order polynomial or 7-segment | Position + vel + acc + jerk | Trapezoidal with rounded corners | Yes |

### Boundary-condition equations (Cubic)

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= q_0 \\
a_1 &= \dot{q}_0 \\
a_2 &= \frac{3}{t_f^2}\bigl(q_f - q_0\bigr) - \frac{2}{t_f}\dot{q}_0 - \frac{1}{t_f}\dot{q}_f \\
a_3 &= -\frac{2}{t_f^3}\bigl(q_f - q_0\bigr) + \frac{1}{t_f^2}\bigl(\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f\bigr)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Boundary-condition equations (Quintic)

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= q_0 \\
a_1 &= \dot{q}_0 \\
a_2 &= \tfrac{1}{2}\ddot{q}_0 \\
a_3 &= \tfrac{1}{2 t_f^3}\bigl[20(q_f - q_0) - (8\dot{q}_f + 12\dot{q}_0)t_f - (3\ddot{q}_0 - \ddot{q}_f)t_f^2\bigr] \\
a_4 &= \tfrac{1}{2 t_f^4}\bigl[30(q_0 - q_f) + (14\dot{q}_f + 16\dot{q}_0)t_f + (3\ddot{q}_0 - 2\ddot{q}_f)t_f^2\bigr] \\
a_5 &= \tfrac{1}{2 t_f^5}\bigl[12(q_f - q_0) - 6(\dot{q}_f + \dot{q}_0)t_f - (\ddot{q}_0 - \ddot{q}_f)t_f^2\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

### LSPB core times (Trapezoidal)

Let $t_b$ = parabolic blend time, $t_c$ = constant-velocity cruise time.

$$
t_b = \frac{\dot{q}_{\max}}{\ddot{q}_{\max}} \cdot 2 \quad ; \quad t_c = t_f - t_b
$$

Required condition: $\Delta q \geq \dot{q}_{\max} \cdot (t_f - t_b)$; otherwise the profile degenerates to a triangular one.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Welding robots (e.g., KUKA KR 16, FANUC ArcMate):** The manipulator must stop at the start and end of the weld; quintic joint trajectories eliminate the "jerk spike" that would otherwise crack the weld pool.
- **Collaborative robots (cobots — Universal Robots UR5e):** Use cubic / quintic joint schemes to maintain smooth, human-safe accelerations compliant with **ISO/TS 15066**.
- **Pick-and-place (SCARA, Delta):** Linear-with-parabolic-blend schemes are universally used for cycle-time optimization.
- **Space manipulators (Canadarm2 on the ISS):** Quintic joint schemes minimize structural vibrations of the long, flexible booms.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cubic Polynomial Joint Trajectory

**Problem Setup.** A single revolute joint must move from $q_0 = 20°$ to $q_f = 80°$ in $t_f = 4$ s, with zero initial and final velocities: $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$.

**Step 1 — Propose the polynomial form.**

$$
q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3
$$

We need 4 unknowns $(a_0, a_1, a_2, a_3)$ → we need 4 independent equations.

**Step 2 — Write the time derivatives.**

$$
\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2
$$

$$
\ddot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the four boundary conditions.**

Condition (i): $q(0) = q_0$:

$$
a_0 = q_0 = 20°
$$

Condition (ii): $\dot{q}(0) = \dot{q}_0$:

$$
a_1 = \dot{q}_0 = 0
$$

Condition (iii): $q(t_f) = q_f$:

$$
a_0 + a_1 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = q_f
$$

Condition (iv): $\dot{q}(t_f) = \dot{q}_f$:

$$
a_1 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{q}_f
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the linear system in matrix form.**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & t_f & t_f^2 & t_f^3 \\
0 & 1 & 2t_f & 3t_f^2
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} q_0 \\ \dot{q}_0 \\ q_f \\ \dot{q}_f \end{bmatrix}
$$

Substituting $t_f = 4$, $q_0 = 20$, $q_f = 80$, $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 1 & 0 & 0 \\
1 & 4 & 16 & 64 \\
0 & 1 & 8 & 48
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix} a_0 \\ a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix} 20 \\ 0 \\ 80 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve row by row (back-substitution).**

From row 1: $a_0 = 20°$

From row 2: $a_1 = 0$

From row 4: $0 + 8 a_2 + 48 a_3 = 0 \Rightarrow a_2 = -6 a_3$

Substitute into row 3:

$$
20 + 0 + 16(-6 a_3) + 64 a_3 = 80
$$

$$
20 - 96 a_3 + 64 a_3 = 80
$$

$$
20 - 32 a_3 = 80
$$

$$
-32 a_3 = 60
$$

$$
a_3 = -1.875 \; °/\text{s}^3
$$

Then:

$$
a_2 = -6 \times (-1.875) = +11.25 \; °/\text{s}^2
$$

**Step 6 — Final polynomial and verification.**

$$
q(t) = 20 + 11.25\, t^2 - 1.875\, t^3
$$

**Verification at $t = t_f = 4$ s:**

$$
q(4) = 20 + 11.25(16) - 1.875(64) = 20 + 180 - 120 = 80° \; ✓
$$

**Verification at $t = t_f = 4$ s (velocity):**

$$
\dot{q}(4) = 2(11.25)(4) + 3(-1.875)(16) = 90 - 90 = 0 \; ✓
$$

**Peak velocity** (occurs at $\ddot{q}(t) = 0$):

$$
\ddot{q}(t) = 22.5 - 11.25 t = 0 \Rightarrow t = 2 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\dot{q}_{\max} = \dot{q}(2) = 22.5(2) - 11.25(4) = 45 - 45 \; \text{(re-evaluate)} \quad \text{wait, redo:}
$$

Re-evaluate correctly: $\dot{q}(t) = 22.5 t - 5.625 t^2$

At $t = 2$ s: $\dot{q}(2) = 45 - 22.5 = 22.5 \; °/\text{s}$

So $\dot{q}_{\max} = 22.5 \; °/\text{s}$, occurring at the mid-point of the trajectory.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quintic Polynomial Joint Trajectory

**Problem Setup.** Same joint motion: $q_0 = 20°$, $q_f = 80°$, $t_f = 4$ s, but with **additional acceleration constraints** $\ddot{q}_0 = \ddot{q}_f = 0$.

**Step 1 — Polynomial form (5th order).**

$$
q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5
$$

**Step 2 — Derivatives.**

$$
\dot{q}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2 + 4 a_4 t^3 + 5 a_5 t^4
$$

$$
\ddot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t + 12 a_4 t^2 + 20 a_5 t^3
$$

**Step 3 — Six boundary conditions (full KTU standard form).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= q_0 \\
a_1 &= \dot{q}_0 \\
a_2 &= \tfrac{1}{2}\ddot{q}_0 \\
a_3 + a_4 t_f + a_5 t_f^2 &= \tfrac{1}{t_f^3}\bigl[10(q_f - q_0) - 6\dot{q}_0 t_f - \tfrac{3}{2}\ddot{q}_0 t_f^2 - 4\dot{q}_f t_f + \tfrac{1}{2}\ddot{q}_f t_f^2\bigr] \\
a_4 t_f + 2 a_5 t_f &= \tfrac{1}{t_f^3}\bigl[15(q_0 - q_f) + 8\dot{q}_0 t_f + \tfrac{3}{2}\ddot{q}_0 t_f^2 - 7\dot{q}_f t_f + \ddot{q}_f t_f^2\bigr] \\
a_5 &= \tfrac{1}{2 t_f^5}\bigl[12(q_0 - q_f) + 6(\dot{q}_0 + \dot{q}_f) t_f + (\ddot{q}_0 - \ddot{q}_f) t_f^2\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numbers.**

With $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$, $\ddot{q}_0 = \ddot{q}_f = 0$, $t_f = 4$, $q_f - q_0 = 60°$:

$$
a_0 = 20,\quad a_1 = 0,\quad a_2 = 0
$$

For $a_5$:

$$
a_5 = \frac{1}{2 \cdot 1024}\bigl[12(20 - 80) + 0 + 0\bigr] = \frac{-720}{2048} = -0.3515625 \; °/\text{s}^5
$$

For $a_4$:

$$
a_4 = \frac{1}{t_f^4}\bigl[15(q_0 - q_f) + 0 + 0 - 0 + 0\bigr] = \frac{15(-60)}{256} = -3.515625 \; °/\text{s}^4
$$

For $a_3$:

$$
a_3 = \frac{1}{t_f^3}\bigl[10(q_f - q_0) - 0 - 0 - 0 + 0\bigr] = \frac{10(60)}{64} = 9.375 \; °/\text{s}^3
$$

**Step 5 — Final quintic polynomial.**

$$
q(t) = 20 + 9.375 t^3 - 3.515625 t^4 - 0.3515625 t^5
$$

**Step 6 — Verification at $t = 4$ s:**

Position:

$$
q(4) = 20 + 9.375(64) - 3.515625(256) - 0.3515625(1024) = 20 + 600 - 900 - 360 = 20 + 600 - 1260 = 20 - 660 + \dots
$$

Let me re-evaluate: $9.375 \times 64 = 600$; $3.515625 \times 256 = 900$; $0.3515625 \times 1024 = 360$.

So $q(4) = 20 + 600 - 900 - 360 = 20 - 660 = -640$. That's clearly wrong — let me recheck signs.

Going back: $a_4 = \frac{1}{256}[15 \times (-60)] = \frac{-900}{256} = -3.515625$ ✓
$a_5 = \frac{1}{2048}[12 \times (-60)] = \frac{-720}{2048} = -0.3515625$ ✓
$a_3 = \frac{1}{64}[10 \times 60] = \frac{600}{64} = 9.375$ ✓

So $q(4) = 20 + 9.375(64) + (-3.515625)(256) + (-0.3515625)(1024)$

$= 20 + 600 - 900 - 360 = -640$?

That cannot be. Let me reconsider — the formula for $a_3$ in many textbooks is:

$$
a_3 = \frac{1}{2t_f^3}\bigl[20(q_f-q_0) - 12\dot{q}_0 t_f - 3\ddot{q}_0 t_f^2 + 8\dot{q}_f t_f - \ddot{q}_f t_f^2\bigr]
$$

Let me use the **standard clean form** (Spong, Hutchinson, Vidyasagar):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_0 &= q_0 \\
a_1 &= \dot{q}_0 \\
a_2 &= \frac{\ddot{q}_0}{2} \\
a_3 &= \frac{20q_f - 20q_0 - (8\dot{q}_f + 12\dot{q}_0)t_f - (3\ddot{q}_0 - \ddot{q}_f)t_f^2}{2 t_f^3} \\
a_4 &= \frac{30q_0 - 30q_f + (14\dot{q}_f + 16\dot{q}_0)t_f + (3\ddot{q}_0 - 2\ddot{q}_f)t_f^2}{2 t_f^4} \\
a_5 &= \frac{12q_f - 12q_0 - 6(\dot{q}_f + \dot{q}_0)t_f - (\ddot{q}_0 - \ddot{q}_f)t_f^2}{2 t_f^5}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting $q_0 = 20$, $q_f = 80$, $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$, $\ddot{q}_0 = \ddot{q}_f = 0$, $t_f = 4$:

$a_0 = 20$, $a_1 = 0$, $a_2 = 0$.

$$
a_3 = \frac{20(80) - 20(20)}{2(64)} = \frac{1600 - 400}{128} = \frac{1200}{128} = 9.375
$$

$$
a_4 = \frac{30(20) - 30(80)}{2(256)} = \frac{600 - 2400}{512} = \frac{-1800}{512} = -3.515625
$$

$$
a_5 = \frac{12(80) - 12(20)}{2(1024)} = \frac{960 - 240}{2048} = \frac{720}{2048} = 0.3515625
$$

**Note the sign correction on $a_5$** — the formula is symmetric in $q_0, q_f$ for the leading term but with a positive sign in the numerator for the $q_f - q_0$ form. So the correct polynomial is:

$$
q(t) = 20 + 9.375 t^3 - 3.515625 t^4 + 0.3515625 t^5
$$

**Step 6 (re-verify):**

At $t = 4$ s:

$q(4) = 20 + 9.375(64) - 3.515625(256) + 0.3515625(1024)$

$= 20 + 600 - 900 + 360 = 80°$ ✓

At $t = 0$:

$q(0) = 20°$ ✓

$\dot{q}(t) = 0 + 3(9.375)t^2 - 4(3.515625)t^3 + 5(0.3515625)t^4$

$= 28.125 t^2 - 14.0625 t^3 + 1.7578125 t^4$

At $t = 4$: $\dot{q}(4) = 28.125(16) - 14.0625(64) + 1.7578125(256) = 450 - 900 + 450 = 0$ ✓

$\ddot{q}(t) = 56.25 t - 42.1875 t^2 + 7.03125 t^3$

At $t = 0$: $\ddot{q}(0) = 0$ ✓
At $t = 4$: $\ddot{q}(4) = 225 - 675 + 450 = 0$ ✓

All boundary conditions are satisfied. The quintic polynomial is verified.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: LSPB (Trapezoidal) Joint Trajectory

**Problem Setup.** Joint moves from $q_0 = 0°$ to $q_f = 90°$ in $t_f = 6$ s. Max velocity $\dot{q}_{\max} = 30°/\text{s}$, max acceleration $\ddot{q}_{\max} = 40°/\text{s}^2$.

**Step 1 — Compute the required blend time $t_b$.**

$$
t_b = \frac{\dot{q}_{\max}}{\ddot{q}_{\max}} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the constant-velocity duration $t_c$.**

The total distance covered during acceleration = $\frac{1}{2} \dot{q}_{\max} t_b$ (one blend).

Two such blends: $2 \times \frac{1}{2} \dot{q}_{\max} t_b = \dot{q}_{\max} t_b$

So:

$$
t_c = t_f - \dot{q}_{\max} t_b / \dot{q}_{\max} = t_f - t_b = 6 - 0.75 = 5.25 \;\text{s}
$$

**Check feasibility:** Distance covered = $\dot{q}_{\max} \cdot t_b + \dot{q}_{\max} \cdot t_c = 30(0.75) + 30(5.25) = 22.5 + 157.5 = 180°$.

But required $\Delta q = 90°$, which is **less** than 180°. This means the trapezoidal profile **degenerates** to a triangular profile. We need to recalculate.

**Step 3 — Recompute for triangular profile (no constant-velocity region).**

The motion is: accelerate for $t_b/2$, then decelerate for $t_b/2$:

$$
t_b = t_f = 6 \;\text{s}
$$

The required max velocity for this $t_b$:

$$
\dot{q}_{\max} = \ddot{q}_{\max} \cdot \frac{t_b}{2} = 40 \cdot 3 = 120 \; °/\text{s}
$$

But our constraint was $30 °/\text{s}$, so we use the **minimum** of the two:

Since $30 < 120$, the limit of $30°/\text{s}$ will be hit. Let me solve differently.

For a triangular profile (when $\Delta q$ is too small to reach $\dot{q}_{\max}$ and hold it):

Peak velocity attained: $\dot{q}_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2 \Delta q}{t_f} = \frac{2 \cdot 90}{6} = 30 \; °/\text{s}$ ✓ (matches constraint)

Time to reach peak: $t_1 = \frac{\dot{q}_{\text{peak}}}{\ddot{q}_{\max}} = \frac{30}{40} = 0.75 \;\text{s}$

Acceleration phase: $0 \leq t \leq 0.75$ s, $q(t) = \frac{1}{2} \ddot{q}_{\max} t^2 = 20 t^2$

Deceleration phase: $0.75 \leq t \leq 6$ s (duration 5.25 s, deceleration $-40°/\text{s}^2$)

$q(t) = 90 - 20(t - 0.75)^2$ — this is the symmetric triangular profile.

**Step 4 — Verify at $t = 6$ s:**

$q(6) = 90 - 20(5.25)^2 = 90 - 20(27.5625) = 90 - 551.25 \dots$ that's wrong.

I made an error. The triangular profile is symmetric so the deceleration time equals the acceleration time = 0.75 s. The remaining time in the middle is the constant-velocity plateau.

Let me re-think: For a triangular profile to have $q(0) = 0$ and $q(t_f) = 90$:

- Acceleration for time $t_a$, then immediate deceleration for time $t_d = t_a$ (symmetric).
- Total time: $t_f = 2 t_a \Rightarrow t_a = 3$ s.
- Peak velocity: $\dot{q}_{\text{peak}} = \ddot{q}_{\max} \cdot t_a = 40 \cdot 3 = 120°/\text{s}$
- But $t_a = t_f/2 = 3$ s, total distance = $\dot{q}_{\text{peak}} \cdot t_f / 2 = 120 \cdot 3 = 360° \neq 90°$.

The issue is the constraint $\dot{q}_{\max} = 30°/\text{s}$ is too low. With this constraint:

For a trapezoidal profile, distance covered during accel = $0.5 \cdot \dot{q}_{\max} \cdot t_b$, where $t_b = \dot{q}_{\max}/\ddot{q}_{\max} = 0.75$ s. So accel distance = $0.5 \cdot 30 \cdot 0.75 = 11.25°$. Two blends = $22.5°$.

Required: $\Delta q = 90°$. Cruise distance: $90 - 22.5 = 67.5°$. Cruise time: $67.5 / 30 = 2.25$ s.

Total time: $t_f = t_b + t_c = 0.75 + 2.25 = 3$ s. But problem says $t_f = 6$ s, so there's a contradiction.

**Resolving the contradiction:** The given $t_f = 6$ s is **longer** than required. The trajectory will, in practice, accelerate to $\dot{q}_{\max} = 30°/\text{s}$, cruise, then decelerate, completing in **3 s**, then sit idle for 3 s. The **active** motion time is 3 s, not 6 s.

OR — alternatively — the problem intends us to **reduce** $\ddot{q}_{\max}$ to extend the trajectory. Let me re-solve to find the correct $\ddot{q}_{\max}$ for $t_f = 6$ s.

Required cruise time for $t_f = 6$ s: $t_c = 6 - 0.75 = 5.25$ s. Cruise distance = $30 \cdot 5.25 = 157.5°$. Total distance covered = $11.25 + 157.5 + 11.25 = 180°$. But we need only 90°.

So actually we need to **slow down**: the trapezoidal profile is achievable only if we reduce the cruise velocity. The required cruise velocity is:

$\dot{q}_{\text{cruise}} = \frac{\Delta q - 2 \cdot (0.5 \cdot \dot{q}_{\text{cruise}} \cdot t_b)}{t_c}$

This is circular. Let me parameterize by $\ddot{q}_{\max}$ (free parameter) and find what's required.

Let $\alpha = \ddot{q}_{\max}$ (free). Then $t_b = \dot{q}_{\max}/\alpha$.

Distance during accel = $0.5 \cdot \dot{q}_{\max} \cdot t_b = 0.5 \cdot \dot{q}_{\max}^2 / \alpha$.

Two such phases: $\dot{q}_{\max}^2 / \alpha$. Cruise distance = $\Delta q - \dot{q}_{\max}^2/\alpha$.

Cruise time: $t_c = (\Delta q - \dot{q}_{\max}^2/\alpha) / \dot{q}_{\max} = \Delta q/\dot{q}_{\max} - \dot{q}_{\max}/\alpha$.

Total time: $t_f = t_b + t_c = \dot{q}_{\max}/\alpha + \Delta q/\dot{q}_{\max} - \dot{q}_{\max}/\alpha = \Delta q/\dot{q}_{\max}$.

Wait! That gives $t_f = \Delta q / \dot{q}_{\max} = 90/30 = 3$ s. So the trapezoidal profile with $\dot{q}_{\max} = 30°/\text{s}$ and $\Delta q = 90°$ takes exactly 3 s — the accel/decel phases are essentially "instantaneous" relative to the cruise. This means for $t_f = 6$ s, the manipulator cruises at 30°/s for 3 s, then idles for 3 s.

Alternatively, with $t_f = 6$ s, we must use a smaller $\dot{q}_{\text{avg}}$: $\dot{q}_{\text{avg}} = 90/6 = 15°/\text{s}$, and we can set $\dot{q}_{\max} = 15°/\text{s}$ with very small blend times, etc.

For a clean pedagogical answer, the standard KTU approach is:

**Final LSPB answer (clean form):**

Given $\Delta q = 90°$, $t_f = 6$ s, $\dot{q}_{\max} = 30°/\text{s}$, $\ddot{q}_{\max} = 40°/\text{s}^2$:

The minimum time to traverse 90° at $\dot{q}_{\max} = 30°/\text{s}$ is $\Delta q/\dot{q}_{\max} = 3$ s. Since $t_f = 6$ s > 3 s, the manipulator cruises at 30°/s and dwells for the remaining 3 s.

**Alternative interpretation** — assume $\ddot{q}_{\max}$ is a free design variable and find what value makes $t_f = 6$ s for a triangular profile (no cruise):

For triangular: $t_f = 2 \sqrt{\Delta q / \ddot{q}_{\max}} \Rightarrow \ddot{q}_{\max} = 4 \Delta q / t_f^2 = 4 \cdot 90 / 36 = 10°/\text{s}^2$.

**This is the correct pedagogical answer.** The lesson: the **given constraints are inconsistent**; one must either reduce $\ddot{q}_{\max}$ to extend the motion (yielding a triangular profile) or accept that the manipulator dwells.

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Cubic vs Quintic vs LSPB)

| Property | Cubic | Quintic | LSPB (Trapezoidal) |
|----------|-------|---------|--------------------|
| Polynomial order | 3 | 5 | Piecewise (1 & 2) |
| Position continuous at boundaries | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Velocity continuous at boundaries | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Acceleration continuous at boundaries | ✗ (jumps) | ✓ | ✓ (at blend points) |
| Computational cost | Low | Medium | Low |
| Singularity-free | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| Smoothness of motion | Good | Excellent | Acceptable (corners) |
| Best use case | Free-space PTP moves | Precision / welding | High-speed pick & place |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Joint Space Planning — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User-Specified Start Pose in Cartesian Space X0] --> B[Inverse Kinematics Module]
    C[User-Specified End Pose in Cartesian Space Xf] --> B
    B --> D[Initial Joint Vector q0]
    B --> E[Final Joint Vector qf]
    D --> F[Joint Space Trajectory Generator]
    E --> F
    G[Trajectory Time tf] --> F
    H[Scheme Selector: Cubic / Quintic / LSPB] --> F
    F --> I[Interpolated Joint Commands qit for i=1..n]
    I --> J[Forward Kinematics Module]
    J --> K[End-Effector Cartesian Path Xt]
    I --> L[Joint Servo Loops]
    L --> M[Robot Actuator Commands]
    M --> N[Physical Motion of Manipulator]
```

## 4.2 Cubic vs Quintic vs LSPB — Profile Comparison

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph ProfileShapes["Velocity Profile Comparison"]
        direction LR
        P1["Cubic: S-curve-like, smooth single peak"]
        P2["Quintic: Bell-shape, zero acc at endpoints"]
        P3["LSPB: Trapezoidal, flat top with rounded corners"]
    end
    P1 --> P2 --> P3
```

## 4.3 Joint-Space Scheme Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start: Need a Joint Trajectory]) --> Q1{Cartesian Path Strictly Specified?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> CartPlan[Use Cartesian Space Planning]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Multiple Via-Points?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3{Acceleration Continuity Required?}
    Q2 -- No --> Q3
    Q3 -- Yes --> Quintic[Choose Quintic Polynomial]
    Q3 -- No --> Q4{High-Speed Throughput Priority?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> LSPB[Choose LSPB / Trapezoidal]
    Q4 -- No --> Cubic[Choose Cubic Polynomial]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram of a Single-Joint Cubic Trajectory Generator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Input: q0] --> Mux[Mux: Boundary Conditions]
    A2[Input: qf] --> Mux
    A3[Input: dq0 = 0] --> Mux
    A4[Input: dqf = 0] --> Mux
    A5[Input: tf] --> Mux
    Mux --> Solver[Matrix Solver: 4x4 Linear System]
    Solver --> A0[Output: a0]
    Solver --> A1c[Output: a1]
    Solver --> A2c[Output: a2]
    Solver --> A3c[Output: a3]
    A0 --> Poly[Evaluate qit = a0 + a1*t + a2*t^2 + a3*t^3]
    A1c --> Poly
    A2c --> Poly
    A3c --> Poly
    Poly --> Out[Joint Servo Command]
```
