# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q:** Define **Joint Space Trajectory Planning**. List any **two advantages** of planning a robot's trajectory in joint space rather than in Cartesian space.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Joint Space Trajectory Planning** is a method in which the motion of the robot is specified as a function of time at the level of the individual joint variables $q_i(t)$, rather than as a function of the end-effector's Cartesian position $\mathbf{x}(t)$.

**Two advantages:**

1. **Singularity avoidance:** The inverse Jacobian $\mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q})$ is never evaluated, so the trajectory cannot pass through (or be blocked by) a kinematic singularity.
2. **Computational simplicity:** Only the forward kinematics $f(\mathbf{q})$ is needed to map to Cartesian space; no online inverse kinematics is required at every sampling instant.

*(Any one of: bounded joint motion, smooth motor commands, real-time feasibility — also accepted.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q:** A cubic polynomial trajectory is used to move a single revolute joint from $q_0 = 10°$ to $q_f = 50°$ in $t_f = 2$ s with zero initial and final velocities. Compute the **coefficient $a_2$**.

**Model Answer:**

Using the standard cubic boundary-condition formula:

$$
a_2 = \frac{3}{t_f^2}(q_f - q_0) - \frac{2}{t_f}\dot{q}_0 - \frac{1}{t_f}\dot{q}_f
$$

Substituting $q_f - q_0 = 40°$, $t_f = 2$ s, $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$:

$$
a_2 = \frac{3}{4}(40) - 0 - 0 = 30 \; °/\text{s}^2
$$

**Answer:** $a_2 = 30°/\text{s}^2$

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Evaluation pattern**, Part B questions carry **14 marks each**, typically split as (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. **Answer ANY ONE** of the two full questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**A(a) [7 Marks]:** A single joint of a robot manipulator must move from $q_0 = 15°$ to $q_f = 75°$ in $t_f = 5$ s. The initial and final velocities are both **zero**, and the initial and final accelerations are both **zero**. **Derive** the complete quintic polynomial trajectory $q(t)$, and find the **peak velocity** of the joint.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply boundary conditions to the quintic polynomial:**

$$
q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5
$$

With $q_0 = 15°$, $q_f = 75°$, $t_f = 5$ s, $\dot{q}_0 = \dot{q}_f = 0$, $\ddot{q}_0 = \ddot{q}_f = 0$:

- $a_0 = q_0 = 15°$
- $a_1 = \dot{q}_0 = 0$
- $a_2 = \ddot{q}_0 / 2 = 0$

**Step 2 — Use the standard quintic coefficient formulas:**

$$
a_3 = \frac{10(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^3} = \frac{10(60)}{125} = 4.8 \; °/\text{s}^3
$$

$$
a_4 = \frac{-15(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^4} = \frac{-15(60)}{625} = -1.44 \; °/\text{s}^4
$$

$$
a_5 = \frac{6(q_f - q_0)}{t_f^5} = \frac{6(60)}{3125} = 0.1152 \; °/\text{s}^5
$$

**Step 3 — Final trajectory polynomial:**

$$
q(t) = 15 + 4.8 t^3 - 1.44 t^4 + 0.1152 t^5
$$

**Step 4 — Find the peak velocity:**

The peak velocity occurs when $\ddot{q}(t) = 0$:

$$
\ddot{q}(t) = 28.8 t^2 - 17.28 t^3 + 2.304 t^4 = t^2(28.8 - 17.28 t + 2.304 t^2)
$$

Setting the bracket to zero:

$$
2.304 t^2 - 17.28 t + 28.8 = 0
$$

Dividing by 2.304:

$$
t^2 - 7.5 t + 12.5 = 0
$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$
t = \frac{7.5 \pm \sqrt{56.25 - 50}}{2} = \frac{7.5 \pm 2.5}{2}
$$

$$
t_1 = 2.5 \text{ s}, \quad t_2 = 5.0 \text{ s}
$$

The non-trivial solution is $t_1 = 2.5$ s (the trajectory's mid-point).

**Step 5 — Compute the peak velocity at $t = 2.5$ s:**

$$
\dot{q}(t) = 14.4 t^2 - 5.76 t^3 + 0.576 t^4
$$

$$
\dot{q}(2.5) = 14.4(6.25) - 5.76(15.625) + 0.576(39.0625)
$$

$$
= 90 - 90 + 22.5 = 22.5 \; °/\text{s}
$$

**Answer:** Peak velocity $\dot{q}_{\max} = 22.5°/\text{s}$ at $t = 2.5$ s.

---

**A(b) [7 Marks]:** Compare **Cubic Polynomial**, **Quintic Polynomial**, and **LSPB (Trapezoidal)** joint space schemes under the heads: (i) continuity of acceleration, (ii) computational complexity, (iii) typical industrial application.

**Model Solution:**

| Head | Cubic Polynomial | Quintic Polynomial | LSPB (Trapezoidal) |
|------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| (i) Acceleration continuity | ✗ Acceleration jumps at both endpoints (finite discontinuities) | ✓ Acceleration is continuous; zero at both endpoints | ✓ Acceleration is piecewise constant; discontinuous at blend transitions |
| (ii) Computational complexity | Low — 4 × 4 linear system, solved once per joint | Medium — 6 × 6 linear system, solved once per joint | Lowest — analytic closed-form expressions for blend and cruise times |
| (iii) Typical application | Free-space point-to-point (PTP) moves, low-payload arms | Precision tasks: welding, painting, medical robots, vibration-sensitive applications | High-throughput pick-and-place, conveyor tracking, assembly lines |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> A very common error students make is to state that the **cubic polynomial** has continuous acceleration. **It does not.** The cubic enforces zero velocity at the endpoints, but the second derivative $\ddot{q}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t$ is generally **non-zero** at $t=0$ and $t=t_f$. Only the quintic (and higher) enforces acceleration continuity. Losing 1 mark for this confusion is standard.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

**B(a) [7 Marks]:** For a single joint, the **Linear Segments with Parabolic Blends (LSPB)** trajectory is required. Given $\Delta q = 120°$, $t_f = 8$ s, $\dot{q}_{\max} = 40°/\text{s}$, and $\ddot{q}_{\max} = 50°/\text{s}^2$, determine:
(i) The blend time $t_b$
(ii) The constant-velocity cruise time $t_c$
(iii) The peak acceleration phase duration

**Model Solution:**

**Step (i) — Blend time:**

$$
t_b = \frac{\dot{q}_{\max}}{\ddot{q}_{\max}} = \frac{40}{50} = 0.8 \;\text{s}
$$

**Step (ii) — Cruise time:**

Distance covered during one parabolic blend: $\frac{1}{2} \dot{q}_{\max} t_b = \frac{1}{2}(40)(0.8) = 16°$.

Two blends (one at start, one at end): $32°$.

Cruise distance: $\Delta q - 32° = 120° - 32° = 88°$.

$$
t_c = \frac{88°}{40°/\text{s}} = 2.2 \;\text{s}
$$

**Verification:** $t_b + t_c = 0.8 + 2.2 = 3$ s. But $t_f = 8$ s.

**The given $t_f$ is too long** for this profile. Two interpretations:
- (a) The robot dwells for $8 - 3 = 5$ s after the trajectory completes.
- (b) Use a triangular profile (no cruise) with reduced acceleration.

For pedagogical completeness, the **standard KTU answer** assumes (b) — reduce $\ddot{q}_{\max}$ to fit $t_f$:

For a triangular profile:

$$
t_f = 2 \sqrt{\frac{\Delta q}{\ddot{q}_{\max}'}} \Rightarrow \ddot{q}_{\max}' = \frac{4 \Delta q}{t_f^2} = \frac{4 \cdot 120}{64} = 7.5 \; °/\text{s}^2
$$

**Answer (assuming problem expects feasible values):**
- (i) $t_b = 0.8$ s (if using original $\ddot{q}_{\max}$)
- (ii) $t_c = 2.2$ s
- (iii) Peak acceleration phase duration = $t_b/2 = 0.4$ s per blend (acceleration phase is half the blend duration in trapezoidal)

**Final structured answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Blend time $t_b$ | 0.8 s |
| Cruise time $t_c$ | 2.2 s |
| Acceleration phase duration | 0.4 s |
| Deceleration phase duration | 0.4 s |
| Active motion time | 3.0 s |
| Idle time (dwell) | 5.0 s |

---

**B(b) [7 Marks]:** Explain the **need for higher-order polynomials** (quintic) in joint space trajectory planning. Why is the cubic polynomial insufficient for **welding** and **medical robot** applications?

**Model Solution:**

**Why higher-order polynomials are needed:**

In real-world industrial applications, the **actuator and gearbox assemblies** have finite stiffness, and the **load inertia** is non-zero. A discontinuous acceleration profile — which the cubic polynomial produces at the trajectory endpoints — implies a **theoretically infinite jerk** ($d^3q/dt^3$) at the boundary instant. This causes:

1. **Structural vibration** of the robot links, leading to **positional overshoot** and **oscillation**.
2. **Mechanical stress** on gear teeth, bearings, and the drive train, reducing service life.
3. **Tracking error** in the joint servo, since the controller's internal model assumes smooth command signals.
4. **Excited resonant modes** in flexible links, which is catastrophic for long-reach arms.

The **quintic polynomial**, with its **6 boundary conditions** (position, velocity, acceleration at both ends), guarantees **zero jerk at the endpoints**, eliminating the impulsive force that the cubic leaves unaddressed.

**Why cubic is insufficient for welding:**

- Welding torches are sensitive to **velocity discontinuities** and **acceleration spikes**, which manifest as **arc length fluctuations** and **bead irregularities**.
- The weld pool solidifies within milliseconds; any sudden jerk can cause **porosity** or **cracks** in the joint.

**Why cubic is insufficient for medical robots:**

- Surgical robots (e.g., **da Vinci**, **MAKO**) operate in human anatomy; the controller must guarantee **bounded force and bounded jerk** for patient safety.
- Regulatory standards (e.g., **IEC 80601-2-77**) require **smooth, predictable motion** with documented acceleration/jerk bounds.
- A discontinuous acceleration is a **Class A safety violation** in such systems.

**Conclusion:** Higher-order polynomials (quintic, 7th-order) are **mandatory** in precision, vibration-sensitive, and human-interactive applications, even though they cost more computational effort.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> Do **not** confuse the cubic's smoothness (continuous velocity) with **overall physical smoothness**. The cubic's *acceleration* is still discontinuous; a real physical system feels this as a jerk. Always state explicitly which derivative is continuous and which is not — a 1-mark penalty is the minimum for vague wording.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Joint Space Schemes** define motion at the **joint level** — interpolation is done on $q_i(t)$ for $i = 1, \dots, n$, not on Cartesian coordinates.
- **Inverse kinematics** is needed **only at the endpoints**, not along the trajectory — making the scheme computationally efficient and **singularity-free**.
- **Cubic polynomial** enforces **position + velocity** continuity (4 boundary conditions, 4 unknowns).
- **Quintic polynomial** enforces **position + velocity + acceleration** continuity (6 boundary conditions, 6 unknowns) — required for **smooth motion** with zero jerk at boundaries.
- **LSPB (Trapezoidal)** uses a **piecewise** profile: parabolic acceleration → constant velocity → parabolic deceleration. Best for **high-speed pick-and-place**.
- **S-curve / 7th-order** profiles further bound the **jerk** — used in **cobot** and **medical robot** applications per **ISO/TS 15066** and **IEC 80601-2-77**.
- The **Cartesian end-effector path** is *not* directly controlled in joint space schemes — the user gets "some path" between the two endpoints in task space.
- **Peak velocity** in a cubic/quintic trajectory with zero boundary velocities occurs at the **mid-point** of the trajectory ($t = t_f/2$).
- The **peak acceleration** of a cubic trajectory is at the **endpoints** (jumps); for a quintic, the peak is **internal** and bounded.
- For **feasibility** of LSPB, always check: $\Delta q \geq \dot{q}_{\max} \cdot (t_f - t_b)$; otherwise the profile **degenerates to triangular**.
- **Industry standards to remember:** ISO 10218 (industrial robots), ISO/TS 15066 (cobots), IEC 80601-2-77 (medical robots).
- **Standard exam cross-check:** If the problem gives zero initial/final velocities **and** zero initial/final accelerations → **use quintic**; if only velocities are zero → **cubic** suffices.

topics.title: Cartesian Space Schemes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Cartesian Space Schemes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cartesian Space Schemes (Task-Space Control Schemes)** are a family of robotic motion planning and control methodologies in which the desired motion, position, trajectory, and force references of the end-effector are specified in the **Cartesian (operational) coordinate frame** — i.e., along the orthogonal $X$, $Y$, $Z$ axes and Euler/RPY orientation parameters — rather than in the **joint space** of the manipulator. The control architecture then maps these task-space specifications back into joint actuator commands through the manipulator's Inverse Kinematics and the **Jacobian matrix** $J(q)$.

In the formal KTU 2024 nomenclature, Cartesian Space Schemes encompass four principal techniques:
1. **Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC)** — resolved rate control of task-space velocities.
2. **Resolved Motion Position Control (RMPC)** — discrete position-level inverse kinematics.
3. **Cartesian Trajectory Generation** — point-to-point and continuous-path planning in the $X\text{-}Y\text{-}Z$ frame.
4. **Cartesian Force / Impedance Control** — explicit regulation of contact forces in the task frame.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Pen Tip vs. The Fingers"

Imagine you are asked to sign your name on a piece of paper:

- **Joint-Space Control** is like telling a pianist: *"move finger 1 down 3 cm, finger 2 down 1.5 cm, finger 3 sideways 2 cm..."*. You are controlling the **interior mechanism** — awkward, unintuitive, and joint-specific.
- **Cartesian-Space Control** is like telling a person: *"move the pen tip to the point (15 mm, 22 mm), draw a 10 mm line, and apply 0.3 N downward pressure."* The brain (the controller) automatically computes how every finger joint must cooperate to make the pen behave that way.

A robotic manipulator is exactly the same: the **human operator (or the high-level planner) thinks in tip coordinates**; the **controller translates** these into joint torques using the Jacobian — a mathematical "cerebellum."

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard reference frames in Cartesian Schemes:**
> - **Base frame** $\Sigma_B$ — fixed to the robot pedestal.
> - **End-effector frame** $\Sigma_E$ — attached to the tool tip.
> - **Task frame** $\Sigma_T$ — attached to the workpiece or environment.
> - **Desired pose** ${}^{B}T_{E}^{d} \in SE(3)$ — the $4 \times 4$ homogeneous transform expressing where the tip **should** be.

### 1.3 Why Cartesian Schemes Are Necessary

| Driving Need | Joint-Space Limitation | Cartesian-Space Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| **CAD/CAM integration** | Designers think in mm, not in radians | Direct download of $X\text{-}Y\text{-}Z$ part coordinates |
| **Welding / painting** | Constant tip speed is hard to enforce | Tip velocity controlled uniformly along the path |
| **Force-controlled assembly** | Joint torques are not human-meaningful | Forces specified in Newtons along $X\text{-}Y\text{-}Z$ |
| **Vision-based servoing** | Cameras return pixel/task coordinates | Closed-loop servo on visual errors is natural |
| **Obstacle avoidance** | Joint trajectories may self-collide | Straight-line Cartesian paths are geometrically clear |

> [!TIP]
> The trade-off: **Cartesian schemes require more computation per cycle** (matrix inversion, pseudo-inverse, SVD), but they yield **superior task performance** and **operator transparency**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Foundational Mathematics — Why the Jacobian Is Central

Every Cartesian scheme is built on a single kinematic bridge:

$$
\dot{x} \;=\; J(q)\,\dot{q}
$$

where
- $x \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$ is the **task-space vector** (typically $m = 6$: 3 translations + 3 rotations),
- $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the **joint-space vector** ($n$ = number of actuated joints),
- $J(q) = \dfrac{\partial f}{\partial q}$ is the **geometric Jacobian** relating differential joint motion to differential tip motion.

> [!NOTE]
> When $m = n = 6$ (a 6-DOF manipulator in a general configuration), $J$ is square and invertible, and the **Resolved Motion Rate Control law** is exact.

### 2.2 The Four Pillars of Cartesian Schemes

#### Pillar 1 — Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC)
- **Idea:** Specify a desired tip velocity $\dot{x}_{d}$. Compute the joint velocity $\dot{q}$ that produces it.
- **Why:** The Jacobian equation is linear in $\dot{q}$, so inversion is immediate.
- **How:** At every control cycle, $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\,\dot{x}_{d}$, then integrate to get $q(t)$.

#### Pillar 2 — Resolved Motion Position Control (RMPC)
- **Idea:** Same as RMRC but for **discrete position increments** $\Delta x$.
- **Why:** Useful when only a target pose (not a velocity profile) is known, e.g., from a vision system.
- **How:** $\Delta q = J^{-1}(q)\,\Delta x$ — repeated iteratively until $\Vert \Delta x\Vert < \varepsilon$.

#### Pillar 3 — Cartesian Trajectory Generation
- **Idea:** Interpolate a smooth function $s(t)$ from 0 to 1, then map it to a sequence of Cartesian waypoints.
- **Why:** Guarantees that the tip traces a **straight line** (or any other geometric primitive) with **bounded velocity and acceleration**.
- **How:** $x(t) = x_{0} + (x_{f}-x_{0})\,s(t)$.

#### Pillar 4 — Cartesian Force / Impedance Control
- **Idea:** Regulate the contact force $F$ exerted by the tip on the environment, using the **Principle of Virtual Work**.
- **Why:** Pure position control is unstable in stiff contact; pure force control cannot move freely in free space.
- **How:** Combine both via a stiffness $K$ matrix: $F = K\,(x_{d} - x)$ (impedance relationship).

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of an RMRC Loop

1. **Sense** the current joint angles $q$ via encoders.
2. **Compute** the forward kinematics $x = f(q)$ and the Jacobian $J(q)$.
3. **Read** the desired task-space velocity profile $\dot{x}_{d}(t)$ from the trajectory planner.
4. **Solve** the linear system $J(q)\,\dot{q} = \dot{x}_{d}$ for $\dot{q}$ (use pseudo-inverse if $J$ is non-square or singular).
5. **Integrate** $\dot{q}$ to obtain $q_{cmd} = q + \dot{q}\,\Delta t$.
6. **Send** $q_{cmd}$ to the joint servo loops.
7. **Repeat** at the next control instant (typically $1\text{ kHz}$ for industrial arms).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $x = f(q)$ | Forward kinematics | $x \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, $q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 2 | $\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$ | Differential kinematics | $J \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ |
| 3 | $v = (v_{lin},\omega)^{T}$ | Task-space twist (linear + angular velocity) | $v_{lin} \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$, $\omega \in \mathbb{R}^{3}$ |
| 4 | $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\dot{x}_{d}$ | **Resolved Motion Rate Control law** | Requires $\det(J) \neq 0$ |
| 5 | $\dot{q} = J^{+}(q)\dot{x}_{d}$ | Pseudo-inverse (Moore-Penrose) for redundant ($n>m$) manipulators | $J^{+} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$ |
| 6 | $\Delta q = J^{-1}(q)\,\Delta x$ | **Resolved Motion Position Control** — discrete increment | Iterated until convergence |
| 7 | $x(t) = x_{0} + (x_{f}-x_{0})\,s(t)$ | Cartesian linear interpolation | $s(0)=0$, $s(T)=1$ |
| 8 | $s(t) = 3\tau^{2} - 2\tau^{3}$ | Parabolic-blend (cubic) scalar profile, $\tau = t/T$ | Continuous velocity, **discontinuous** acceleration |
| 9 | $s(t) = 10\tau^{3} - 15\tau^{4} + 6\tau^{5}$ | Quintic polynomial profile | Continuous velocity **and** acceleration |
| 10 | $\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F$ | **Principle of Virtual Work** — joint torque from tip force | $F \in \mathbb{R}^{m}$, $\tau \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$ |
| 11 | $F = K\,(x_{d}-x) - B\,\dot{x}$ | Cartesian **impedance / stiffness** control law | $K,B$ positive-definite $6\times 6$ |
| 12 | $\dot{x}_{e} = \dot{x}_{d} - \dot{x}$ | Task-space velocity error | Feeds back to RMRC |
| 13 | $e_{p} = x_{d} - x$ | Task-space position error | Feeds back to position control |
| 14 | $\sigma_{min}(J)$ | Smallest singular value | Used to detect **singularities** (kinematic) |
| 15 | $M = \rho\,I$ | Manipulability ellipsoid | $\rho = \sqrt{\det(JJ^{T})}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU pitfall:** Never use the symbol $\vert \cdot \vert$ (vertical bar) inside the cells of the formula table — it **breaks the markdown parser**. Use $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ instead, as shown above.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive welding (e.g., KUKA KR 1000):** Cartesian schemes generate the seam trajectory in CAD coordinates, then the on-board controller resolves it into joint commands at $250\text{ Hz}$.
- **Surgical robots (e.g., Da Vinci):** The surgeon commands a tip motion in 3-D camera coordinates; the slave arms execute via RMRC with sub-millimetre accuracy.
- **Pick-and-place SCARA:** Cartesian-style point-to-point moves are downloaded as $(X,Y,Z,\theta)$ quadruples from the line PLC.
- **Space robotics (Canadarm2 on the ISS):** Resolved rate control is essential because the Jacobian changes continually as the arm grapples new payloads.
- **Additive manufacturing / 3-D printing heads:** Impedance control maintains a constant contact force on the build plate.

> [!TIP]
> Whenever an examiner asks *"give one application of Cartesian control"*, the safest answer is: **"closed-loop control of the end-effector pose and contact force in a manufacturing task such as deburring, polishing, or peg-in-hole assembly."**

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC) Law

We start from the **forward kinematics** that maps joint coordinates to the task-space pose:

$$
x \;=\; f(q), \qquad x \in \mathbb{R}^{m},\ q \in \mathbb{R}^{n}.
$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate with respect to time.**

Applying the chain rule of multivariable calculus:

$$
\frac{dx}{dt} \;=\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\,\frac{dq}{dt}.
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Jacobian.**

By definition,

$$
J(q) \;\equiv\; \frac{\partial f}{\partial q} \;\in\; \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}.
$$

Therefore

$$
\dot{x} \;=\; J(q)\,\dot{q}. \tag{1}
$$

**Step 3 — Invert for the joint velocity (square Jacobian case).**

If $m = n$ and $\det(J) \neq 0$ (i.e., the manipulator is in a **non-singular configuration**), $J$ is invertible and

$$
\dot{q} \;=\; J^{-1}(q)\,\dot{x}. \tag{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the desired task-space velocity.**

Let $\dot{x}_{d}(t)$ be the velocity profile produced by the trajectory planner. The **RMRC control law** is therefore

$$
\boxed{\;\dot{q}(t) \;=\; J^{-1}\!\bigl(q(t)\bigr)\,\dot{x}_{d}(t)\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Integrate to recover joint positions.**

$$
q(t+\Delta t) \;=\; q(t) \;+\; \dot{q}(t)\,\Delta t.
$$

**Step 6 — Redundant / non-square case (Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse).**

If $n > m$ (kinematically redundant arm), the exact inverse does not exist. The least-squares minimum-norm solution is

$$
\dot{q} \;=\; J^{+}(q)\,\dot{x}_{d} \;=\; J^{T}(q)\,\bigl(J(q)J^{T}(q)\bigr)^{-1}\,\dot{x}_{d}. \tag{3}
$$

This minimises $\Vert \dot{q}\Vert^{2}$ subject to satisfying (1).

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Cartesian Linear Trajectory with Quintic Profile

We wish the tip to move from $x_{0}$ to $x_{f}$ in time $T$ with **zero initial and final velocity and acceleration**.

**Step 1 — Define the scalar interpolation function.**

Let $\tau = t/T$ with $\tau \in [0,1]$. We seek a quintic polynomial

$$
s(\tau) \;=\; a_{0} + a_{1}\tau + a_{2}\tau^{2} + a_{3}\tau^{3} + a_{4}\tau^{4} + a_{5}\tau^{5}.
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the six boundary conditions.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s(0) &= 0          &\Rightarrow&\quad a_{0} = 0 \\
s(1) &= 1          &\Rightarrow&\quad a_{0}+a_{1}+a_{2}+a_{3}+a_{4}+a_{5} = 1 \\
\dot{s}(0) &= 0    &\Rightarrow&\quad a_{1} = 0 \\
\dot{s}(1) &= 0    &\Rightarrow&\quad a_{1}+2a_{2}+3a_{3}+4a_{4}+5a_{5} = 0 \\
\ddot{s}(0) &= 0   &\Rightarrow&\quad a_{2} = 0 \\
\ddot{s}(1) &= 0   &\Rightarrow&\quad 2a_{2}+6a_{3}+12a_{4}+20a_{5} = 0.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve the resulting linear system.**

After substitution, the only non-zero coefficients are

$$
a_{3} = 10, \qquad a_{4} = -15, \qquad a_{5} = 6.
$$

**Step 4 — Write the final scalar profile.**

$$
\boxed{\;s(\tau) \;=\; 10\tau^{3} - 15\tau^{4} + 6\tau^{5}, \qquad \tau = \dfrac{t}{T}\;}
$$

**Step 5 — Construct the Cartesian trajectory.**

$$
x(t) \;=\; x_{0} \;+\; \bigl(x_{f} - x_{0}\bigr)\,s\!\bigl(\tfrac{t}{T}\bigr).
$$

Differentiating once and twice with respect to time:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{x}(t) &= \frac{1}{T}\,(x_{f}-x_{0})\,\dot{s}(\tau), \\
\ddot{x}(t) &= \frac{1}{T^{2}}\,(x_{f}-x_{0})\,\ddot{s}(\tau),
\end{aligned}
$$

where

$$
\dot{s}(\tau) = 30\tau^{2} - 60\tau^{3} + 30\tau^{4}, \qquad \ddot{s}(\tau) = 60\tau - 180\tau^{2} + 120\tau^{3}.
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Cartesian Force Transformation (Virtual Work)

**Step 1 — State the Principle of Virtual Work.**

For a static manipulator (or quasi-static, where inertial forces are absorbed separately), the virtual work done by the **tip force** $F$ through a virtual tip displacement $\delta x$ must equal the virtual work done by the **joint torques** $\tau$ through the corresponding virtual joint displacement $\delta q$:

$$
F^{T}\,\delta x \;=\; \tau^{T}\,\delta q.
$$

**Step 2 — Use the differential kinematic constraint.**

From (1), infinitesimal motions are related by

$$
\delta x \;=\; J(q)\,\delta q.
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute.**

$$
F^{T}\,J(q)\,\delta q \;=\; \tau^{T}\,\delta q \quad \forall\,\delta q.
$$

**Step 4 — Deduce the force mapping.**

Since the equality must hold for any admissible $\delta q$, the coefficients must match:

$$
\boxed{\;\tau \;=\; J^{T}(q)\,F\;}
$$

This is the **transpose-Jacobian** force control law. It is the cornerstone of **Cartesian force control** and of operational-space dynamic formulations.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Cartesian Impedance / Stiffness Control Law

**Step 1 — Define a mass-spring-damper relationship in task space.**

We want the tip to behave as if it were connected to the **desired pose** $x_{d}$ by a 6-DOF spring of stiffness $K$ and a damper of damping $B$:

$$
F_{ext} \;=\; K\,(x_{d} - x) \;+\; B\,(\dot{x}_{d} - \dot{x}).
$$

**Step 2 — Convert this virtual force into a joint torque via (3.3).**

$$
\tau_{cmd} \;=\; J^{T}(q)\Bigl[K\,e_{p} \;+\; B\,e_{v}\Bigr],
$$

where $e_{p} = x_{d} - x$ and $e_{v} = \dot{x}_{d} - \dot{x}$.

**Step 3 — Add gravity compensation** (for a manipulator on a stationary base):

$$
\boxed{\;\tau_{cmd} \;=\; J^{T}(q)\bigl[K\,e_{p} + B\,e_{v}\bigr] \;+\; G(q)\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **classical Cartesian compliance / impedance control law** used in research robots and the Hogan impedance-control framework. It is the form most often requested in KTU 14-mark derivations.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — RMRC on a 2-Link Planar Arm

A 2-link planar arm has link lengths $L_{1}=1\,\text{m}$, $L_{2}=0.5\,\text{m}$ and is at $q = (30^{\circ}, -45^{\circ})^{T}$. A Cartesian tip velocity of $\dot{x}_{d} = (0.2,\, 0.1)^{T}\,\text{m/s}$ is commanded.

**Step 1 — Forward position kinematics.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_{1}\cos q_{1} + L_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}), \\
y &= L_{1}\sin q_{1} + L_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Jacobian.**

$$
J(q) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
-\,L_{1}\sin q_{1} - L_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) & -\,L_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) \\
\;\;L_{1}\cos q_{1} + L_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) & \;\;L_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2})
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution.**

With $q_{1}=30^{\circ}$ and $q_{1}+q_{2}=-15^{\circ}$:

$$
J \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
-1\cdot 0.5 - 0.5\cdot(-0.2588) & -0.5\cdot(-0.2588) \\
\;\;1\cdot 0.866 + 0.5\cdot 0.9659 & \;\;0.5\cdot 0.9659
\end{bmatrix}
\;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
-0.3706 & 0.1294 \\
\;\;1.3490 & 0.4830
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Step 4 — Determinant.**

$$
\det(J) \;=\; (-0.3706)(0.4830) - (0.1294)(1.3490) \;=\; -0.1790 - 0.1745 \;=\; -0.3535.
$$

Since $\det(J)\neq 0$, the Jacobian is invertible.

**Step 5 — Inverse Jacobian.**

$$
J^{-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{\det J}\begin{bmatrix} 0.4830 & -0.1294 \\ -1.3490 & -0.3706 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -1.3663 & 0.3660 \\ \;\;3.8156 & 1.0484 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Step 6 — Apply the RMRC law.**

$$
\dot{q} \;=\; J^{-1}\,\dot{x}_{d} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -1.3663 & 0.3660 \\ \;\;3.8156 & 1.0484 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.2 \\ 0.1 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -0.2367 \\ \;\;0.8680 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{rad/s}.
$$

**Step 7 — Interpretation.**

Joint 1 must rotate at $-0.237\,\text{rad/s}$ (clockwise) and joint 2 at $+0.868\,\text{rad/s}$ (counter-clockwise) for the tip to move at $(0.2,\, 0.1)\,\text{m/s}$ in the world frame.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Overall Block Diagram of a Cartesian-Space Control Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the information flow of a generic **Cartesian-space trajectory-tracking controller**. Every block corresponds to a concrete mathematical operation introduced in Section 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["User or CAD file"] --> B["Task space trajectory planner x_d of t, xdot_d of t, xddot_d of t"]
    B --> C["Cartesian position and velocity error e_p = x_d minus x, e_v = xdot_d minus xdot"]
    C --> D["Cartesian control law F = K e_p plus B e_v"]
    D --> E["Jacobian transpose mapping tau = J transpose of q times F"]
    E --> F["Add gravity compensation G of q"]
    F --> G["Joint level servo amplifiers"]
    G --> H["Robot manipulator q of t, x of t"]
    H --> I["Forward kinematics f of q gives x of t"]
    H --> J["Geometric Jacobian J of q"]
    I --> C
    J --> E
    J --> D
```

### 4.2 Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC) — Inner Loop

This diagram isolates the **rate-level** Cartesian scheme. Note that the Jacobian inverse is the only nonlinear element in the forward path.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Desired Cartesian velocity xdot_d of t"] --> B["Jacobian inverse J inverse of q"]
    B --> C["Computed joint velocity qdot"]
    C --> D["Numerical integration q of t"]
    D --> E["Robot joint actuators"]
    E --> F["Robot geometry q of t"]
    F --> G["Forward kinematics x of t"]
    F --> H["Geometric Jacobian J of q"]
    G --> A2["Feedback of actual x for error check"]
    H --> B
    A2 --> A
```

### 4.3 Cartesian Trajectory Generator (Quintic Profile)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initial pose x0"] --> C["Cartesian interpolation x of t = x0 plus xf minus x0 times s of t"]
    B["Final pose xf"] --> C
    E["Total time T"] --> D["Quintic scalar profile s of tau = 10 tau cubed minus 15 tau to the 4 plus 6 tau to the 5"]
    D --> C
    C --> F["Cartesian position x of t"]
    C --> G["Cartesian velocity xdot of t"]
    C --> H["Cartesian acceleration xddot of t"]
```

### 4.4 Cartesian Impedance / Force Control Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Desired tip pose x_d of t"] --> E["Impedance law F = K e_p plus B e_v"]
    B["Actual tip pose x of t"] --> E
    C["Actual tip velocity xdot of t"] --> E
    E --> F["Transpose Jacobian tau = J transpose of q times F"]
    F --> G["Plus gravity G of q"]
    G --> H["Joint torque command tau_cmd"]
    H --> I["Robot"]
    I --> J["Environment with contact force F_ext"]
    I --> B
    I --> C
    J --> A
```

### 4.5 Decision Tree — Choosing the Correct Cartesian Scheme

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Task specification available"] --> B{"Is the command a velocity or a position increment?"}
    B -- "Velocity profile" --> C["Use RMRC: qdot = J inverse xdot_d"]
    B -- "Position increment" --> D["Use RMPC: Delta q = J inverse Delta x, iterate"]
    A2["Environment present?"] --> E{"Is the robot in contact with an environment?"}
    E -- "No free motion" --> F["Pure position or RMRC tracking"]
    E -- "Yes constrained motion" --> G["Cartesian impedance or hybrid force position control"]
    A3["Trajectory shape required?"] --> H{"Straight line in task space?"}
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Use Cartesian linear interpolation with quintic s of t"]
    H -- "Curved path" --> J["Use parametric Cartesian path planner"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Read these diagrams left-to-right and top-to-bottom.** The Mermaid syntax is intentionally kept simple — alphanumeric node IDs, plain uppercase labels, and no bold/italic tags inside the labels — so that any standard Mermaid renderer (GitHub, VS Code, Mermaid Live) will display the figures correctly.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

**Q1.** Differentiate between **joint-space control** and **Cartesian-space control** of a robotic manipulator. State any two situations in which Cartesian-space control is preferred. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Joint-space control** specifies the desired motion in terms of the individual joint variables $q_{i}(t)$ (angles for revolute, displacements for prismatic). The control loop closes directly on the joint encoders.
- **Cartesian-space control** specifies the desired motion of the end-effector in terms of its position $(X, Y, Z)$ and orientation (Euler/RPY angles) with respect to a world or task frame. The control loop closes on a **forward-kinematics** estimate of the tip pose, and the **Jacobian** is used to convert task-space errors into joint-space commands.
- **Preferred situations:**
  1. **CAD/CAM-based trajectory download** — the path is naturally defined in world coordinates.
  2. **Force-controlled assembly** (peg-in-hole, deburring, polishing) — forces are intuitive in the task frame.
  3. **Vision servoing** — the camera returns pixel/task-frame errors.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** What is the **Jacobian** of a robotic manipulator? Why is its **inverse** central to Cartesian-space trajectory tracking? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- The **geometric Jacobian** $J(q) = \partial f / \partial q$ is an $m \times n$ matrix that maps the **joint velocity** $\dot{q}$ to the **task-space (Cartesian) velocity** of the end-effector through the relation $\dot{x} = J(q)\,\dot{q}$.
- Its **inverse** is central to Cartesian-space tracking because it allows the controller to **resolve** a desired Cartesian velocity $\dot{x}_{d}$ into the joint velocity $\dot{q}$ that the actuators must produce — the heart of **Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC)**: $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\,\dot{x}_{d}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper]**

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **Resolved Motion Rate Control (RMRC)** scheme for a robotic manipulator. Derive the control law. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF planar manipulator has link lengths $L_{1} = 0.5\,\text{m}$ and $L_{2} = 0.3\,\text{m}$. At the current instant the joint angles are $q_{1} = 60^{\circ}$ and $q_{2} = -30^{\circ}$. A Cartesian velocity of $\dot{x}_{d} = (0.4,\ -0.2)^{T}\,\text{m/s}$ is commanded. Using RMRC, compute the required joint velocities. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

1. **Block-diagram description (3 marks):** The RMRC loop consists of — (i) a trajectory planner that generates the desired tip velocity $\dot{x}_{d}(t)$; (ii) a forward-kinematics block that computes the current tip pose $x = f(q)$; (iii) an error generator that forms $e_{v} = \dot{x}_{d} - \dot{x}$; (iv) a Jacobian-inverse block that converts the task-space error into joint velocity $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\,(\dot{x}_{d}-\dot{x})$; and (v) an integrator that updates the joint command $q_{cmd}$.

2. **Derivation of the law (4 marks):** From forward kinematics $x = f(q)$, differentiate:

$$
\dot{x} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial q}\dot{q} = J(q)\dot{q}.
$$

If $J$ is square and non-singular,

$$
\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\,\dot{x}_{d}.
$$

Including the tracking error,

$$
\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\,(\dot{x}_{d} - \dot{x}) = J^{-1}(q)\,(K_{p}e_{p} + e_{v}),
$$

where $e_{p} = x_{d} - x$ and $K_{p}$ is a position-error gain.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Forward-kinematics of a 2-link planar arm.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= L_{1}\cos q_{1} + L_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}), \\
y &= L_{1}\sin q_{1} + L_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}).
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Jacobian.**

$$
J(q) \;=\; \begin{bmatrix}
-L_{1}\sin q_{1} - L_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) & -L_{2}\sin(q_{1}+q_{2}) \\
\;\;L_{1}\cos q_{1} + L_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2}) & \;\;L_{2}\cos(q_{1}+q_{2})
\end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute numerical values.**

$q_{1} = 60^{\circ},\ q_{1}+q_{2} = 30^{\circ}$. Using $\sin 60^{\circ} = 0.866$, $\cos 60^{\circ} = 0.5$, $\sin 30^{\circ} = 0.5$, $\cos 30^{\circ} = 0.866$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
J_{11} &= -0.5\cdot 0.866 - 0.3\cdot 0.5 = -0.433 - 0.150 = -0.583, \\
J_{12} &= -0.3\cdot 0.5 = -0.150, \\
J_{21} &= \;\;0.5\cdot 0.5 + 0.3\cdot 0.866 = 0.250 + 0.260 = 0.510, \\
J_{22} &= \;\;0.3\cdot 0.866 = 0.260.
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
J \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -0.583 & -0.150 \\ \;\;0.510 & \;\;0.260 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Step 4 — Determinant and inverse.**

$$
\det J = (-0.583)(0.260) - (-0.150)(0.510) = -0.1516 + 0.0765 = -0.0751.
$$

$$
J^{-1} \;=\; \frac{1}{-0.0751}\begin{bmatrix} \;\;0.260 & 0.150 \\ -0.510 & -0.583 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -3.464 & -1.999 \\ \;\;6.795 & \;\;7.766 \end{bmatrix}.
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the RMRC law.**

$$
\dot{q} \;=\; J^{-1}\,\dot{x}_{d} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -3.464 & -1.999 \\ \;\;6.795 & \;\;7.766 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \;\;0.4 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} -0.986 \\ \;\;1.165 \end{bmatrix}\,\text{rad/s}.
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

Joint 1 must rotate at $-0.986\,\text{rad/s}$ (clockwise) and joint 2 at $+1.165\,\text{rad/s}$ (counter-clockwise) to realise the commanded Cartesian velocity.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** Derive the **Principle of Virtual Work** as applied to a robotic manipulator and show that the joint torque vector is given by $\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **Cartesian impedance (stiffness) control** scheme. How does it differ from **hybrid force/position control**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Statement of the principle.**

For a static or quasi-static manipulator, the **virtual work** done by the external tip force $F$ through a virtual tip displacement $\delta x$ must equal the virtual work done by the joint torques $\tau$ through the corresponding virtual joint displacement $\delta q$:

$$
\delta W = F^{T}\,\delta x = \tau^{T}\,\delta q.
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the differential kinematic constraint.**

Differentiating $x = f(q)$ gives

$$
\delta x = J(q)\,\delta q.
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the work equation.**

$$
F^{T}\,J(q)\,\delta q = \tau^{T}\,\delta q.
$$

**Step 4 — Equate coefficients.**

Since this must hold for **every** admissible $\delta q$, the multiplying vectors must be equal (transposed):

$$
\tau \;=\; J^{T}(q)\,F.
$$

This is the fundamental equation used to **map a task-space force into a joint-space torque**. It is exact only for static cases; in the dynamic case the **operational-space formulation** (Khatib) extends it with an inertia matrix $M_{x}(q)$ and a centrifugal/Coriolis term $C_{x}(q,\dot{q})$.

**Model Solution to (b):**

1. **Block-diagram description (3 marks):** A Cartesian impedance controller computes a position error $e_{p} = x_{d} - x$ and a velocity error $e_{v} = \dot{x}_{d} - \dot{x}$, multiplies them by stiffness and damping matrices $K$ and $B$, and converts the resulting virtual force into joint torques through the transpose Jacobian. Gravity compensation $G(q)$ is added. The closed-loop dynamics emulate a **mass-spring-damper** in the task frame.

$$
\tau_{cmd} \;=\; J^{T}(q)\,\bigl[K\,e_{p} + B\,e_{v}\bigr] \;+\; G(q).
$$

2. **Behaviour in free space:** When the tip is far from contact, $e_{p}$ is small and the dominant effect is **position tracking** through the spring $K$ — the tip behaves as if tethered to $x_{d}$ by a soft 6-DOF spring.

3. **Behaviour in contact:** When the environment resists motion, the tip displacement $x_{d}-x$ becomes limited by the contact geometry, and the contact force emerges naturally as $F_{c} = K\,e_{p}$. By choosing $K$ appropriately, the engineer can trade off **stiffness** (precise position, brittle contact) versus **compliance** (robust contact, sloppy position).

4. **Comparison with hybrid force/position control (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Impedance Control | Hybrid Force/Position |
|---|---|---|
| Control law | Unified: $\tau = J^{T}(K e_{p} + B e_{v}) + G$ | Two parallel loops: one in position, one in force |
| Constraint handling | Implicit (emerges from the impedance) | Explicit (compliance frame is decomposed into position- and force-controlled directions) |
| Stability in free space | Excellent | Requires switching logic |
| Stability in contact | Depends on $K,B$ choice | Guaranteed along force-controlled axes |
| Tuning | $K, B$ chosen by desired dynamics | Gains chosen per axis, plus selection matrix $S$ |
| Transitions | Seamless, no mode switching | Requires *guarded moves* or hybrid position/force mode-switching |
| Implementation complexity | Low–moderate | High (needs task-frame decomposition) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the transpose.** Students frequently write $\tau = J(q)\,F$ instead of $\tau = J^{T}(q)\,F$. This **costs 2 full marks** in a derivation question. Always remember: **velocities map with $J$, forces map with $J^{T}$**.
> 2. **Inverting without checking singularity.** Writing $\dot{q} = J^{-1}\dot{x}_{d}$ is **only valid** if $\det(J) \neq 0$. At a singularity $J^{-1}$ does not exist and the joint velocities blow up to infinity. The correct fallback is the **damped least-squares (DLS) inverse** $\dot{q} = J^{T}(JJ^{T} + \lambda^{2}I)^{-1}\dot{x}_{d}$ with damping factor $\lambda$.
> 3. **Confusing RMRC and RMPC.** RMRC is **velocity-level** (continuous), RMPC is **position-increment-level** (iterative, discrete). Examiners love to trap students on this.
> 4. **Writing the cubic/quintic profile as a function of $t$** rather than the dimensionless $\tau = t/T$. The boundary conditions become very ugly if you do not normalise time first.
> 5. **Skipping the block diagram.** Even in a derivation question, the **diagram carries 1–2 marks** in the KTU marking scheme — never omit it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Cartesian Space Schemes**

- [x] **Cartesian space** = the orthogonal $X\text{-}Y\text{-}Z$ (plus orientation) world in which the **end-effector pose** is described.
- [x] The **forward kinematics** $x = f(q)$ and the **Jacobian** $J(q) = \partial f/\partial q$ are the two fundamental ingredients of every Cartesian scheme.
- [x] **Velocity bridge:** $\dot{x} = J(q)\dot{q}$.
- [x] **Force bridge (Virtual Work):** $\tau = J^{T}(q)F$. Velocities use $J$, forces use $J^{T}$ — **never confuse them**.
- [x] **RMRC law:** $\dot{q} = J^{-1}(q)\dot{x}_{d}$ — exact only when $J$ is square and non-singular.
- [x] **Redundant manipulators** ($n>m$): use the **Moore-Penrose pseudo-inverse** $J^{+} = J^{T}(JJ^{T})^{-1}$.
- [x] **RMPC law:** $\Delta q = J^{-1}(q)\Delta x$ — applied iteratively until the residual falls below a threshold $\varepsilon$.
- [x] **Singularities** cause $\Vert\dot{q}\Vert \to \infty$. Use **damped least-squares** to regularise.
- [x] **Cartesian linear trajectory:** $x(t) = x_{0} + (x_{f}-x_{0})\,s(t/T)$, with $s$ either cubic ($3\tau^{2}-2\tau^{3}$) or **quintic** ($10\tau^{3}-15\tau^{4}+6\tau^{5}$).
- [x] **Quintic** is the safer choice because it ensures **continuous velocity *and* acceleration** at start and end.
- [x] **Impedance control** unifies position and force behaviour: $\tau = J^{T}(K e_{p} + B e_{v}) + G(q)$.
- [x] **Hybrid force/position** decomposes the task frame into **position-controlled** and **force-controlled** subspaces using a **selection matrix** $S$.
- [x] **Manipulability** $\rho = \sqrt{\det(JJ^{T})}$ is a scalar measure of how "isotropic" the Jacobian is — drop to zero at a singularity.
- [x] **Real-world users:** automotive welding (KUKA), surgical robots (Da Vinci), space arms (Canadarm2), 3-D printing heads, polishing/deburring cells.
- [x] **Draw the block diagram** in every long-answer question — it is a guaranteed 1–2 mark earner.
- [x] **Always state the dimension** of the Jacobian ($m \times n$) before inverting it; this shows the examiner that you understand the square vs. non-square case.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute memory hook — the "J-and-JT" mnemonic:**
> - **"J-just velocity"** → $\dot{x} = J\dot{q}$
> - **"J-Torque"** → $\tau = J^{T}F$
> If you remember this one pairing, the rest of Cartesian schemes is just combinations of it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Path Generation in Run Time
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Path Generation in Run Time ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Path Generation in Run Time** is the real-time computation and execution of a robot manipulator's trajectory (position, velocity, and acceleration profiles) during the actual operation of the robot, as opposed to being fully pre-computed offline. It involves on-the-fly interpolation, dynamic trajectory modification, and sensor-driven path adjustments while the robot is in motion, with hard real-time computational constraints typically measured in milliseconds (often **1 ms to 10 ms** cycle times for industrial controllers). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Run-time path generation is the bridge between high-level motion planning (which decides *where* to go) and low-level servo control (which decides *how* to actuate). It is essential for adaptive, sensor-driven, and human-collaborative robotic systems. Mathematically, run-time path generation solves the inverse problem of producing a continuous time-parameterized trajectory $\mathbf{q}(t)$ (or $\mathbf{x}(t)$ in Cartesian space) subject to dynamic constraints, where the goal points, via points, or velocity constraints can be modified during execution. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are driving a car from your home to the office using GPS navigation: - **Pre-planned (offline) path generation** is like setting your destination in the morning, locking the route, and driving it without deviation. If a road is closed, you are stuck. - **Run-time (online) path generation** is like having a live, connected GPS that recalculates your route **continuously** as traffic, accidents, and roadblocks appear. The system *generates* a new path segment *while you are driving*. In robotics, the "traffic" comes from sensors (vision, force, proximity) or the human operator (in teach-pendant or lead-through programming). The robot's controller must regenerate the remaining path segments every few milliseconds, ensuring smooth continuity, bounded velocity (**typical industrial limit: $\leq$ 2 m/s**), bounded acceleration (**$\leq$ 10 m/s²**), and bounded jerk (**$\leq$ 50 m/s³**) — all without ever stopping the motion abruptly. > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction (Frequently tested in KTU):** > - **Path** = Pure geometric description in space (sequence of points/poses), parameter-free. > - **Trajectory** = Path **plus** a time law (velocity and acceleration profile). > - **Run-time generation** = Both path and trajectory are *created or modified* during execution. ### 1.3 Why Run-Time Path Generation Matters | Domain | Why Run-Time? | |---|---| | **Welding / Cutting** | Seam tracking via laser sensor adjusts path in real time | | **Collaborative Robots** | Human proximity forces speed & path adaptation | | **Mobile Robots** | Dynamic obstacle avoidance requires online replanning | | **Bin Picking** | Vision system updates grasp pose every cycle | | **Surgery Robots** | Heart motion compensation demands $\geq$ 100 Hz path updates | > [!TIP] > In KTU ESE questions, students often confuse *path planning* (geometric, AI/search based) with *path generation* (interpolation, trajectory profiles). Remember: **planning is AI; generation is control theory and real-time interpolation.** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Fundamental Components of Run-Time Path Generation A run-time path generator performs **four concurrent tasks** every control cycle (typically **1 kHz to 4 kHz** for modern industrial robots): 1. **Command Interpretation** — Decode the next setpoint (target pose, via points, blending parameters). 2. **Interpolation** — Compute intermediate points along the geometric path (linear, circular, spline). 3. **Trajectory Profiling** — Apply a velocity/acceleration/jerk profile (trapezoidal, S-curve, polynomial). 4. **Coordinate Transformation** — Convert Cartesian setpoints to joint space via inverse kinematics, then output joint commands to servo loops. ### 2.2 Interpolation Schemes Used in Run Time #### (a) Linear Interpolation (LERP) Used for straight-line moves. The parametric form is: $$ P(s) = P_0 + s \cdot (P_f - P_0), \quad s \in [0,1] $$ - **Pros:** Computationally trivial — a single multiply-add per axis per cycle. - **Cons:** Direction discontinuities at via points; the robot must stop or use a small blend radius. #### (b) Circular Interpolation Used for arcs. The midpoint method or center-point method defines the circle by three points $P_0$, $P_m$, $P_f$: $$ \mathbf{c} = \text{circumcenter of triangle } \triangle P_0 P_m P_f $$ The interpolation follows an angular parameter $\theta$: $$ P(\theta) = \mathbf{c} + R \cdot \begin{bmatrix} \cos\theta \\ \sin\theta \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} $$ #### (c) Cubic Spline Interpolation For smooth, continuous-curvature paths, a cubic spline is fitted through a sequence of via points. Each segment $i$ has the form: $$ P_i(s) = a_{i,0} + a_{i,1} s + a_{i,2} s^2 + a_{i,3} s^3, \quad s \in [0,1] $$ with $C^2$ continuity at the knots (position, tangent, and curvature match). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Hot Topic:** Industrial controllers (FANUC, ABB, KUKA) predominantly use **cubic or quintic splines** for run-time blending because they preserve $C^2$ continuity, eliminating velocity jumps at via points. #### (d) Trapezoidal Velocity Profile (TVP) The most common run-time profile. Velocity rises linearly, holds constant, then falls linearly: - **Acceleration phase:** $v(t) = v_0 + a_c \cdot t$ - **Cruise phase:** $v(t) = v_{\max}$ (constant) - **Deceleration phase:** $v(t) = v_{\max} - a_c \cdot t$ For a segment of length $L$ and cruise velocity $v_{\max}$: $$ t_{\text{accel}} = \frac{v_{\max} - v_0}{a_c}, \quad t_{\text{decel}} = \frac{v_f - v_{\max}}{-a_c} $$ If $L \geq v_{\max} \cdot t_{\text{accel}} + \frac{1}{2} v_{\max} \cdot t_{\text{decel}}$, a cruise phase exists; otherwise the profile becomes **triangular** (no cruise). #### (e) S-Curve (Jerk-Limited) Profile The modern industrial standard. It limits **jerk** $j = \frac{da}{dt}$, producing seven phases: > Constant jerk → constant acceleration → constant velocity → constant deceleration → constant negative jerk → coast → settle. This eliminates mechanical vibration and is **mandatory** in semiconductor and high-precision assembly robots. ### 2.3 Joint-Space vs Cartesian-Space Run-Time Generation | Aspect | Joint Space | Cartesian Space | |---|---|---| | Variable | Joint angles $q(t)$ | End-effector pose $x(t), y(t), z(t)$ | | Singularity handling | No issue | Requires Jacobian monitoring | | Path shape | Uncontrolled in Cartesian | Exactly defined | | Computation | Cheaper | Requires FK every cycle | | KTU typical use | PTP (point-to-point) moves | LIN, CIRC moves | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Run-time reality:** Even Cartesian-space commands are converted to joint commands every cycle via the inverse Jacobian: > $$\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1}(\mathbf{q}) \cdot \dot{\mathbf{x}}$$ > This matrix inversion is one of the heaviest real-time computational loads. ### 2.4 Look-Ahead and Corner Blending Modern controllers **do not** stop at each via point. Instead, they look ahead $N$ setpoints (typically **N = 15 to 50**) and pre-compute a blended trajectory. The blend radius $r_b$ is chosen such that the corner deviation $\delta$ stays within a tolerance: $$ r_b \approx \sqrt{\frac{v_{\text{path}}^2 \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}}{\tan(\alpha/2)}} $$ where $\alpha$ is the corner angle and $T_{\text{cycle}}$ is the controller cycle time. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Units** | **When Used** | |---|---|---|---| | $P(s) = P_0 + s(P_f - P_0)$ | Linear interpolation, $s \in [0,1]$ | m or mm | Straight-line moves | | $a_3 = \frac{\theta_f - \theta_0}{t_f^3}(2t_f) - \frac{2\dot\theta_0}{t_f^2} + \frac{\ddot\theta_f}{t_f}$ | Cubic poly coefficient | rad/s³ | Joint trajectory | | $v(t) = v_0 + a_c t$ | Trapezoidal acceleration phase | m/s | Velocity profile | | $t_{\text{accel}} = v_{\max} / a_c$ | Time to reach max velocity | s | TVP design | | $j = da/dt$ | Jerk (rate of acceleration change) | m/s³ | S-curve profile | | $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{J}^{-1} \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ | Cartesian-to-joint velocity | rad/s | Real-time IK | | $\delta = r_b \cdot (1 - \cos(\alpha/2))$ | Corner deviation | mm | Blend radius design | | $T_{\text{cycle}} \leq 1/f_{\text{servo}}$ | Real-time deadline | s | Controller design | | $s = \frac{t - t_0}{t_f - t_0}$ | Normalized time parameter | — | Any interpolation | | $\kappa = \frac{\vert \dot{P} \times \ddot{P} \vert}{\vert \dot{P} \vert^3}$ | Path curvature | 1/m | Spline smoothing | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Run-time path generation is the heart of **CNC machining**, **3D printing**, **robotic surgery (Da Vinci system runs at 1 kHz)**, and **autonomous vehicles (Apollo, comma.ai run at 100 Hz)**. The principles are universal across any machine that must follow a geometric path with bounded derivatives. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Cubic Polynomial Trajectory in Joint Space A cubic polynomial is the **simplest trajectory** that satisfies four boundary conditions (two position, two velocity) at the start and end of a segment: $$ \theta(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 $$ $$ \dot{\theta}(t) = a_1 + 2 a_2 t + 3 a_3 t^2 $$ #### Boundary Conditions $$ \theta(t_0) = \theta_0, \quad \theta(t_f) = \theta_f $$ $$ \dot{\theta}(t_0) = \dot{\theta}_0, \quad \dot{\theta}(t_f) = \dot{\theta}_f $$ #### Step 1 — Apply Initial Position Condition $$ \theta(t_0) = a_0 + a_1 t_0 + a_2 t_0^2 + a_3 t_0^3 = \theta_0 $$ For simplicity, we set $t_0 = 0$ (a common KTU simplification): $$ \boxed{a_0 = \theta_0} $$ #### Step 2 — Apply Initial Velocity Condition $$ \dot{\theta}(0) = a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0 $$ $$ \boxed{a_1 = \dot{\theta}_0} $$ #### Step 3 — Apply Final Position Condition $$ \theta(t_f) = \theta_0 + \dot{\theta}_0 t_f + a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f $$ Rearranging: $$ a_2 t_f^2 + a_3 t_f^3 = \theta_f - \theta_0 - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f $$ #### Step 4 — Apply Final Velocity Condition $$ \dot{\theta}(t_f) = \dot{\theta}_0 + 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{\theta}_f $$ Rearranging: $$ 2 a_2 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f^2 = \dot{\theta}_f - \dot{\theta}_0 $$ #### Step 5 — Solve the 2×2 Linear System Let $\Delta\theta = \theta_f - \theta_0$ and $\Delta\dot\theta = \dot\theta_f - \dot\theta_0$. Dividing Step 3 by $t_f^2$ and Step 4 by $t_f$: $$ \begin{aligned} a_2 + a_3 t_f &= \frac{\Delta\theta - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f}{t_f^2} \\[4pt] 2 a_2 + 3 a_3 t_f &= \frac{\Delta\dot\theta}{t_f} \end{aligned} $$ From the first equation: $a_2 = \frac{\Delta\theta - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f}{t_f^2} - a_3 t_f$. Substitute into the second: $$ 2 \left[ \frac{\Delta\theta - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f}{t_f^2} - a_3 t_f \right] + 3 a_3 t_f = \frac{\Delta\dot\theta}{t_f} $$ $$ \frac{2(\Delta\theta - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f)}{t_f^2} - 2 a_3 t_f + 3 a_3 t_f = \frac{\Delta\dot\theta}{t_f} $$ $$ \frac{2(\Delta\theta - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f)}{t_f^2} + a_3 t_f = \frac{\Delta\dot\theta}{t_f} $$ $$ a_3 t_f = \frac{\Delta\dot\theta}{t_f} - \frac{2(\Delta\theta - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f)}{t_f^2} $$ Multiply through by $1/t_f$: $$ \boxed{a_3 = \frac{\Delta\dot\theta}{t_f^2} - \frac{2(\Delta\theta - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f)}{t_f^3}} $$ #### Step 6 — Back-Substitute to Find $a_2$ $$ \boxed{a_2 = \frac{\Delta\theta - \dot{\theta}_0 t_f}{t_f^2} - a_3 t_f} $$ #### Step 7 — Final Compact Form $$ \begin{aligned} a_0 &= \theta_0 \\[3pt] a_1 &= \dot{\theta}_0 \\[3pt] a_2 &= \frac{3(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^2} - \frac{2\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f} \\[3pt] a_3 &= \frac{-2(\theta_f - \theta_0)}{t_f^3} + \frac{\dot{\theta}_0 + \dot{\theta}_f}{t_f^2} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification (KTU Examiner's Trick):** A common sanity check is the special case $\dot{\theta}_0 = \dot{\theta}_f = 0$ (rest-to-rest motion). Then the trajectory reduces to $\theta(t) = \theta_0 + 3(\theta_f - \theta_0)(t/t_f)^2 - 2(\theta_f - \theta_0)(t/t_f)^3$, which has the well-known symmetric S-shaped profile. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 7-Mark Problem) **Problem:** A single joint of a robot must move from $\theta_0 = 10°$ to $\theta_f = 50°$ in $t_f = 2$ s, with $\dot\theta_0 = 0$ and $\dot\theta_f = 0$. Compute the cubic polynomial coefficients and the joint acceleration at $t = 1$ s. **Given:** $\theta_0 = 10° = 0.1745$ rad, $\theta_f = 50° = 0.8727$ rad, $t_f = 2$ s, $\dot\theta_0 = \dot\theta_f = 0$. **Step 1 — Compute $\Delta\theta$:** $$ \Delta\theta = 0.8727 - 0.1745 = 0.6981 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $a_0$ and $a_1$:** $$ a_0 = \theta_0 = 0.1745 \text{ rad} $$ $$ a_1 = \dot\theta_0 = 0 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $a_2$:** $$ a_2 = \frac{3(0.6981)}{2^2} - \frac{2(0) + 0}{2} = \frac{2.0944}{4} = 0.5236 \text{ rad/s}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $a_3$:** $$ a_3 = \frac{-2(0.6981)}{2^3} + \frac{0 + 0}{2^2} = \frac{-1.3963}{8} = -0.1745 \text{ rad/s}^3 $$ **Step 5 — Final Trajectory:** $$ \theta(t) = 0.1745 + 0.5236 \, t^2 - 0.1745 \, t^3 \quad \text{(rad)} $$ **Step 6 — Acceleration Profile:** $$ \ddot{\theta}(t) = 2 a_2 + 6 a_3 t = 1.0472 - 1.0472 \, t $$ **Step 7 — Evaluate at $t = 1$ s:** $$ \ddot{\theta}(1) = 1.0472 - 1.0472(1) = 0 \text{ rad/s}^2 $$ > [!TIP] > **Physical insight:** At the midpoint, acceleration is exactly zero — the motion is symmetric. This is the hallmark of a rest-to-rest cubic trajectory and is **expected to appear in KTU derivations.** ### 3.3 Derivation: Trapezoidal Velocity Profile — Time to Cruise For a segment of length $L$ with acceleration $a_c$ and maximum velocity $v_{\max}$, starting and ending at rest: **Step 1 — Distance during acceleration:** $$ d_1 = \tfrac{1}{2} a_c \, t_{\text{accel}}^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} a_c \left(\tfrac{v_{\max}}{a_c}\right)^2 = \tfrac{v_{\max}^2}{2 a_c} $$ **Step 2 — Distance during deceleration (by symmetry, same as acceleration):** $$ d_3 = \tfrac{v_{\max}^2}{2 a_c} $$ **Step 3 — Cruise distance:** $$ d_2 = L - d_1 - d_3 = L - \tfrac{v_{\max}^2}{a_c} $$ **Step 4 — Cruise time:** $$ t_{\text{cruise}} = \frac{d_2}{v_{\max}} = \frac{L}{v_{\max}} - \frac{v_{\max}}{a_c} $$ **Step 5 — Total motion time:** $$ t_{\text{total}} = t_{\text{accel}} + t_{\text{cruise}} + t_{\text{decel}} = \frac{v_{\max}}{a_c} + \frac{L}{v_{\max}} - \frac{v_{\max}}{a_c} $$ $$ \boxed{t_{\text{total}} = \frac{L}{v_{\max}}} $$ This beautifully simple result holds **only** when the cruise phase exists. If $L$ is too short, the profile degenerates to a triangle. > [!WARNING] > **Triangle Profile Check:** A triangle profile occurs when $L \leq \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a_c}$. The peak velocity is then $v_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{a_c L}$, and the total time is $t_{\text{total}} = 2\sqrt{L/a_c}$. **Many KTU students miss this conditional — examiners test it!** ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Real-Time Path Generator Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[High-Level Planner
Goal Pose / Via Points] --> B[Command Queue
Look Ahead Buffer N=15 to 50] B --> C{Interpolation Mode} C -->|PTP| D1[Joint Space
Cubic or Quintic Poly] C -->|LIN| D2[Linear Interp
in Cartesian Space] C -->|CIRC| D3[Circular Interp
Center + Radius Method] C -->|SPLINE| D4[Parametric Cubic Spline
C2 Continuous] D1 --> E[Trajectory Profiler
TVP or S Curve Jerk Limited] D2 --> E D3 --> E D4 --> E E --> F[Inverse Kinematics
Jacobian or Closed Form] F --> G[Joint Servo Loops
1 to 4 kHz] G --> H[Robot Actuators
Servo Motors] H --> I[Encoder Feedback
Position and Velocity] I --> J[Sensor Fusion
Vision, Force, IMU] J --> K[Run Time Modifier
Path and Speed Update] K --> B I --> G ``` ### 4.2 Process Flow: Trajectory Generation Cycle ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Cycle Start
t = t k] --> P2[Read Next Setpoint] P2 --> P3{New Via Point?} P3 -->|Yes| P4[Recompute Spline Coeffs
and Blend Radius] P3 -->|No| P5[Advance Parameter s] P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6[Compute Path Point P s] P6 --> P7[Compute Velocity vd s dot] P7 --> P8[Inverse Kinematics
q and q dot] P8 --> P9[Check Limits
Joint Torque Velocity] P9 -->|OK| P10[Output q to Servos] P9 -->|Violation| P11[Clamp and Log Warning] P11 --> P10 P10 --> P12[Wait for Next Cycle
T cycle = 1 ms] P12 --> P1 ``` ### 4.3 Comparison Diagram: Trajectory Profile Types ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph TVP[Trapezoidal Velocity Profile] T1[Phase 1 Constant Accel] --> T2[Phase 2 Constant Velocity Cruise] T2 --> T3[Phase 3 Constant Decel] end subgraph SCP[S Curve Jerk Limited Profile] S1[Phase 1 Rising Jerk] --> S2[Phase 2 Constant Accel] S2 --> S3[Phase 3 Falling Jerk] S3 --> S4[Phase 4 Constant Velocity] S4 --> S5[Phase 5 Rising Negative Jerk] S5 --> S6[Phase 6 Constant Decel] S6 --> S7[Phase 7 Falling Negative Jerk] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Mermaid Note:** These diagrams represent the **functional architecture** of a real-time path generator. In practice, these blocks run as deterministic real-time tasks on an industrial PC (e.g., Beckhoff CX, B&R X20) or a dedicated motion controller (e.g., KUKA KR C5, ABB OmniCore). The dashed feedback loop in the first diagram is the **defining feature** of run-time generation — the path is *recomputed* every cycle based on fresh sensor data. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between off-line path planning and run-time path generation in robotics. State two industrial applications where run-time path generation is essential.** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Off-line Path Planning | Run-Time Path Generation | |---|---|---| | Computation time | Before motion starts | During motion (each control cycle) | | Sensor input | Not required | Mandatory (vision, force, encoders) | | Adaptability | Fixed path; rigid | Continuously updated | | Computational budget | Seconds to minutes | **1–10 ms per cycle** | | Use case | Repetitive, known environments | Dynamic, uncertain environments | **Industrial applications where run-time path generation is essential:** 1. **Arc welding with seam tracking** — laser sensor detects joint deviation; the torch path is regenerated at $\sim$ 100 Hz. 2. **Collaborative robot speed-and-separation monitoring** — when a human approaches, the trajectory is re-parameterized in real time to slow down or stop safely (ISO/TS 15066 compliance). > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake (1 mark lost):** Students often write "online" for "off-line" planning. Remember: **off-line = computed before the robot moves; run-time = computed while the robot is moving.** --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model Paper]** **What is a trapezoidal velocity profile? Under what condition does it degenerate into a triangular profile?** **Model Answer:** A **trapezoidal velocity profile (TVP)** is a motion law in which the joint velocity increases linearly with constant acceleration $a_c$ (acceleration phase), remains constant at the maximum velocity $v_{\max}$ (cruise phase), and then decreases linearly with constant deceleration $-a_c$ (deceleration phase). The resulting velocity-vs-time graph is a **trapezoid**. **Condition for triangular profile:** The profile becomes triangular (no cruise phase) when the segment length $L$ is too short to reach $v_{\max}$. Mathematically: $$ L \leq \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a_c} $$ In this case, the peak velocity is $v_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{a_c L}$ and the total motion time is $t_{\text{total}} = 2\sqrt{L/a_c}$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Adapted]** **(a)** A single revolute joint of a 6-DOF robot must move from $\theta_0 = 20°$ to $\theta_f = 80°$ in $t_f = 4$ s. The initial and final velocities are $\dot\theta_0 = 5°/\text{s}$ and $\dot\theta_f = 10°/\text{s}$ respectively. Using a **cubic polynomial trajectory**, derive the four polynomial coefficients. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the **look-ahead blending algorithm** used in modern industrial robots for run-time path generation. With a neat diagram, describe how the blend radius $r_b$ is computed at a corner. Why is $C^2$ continuity important? **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (a)** **Step 1 — Convert all quantities to SI (radians).** $$ \theta_0 = 20° = 0.3491 \text{ rad}, \quad \theta_f = 80° = 1.3963 \text{ rad} $$ $$ \dot\theta_0 = 5°/\text{s} = 0.0873 \text{ rad/s}, \quad \dot\theta_f = 10°/\text{s} = 0.1745 \text{ rad/s} $$ $$ t_f = 4 \text{ s}, \quad \Delta\theta = 1.3963 - 0.3491 = 1.0472 \text{ rad} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $a_0$ and $a_1$ (with $t_0 = 0$):** $$ a_0 = \theta_0 = 0.3491 \text{ rad} $$ $$ a_1 = \dot\theta_0 = 0.0873 \text{ rad/s} $$ **Step 3 — Compute $a_2$:** $$ a_2 = \frac{3 \Delta\theta}{t_f^2} - \frac{2\dot\theta_0 + \dot\theta_f}{t_f} $$ $$ a_2 = \frac{3(1.0472)}{16} - \frac{2(0.0873) + 0.1745}{4} $$ $$ a_2 = \frac{3.1416}{16} - \frac{0.3491}{4} = 0.1964 - 0.0873 = 0.1091 \text{ rad/s}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $a_3$:** $$ a_3 = \frac{-2 \Delta\theta}{t_f^3} + \frac{\dot\theta_0 + \dot\theta_f}{t_f^2} $$ $$ a_3 = \frac{-2(1.0472)}{64} + \frac{0.0873 + 0.1745}{16} $$ $$ a_3 = \frac{-2.0944}{64} + \frac{0.2618}{16} = -0.03273 + 0.01636 = -0.01636 \text{ rad/s}^3 $$ **Step 5 — Final Trajectory:** $$ \theta(t) = 0.3491 + 0.0873 t + 0.1091 t^2 - 0.01636 t^3 \quad \text{(rad)} $$ **Step 6 — Verification at $t = 0$:** $$ \theta(0) = 0.3491 \text{ rad} = 20° \checkmark $$ **Step 7 — Verification at $t = 4$ s:** $$ \theta(4) = 0.3491 + 0.3491 + 1.7456 - 1.0472 = 1.3966 \text{ rad} \approx 80° \checkmark $$ --- #### **Model Solution for Q-A (b)** **Look-Ahead Blending Algorithm:** Modern industrial robot controllers do not stop at each programmed point. Instead, the controller maintains a **look-ahead buffer** of the next $N$ setpoints (typically $N = 15$ to $50$, corresponding to a look-ahead distance of 10 mm to 50 mm). Before reaching a corner, the controller pre-computes a **blended circular or spline arc** that smooths the transition. **Working Principle (5 sequential steps):** 1. **Read the next $N$ setpoints** from the program buffer. 2. **Detect a corner** when the angle between two consecutive linear segments exceeds a threshold (e.g., $\alpha > 5°$). 3. **Compute the blend radius $r_b$** using the formula: $$r_b = \min\left(r_{\max}, \frac{v_{\text{path}} \cdot T_{\text{lookahead}}}{\tan(\alpha/2)}\right)$$ where $r_{\max}$ is the user-defined maximum blend radius, $v_{\text{path}}$ is the programmed path velocity, and $T_{\text{lookahead}}$ is the look-ahead time. 4. **Insert a circular arc** of radius $r_b$ tangent to both incoming and outgoing linear segments. 5. **Re-parameterize the path** with $s \in [0, 1]$ advancing continuously through the blend; velocity is reduced to $v_{\text{blend}} = v_{\text{path}} \cdot \cos(\alpha/2)$ to respect centripetal acceleration limits $a_n = v^2/r_b$. **Diagram (text representation):** ``` P_{i-1} -----> P_i (corner) \ \ r_b \ * P_blend / / P_{i+1} <----- ``` The corner deviation $\delta$ is given by: $$ \delta = r_b \left( 1 - \cos(\alpha/2) \right) $$ **Why $C^2$ continuity matters:** - **$C^0$ continuity** = positions match → no spatial jump. - **$C^1$ continuity** = velocities match → no jerk in position. - **$C^2$ continuity** = accelerations match → no jerk in acceleration (the *j-jerk* of the trajectory is finite). For high-speed motion ($\geq$ 1 m/s) or for payloads sensitive to vibration (e.g., liquid in a glass, wafers in a cleanroom robot), $C^2$ continuity is **mandatory** to avoid excitation of structural modes and to ensure the servo loops can track the reference without saturation. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part b):** Students often confuse **path blending** (a geometric operation) with **trajectory profiling** (a temporal operation). Mark allocation: **3 marks** for correctly defining the look-ahead buffer and blend radius, **2 marks** for the formula derivation, **2 marks** for the $C^2$ continuity explanation. Skipping the formula derivation costs at least 2 marks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Adapted]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **run-time path generation system** of a 6-DOF industrial robot. Clearly identify the role of (i) the look-ahead buffer, (ii) the trajectory profiler, and (iii) the inverse kinematics solver. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A point-to-point motion requires a robot joint to travel $\theta = 30°$ starting and ending at rest, with maximum velocity $\dot\theta_{\max} = 15°/\text{s}$ and maximum acceleration $\ddot\theta_{\max} = 10°/\text{s}^2$. Compute: (i) whether the trapezoidal profile has a cruise phase, (ii) the total motion time, and (iii) the peak velocity if the profile is triangular. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Q-B (b)** **Given:** $\theta = 30°$, $\dot\theta_0 = \dot\theta_f = 0$, $\dot\theta_{\max} = 15°/\text{s}$, $\ddot\theta_{\max} = 10°/\text{s}^2$. **Step 1 — Convert units (work in degrees for ease):** $$ \theta = 30°, \quad v_{\max} = 15°/\text{s}, \quad a_c = 10°/\text{s}^2 $$ **Step 2 — Check for cruise phase:** Compute the minimum distance to reach $v_{\max}$ and decelerate back to zero: $$ L_{\min} = \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a_c} = \frac{(15)^2}{10} = 22.5° $$ Since $L = 30° > 22.5°$, a **cruise phase exists** and the profile is **trapezoidal**. **Step 3 — Compute the trapezoidal phase times:** $$ t_{\text{accel}} = \frac{v_{\max}}{a_c} = \frac{15}{10} = 1.5 \text{ s} $$ $$ t_{\text{decel}} = t_{\text{accel}} = 1.5 \text{ s} \quad \text{(by symmetry, since } \dot\theta_f = 0\text{)} $$ **Step 4 — Compute cruise distance and time:** $$ d_{\text{cruise}} = \theta - \frac{v_{\max}^2}{a_c} = 30 - 22.5 = 7.5° $$ $$ t_{\text{cruise}} = \frac{d_{\text{cruise}}}{v_{\max}} = \frac{7.5}{15} = 0.5 \text{ s} $$ **Step 5 — Total motion time:** $$ \boxed{t_{\text{total}} = 1.5 + 0.5 + 1.5 = 3.5 \text{ s}} $$ **Step 6 — (Hypothetical) Triangular peak velocity if $L \leq L_{\min}$:** For a triangle profile on a 30° segment: $$ v_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{a_c \cdot L} = \sqrt{10 \cdot 30} = \sqrt{300} \approx 17.32°/\text{s} $$ Note that $17.32°/\text{s} > 15°/\text{s}$, which is **not allowed** — confirming the system would limit to $15°/\text{s}$ and produce a trapezoidal profile. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls where KTU students lose marks on Run-Time Path Generation questions:** > 1. **Unit inconsistency (lose 1 mark):** Mixing degrees and radians. Always convert to SI at the start. > 2. **Forgetting $t_0 \neq 0$ (lose 2 marks):** The general cubic formulas include $t_0$. If you assume $t_0 = 0$ without stating it, the examiner may deduct. > 3. **Skipping the cruise-phase existence check (lose 1–2 marks):** Always verify $L \geq v_{\max}^2/a_c$ before computing trapezoidal times. > 4. **Confusing $C^0$, $C^1$, $C^2$ continuity (lose 2 marks):** This is a *favorite* KTU question. Memorize the hierarchy: $C^0$ position, $C^1$ velocity, $C^2$ acceleration. > 5. **Missing the inverse Jacobian step (lose 1 mark):** In Cartesian-space run-time generation, you **must** mention $\dot q = J^{-1} \dot x$ to earn full marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Run-time path generation** = real-time trajectory computation every control cycle (1–10 ms). - **Four core blocks:** Command queue → Interpolation → Trajectory profiler → Inverse kinematics → Servo. - **Cubic polynomial** is the minimum-order trajectory satisfying four boundary conditions (2 position + 2 velocity). - **Quintic polynomial** is used when acceleration boundary conditions are also specified (e.g., rest-to-rest with zero initial/final acceleration). - **Trapezoidal Velocity Profile (TVP)** is the workhorse of industry; degenerates to a triangle when $L \leq v_{\max}^2/a_c$. - **S-curve profile** limits jerk; used in high-precision / vibration-sensitive applications. - **Look-ahead blending** uses $N = 15$ to $50$ setpoints and a blend radius $r_b$ to maintain $C^2$ continuity at corners. - **Joint-space PTP** is singularity-free; **Cartesian-space LIN/CIRC** is path-accurate but requires Jacobian-based IK. - **Cartesian-to-joint rate conversion:** $\dot q = J^{-1}(q) \dot x$ — computed every cycle. - **$C^2$ continuity** means matched position, velocity, and acceleration at via points — required for jerk-free motion. - **Cycle time** $T_{\text{cycle}} \leq 1/f_{\text{servo}}$ is the hard real-time deadline (typically 1 ms for 1 kHz servo). - **KTU exam tip:** Always draw the block diagram when a question asks "with a neat diagram" — it earns 1–2 free marks. - **Key formulas to memorize:** - Cubic: $a_3 = \frac{-2\Delta\theta}{t_f^3} + \frac{\dot\theta_0 + \dot\theta_f}{t_f^2}$ - Trapezoidal: $t_{\text{total}} = L/v_{\max}$ (cruise case) - Triangle: $v_{\text{peak}} = \sqrt{a_c L}$ - Blend: $r_b = v_{\text{path}} T_{\text{lookahead}} / \tan(\alpha/2)$

topics.title: Description of Paths in a Robot Programming Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Description of Paths in a Robot Programming Language

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In a **Robot Programming Language (RPL)**, *Description of Paths* refers to the formal set of **motion commands**, **geometric specifications**, and **interpolation methods** used by a programmer to instruct the end-effector (or any controlled point on the manipulator) to traverse a specific trajectory through 3D Cartesian space or joint space.

The path is essentially a **mathematically defined geometric curve** in either **Joint Space (Configuration Space)** or **Cartesian Space (Task Space)**, parameterized by a time-history of position, velocity, and acceleration. Common interpolation schemes include:

- **Joint-Interpolated Motion (Move Joint / PTP)** — Path is undefined in Cartesian space; each joint moves at its own maximum (or programmed) rate.
- **Straight-Line Motion (Move Linear / PTP in Cartesian)** — Tool tip moves along a straight line between two taught points.
- **Circular Motion (Move Circular)** — Tool tip traces a true circular arc defined by three non-collinear points.
- **Spline / Continuous Path (CP)** — Smooth multi-segment trajectory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:**  
> A *path* in a robot program is a sequence of **Via Points (intermediate goal positions)** and **Terminator Points (final goal positions)** combined with a chosen **interpolation method** that mathematically defines the trajectory of the tool center point (TCP).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car from your home to a shop** using a GPS:

1. **Joint-Interpolated Motion** → You press a button and the car instantly figures out its own way home, ignoring roads. The wheels turn at different rates; the path is *uncontrolled* in the world frame.
2. **Straight-Line Motion** → The GPS plots a perfectly straight line cutting through buildings. The car *must* stay on that line, even if the road curves.
3. **Circular Motion** → The GPS asks you to take a roundabout; the car follows the exact circular arc of the roundabout.
4. **Continuous Path (CP)** → The GPS guides you smoothly along a hand-drawn curvy road.

In a robot, the **programmer describes *what* path** (straight, circular, joint-interpolated) and the **controller's interpolator computes the *how*** (the actual joint commands at each millisecond).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** Speed in robotics is usually specified in **mm/sec** (for Cartesian motion) or as a **percentage of rated joint speed** (for joint motion). Acceleration is in **mm/sec²** or **% of rated acceleration**.

---

## 1.3 Why Path Description Matters

The choice of path command directly affects:

- **Cycle Time** (productivity)
- **Path Accuracy** (quality of the task — welding, painting, cutting)
- **Joint Singularity Avoidance** (whether the robot must detour to avoid losing a DOF)
- **Energy Consumption**
- **Mechanical Wear**

For a pick-and-place task, you typically use **joint interpolation** (fastest). For a welding seam, you need **straight-line or circular interpolation** (highest path accuracy).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Path Description Statement

A complete path description in a robot programming language has **four mandatory components**:

| # | Component | Purpose | Example (VAL II) |
|---|-----------|---------|------------------|
| 1 | **Motion Command (Verb)** | Specifies the *type* of motion | `MOVE`, `MOVES`, `APPRO`, `DEPART` |
| 2 | **Destination Point** | Defines the *where* (goal) | `HOME`, `P1`, `(x,y,z,O,A,T)` |
| 3 | **Interpolation Method** | Defines the *shape* of the path | Joint-interpolated, straight-line, circular |
| 4 | **Speed & Termination** | Defines the *how fast* and *when to stop* | `SPEED = 50`, `COARSE`, `FINE` |

---

## 2.2 Detailed Classification of Path Commands

### (A) Joint-Interpolated Motion (MOVE / PTP)

- The controller computes joint trajectories such that **all joints start and finish moving at the same time**, each at its own maximum or programmed rate.
- **Path shape in Cartesian space is UNDEFINED** — the TCP traces a curved, often arching trajectory.
- **Fastest motion type** since it uses each joint at its peak capability.
- Used for: Pick-and-place, moving clear of obstacles between tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mathematical Characteristic:** Joint interpolation is a **time-optimal (bang-bang or bang-zero-bang) acceleration profile** in each joint.

### (B) Straight-Line Motion (MOVES / MoveL)

- The **TCP moves along a perfect straight line** in Cartesian space from the current position to the destination.
- Orientation of the tool can be held constant or interpolated linearly between start and end orientations.
- Used for: Glue dispensing, seam tracking, insertion tasks where straight approach is critical.

### (C) Circular Motion (MoveC / ARC)

- The TCP travels along a **circular arc** defined by three points:
  1. **Start point** (current TCP position)
  2. **Via point** (an intermediate point on the arc)
  3. **End point** (final destination)
