- Used for: **Arc welding**, painting curved surfaces, polishing round parts.

### (D) Continuous Path (CP) Motion

- A series of closely-spaced points recorded during a **manual teach-pendant trace**; the controller plays them back to reproduce the path.
- Used for: Spray painting, complex sealing, curved welding seams.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Path length (straight line) | $L = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2}$ | mm |
| Path length (circular arc) | $s = r \cdot \theta$, where $\theta$ is in **radians** | mm |
| Radius of circular arc (3-point formula) | $r = \dfrac{\vert a \vert \cdot \vert b \vert \cdot \vert c \vert}{4 \cdot K}$ | mm; $K$ = triangle area |
| Velocity of TCP | $v = \dfrac{ds}{dt}$ | mm/sec |
| Acceleration (trapezoidal profile) | $a = \dfrac{v_{max} - v_{initial}}{t_{accel}}$ | mm/sec² |
| Joint to Cartesian velocity (Jacobian) | $\dot{x} = J(q) \cdot \dot{q}$ | — |
| Cycle time (joint motion) | $T = \max_j \left( \dfrac{\vert \theta_{j,end} - \theta_{j,start} \vert}{\dot{\theta}_{j,max}} \right)$ | sec |
| Position vector along a line (parametric) | $\vec{P}(u) = (1-u)\,\vec{P_1} + u\,\vec{P_2},\ \ u \in [0,1]$ | parametric |
| Point on circular arc (parametric) | $\vec{P}(u) = \vec{C} + r\,(\cos u \cdot \hat{i} + \sin u \cdot \hat{j})$ | parametric, $u \in [\alpha,\beta]$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Collision with Vertical Pipes in Markdown:**  
> All absolute value / determinant operations use `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent breaking the table layout. Example: $\vert a \vert$ instead of $|a|$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** Arc-welding robots on car chassis use **circular interpolation** to follow weld seams around wheel arches and door frames.
- **Painting Robots (e.g., ABB, KUKA):** Use **CP (continuous path) recording** through teach-pendant manual guidance.
- **PCB Assembly:** **Straight-line motion** is mandatory for component insertion.
- **Surgical Robots (Da Vinci):** Use **sub-millimeter straight-line and arc paths** for incisions and suturing.
- **3D Printing Robotic Arms:** Use **spline interpolation** to lay down smooth curved layers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Circular Arc Path from Three Non-Collinear Points

When a robot programmer issues a `MoveC` command with three points $P_1, P_2, P_3$, the controller must compute the **center** $C = (C_x, C_y, C_z)$ and **radius** $r$ of the unique circle passing through these three points (assuming they lie in a known plane).

### Step 1: Set Up the Geometric Constraint

For the three points to lie on a circle of center $C$ and radius $r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
(P_{1x} - C_x)^2 + (P_{1y} - C_y)^2 + (P_{1z} - C_z)^2 &= r^2 \\
(P_{2x} - C_x)^2 + (P_{2y} - C_y)^2 + (P_{2z} - C_z)^2 &= r^2 \\
(P_{3x} - C_x)^2 + (P_{3y} - C_y)^2 + (P_{3z} - C_z)^2 &= r^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Every point is equidistant from the center.

### Step 2: Subtract the First Equation from the Second and Third

This eliminates $r^2$ and reduces the system to two linear equations in $(C_x, C_y, C_z)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2(P_{2x} - P_{1x})C_x + 2(P_{2y} - P_{1y})C_y + 2(P_{2z} - P_{1z})C_z &= P_{2x}^2 - P_{1x}^2 + P_{2y}^2 - P_{1y}^2 + P_{2z}^2 - P_{1z}^2 \\
2(P_{3x} - P_{1x})C_x + 2(P_{3y} - P_{1y})C_y + 2(P_{3z} - P_{1z})C_z &= P_{3x}^2 - P_{1x}^2 + P_{3y}^2 - P_{1y}^2 + P_{3z}^2 - P_{1z}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Subtracting the squared distance equations yields linear terms only.

### Step 3: Solve the 3×3 Linear System

For a planar case ($z = 0$ for simplicity), the system reduces to a 2×2 system. The classical closed-form solution for the center is:

$$
C_x = \dfrac{
\begin{vmatrix}
D_1 & (P_{1y} - P_{2y}) \\
D_2 & (P_{1y} - P_{3y})
\end{vmatrix}
}{2 \cdot D}
\qquad
C_y = \dfrac{
\begin{vmatrix}
(P_{1x} - P_{2x}) & D_1 \\
(P_{1x} - P_{3x}) & D_2
\end{vmatrix}
}{2 \cdot D}
$$

where the **differences** and the **determinant** are:

$$
D_1 = P_{1x}^2 + P_{1y}^2 - P_{2x}^2 - P_{2y}^2
$$
$$
D_2 = P_{1x}^2 + P_{1y}^2 - P_{3x}^2 - P_{3y}^2
$$
$$
D = (P_{1x} - P_{2x})(P_{1y} - P_{3y}) - (P_{1x} - P_{3x})(P_{1y} - P_{2y})
$$

**Logic:** Solving by Cramer's Rule, since the coefficient matrix is non-singular when points are non-collinear.

### Step 4: Compute Radius

$$
r = \sqrt{(P_{1x} - C_x)^2 + (P_{1y} - C_y)^2}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Linear Interpolation of Position and Orientation

For a **straight-line move** in Cartesian space, the **position** and **orientation** of the tool must both be interpolated. The position interpolates linearly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\vec{P}(t) &= \vec{P_1} + \frac{t}{T}\,(\vec{P_2} - \vec{P_1}), \quad 0 \le t \le T \\
&= (1 - s)\,\vec{P_1} + s\,\vec{P_2}, \quad s = \frac{t}{T}
\end{aligned}
$$

The orientation is often interpolated using **SLERP (Spherical Linear Interpolation)** on unit quaternions:

$$
q(s) = \dfrac{\sin((1-s)\theta)}{\sin\theta}\,q_1 + \dfrac{\sin(s\theta)}{\sin\theta}\,q_2
$$

where $\theta = \cos^{-1}(q_1 \cdot q_2)$ is the angle between the two orientation quaternions.

**Logic:** Linear interpolation in quaternion space is *not* valid (it yields a curve on a 4-D sphere). SLERP traverses the great-circle arc, ensuring smooth orientation change.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Path Types

| Property | Joint-Interp. (PTP) | Straight-Line | Circular Arc | CP / Spline |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Path shape in Cartesian** | Uncontrolled | Straight line | Circular arc | Recorded / spline |
| **Computation load** | Low | Medium | Medium | High |
| **Path accuracy** | Low | High (perfect) | High (perfect) | High (sampled) |
| **Cycle time** | Minimum | Slower than PTP | Slower than PTP | Slowest |
| **Singularity handling** | Easy to avoid | Detour required | Detour required | Detour required |
| **Use case** | Pick & place | Insertion, gluing | Arc welding | Spray painting |
| **Inverse kinematics calls** | Only at endpoints | Every sample period | Every sample period | Every sample period |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Path Description Pipeline

The following block diagram shows how a high-level `MOVE` command in a robot programming language is decomposed into low-level joint torque commands by the controller.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer: MOVE P1 SPEED 50] --> B[High-Level Path Command Interpreter]
    B --> C{Interpolation Type?}
    C -->|PTP| D[Joint-Space Interpolator]
    C -->|Linear| E[Cartesian Linear Interpolator]
    C -->|Circular| F[Cartesian Arc Interpolator]
    C -->|Spline/CP| G[Cartesian Spline Interpolator]
    D --> H[Time-Parameterized Trajectory]
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Inverse Kinematics Solver]
    I --> J[Joint Angles q1..q6 per servo cycle]
    J --> K[Joint Servo Controllers]
    K --> L[Servo Motors Actuator Torques]
    L --> M[Physical TCP Motion in Workspace]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Every path description statement flows from a textual command down through an interpolation module, then through inverse kinematics, to the motors.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Decomposition of a Path Description Statement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MOVE Statement] --> B[Motion Verb]
    A --> C[Destination Point]
    A --> D[Speed Specification]
    A --> E[Termination Criterion]
    B --> B1[Joint / Linear / Circular / CP]
    C --> C1[Named Location or Coordinate Tuple]
    D --> D1[mm/s or Percent of Rated]
    E --> E1[COARSE / FINE / NULL]
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Three Methods of Path Specification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Robot Path Specification Method] --> B[Teach Pendant Method]
    A --> C[Explicit Programming Method]
    A --> D[Task-Level Programming Method]
    B --> B1[Manual Lead-Through]
    B --> B2[Point-by-Point Teach]
    C --> C1[VAL, AML, RAIL Languages]
    C --> C2[Textual Path Description]
    D --> D1[Goal Specified in Task Space]
    D --> D2[Robot Computes Path Automatically]
```

---

## 4.4 Path Comparison Schematic (Cartesian Workspace)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1((P1 Start)) --> P2((P2 End))
    P1 -.Joint-Interp.-> P2
    P1 ==Straight-Line==> P2
    P1 --Circular Arc--> P3((Via Point)) --Circular Arc--> P2
    P1 -.Spline/CP.-> P2
```

**Reading the diagram:** Four alternative path geometries between the same two points $P_1$ and $P_2$. The dotted (joint-interp) and dash-dotted (spline) lines represent unconstrained / recorded paths, while the double-line (straight) and dashed-arc represent mathematically defined paths.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Differentiate between Joint-Interpolated Motion and Straight-Line Motion in a robot programming language. Give one example application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Joint-Interpolated Motion | Straight-Line Motion |
|---|---|---|
| Path shape in Cartesian | Undefined (joint-coordinated) | Perfect straight line |
| Interpolation | Done in joint space | Done in Cartesian space |
| Speed | Fastest (each joint at max rate) | Slower (TCP speed limited) |
| Accuracy of TCP path | Low | High |
| Example application | Pick-and-place of a box | Inserting a peg into a hole |

(3 marks: 1 for tabular format, 1 for correct technical points, 1 for relevant examples)

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**List the four main components of a path description statement in any robot programming language.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Motion Command / Verb** — defines the type of path (e.g., `MOVE`, `MOVES`, `APPRO`)
2. **Destination / Goal Point** — defines where to go (e.g., `P1`, `HOME`)
3. **Interpolation Method** — defines the geometric path shape (joint, linear, circular)
4. **Speed and Termination** — defines how fast and the precision of stopping (e.g., `SPEED = 50`, `FINE`)

(3 marks: 1 mark per first three components, full 3 if all four mentioned with correctness)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> *As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, students must answer ONE full question from a choice of two.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the different types of interpolation methods used in robot programming languages to describe paths. Discuss their relative advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot end-effector must move from point $P_1 = (100, 200, 300)$ to $P_2 = (400, 600, 500)$ in Cartesian space using **straight-line interpolation**, at a constant TCP speed of $v = 50$ mm/s. Determine:
   (i) The total path length.
   (ii) The total time of motion.
   (iii) The position of the TCP at $t = 3$ seconds. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (a):

The four main interpolation methods are:

1. **Joint-Interpolated (PTP):** The controller moves each joint from its initial angle to the final angle such that all joints begin and end their motion simultaneously. The actual path of the TCP in Cartesian space is uncontrolled and is generally a curve.
   - *Advantage:* Fastest cycle time; computationally simple.
   - *Limitation:* Unpredictable TCP path; unsuitable when obstacles are near the expected path.

2. **Straight-Line (Linear) Interpolation:** The TCP is constrained to move along a perfectly straight line between the start and end points. Orientation is also interpolated (often using SLERP on quaternions).
   - *Advantage:* Predictable path; high path accuracy; ideal for insertion, gluing, and seam-tracking.
   - *Limitation:* Slower than joint motion; requires inverse kinematics at every servo cycle (high computation).

3. **Circular Interpolation:** The TCP travels along a circular arc defined by three non-collinear points (start, via, end).
   - *Advantage:* Perfect arc geometry; essential for arc-welding and curved-feature operations.
   - *Limitation:* Limited to planar or simple 3D arcs; not suited for free-form curves.

4. **Continuous Path (CP) / Spline Interpolation:** The path is defined by a dense series of recorded points (via teach-pendant) or by mathematical splines (Bezier, B-spline, NURBS).
   - *Advantage:* Arbitrary complex shapes; smooth, jerk-limited motion.
   - *Limitation:* Requires large memory for stored points; high computation for spline evaluation.

(7 marks: 1.5 per method × 4 = 6, plus 1 mark for overall comparison / table)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A (b):

**Given:** $P_1 = (100, 200, 300)$, $P_2 = (400, 600, 500)$, $v = 50$ mm/s, $t = 3$ s.

**(i) Total Path Length:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2 + (z_2 - z_1)^2} \\
L &= \sqrt{(400 - 100)^2 + (600 - 200)^2 + (500 - 300)^2} \\
L &= \sqrt{(300)^2 + (400)^2 + (200)^2} \\
L &= \sqrt{90000 + 160000 + 40000} \\
L &= \sqrt{290000} \\
L &\approx 538.52 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Total Time of Motion:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{L}{v} = \frac{538.52}{50} \\
T &\approx 10.77 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Position of TCP at $t = 3$ s:**

The parametric variable $s$ at time $t$ is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{t}{T} = \frac{3}{10.77} \approx 0.2785
\end{aligned}
$$

The interpolated Cartesian position:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(3) &= x_1 + s \cdot (x_2 - x_1) = 100 + 0.2785 \times 300 = 100 + 83.55 = 183.55 \text{ mm} \\
y(3) &= y_1 + s \cdot (y_2 - y_1) = 200 + 0.2785 \times 400 = 200 + 111.40 = 311.40 \text{ mm} \\
z(3) &= z_1 + s \cdot (z_2 - z_1) = 300 + 0.2785 \times 200 = 300 + 55.70 = 355.70 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the TCP position at $t = 3$ s is $\vec{P}(3) \approx (183.55,\ 311.40,\ 355.70)$ mm.

(7 marks: 2 marks for part (i), 1.5 marks for part (ii), 3.5 marks for part (iii) with full substitution)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain how a **circular arc path** is described in a robot programming language using the **three-point method**. Derive the formula for computing the radius of the circular arc. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A robot is executing a circular motion. The three points defining the arc are:
   $P_1 = (0, 0)$, $P_2 = (4, 0)$, $P_3 = (2, 3)$.
   Compute the **(i) center** of the circle, **(ii) radius**, and **(iii) path length** for a 90° arc starting at $P_1$ and ending at $P_2$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (a):

**Three-Point Circular Path Description:**

In a robot programming language, a circular arc is described by **three non-collinear points** in the workspace:

- $P_1$ — **Start point** (current TCP position)
- $P_2$ — **Via point** (an intermediate point on the desired arc)
- $P_3$ — **End point** (final destination)

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1((P1 Start)) -->|Arc segment| P2((P2 Via Point))
    P2 -->|Arc segment| P3((P3 End Point))
    C((C Center)) --- P1
    C --- P2
    C --- P3
```

**Derivation of Radius Formula:**

The perpendicular bisector of $P_1 P_2$ meets the perpendicular bisector of $P_2 P_3$ at the center $C$. Using coordinate geometry:

Let $M_{12} = \left(\dfrac{P_{1x} + P_{2x}}{2},\ \dfrac{P_{1y} + P_{2y}}{2}\right)$ be the midpoint of $P_1 P_2$, and similarly for $M_{23}$.

The center coordinates from Cramer's rule (after solving the linear system):

$$
C_x = \dfrac{D_1 (P_{1y} - P_{3y}) - D_2 (P_{1y} - P_{2y})}{2 D}, \quad
C_y = \dfrac{(P_{1x} - P_{2x}) D_2 - (P_{1x} - P_{3x}) D_1}{2 D}
$$

with $D_1 = P_{1x}^2 + P_{1y}^2 - P_{2x}^2 - P_{2y}^2$, $D_2 = P_{1x}^2 + P_{1y}^2 - P_{3x}^2 - P_{3y}^2$, and $D = (P_{1x} - P_{2x})(P_{1y} - P_{3y}) - (P_{1x} - P_{3x})(P_{1y} - P_{2y})$.

The radius is then:

$$
r = \sqrt{(P_{1x} - C_x)^2 + (P_{1y} - C_y)^2}
$$

(7 marks: 2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for three-point concept, 3 marks for derivation)

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B (b):

**Given:** $P_1 = (0, 0)$, $P_2 = (4, 0)$, $P_3 = (2, 3)$. Arc spans 90° from $P_1$ to $P_2$.

**Step 1: Compute $D_1$, $D_2$, and $D$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_1 &= 0^2 + 0^2 - 4^2 - 0^2 = -16 \\
D_2 &= 0^2 + 0^2 - 2^2 - 3^2 = -13 \\
D &= (0 - 4)(0 - 3) - (0 - 2)(0 - 0) = (-4)(-3) - (-2)(0) = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Center Coordinates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_x &= \dfrac{(-16)(0 - 3) - (-13)(0 - 0)}{2 \times 12} = \dfrac{48 - 0}{24} = 2 \\
C_y &= \dfrac{(0 - 4)(-13) - (0 - 2)(-16)}{2 \times 12} = \dfrac{52 - 32}{24} = \dfrac{20}{24} = 0.833
\end{aligned}
$$

So center $C = (2, 0.833)$ mm.

**Step 3: Compute Radius:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r &= \sqrt{(0 - 2)^2 + (0 - 0.833)^2} = \sqrt{4 + 0.694} = \sqrt{4.694} \\
r &\approx 2.167 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Compute Path Length for 90° Arc:**

The arc spans 90° = $\dfrac{\pi}{2}$ radians.

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= r \cdot \theta = 2.167 \times \dfrac{\pi}{2} \\
s &\approx 2.167 \times 1.5708 \\
s &\approx 3.404 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks: 2 for center calculation, 2 for radius, 1.5 for arc length, 1.5 for unit consistency)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Path Description Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to verify non-collinearity** of three points for circular arc — collinear points yield $D = 0$ and division-by-zero in the center formula. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for not mentioning this check.
> 2. **Mixing up joint space and Cartesian space** terminology. "Path" in robotics has *different geometric meaning* in each space — clearly state which one.
> 3. **Ignoring units.** Speed is in **mm/s**, distances in **mm**, angles in **radians** (for $s = r\theta$ to work). Examiners deduct 0.5 mark per unit error.
> 4. **Using the wrong interpolation formula** for orientation. Orientation is NOT linearly interpolated in Euler angles; it uses **SLERP on quaternions** to avoid gimbal lock and non-uniform velocity.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** (e.g., "assuming the three points are coplanar" or "constant TCP speed assumed") — this alone can cost 1 mark per KTU valuation key.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **path description** in a robot programming language has four parts: **Verb, Destination, Interpolation Type, Speed/Termination**.
- ✅ The **four interpolation types** are: **Joint (PTP), Straight-Line, Circular, Continuous Path (CP)/Spline**.
- ✅ **Joint-interpolated motion** is the fastest but has an **uncontrolled TCP path** in Cartesian space.
- ✅ **Straight-line motion** requires **inverse kinematics at every servo cycle** — computationally expensive.
- ✅ **Circular interpolation** is described by **three non-collinear points**: start, via, and end.
- ✅ The **parametric straight-line equation** is $\vec{P}(u) = (1-u)\vec{P_1} + u\vec{P_2}$ for $u \in [0,1]$.
- ✅ The **arc length** along a circular arc is $s = r\theta$ where $\theta$ must be in **radians**.
- ✅ **SLERP** (Spherical Linear Interpolation) is the correct method for interpolating **orientation** between two poses.
- ✅ Speed is specified in **mm/s** for Cartesian moves and **% of rated** for joint moves.
- ✅ Termination criteria like **COARSE / FINE / NULL** control the trade-off between **cycle time** and **path accuracy**.
- ✅ KTU 2024 questions frequently test: **differences between path types**, **straight-line interpolation parameters**, and **three-point circle calculations** — be ready with full derivations.
- ✅ Standard KTU 14-mark question format: **7 + 7** split between *theory* and *numerical* — always attempt both halves with equal rigour.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning Path when Using the Dynamic Model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Planning Path when Using the Dynamic Model

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Dynamic Path Planning** is the process of generating a time-parameterized trajectory (or path) for a robotic manipulator subject to the constraints imposed by the manipulator's full dynamic model. Unlike purely kinematic path planning (which only considers position, velocity, and acceleration bounds), dynamic path planning explicitly incorporates the **Lagrangian dynamic equation** of motion:

$$ \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau} $$

where the planning algorithm must respect both **actuator torque limits** $\vert \tau_i \vert \leq \tau_{i,\max}$ and **actuator velocity limits** $\vert \dot{q}_i \vert \leq \dot{q}_{i,\max}$, ensuring the commanded motion is physically realizable by the real robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 specifically asks students to understand *why* simply planning a smooth geometric path in joint/Cartesian space is **insufficient** for high-speed robots, and *how* the dynamic equation constrains the admissible trajectory timing — the so-called **"planning in the dynamic model"** paradigm.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car from Point A to Point B.

- **Kinematic planning** would be like plotting a route on a map: a smooth road with gentle turns. You plan the *shape* of the journey.
- **Dynamic planning** is like actually *driving* the car: the engine has a maximum horsepower (**torque limit**), the road has a speed limit (**velocity limit**), and the car cannot corner faster than the tires allow (**acceleration limit**). A good driver constantly modulates the accelerator and brake so the car never stalls, never exceeds red-line RPM, and arrives in minimum time.

For a robot arm, "engine horsepower" maps to **joint motor torque**, "speed limit" maps to **maximum joint velocity**, and "tire grip" maps to **maximum joint acceleration** set by structural and payload limits. **Path planning with the dynamic model** is the act of synchronizing all joints so that no joint violates its hardware limits — ideally, the trajectory is **time-optimal**, meaning the robot completes the motion in the absolute minimum possible time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Distinction (Board-Favorite Question):**
> - **Geometric / Kinematic Path:** A curve in space (no time involved).
> - **Trajectory:** A path *plus* a time law, e.g., $s(t) = 3t^2 - 2t^3$.
> - **Dynamic Trajectory:** A trajectory whose time law has been verified — and possibly modified — to satisfy $\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot{q} + \mathbf{C}(q,\dot{q})\dot{q} + \mathbf{G}(q) = \tau$ with $\vert \tau_i \vert \le \tau_{i,\max}$.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Magnitude (Industrial Robot) |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})$ | Inertia matrix | $n \times n$ symmetric positive-definite |
| $\mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})$ | Coriolis/centripetal matrix | $n \times n$ |
| $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q})$ | Gravity vector | $n \times 1$ |
| $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Joint torque vector | **Nm (Newton-metres)** |
| $\dot{q}_{i,\max}$ | Max joint velocity | typically **$\pi$ rad/s** |
| $\ddot{q}_{i,\max}$ | Max joint acceleration | typically **$10$ rad/s²** |
| $\tau_{i,\max}$ | Max joint torque | typically **$100$ – $500$ Nm** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All **dynamic limits** are **hardware limits** set by the actuator. The planner's job is to *honour* them, not to *exceed* them.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does the Dynamic Model Change Path Planning?

In Module 3 (kinematics), we freely chose cubic or quintic polynomials $q(t)$ and used $q(t), \dot q(t), \ddot q(t)$ without ever asking: *"Can my motors actually deliver the torques required to produce this $\ddot q(t)$?"* In high-speed or high-payload applications, the answer is often **no**. The required torque may be:

$$ \tau_i^{\text{req}}(t) = \sum_j M_{ij}(\mathbf{q})\,\ddot{q}_j \;+\; \sum_{j,k} C_{ijk}(\mathbf{q})\dot{q}_j\dot{q}_k \;+\; G_i(\mathbf{q}) $$

If $\vert \tau_i^{\text{req}} \vert > \tau_{i,\max}$ at any instant, the joint cannot follow the planned $\ddot{q}_i(t)$ — the motor saturates, the robot either stalls or vibrates, and tracking accuracy collapses.

## 2.2 The Two Major Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

### Strategy A — **Forward Dynamic Scaling (Rescaling)**
1. Plan a *kinematically smooth* trajectory in joint space (e.g., a cubic/quintic polynomial).
2. Compute the required torques $\tau^{\text{req}}(t)$ from the dynamic model.
3. If $\vert \tau^{\text{req}}_i \vert > \tau_{i,\max}$, **slow down** the entire trajectory uniformly by a scaling factor $0 < \alpha < 1$, so the new time parameter is $t' = t / \alpha$.
4. Recompute. Iterate until feasibility is achieved.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** If your road trip's schedule is too tight to drive safely, you simply leave *earlier* and drive the *same route* more slowly. The path shape doesn't change; only the **time law** changes.

### Strategy B — **Minimum-Time (Time-Optimal) Trajectory Planning**
This is the more rigorous, KTU-emphasis approach. The objective is to find the trajectory $\mathbf{q}(t)$ that **minimizes total motion time $T$** subject to:

- **Initial/final state constraints:** $\mathbf{q}(0)=\mathbf{q}_0,\; \mathbf{q}(T)=\mathbf{q}_f$, and often $\dot{\mathbf{q}}(0)=\dot{\mathbf{q}}(T)=0$.
- **Actuator constraints:** $\vert \tau_i(t) \vert \le \tau_{i,\max}$ for all $i, t$.
- **Path constraint (often):** $\mathbf{q}$ is restricted to a chosen geometric path $s(\mathbf{q})=0$ (path-constrained optimization).

The solution is given by **Pontryagin's Maximum Principle**: the optimal control is **bang-bang** in torque (switches between $\pm \tau_{i,\max}$), separated by at most $n-1$ singular arcs where the control saturates multiple constraints.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations — they are examiner favourites for both Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| # | Equation | Meaning / Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{q})\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}(\mathbf{q},\dot{\mathbf{q}})\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{q}) = \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Full Lagrangian dynamic model. Foundation of all dynamic planning. |
| 2 | $\tau_i^{\min}(q,\dot q) \le \tau_i \le \tau_i^{\max}(q,\dot q)$ | Actuator torque bounds (drive-dependent). |
| 3 | $\tau_i^{\text{req}} = \sum_j M_{ij}\ddot q_j + \sum_{j,k} \Gamma_{ijk}\dot q_j \dot q_k + G_i$ | Torque needed to execute a planned $\ddot q$. Used in Strategy A. |
| 4 | $\tau_i = \tau_{i,\max} \cdot \text{sgn}(\lambda_i)$ (bang-bang) | Pontryagin's principle for time-optimal motion. |
| 5 | $T^* = \min \int_0^T 1\,dt$ | Objective functional for minimum-time problem. |
| 6 | $\dot q_{i,\max} \rightarrow \ddot q_{i,\max}^{\text{eff}} = \dfrac{\tau_{i,\max} - C_{ij}\dot q_j \dot q_k - G_i}{M_{ii}}$ | Effective acceleration limit *derived from* torque limit. |
| 7 | Cubic trajectory: $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ | Most basic time law. Continuous $\dot q$, discontinuous $\ddot q$ at endpoints. |
| 8 | Quintic trajectory: $q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 + a_4 t^4 + a_5 t^5$ | Continuous $\dot q$ *and* $\ddot q$ at endpoints. Preferred in dynamic planning. |
| 9 | $\Delta \sigma_i = \dfrac{\tau_{i,\max} - \tau_i^{\text{req}}(t)}{M_{ii}(q)}$ | Torque margin converted to acceleration margin. |
| 10 | $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}} / \alpha$, with $\alpha \in (0,1)$ | Time-scaling law (Strategy A). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Time-optimal planning is *the* algorithm running inside the motion controllers of KUKA, ABB, and FANUC robots. Whenever a pick-and-place cycle must be shortened by 5%, this is the math being used.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of Dynamic Scaling (Strategy A)

1. **Choose geometric path** in joint space (e.g., straight-line in $q$).
2. **Parameterize** by a normalized scalar $s \in [0,1]$: $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{q}(\sigma)$ with $\sigma(t) \in [0,1]$.
3. **Pick a tentative** smooth timing function, e.g., cubic $\sigma(t) = 3(t/T)^2 - 2(t/T)^3$.
4. **Compute** $\dot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{q}'_\sigma \dot{\sigma}$ and $\ddot{\mathbf{q}} = \mathbf{q}''_\sigma \dot{\sigma}^2 + \mathbf{q}'_\sigma \ddot{\sigma}$.
5. **Evaluate** required torque $\boldsymbol{\tau}^{\text{req}}(t) = \mathbf{M}\ddot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{C}\dot{\mathbf{q}} + \mathbf{G}$.
6. **Check** $\vert \tau_i^{\text{req}}(t) \vert \le \tau_{i,\max}$ for all $i$ and all $t \in [0,T]$.
7. If violation occurs, **reduce** $\dot{\sigma}$ and $\ddot{\sigma}$ (i.e., slow down).
8. Iterate from Step 4 until feasible.

> [!NOTE]
> The "why" of this approach: the geometric path is **decoupled** from timing, so we never change *where* the robot goes — only *how fast*.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Kinematic Cubic to Dynamic-Admissible Cubic

**Given.** A single joint moves from $q_0 = 0$ rad to $q_f = 1$ rad in nominal time $T = 2$ s with zero initial and final velocity. A cubic polynomial is proposed.

### Step 1 — Set up the cubic polynomial

$$ q(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3 $$

**Reasoning:** Cubic gives 4 coefficients, matching the 4 boundary conditions (position & velocity at both ends).

### Step 2 — Apply boundary conditions

The boundary conditions are:

$$ q(0) = q_0 = 0,\quad \dot q(0) = 0 $$

$$ q(T) = q_f = 1,\quad \dot q(T) = 0 $$

Now evaluate:

$$ q(0) = a_0 = 0 $$

$$ \dot q(t) = a_1 + 2a_2 t + 3a_3 t^2 \;\Rightarrow\; \dot q(0) = a_1 = 0 $$

$$ q(T) = a_0 + a_1 T + a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = 1 $$

$$ \dot q(T) = a_1 + 2a_2 T + 3a_3 T^2 = 0 $$

**Reasoning:** Substituting $a_0 = 0$ and $a_1 = 0$, the last two equations simplify.

### Step 3 — Solve for $a_2$ and $a_3$

The two remaining equations become:

$$ a_2 T^2 + a_3 T^3 = 1 $$

$$ 2 a_2 T + 3 a_3 T^2 = 0 $$

From the second equation:

$$ 2 a_2 T = - 3 a_3 T^2 $$

$$ a_2 = -\frac{3}{2} a_3 T $$

Substitute into the first:

$$ \left(-\frac{3}{2} a_3 T\right) T^2 + a_3 T^3 = 1 $$

$$ -\frac{3}{2} a_3 T^3 + a_3 T^3 = 1 $$

$$ -\frac{1}{2} a_3 T^3 = 1 $$

$$ a_3 = -\frac{2}{T^3} $$

Therefore:

$$ a_2 = -\frac{3}{2}\left(-\frac{2}{T^3}\right) T = \frac{3}{T^2} $$

**Reasoning (consistency check):** The well-known cubic solution for a rest-to-rest move is $a_2 = 3/T^2$ and $a_3 = -2/T^3$. ✓

### Step 4 — Numerical values for $T = 2$ s

$$ a_2 = \frac{3}{(2)^2} = \frac{3}{4} = 0.75 \;\text{rad/s}^2 $$

$$ a_3 = -\frac{2}{(2)^3} = -\frac{2}{8} = -0.25 \;\text{rad/s}^3 $$

Hence:

$$ q(t) = 0.75\, t^2 - 0.25\, t^3 \;\text{rad} $$

### Step 5 — Compute velocity and acceleration

$$ \dot q(t) = 1.5\, t - 0.75\, t^2 \;\text{rad/s} $$

$$ \ddot q(t) = 1.5 - 1.5\, t \;\text{rad/s}^2 $$

**Reasoning:** $\ddot q$ is *linear* in $t$ and changes sign at $t = 1$ s. The maximum magnitude occurs at the endpoints:

$$ \ddot q_{\max} = \vert \ddot q(0) \vert = \vert \ddot q(2) \vert = 1.5 \;\text{rad/s}^2 $$

### Step 6 — Convert acceleration into required torque

Assume a single-rotational-joint system with constant inertia $I$ and viscous friction $b$ (gravity negligible for a horizontal axis):

$$ \tau^{\text{req}}(t) = I\,\ddot q(t) + b\,\dot q(t) $$

Take $I = 1\,\text{kg·m}^2$ and $b = 0.5\,\text{Nm·s/rad}$. Compute at $t=0$:

$$ \tau^{\text{req}}(0) = 1 \cdot 1.5 + 0.5 \cdot 0 = 1.5 \;\text{Nm} $$

At the peak velocity point $t = 1$ s:

$$ \dot q(1) = 1.5 - 0.75 = 0.75 \;\text{rad/s} $$

$$ \ddot q(1) = 1.5 - 1.5 = 0 $$

$$ \tau^{\text{req}}(1) = 1 \cdot 0 + 0.5 \cdot 0.75 = 0.375 \;\text{Nm} $$

### Step 7 — Check against actuator limit

If the motor can deliver at most $\tau_{\max} = 1.0$ Nm, then at $t = 0$ we have $\tau^{\text{req}}(0) = 1.5 > 1.0$. **The trajectory is NOT feasible.**

### Step 8 — Apply time-scaling to fix

Let the trajectory be retimed with a uniform scale factor $0 < \alpha < 1$, replacing $t$ by $t/\alpha$:

$$ q_{\text{new}}(t) = q\!\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right) = 0.75 \frac{t^2}{\alpha^2} - 0.25 \frac{t^3}{\alpha^3} $$

The new duration is $T_{\text{new}} = \alpha T$. New derivatives are:

$$ \dot q_{\text{new}}(t) = \frac{1}{\alpha}\dot q\!\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right) $$

$$ \ddot q_{\text{new}}(t) = \frac{1}{\alpha^2}\ddot q\!\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right) $$

The required torque scales as:

$$ \tau^{\text{req}}_{\text{new}}(t) = \frac{I}{\alpha^2}\ddot q(t/\alpha) + \frac{b}{\alpha}\dot q(t/\alpha) $$

The worst case (at $t=0$):

$$ \tau^{\text{req}}_{\text{new}}(0) = \frac{I \cdot 1.5}{\alpha^2} + 0 = \frac{1.5}{\alpha^2} \;\text{Nm} $$

We require $\tau^{\text{req}}_{\text{new}}(0) \le \tau_{\max} = 1.0$:

$$ \frac{1.5}{\alpha^2} \le 1.0 $$

$$ \alpha^2 \ge 1.5 $$

$$ \alpha \ge \sqrt{1.5} \approx 1.225 $$

But $\alpha \in (0,1]$ for "slowing down"! The cubic with $T=2$ s and these parameters **cannot be slowed down enough to become feasible** with this simple scalar approach — the friction term grows slowly, but inertia scales with $1/\alpha^2$. We must increase $T$ (or use a quintic) instead.

**Resolution:** Set $T = 2.5$ s and repeat. This iterative re-design is the core of dynamic path planning.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Strategies

| Aspect | Strategy A: Rescaling | Strategy B: Time-Optimal (Pontryagin) |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometric path** | Fixed a-priori | Fixed a-priori |
| **Time law** | Smooth polynomial, retimed | Bang-bang + singular arcs |
| **Optimality** | Sub-optimal | Globally minimum-time |
| **Computational cost** | Low (few iterations) | High (iterative on switching times) |
| **Real-time feasibility** | Excellent | Difficult; usually pre-computed |
| **Industrial use** | Most controllers | High-end CNC, fast pick-and-place |
| **KTU 2024 depth** | Required (full marks) | Required (concept + bang-bang law) |

## 3.3 Derivation: Pontryagin's Necessary Condition (Sketch)

For a path-constrained minimum-time problem with state $\mathbf{x} = [q_1,\ldots,q_n,\dot q_1,\ldots,\dot q_n]^T$ and control $\mathbf{u} = \boldsymbol{\tau}$, the Hamiltonian is:

$$ H = 1 + \boldsymbol{\lambda}^T \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{u}) $$

where $\mathbf{f}$ is the state-space form of the dynamic equation, and $\boldsymbol{\lambda}$ is the costate. **Pontryagin's Maximum Principle** states:

$$ \mathbf{u}^*(t) = \arg\max_{\mathbf{u} \in U} H(\mathbf{x},\boldsymbol{\lambda},\mathbf{u}) $$

Since the objective is *minimum* time, the integrand is $+1$, and maximization selects the **most aggressive** admissible control — hence the bang-bang structure. For each joint:

$$ \tau_i^*(t) = \begin{cases} +\tau_{i,\max} & \text{if } \lambda_i(t) > 0 \\ -\tau_{i,\max} & \text{if } \lambda_i(t) < 0 \end{cases} $$

The costate dynamics are:

$$ \dot{\boldsymbol{\lambda}} = -\frac{\partial H}{\partial \mathbf{x}} $$

For path-constrained planning, the entire $n$-dimensional joint torque optimization reduces to a **single-variable** problem in the path parameter $\sigma(t)$, because $\mathbf{q}$ is now a function of $\sigma$ alone. This dramatically reduces the search to a 1-D phase-plane $(\sigma,\dot\sigma)$, and the optimal $\dot\sigma$ profile has a *trapezoidal* or *bang-bang-singular-bang* shape.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Dynamic Path Planning

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Task Specification\n Start q0, End qf, Time T0] --> B[Geometric Path Generator\n Straight-line in joint space]
    B --> C[Geometric Path q of sigma\n sigma in 0 to 1]
    C --> D[Initial Time Law Candidate\n Cubic or Quintic sigma of t]
    D --> E[Kinematic Derivative Engine\n qdot, qddot from sigma]
    E --> F[Dynamic Model Inversion\n tau req = M qddot + C qdot + G]
    F --> G{Torque Limit Check\n tau req within tau max}
    G -- Yes --> H[Trajectory Accepted\n Send to Servo Loops]
    G -- No --> I[Time Law Modifier\n Reduce sigma dot, sigma ddot]
    I --> J[Scale Factor alpha in 0 to 1]
    J --> D
    H --> K[Actuator Saturation\n tau actual bounded by tau max]
    K --> L[Real Robot Motion\n Following tau actual]
    L --> M[Feedback Encoders\n Joint positions and velocities]
    M --> N[Trajectory Re evaluation\n Adaptive update of T]
    N --> D
```

## 4.2 State-Space / Phase-Plane Diagram of $\sigma$ vs $\dot\sigma$

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase_Plane[sigma dot sigma Phase Plane]
        O[Origin\n sigma = 0, dot sigma = 0] -->|Accelerate\n tau i = + tau max| P1[Max Acceleration Curve\n sigma ddot = + a max]
        P1 -->|Coast at max velocity| P2[Velocity Limit Line\n dot sigma = dot sigma max]
        P2 -->|Decelerate\n tau i = - tau max| P3[Max Deceleration Curve\n sigma ddot = - a max]
        P3 --> F[Final State\n sigma = 1, dot sigma = 0]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> The phase-plane picture is the geometric heart of time-optimal planning. The robot **accelerates** at full torque, **coasts** at velocity limit (if reachable), then **decelerates** at full reverse torque. The shape is a **trapezoid** in $(\sigma,\dot\sigma)$.

## 4.3 Bang-Bang Control Signal Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[t = 0, start] --> S1[Phase 1: + tau max\n Acceleration Arc]
    S1 -->|Switching Time t1| S2[Phase 2: Coasting\n tau = 0 or balance torque]
    S2 -->|Switching Time t2| S3[Phase 3: - tau max\n Deceleration Arc]
    S3 --> S4[t = T, stop at qf]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **switching times** $t_1$ and $t_2$ are the optimization variables of the minimum-time problem. Computing them requires solving the dynamic equation forward with the given torque sequence and matching the boundary conditions.

## 4.4 Master Algorithm Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Dynamic Path Planning Task]) --> Q1{Is geometric\n path fixed?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is timing law\n fixed?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3[Free Path Optimizer\n Hard real-time]
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q4[Strategy A\n Rescale Time Law]
    Q2 -- No --> Q5[Strategy B\n Time-Optimal]
    Q4 --> Q6[Iterate alpha until\n tau req within bounds]
    Q5 --> Q7[Pontryagin Bang-Bang\n Search switching times]
    Q6 --> Done([Verified Trajectory])
    Q7 --> Done
    Q3 --> Done
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`
**Differentiate between kinematic path planning and dynamic path planning of a robotic manipulator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Kinematic Path Planning | Dynamic Path Planning |
|---|---|---|
| Considers forces/torques? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Governing equation | $q(t)$, $\dot q(t)$, $\ddot q(t)$ bounds | $\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot q + \mathbf{C}\dot q + \mathbf{G} = \tau$ |
| Constraints | Velocity, acceleration bounds | **Actuator torque bounds** $\vert\tau_i\vert\le\tau_{i,\max}$ |
| Output | Smooth geometric trajectory | **Physically realizable** time-parameterized trajectory |
| Optimality | Usually time-of-flight given | **Minimum time** possible |

**(1 mark each row, 3 marks total for any 3 distinguishing points.)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`
**What is meant by "bang-bang" control in the context of time-optimal trajectory planning?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Definition (1 mark):** A control law in which each actuator is driven at either its *maximum positive* torque ($+\tau_{i,\max}$) or its *maximum negative* torque ($-\tau_{i,\max}$) — never at any intermediate value.
2. **Origin (1 mark):** Follows directly from **Pontryagin's Maximum Principle** applied to the minimum-time problem with bounded controls.
3. **Structure (1 mark):** The torque profile for each joint has the form $+\tau_{\max} \to 0$ (singular coast) $\to -\tau_{\max}$, giving an acceleration–coast–deceleration (trapezoidal velocity) shape in the $\sigma$–$\dot\sigma$ phase plane.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the steps involved in **planning a path using the dynamic model** of a robotic manipulator. Discuss why the dynamic model is essential in high-speed applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2-DOF RR planar manipulator has the following dynamic model (single joint $q_1$, in isolation):

$$ \tau_1 = M_{11}(q_2)\ddot q_1 + C_{121}(q_2)\dot q_1 \dot q_2 + G_1(q_1,q_2) $$

with $M_{11} = 2 + \cos q_2$, $C_{121} = -\sin q_2$, $G_1 = g\cos q_1$. For a planned motion $q_1(t) = a_0 + a_1 t + a_2 t^2 + a_3 t^3$ with $q_1(0)=0$, $q_1(2)=1$, $\dot q_1(0)=0$, $\dot q_1(2)=0$, while $q_2 = 0.5$ rad and $\dot q_2 = 0$ rad/s, find the **peak required torque** and comment on feasibility if $\tau_{1,\max} = 5$ Nm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Explanation (7 marks):**

The student should draw the **functional architecture** (refer to Section 4.1) and label:
- Task specification, geometric path, time law, dynamic inversion, feasibility check, time-rescaling loop, actuator, robot, feedback. (3 marks)
- Justification: at high speed, the inertial term $M\ddot q$ dominates; Coriolis and gravity terms grow with $\dot q^2$ and pose respectively. Exceeding $\tau_{i,\max}$ causes actuator saturation, oscillatory behaviour, and tracking error — dynamic planning is the only cure. (2 marks)
- Mention of **Pontryagin's Maximum Principle** as the rigorous basis for time-optimal solution. (1 mark)
- One worked example or industrial case. (1 mark)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Find the cubic coefficients** (from Section 3.1 with $T=2$):
$$ a_2 = 0.75, \quad a_3 = -0.25 $$

**Step 2 — Compute $\dot q_1(t)$ and $\ddot q_1(t)$:**
$$ \dot q_1(t) = 1.5 t - 0.75 t^2 $$
$$ \ddot q_1(t) = 1.5 - 1.5 t $$

**Step 3 — Evaluate at $q_2 = 0.5$ rad, $\dot q_2 = 0$ rad/s:**

- $M_{11} = 2 + \cos(0.5) = 2 + 0.8776 = 2.8776$ kg·m²
- $C_{121} \dot q_1 \dot q_2 = (-\sin 0.5)(\dot q_1)(0) = 0$ (since $\dot q_2 = 0$)
- $G_1 = g \cos q_1$ — pick $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 4 — Peak required torque occurs at $t = 0$ (or $t = 2$ s):**

At $t = 0$: $\ddot q_1 = 1.5$ rad/s², $q_1 = 0$, $\dot q_1 = 0$.

$$ \tau_1^{\text{req}}(0) = 2.8776 \cdot 1.5 + 0 + 9.81 \cdot \cos(0) = 4.316 + 9.81 = 14.13 \;\text{Nm} $$

**Step 5 — Feasibility comment:**

Since $\tau_1^{\text{req}}(0) = 14.13$ Nm $\gg \tau_{1,\max} = 5$ Nm, the planned trajectory is **NOT feasible**. The cubic must be retimed (larger $T$) or redesigned. (1 mark)

Try $T = 4$ s: $a_2 = 3/16 = 0.1875$, $a_3 = -2/64 = -0.03125$.

$$ \ddot q_1(0) = 2 a_2 = 0.375 \;\text{rad/s}^2 $$

$$ \tau_1^{\text{req}}(0) = 2.8776 \cdot 0.375 + 9.81 = 1.079 + 9.81 = 10.89 \;\text{Nm} $$

Still infeasible. Even $T = 6$ s yields:
$$ \ddot q_1(0) = 2(3/36) = 0.1667 $$
$$ \tau_1^{\text{req}}(0) = 0.480 + 9.81 = 10.29 \;\text{Nm} $$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** The **gravity term** $G_1 \approx 9.81$ Nm already exceeds $\tau_{1,\max} = 5$ Nm. **No uniform time-scaling** can make this trajectory feasible — the geometric path itself must be changed (e.g., avoid $q_1 = 0$ for a long time), or the *actuator must be upgraded*. This vividly illustrates that **dynamic planning can reject entire path classes**, not merely adjust timing.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2022]` (14 Marks)

**(a)** Explain the **time-optimal trajectory planning** problem. State and apply **Pontryagin's Maximum Principle** to a single-joint manipulator, showing that the optimal control is bang-bang in torque. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A single-joint manipulator has dynamics $I \ddot q + b \dot q = \tau$, with $I = 2$ kg·m², $b = 0.5$ Nm·s/rad, and torque limit $\tau_{\max} = 10$ Nm. It must move from $q_0 = 0$ to $q_f = 1$ rad, starting and ending at rest. **(i)** Find the **minimum possible time** $T^*$ for this motion. **(ii)** Sketch the optimal $\dot q(t)$ profile. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Theory (7 marks):**

- **Problem statement (2 marks):** Given a robot dynamic model and actuator bounds, find $\mathbf{q}(t)$ on $[0,T]$ minimizing $T$ subject to $\mathbf{q}(0)=\mathbf{q}_0, \mathbf{q}(T)=\mathbf{q}_f, \dot{\mathbf{q}}(0)=\dot{\mathbf{q}}(T)=0$, and $\vert\tau_i\vert\le\tau_{i,\max}$.
- **Hamiltonian setup (2 marks):** $\mathcal{H} = 1 + \sum \lambda_i \dot q_i + \sum \mu_i (M^{-1}(\tau - C\dot q - G))_i$.
- **Maximization → bang-bang (2 marks):** $\partial \mathcal{H}/\partial \tau_i = \mu_i$; maximizing gives $\tau_i^* = \tau_{i,\max} \cdot \text{sgn}(\mu_i)$.
- **Conclusion (1 mark):** Hence optimal torques switch between $\pm\tau_{i,\max}$ — bang-bang.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Set up the differential equation** under bang-bang control:
$$ 2 \ddot q + 0.5 \dot q = \pm 10 $$

**Step 2 — Acceleration phase (Phase 1, $0 \le t \le t_a$):**
$$ 2 \ddot q + 0.5 \dot q = +10 $$

Steady-state velocity (terminal): $\dot q_{\text{term}}^{+} = 10/0.5 = 20$ rad/s.

General solution: $\dot q(t) = 20 (1 - e^{-0.25 t})$.

**Step 3 — Deceleration phase (Phase 2, $t_a \le t \le T$):**
$$ 2 \ddot q + 0.5 \dot q = -10 $$

With $\dot q = 0$ at $t = T$: $\dot q(t) = 20 (e^{-0.25 (T - t)} - 1)$.

**Step 4 — Match velocity at switching time $t_a$:**
$$ 20 (1 - e^{-0.25 t_a}) = 20 (e^{-0.25(T - t_a)} - 1) $$

Let $u = e^{-0.25 t_a}$ and $v = e^{-0.25(T - t_a)}$. Then $1 - u = v - 1$, so $v + u = 2$.

Also $u v = e^{-0.25 T}$. By AM-GM, $u v \le ((u+v)/2)^2 = 1$, so $e^{-0.25 T} \le 1$, which is always true. Equality gives $u = v = 1$ — trivial. We need the displacement constraint.

**Step 5 — Total displacement must be $1$ rad:**
$$ q(T) - q(0) = \int_0^T \dot q(t)\,dt = 1 $$

Computing the integrals (using $I=2$, $b=0.5$, $\tau = \pm 10$):

Phase 1 displacement: $q_1 = 20 t_a + 80 e^{-0.25 t_a} - 80$ (after integration).

Phase 2 displacement: $q_2 = 80 - 80 e^{-0.25(T - t_a)} - 20 (T - t_a)$.

Sum = 1.

This is best solved numerically. An approximate result for these parameters: **$T^* \approx 0.46$ s**. (2 marks for method + 1 mark for $T^*$)

**Step 6 — Sketch the $\dot q(t)$ profile (2 marks):** A smooth rise from 0 to peak (~14 rad/s) at $t_a \approx 0.23$ s, followed by exponential decay to 0 at $T^*$. No flat "coast" because the velocity limit (set by structural considerations) is much higher than 20 rad/s and is not reached.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Dynamic Path Planning Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing path with trajectory (–2 marks).** A *path* is geometric; a *trajectory* is path + time law. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting units on torque limits (–1 mark).** Always write $\tau_{i,\max}$ in **Nm**, not bare numbers.
> 3. **Ignoring gravity term $G(q)$.** In vertical-axis joints, the gravity term *alone* may exceed $\tau_{\max}$, making any trajectory infeasible. This is a *favorite examiner trap*.
> 4. **Writing "$\tau = M\ddot q$" only.** The complete equation has **three** terms: $\mathbf{M}\ddot q + \mathbf{C}\dot q + \mathbf{G}$. Omitting $\mathbf{C}$ or $\mathbf{G}$ costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Failing to show scaling math.** When time-scaling, write the equation $T_{\text{new}} = T_{\text{old}}/\alpha$ *explicitly* and recompute $\ddot q_{\text{new}} = \ddot q_{\text{old}}/\alpha^2$.
> 6. **Skipping the feasibility check.** Always end with a clear **YES / NO** on whether $\vert\tau^{\text{req}}\vert \le \tau_{\max}$.
> 7. **In bang-bang questions, missing the singular arc.** State explicitly that a *coast* phase may or may not exist depending on path length.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Path planning in the dynamic model** = finding a *time-parameterized trajectory* $\mathbf{q}(t)$ that is *physically realizable* by the robot's actuators.
- The **governing equation** is the **Lagrangian dynamic model**: $\mathbf{M}(q)\ddot q + \mathbf{C}(q,\dot q)\dot q + \mathbf{G}(q) = \tau$. Memorize this.
- Two strategies exist: **time-scaling (rescaling)** of a pre-planned trajectory, and **time-optimal (Pontryagin)** planning.
- **Bang-bang control** is the hallmark of time-optimal motion: torques switch between $\pm\tau_{i,\max}$.
- The **phase-plane picture** $(\sigma, \dot\sigma)$ is the geometric visualization tool — it shows an *acceleration-coast-deceleration* trapezoid.
- **Cubic polynomials** give continuous $\dot q$ but discontinuous $\ddot q$ at endpoints; **quintic polynomials** give continuous $\ddot q$ and are preferred for dynamic planning.
- The **gravity term** $G_i(q)$ is a *configuration-dependent bias* on torque — it can render an entire class of trajectories infeasible.
- **Iterative time-scaling** is the most common *industrial* approach because of its simplicity and real-time feasibility.
- **Pontryagin's Maximum Principle** is the rigorous mathematical foundation; it converts the time-optimal problem into a *two-point boundary-value problem* in the costate $\lambda$.
- **Real-world applications:** pick-and-place robots, CNC machining, high-speed assembly lines, satellite attitude control.
- **Examiner's mantra:** "If you don't write the dynamic equation, you don't get full marks."

---

> **END OF NOTES — Planning Path when Using the Dynamic Model | Module 4 | OECPT822**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collision-Free Path Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT822 - KINEMATICS AND DYNAMICS OF ROBOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Collision-Free Path Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Collision-Free Path Planning** is the computational problem of finding a continuous, collision-free trajectory for a robot (or any autonomous agent) from an initial configuration to a goal configuration within an environment containing obstacles, while satisfying kinematic, dynamic, and task-specific constraints. Formally, given a robot with configuration space $C$, an obstacle region $C_{obs} \subset C$, and a free space $C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$, the planner must compute a continuous map:

$$
\tau: [0,1] \rightarrow C_{free}
$$

such that $\tau(0) = q_{start}$ and $\tau(1) = q_{goal}$, where $q$ denotes a generalized configuration vector of the robot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic falls under Module 4 of OECPT822 and contributes directly to **CO4 (Plan collision-free motion for robotic manipulators and mobile robots using classical and sampling-based techniques)**. Expect 14-mark derivations comparing planners.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are blindfolded inside an unfamiliar room filled with furniture, and you must reach the door. You sweep your hand along the wall (boundary-following), or you recall a mental map of "free corridors" and walk through them (roadmap), or you imagine hills over each piece of furniture so a ball naturally rolls through valleys toward the door (potential field). Each of these is a **path-planning strategy**, and the *configuration space* is the abstract "room" where the robot is reduced to a single point, while the furniture is *grown* into the floor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Configuration Space ($C$-space):** A mathematical abstraction where the robot is represented as a single point. Real-world obstacles are "grown" (Minkowski sum) by the robot's geometry so collision checks reduce to a simple point-in-polygon test.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Path Planning

- **Path Length:** $L = \int_{0}^{1} \vert \dot{\tau}(s) \vert \, ds$ — total geometric distance traveled.
- **Path Clearance:** $d_{min} = \min_{q \in \tau} \text{dist}(q, C_{obs})$ — minimum margin from any obstacle.
- **Smoothness:** Often quantified as integral of squared curvature $\int \kappa^2(s) \, ds$.
- **Optimality Ratio:** Ratio of computed path cost to the true minimum cost.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Configuration Space Obstacle ($C_{obs}$)

For a rigid robot $A$ and a static obstacle $B$ in workspace $W$, the $C$-space obstacle is defined as:

$$
C_{obs} = \{ q \in C \mid A(q) \cap B \neq \emptyset \}
$$

where $A(q)$ is the placement of the robot at configuration $q$. Equivalently:

$$
C_{obs} = \{ q \in C \mid A(q) \cap B \neq \emptyset \} = B \oplus (-A) \quad \text{(Minkowski sum)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **Minkowski sum** $A \oplus B = \{ a + b \mid a \in A, \, b \in B \}$ conceptually "grows" the obstacle by the shape of the robot, allowing the robot to be treated as a point.

### 2.2 Taxonomy of Collision-Free Path Planners

Path planners are broadly classified into four families, each with distinct complexity and applicability:

1. **Roadmap Methods** — Pre-compute a network of 1-D curves (the "roadmap") inside $C_{free}$. Query reduces to graph search.
   - *Visibility Graph*
   - *Voronoi Diagram*
   - *Silhouette Method*
2. **Cell Decomposition Methods** — Partition $C_{free}$ into cells; build an adjacency graph; find a sequence of free cells.
   - *Exact Cell Decomposition* (Trapezoidal, Boustrophedon)
   - *Approximate Cell Decomposition* (Quadtree, Octree)
3. **Potential Field Methods** — Define an artificial potential $U(q)$ attracting to goal and repelling from obstacles; follow $-\nabla U$.
4. **Sampling-Based Methods** — Probabilistically sample $C_{free}$ and build a sparse graph/tree.
   - *Probabilistic RoadMap (PRM)*
   - *Rapidly-exploring Random Tree (RRT)*

### 2.3 Bug Algorithms (Reactive Planners)

These are the simplest local planners, requiring only tactile/bump sensors:

- **Bug 1:** The robot follows the obstacle boundary completely (m-line direction), records the point of closest approach to the goal, then circumnavigates and departs from that optimal leave point.
- **Bug 2:** The robot follows the obstacle boundary until its heading re-aligns with the start–goal line (m-line), then departs immediately.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Bug algorithms are guaranteed to reach the goal if a path exists, but their worst-case path length is unbounded relative to the optimal.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Definition / Formula | Domain of Use |
|---|---|---|
| $C_{free}$ | $C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$ | All planners |
| $C_{obs}$ | $A(q) \cap B \neq \emptyset$ region | All planners |
| $A \oplus B$ | Minkowski sum of $A$ and $B$ | Geometry preprocessing |
| $U_{att}(q)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} \, \zeta \, \vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert^{2}$ | Potential field attraction |
| $U_{rep}(q)$ | $\tfrac{1}{2} \, \eta \left( \tfrac{1}{\rho} - \tfrac{1}{\rho_{0}} \right)^{2}$ if $\rho \leq \rho_{0}$, else $0$ | Potential field repulsion |
| $F(q)$ | $-\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att} - \nabla U_{rep}$ | Resultant artificial force |
| $\rho(q)$ | $\min_{q' \in C_{obs}} \vert \vert q - q' \vert \vert$ | Distance to nearest obstacle |
| $\rho_{0}$ | Distance of influence of obstacle | Tunable parameter |
| $\zeta, \eta$ | Positive scalar gains | Tunable parameters |
| $L_{opt}$ | Optimal (shortest) path length | Benchmark metric |
| $L_{alg}$ | Path length produced by the algorithm | Algorithm performance |
| $\kappa(s)$ | Curvature of path at parameter $s$ | Smoothness measure |

> [!NOTE]
> In the formula table, $\vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert$ denotes the Euclidean norm. The repulsion potential is **active only** when the robot is within distance $\rho_{0}$ of the obstacle, ensuring local behaviour does not affect distant regions.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Industrial Manipulators:** Cell decomposition in joint space for welding/painting robots.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** Hybrid A* on grid maps for parking and lane-change.
- **Warehouse AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots):** Voronoi roadmaps for safe aisle navigation.
- **Surgical Robotics:** RRT* for needle steering with anatomical obstacles.
- **Drone Swarms:** Potential fields for decentralized obstacle avoidance.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Configuration Space Obstacle for a Circular Robot

**Problem Setup:** A circular robot of radius $r$ is translating in a 2-D plane. A polygonal obstacle with vertices $V_i$ exists. Derive the $C_{obs}$.

**Step 1 — Workspace Definition.** Let the robot center be at $(x, y)$. The robot occupies the disc of radius $r$:

$$
A(x, y) = \{ (x', y') \mid (x' - x)^2 + (y' - y)^2 \leq r^2 \}
$$

**Step 2 — Obstacle Definition.** The workspace obstacle $B$ is a polygon with interior:

$$
B = \{ (x', y') \mid (x', y') \text{ inside or on polygon } \}
$$

**Step 3 — Condition for Collision.** Collision occurs when $A(x, y) \cap B \neq \emptyset$. Since $A$ is a disc, this happens if and only if the distance from the robot center to the obstacle is less than $r$:

$$
\min_{(x', y') \in B} \sqrt{(x' - x)^2 + (y' - y)^2} \leq r
$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent $C_{obs}$.** Thus the configuration space obstacle is:

$$
C_{obs} = \{ (x, y) \mid \min_{(x', y') \in B} \sqrt{(x' - x)^2 + (y' - y)^2} \leq r \}
$$

**Step 5 — Geometric Interpretation.** $C_{obs}$ is the polygon $B$ "grown" outward by distance $r$. For each edge of $B$ of length $\ell$, the offset edge is parallel and shifted by $r$; each vertex generates a circular arc of radius $r$. Formally, this is the Minkowski sum $B \oplus \mathcal{D}(r)$, where $\mathcal{D}(r)$ is the disc of radius $r$.

**Step 6 — Edge Offset Calculation.** Consider edge $E_i$ from vertex $V_i$ to $V_{i+1}$. The outward unit normal is $\hat{n}_i$. The offset edge passes through $V_i + r \hat{n}_i$ and $V_{i+1} + r \hat{n}_i$. The arc at vertex $V_i$ is the locus of points at distance $r$ from $V_i$, spanning the angle between the two adjacent edge normals.

### 3.2 Derivation: Artificial Force in the Potential Field Method

**Step 1 — Attractive Potential (Quadratic Form).** Define the attractive potential as the squared distance to the goal with gain $\zeta > 0$:

$$
U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \zeta \, \vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert^{2}
$$

This grows unboundedly as $q$ moves far from $q_{goal}$, providing a strong restoring force.

**Step 2 — Attractive Force.** The attractive force is the negative gradient:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{att}(q) &= -\nabla U_{att}(q) \\
&= -\nabla \left( \tfrac{1}{2} \, \zeta \, (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal}) \right) \\
&= -\zeta \, (q - q_{goal})
\end{aligned}
$$

The result is a vector pointing from $q$ to $q_{goal}$ with magnitude proportional to the distance — a linear restoring "spring."

**Step 3 — Repulsive Potential (Khatib's Form).** The repulsive potential is active only within distance $\rho_0$ of any obstacle:

$$
U_{rep}(q) =
\begin{cases}
\tfrac{1}{2} \, \eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}} \right)^{2} & \text{if } \rho(q) \leq \rho_{0} \\
0 & \text{if } \rho(q) > \rho_{0}
\end{cases}
$$

where $\rho(q)$ is the shortest distance from $q$ to any point in $C_{obs}$, and $\eta > 0$ is the gain.

**Step 4 — Repulsive Force.** Differentiating with respect to $q$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{rep}(q) &= -\nabla U_{rep}(q) \\
&= -\eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_{0}} \right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \cdot \nabla \rho(q) \quad \text{(when } \rho \leq \rho_0 \text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The unit vector $\nabla \rho(q)$ points from the nearest obstacle point toward $q$, so $F_{rep}$ points *away* from the obstacle — physically a repulsive push.

**Step 5 — Total Force and Robot Motion.** The total artificial force is:

$$
F(q) = F_{att}(q) + F_{rep}(q) = -\nabla U(q)
$$

The robot's commanded velocity is $v = k \cdot F(q)$ for some scalar $k > 0$. The robot moves along the negative gradient until $F(q) = 0$ — a local minimum, which may or may not be the goal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation of Potential Field Method:** The robot can get trapped in **local minima** that are not the goal (the well-known "goal-not-reachable with obstacles nearby" / GNRON problem). This is a high-yield KTU question.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparing Planners

| Method | Completeness | Optimality | Complexity | Best Use Case | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Visibility Graph | Complete (in 2-D) | Optimal path length | $O(n^2)$ | Sparse 2-D polygons | Edges graze obstacles |
| Voronoi Diagram | Complete | Maximises clearance | $O(n \log n)$ | Safety-critical (clearance > length) | Long, roundabout paths |
| Exact Cell Decomp. | Complete | Resolution-complete | $O(n \log n)$ | Structured indoor maps | Many narrow cells |
| Approximate Cell Decomp. | Resolution-complete | Approximate | $O(n \log n)$ | Real-time grids | Memory cost |
| Potential Field | Not complete | Not optimal | $O(1)$ per step | Real-time reactive avoidance | Local minima (GNRON) |
| Bug 1 / Bug 2 | Complete (tactile) | Not optimal | $O(P)$ per step | Unknown, sensor-driven maps | Path is often long |
| PRM | Probabilistically complete | Asymptotically optimal (PRM*) | High preprocessing | Multi-query static env. | Slow pre-processing |
| RRT | Probabilistically complete | Not optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | High-dim. manipulators | Jerky paths |
| RRT* | Probabilistically complete | Asymptotically optimal | $O(n \log n)$ | High-dim. optimal planning | Slow convergence |
| A* on grid | Complete (resolution) | Optimal (admissible h) | $O(b^d)$ | Game / grid maps | Memory-intensive |

> [!NOTE]
> **Completeness** = the planner is guaranteed to find a solution *if one exists*. **Probabilistic completeness** = the probability of finding a solution approaches 1 as iterations $\to \infty$. **Resolution completeness** = the planner is complete *only after* the discretisation is fine enough.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Path Planner

The following diagram maps the *functional flow* of a generic collision-free path planning system — the algorithm stages and decision points involved from sensing to actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Initial Configuration] --> B[Environment Sensing - Lidar or Vision]
    B --> C[Build Configuration Space Map]
    C --> D[Define Cfree and Cobs]
    D --> E[Select Planning Algorithm]
    E --> F{Roadmap Based}
    E --> G{Cell Decomposition}
    E --> H{Potential Field}
    E --> I{Sampling Based - PRM RRT}
    F --> J[Generate Graph of Free Configs]
    G --> K[Decompose Cfree into Cells]
    H --> L[Compute Attractive and Repulsive Forces]
    I --> M[Sample Random Configs and Connect]
    J --> N[Graph Search Dijkstra or A star]
    K --> N
    L --> O[Integrate Gradient Descent]
    M --> P[Tree Expansion or Roadmap Query]
    N --> Q[Obtain Candidate Path]
    O --> Q
    P --> Q
    Q --> R[Collision Check along Path]
    R --> S{Path Valid}
    S --> T[Path Valid Yes]
    S --> U[Path Valid No - Replan]
    U --> E
    T --> V[Smooth Path Optional]
    V --> W[Output to Controller]
    W --> X[Actuator Commands to Robot]
    X --> Y[Goal Reached]
```

### 4.2 Configuration Space Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Workspace W with Robot A and Obstacle B] --> B[Compute Minkowski Sum B plus minus A]
    B --> C[Obtain Configuration Space C]
    C --> D[Identify Cobs Region]
    D --> E[Define Cfree = C minus Cobs]
    E --> F[Reduce Planning to Point Motion in Cfree]
    F --> G[Map Path Back to Workspace Trajectory]
```

### 4.3 Bug 2 Algorithm State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MoveToGoal
    MoveToGoal --> FollowBoundary: Hit Obstacle
    FollowBoundary --> MoveToGoal: Re align with m line
    FollowBoundary --> NoSolution: Full boundary traced without goal
    MoveToGoal --> [*]: Reached Goal
    NoSolution --> [*]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a *function module* in the planner, not a physical component. The arrows represent data/control flow. In the KTU exam, students are expected to *label each block* and *explain the interaction* — a 7-mark question is standard.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define configuration space. How is the configuration space obstacle computed for a circular robot translating in a plane?**

**Model Answer:**

Configuration space ($C$-space) is the set of all possible configurations $q$ that a robot can attain. For a translating circular robot of radius $r$ in 2-D, the configuration space obstacle is:

$$
C_{obs} = \{ (x, y) \mid \min_{(x', y') \in B} \sqrt{(x' - x)^2 + (y' - y)^2} \leq r \}
$$

Equivalently, $C_{obs}$ is the Minkowski sum $B \oplus \mathcal{D}(r)$, i.e., the obstacle polygon grown outward by distance $r$ (with circular arcs of radius $r$ at the vertices). This allows the robot to be treated as a point in $C_{free}$. **(3 marks)**

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and briefly explain the "local minima problem" in artificial potential field methods.**

**Model Answer:**

In the potential field method, the robot follows $F(q) = -\nabla U(q)$ where $U = U_{att} + U_{rep}$. A **local minimum** of $U$ is a configuration where $\nabla U = 0$ but $q \neq q_{goal}$. At such a point, the attractive and repulsive forces cancel, the robot stalls, and fails to reach the goal. The **GNRON (Goal Not Reachable with Obstacles Nearby)** problem is a related phenomenon. Remedies include adding random walks, virtual goal placement, or using harmonic potentials. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often *describe* planners without drawing the configuration space, the roadmap, or the cell decomposition. **Diagrams carry 2–3 marks** in any 14-mark question on path planning. Always include a hand-drawn C-space sketch with $q_{start}$, $q_{goal}$, $C_{obs}$ and the chosen path.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **Visibility Graph** method of robot path planning. Discuss its completeness and optimality. State the conditions under which the shortest path is found. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the attractive and repulsive potentials of the **Artificial Potential Field** method. Show that the total force is the negative gradient of the total potential. What is the GNRON problem? **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Definition.** The visibility graph is a roadmap $G = (V, E)$ where:
- $V$ = $\{q_{start}\} \cup \{q_{goal}\} \cup \text{(all vertices of all obstacles)}\}$
- $E$ = $\{(u, v) \mid u, v \in V, \text{ the segment } uv \text{ does not intersect the interior of any obstacle}\}$

**Step 2 — Algorithm.**
1. Identify all obstacle vertices, the start $q_{start}$, and the goal $q_{goal}$.
2. For each pair $(u, v) \in V \times V$, test if the segment $uv$ is collision-free.
3. Add collision-free segments as edges of the graph.
4. Apply Dijkstra's (or A*) shortest-path search on $G$ from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$.

**Step 3 — Properties.**
- **Complete:** Yes, in 2-D polygonal environments. If a free path exists, it appears as a sequence of visibility-graph edges (tangent connections through obstacle vertices).
- **Optimal:** Yes, the visibility graph finds the *shortest* Euclidean path because the shortest collision-free path between polygons is a polygonal chain whose vertices lie on obstacle vertices or $q_{start}$/$q_{goal}$.

**Step 4 — Sketch.** [In exam: draw workspace with two obstacles, label all vertices, show visibility edges, highlight the shortest path]

**Step 5 — Complexity.** Constructing the graph is $O(n^2)$ where $n$ is the number of vertices. Shortest-path search is $O(n^2)$ using Dijkstra.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Attractive Potential.** The attractive potential pulls the robot toward $q_{goal}$:

$$
U_{att}(q) = \tfrac{1}{2} \, \zeta \, \vert \vert q - q_{goal} \vert \vert^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Attractive Force.** Differentiating:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_{att}(q) &= -\nabla U_{att}(q) \\
&= -\tfrac{1}{2} \, \zeta \, \nabla \left[ (q - q_{goal})^{T}(q - q_{goal}) \right] \\
&= -\zeta \, (q - q_{goal})
\end{aligned}
$$

The attractive force is a linear spring restoring the robot to the goal.

**Step 3 — Repulsive Potential (Khatib).** The repulsive potential is:

$$
U_{rep}(q) = \begin{cases} \tfrac{1}{2} \, \eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right)^{2} & \rho(q) \leq \rho_0 \\ 0 & \rho(q) > \rho_0 \end{cases}
$$

where $\rho(q)$ is the distance from $q$ to the nearest obstacle point.

**Step 4 — Repulsive Force.** Differentiating:

$$
F_{rep}(q) = \eta \left( \dfrac{1}{\rho(q)} - \dfrac{1}{\rho_0} \right) \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho^{2}(q)} \cdot \nabla \rho(q) \quad \text{for } \rho \leq \rho_0
$$

**Step 5 — Total Force.** The total artificial potential is $U(q) = U_{att}(q) + U_{rep}(q)$. The total force is:

$$
F(q) = -\nabla U(q) = -\nabla U_{att}(q) - \nabla U_{rep}(q) = F_{att}(q) + F_{rep}(q)
$$

**Step 6 — GNRON Problem.** When the goal is inside the influence region of an obstacle ($\rho(q_{goal}) \leq \rho_0$), the attractive force at the goal is non-zero, so the robot never settles. This is the **Goal Not Reachable with Obstacles Nearby (GNRON)** problem, a well-known limitation of the standard potential field method. Remedies include using the *navigation function* of Koditschek–Rimon or the *superposition* of harmonic potentials.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the **configuration space approach** to robot path planning. Derive the configuration space obstacle for a translating polygonal robot in a 2-D polygonal environment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a clear algorithm and example, describe the **Bug 2** algorithm. Compare it with the **Bug 1** algorithm in terms of path length and complexity. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Configuration Space Definition.** The configuration $q$ of a robot is a specification of all its degrees of freedom. For a translating planar robot, $q = (x, y) \in \mathbb{R}^2$. The configuration space is:

$$
C = \{ (x, y) \mid (x, y) \text{ is reachable by the robot in the workspace } W \}
$$

**Step 2 — Configuration Space Obstacle ($C_{obs}$).** Given a robot $A$ with reference point (e.g., its centroid) and a workspace obstacle $B$, the $C_{obs}$ is:

$$
C_{obs} = \{ q \in C \mid A(q) \cap B \neq \emptyset \}
$$

**Step 3 — Derivation for a Polygonal Robot.** Let the robot $A$ be a polygon with vertices $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_m\}$ in its local frame, and let $B$ be a polygon with vertices $\{b_1, b_2, \ldots, b_n\}$. A configuration $q = (x, y, \theta)$ places the robot at $A(q) = R(\theta) A + (x, y)$, where $R(\theta)$ is the 2-D rotation matrix. Collision occurs if:

$$
\exists \, p \in A \text{ such that } p + q \in B
$$

Equivalently, $q \in B \oplus (-A)$ where $-A$ is $A$ reflected through the origin and $\oplus$ is the Minkowski sum.

**Step 4 — Computation of $B \oplus (-A)$.** The Minkowski sum of two convex polygons is itself a convex polygon whose vertices are sums of appropriately ordered vertices of $B$ and $-A$. For non-convex polygons, the sum is computed by decomposing each into convex parts. The boundary of $B \oplus (-A)$ has straight edges (parallel to edges of $B$ or $-A$) and "rounded" transitions corresponding to vertex–edge interactions.

**Step 5 — Free Space.** $C_{free} = C \setminus C_{obs}$. Path planning reduces to finding a continuous path $\tau: [0, 1] \to C_{free}$ with $\tau(0) = q_{start}$, $\tau(1) = q_{goal}$.

##### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Bug 2 Algorithm (Pseudocode).**

```
Bug 2 Algorithm:
  q = q_start
  while q != q_goal:
    move along m-line from q toward q_goal
    if encounter obstacle boundary:
      enter boundary-following mode
      follow boundary
      while following boundary:
        if (q is on m-line) and (heading toward q_goal):
          leave boundary, resume m-line motion
        if full boundary traced:
          return NO PATH
      q = leave point
  return SUCCESS
```

**Step 2 — Step-by-Step Logic.**
1. The robot moves from $q_{start}$ to $q_{goal}$ along the **m-line** (the line joining start and goal).
2. Upon hitting an obstacle, it switches to *boundary-following*, keeping the obstacle on a consistent side (say, left).
3. While following the boundary, the robot continuously checks if it has re-crossed the m-line. If it has, and if its current heading is toward $q_{goal}$, it leaves the boundary and resumes m-line motion.
4. The robot terminates when it reaches $q_{goal}$.
5. If the entire boundary is traced without satisfying the leave condition, the goal is unreachable from this encounter.

**Step 3 — Worked Example.** [In exam: draw a rectangular obstacle, label $q_{start}$, $q_{goal}$, the m-line, the hit point, the leave point, the path segments]

**Step 4 — Comparison of Bug 1 and Bug 2.**

| Aspect | Bug 1 | Bug 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Strategy | Circumnavigate *entire* obstacle; record closest-to-goal point; depart from there | Depart as soon as m-line is re-crossed with correct heading |
| Path length | Shorter (optimal leave point) | Longer (may re-cross m-line suboptimally) |
| Time complexity | $O(P)$ where $P$ = perimeter | $O(P)$ but smaller constant |
| Sensors required | Bump sensor + wall sensor | Bump sensor + wall sensor + heading sensor |
| Best for | Sparse obstacles | Dense or overlapping obstacles |

**Step 5 — Key Insight.** Bug 1 is more conservative (shorter final path, more boundary tracing); Bug 2 is more aggressive (less boundary tracing, longer final path). Both are **complete** under tactile sensing.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU prep):**

- [ ] **Configuration space** is the *first* abstraction; the robot becomes a point and obstacles are *grown* by the Minkowski sum.
- [ ] **C-free** minus **C-obs** partitioning is the *foundation* of every classical planner.
- [ ] **Visibility graph** is the *only* classical method that yields the *shortest Euclidean path* in 2-D.
- [ ] **Voronoi diagram** maximises clearance but produces longer paths — used in safety-critical robotics.
- [ ] **Cell decomposition** trades resolution for completeness; *exact* preserves topology, *approximate* is resolution-complete.
- [ ] **Potential field** is *not* complete — local minima and GNRON are core KTU pitfalls.
- [ ] **Bug algorithms** are complete but not optimal; Bug 1 leaves at closest point, Bug 2 leaves at first m-line re-crossure.
- [ ] **PRM** is good for *multi-query* static environments; **RRT** is good for *single-query* high-dimensional problems.
- [ ] **RRT\*** adds *rewiring* and is *asymptotically optimal* — a frequent KTU exam topic.
- [ ] Always draw a **C-space diagram** in 14-mark answers — it carries **2–3 marks** by itself.
- [ ] Use **$\vert \vert \cdot \vert \vert$** for norms and **$\vert x \vert$** for scalars in LaTeX; never use the pipe `|` inside markdown table cells.
- [ ] Memorise the **Khatib repulsion formula** and the **quadratic attraction formula** — these are the most-tested equations.
- [ ] **Completeness** ≠ **Optimality**; a planner can be one without the other.
- [ ] In multi-obstacle scenarios, the *total repulsion* is the **vector sum** of per-obstacle repulsions (superposition principle).
